topics.title: Definition of Adhesives and Adhesive Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of Adhesives and Adhesive Bonding ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (ASTM D 907 Standard Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Adhesive:** A substance capable of holding materials together by surface attachment. It is a non-metallic binder that develops its holding power through adhesion (surface attachment) and cohesion (internal strength). **Adhesive Bonding:** The process of joining two similar or dissimilar materials (adherends) using a non-metallic substance (adhesive) through surface attachment, producing a bonded assembly with measurable joint strength. ### 1.2 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning (KTU Exam Definition) | |---|---| | **Adhesive** | Non-metallic substance that bonds materials via surface attachment | | **Adherend** | The material/substrate being bonded | | **Adhesive Bond** | The union formed between adhesive and adherend | | **Adhesion** | Force holding adhesive to substrate (interfacial) | | **Cohesion** | Internal molecular force within the adhesive itself | | **Substrate** | Base material surface on which adhesive is applied | | **Wetting** | Ability of liquid adhesive to spread over substrate surface | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "Sticky Notes & Molecular Velcro"** > Imagine you place a sticky note on your desk. The note sticks because: > (a) The glue **wets/spreads** over the desk surface (microscopic contact), > (b) The glue forms **surface grip** at the molecular level (adhesion), and > (c) The glue itself doesn't tear apart (cohesion). > > Adhesive bonding works exactly the same: an adhesive flows, wets the substrate, develops **adhesion** at the interface, and maintains **cohesion** within itself. If either adhesion or cohesion fails, the joint fails. ### 1.4 Classification Overview Adhesives are classified by: - **Origin:** Natural (animal glue, starch) vs. Synthetic (epoxy, acrylic, polyurethane) - **Setting mechanism:** Thermosetting, thermoplastic, elastomeric - **Form:** Liquid, paste, film, powder - **Load-bearing capability:** Structural vs. Non-structural > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric (KTU):** The standard unit for measuring adhesive joint strength is **MPa (Mega Pascal)** for stress and **N/mm** for load-bearing capacity. Standard test method: **ASTM D 1002** (single lap shear). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Adhesive Bonding Process — Step Logic The formation of a strong adhesive bond is governed by a sequence of physical and chemical events: 1. **Wetting of the Substrate:** Liquid adhesive must flow and make intimate molecular contact with the adherend surface. Quantified using **contact angle (θ)**. 2. **Adhesion Development:** Interfacial forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, chemisorption) form at the adhesive-substrate interface. 3. **Solidification/Curing:** The adhesive transforms (by cooling, solvent loss, or chemical reaction) into a solid mass. 4. **Cohesion Development:** Internal strength of the cured adhesive must exceed applied load to prevent cohesive failure. 5. **Load Transfer:** The joint transfers mechanical load from one adherend through the adhesive layer to the other adherend. ### 2.2 Adhesion vs. Cohesion — The "Strength Balance" > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** A high-performance adhesive joint requires BOTH high adhesion (interface grip) and high cohesion (internal strength). The weaker of the two determines the joint failure mode. | Property | Location | Failure Mode If Weak | |---|---|---| | **Adhesion** | Adhesive ↔ Substrate interface | *Adhesive Failure* (clean substrate surface) | | **Cohesion** | Within the adhesive bulk | *Cohesive Failure* (residue on both adherends) | | **Substrate strength** | In the adherend itself | *Substrate Failure* (material itself breaks) | ### 2.3 The Five Theories of Adhesion 1. **Mechanical Interlocking Theory** — Adhesive flows into surface pores/roughness and locks mechanically. 2. **Adsorption (Wetting) Theory** — Molecular contact via van der Waals forces; requires good wetting (low contact angle). 3. **Chemical Bonding Theory** — Primary chemical bonds (covalent, ionic) form across the interface. 4. **Diffusion Theory** — Polymer chains interdiffuse across the interface (applies to polymer-polymer bonding). 5. **Electrostatic Theory** — Charge transfer across the interface creates an electrical double layer. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for Board Exams** | Formula | Expression | Variables / Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Young's Equation** | $\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid-vapor surface energy, $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid-liquid interfacial energy, $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapor surface tension (all in **N/m**), $\theta$ = contact angle | Determines wetting ability of adhesive on substrate | | **Dupré Work of Adhesion** | $W_{A} \;=\; \gamma_{S} \;+\; \gamma_{L} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$ | $\gamma_{S}$ = substrate surface energy, $\gamma_{L}$ = liquid surface tension, $W_{A}$ in **J/m²** | Energy released when adhesive detaches from substrate | | **Young–Dupré Combined** | $W_{A} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ | Same as above | Direct calculation of work of adhesion from contact angle | | **Lap Shear Strength** | $\tau \;=\; \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ = applied load (**N**), $A$ = bond area (**mm²**), $\tau$ in **MPa** | Standard joint strength calculation (ASTM D 1002) | | **Wetting Criterion** | $\theta < 90^{\circ}$ | Contact angle in degrees | Wetting is spontaneous | | **Non-wetting Criterion** | $\theta > 90^{\circ}$ | Contact angle in degrees | Adhesive will not spread | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Adhesive bonding is critical in: - **Aerospace:** Composite-to-composite bonding of aircraft panels (replaces rivets → 15–20% weight saving). - **Automotive:** Hem flange bonding, body panel stiffening. - **Electronics:** Die attach, flip-chip underfill. - **Medical:** Tissue adhesives, dental cements. - **Packaging:** Lamination of multilayer films. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Young–Dupré Equation (Step-by-Step) Starting from thermodynamic equilibrium of a liquid drop on a solid surface under three interfacial tensions. **Step 1 — Define the three interfacial tensions acting at the contact line:** - $\gamma_{SV}$ : solid–vapor interfacial tension (substrate vs. air) - $\gamma_{SL}$ : solid–liquid interfacial tension (substrate vs. adhesive) - $\gamma_{LV}$ : liquid–vapor interfacial tension (adhesive vs. air) **Step 2 — Force balance along the substrate plane (horizontal equilibrium):** The solid–vapor tension pulls the contact line outward; the solid–liquid tension pulls it inward; the vertical component of liquid–vapor tension balances via $\cos\theta$. $$ \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ *Logic:* The horizontal pull of the surface must equal the horizontal component of the liquid surface tension. **Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain Young's Equation:** $$ \gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ **Step 4 — Introduce Dupré's work of adhesion definition:** The reversible work of adhesion is the energy required to separate unit area of solid–liquid interface into solid–vapor and liquid–vapor interfaces: $$ W_{A} \;=\; \gamma_{S} \;+\; \gamma_{L} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} $$ **Step 5 — Assume low vapor pressure (substrate in air, $S \equiv V$):** For non-volatile systems, $\gamma_{SV} \approx \gamma_{S}$ and $\gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{L}$. **Step 6 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4:** $$ W_{A} \;=\; \bigl(\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\bigr) \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} $$ **Step 7 — Cancel $\gamma_{SL}$ and simplify:** $$ W_{A} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;+\; \gamma_{LV} $$ **Step 8 — Final Young–Dupré Equation:** $$ W_{A} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1+\cos\theta) $$ *Interpretation:* Higher $W_{A}$ means stronger interfacial adhesion. To maximize $W_{A}$, the adhesive surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ should be high AND the contact angle $\theta$ should be small (good wetting). ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Bonding Failure Diagnosis | Observation in Failed Joint | Predicted Cause | KTU Action | |---|---|---| | Clean substrate surface, adhesive on one side only | Adhesive failure → poor wetting / low $\gamma_{LV}$ | Increase surface energy of substrate (plasma/corona) | | Adhesive residue on BOTH adherends | Cohesive failure → adhesive too weak | Use higher-performance adhesive / increase bond thickness | | Substrate itself fractured | Substrate failure → adhesive stronger than adherend | Acceptable; bond is stronger than material | | Mixed (patchy) failure | Marginal adhesion + cohesion balance | Optimize cure schedule and surface prep | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Lap Shear Strength) **Given:** A single-lap shear adhesive joint (ASTM D 1002) has bond area $A = 25 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm} = 625 \text{ mm}^2$ and fails at load $F = 14062.5 \text{ N}$. **Find:** Lap shear strength $\tau$. **Solution:** $$ A \;=\; 25 \times 25 \;=\; 625 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ \tau \;=\; \frac{F}{A} \;=\; \frac{14062.5 \text{ N}}{625 \text{ mm}^2} $$ $$ \tau \;=\; 22.5 \text{ MPa} $$ **Result:** The joint sustains **22.5 MPa** lap shear strength — typical of a structural epoxy on aluminum. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Adhesive Bonding ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Surface Preparation
Cleaning, Abrasion, Degreasing] B[Adhesive Application
Liquid, Film, Paste] C[Wetting Stage
Contact Angle less than 90 deg] D[Adhesion Formation
Van der Waals, H-bonds, Covalent] E[Curing / Solidification
Heat, UV, Chemical Reaction] F[Cohesion Development
Internal Crosslinking] G[Load Transfer
Stress Distribution at Interface] H[Final Bonded Assembly] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H ``` ### 4.2 Adhesion vs Cohesion — Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Adhesive Layer] -->|Internal Force| A2[Cohesion
Strength within adhesive] B1[Adhesive Substrate Interface] -->|Interfacial Force| B2[Adhesion
Strength at interface] A2 --> A3[Joint Holds If Both Greater Than Applied Load] B2 --> A3 A3 --> A4[Failure Mode Determined by Weaker Link] A4 --> A5[Adhesive Failure] A4 --> A6[Cohesive Failure] A4 --> A7[Substrate Failure] ``` ### 4.3 The Five Theories of Adhesion — Block View ```mermaid flowchart TD T[Adhesion Theories] --> T1[Mechanical Interlocking
Roughness and pores] T --> T2[Adsorption / Wetting
Van der Waals forces] T --> T3[Chemical Bonding
Covalent and ionic bonds] T --> T4[Diffusion
Polymer chain interdiffusion] T --> T5[Electrostatic
Charge transfer at interface] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **[KTU University Exam – May 2024] Q1. Define an adhesive. Differentiate between adhesion and cohesion.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Adhesive (ASTM D 907):** A non-metallic substance capable of holding materials together by surface attachment. *Example: epoxy, cyanoacrylate, polyurethane.* > **Adhesion:** The force of attraction between the adhesive and the substrate at their interface (interfacial force). Depends on wetting, surface energy, and contact angle. > **Cohesion:** The internal molecular force that holds the bulk adhesive together (intramolecular force). Depends on crosslink density and molecular weight. > *Adhesion occurs at the boundary; cohesion occurs within the body of the adhesive.* (Distinction = 1 mark each, definition = 1 mark) --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Q2. State Young's equation and explain the wetting criterion.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Young's equation for a liquid drop on a solid surface: > $$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$ > **Wetting Criterion:** If $\theta < 90^{\circ}$, the liquid adhesive spontaneously wets the substrate and spreads — a prerequisite for good adhesion. If $\theta > 90^{\circ}$, the adhesive beads up and will not form a strong bond. (Equation = 1 mark, explanation = 2 marks) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1A] Q.A. (a)** Define adhesive bonding. Explain in detail the **five theories of adhesion** with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** Adhesive bonding is the process of joining two materials (adherends) using a non-metallic substance (adhesive) that develops its strength through surface attachment. The bond is governed by interfacial adhesion and internal cohesion. (1 mark) The five theories of adhesion are: 1. **Mechanical Interlocking Theory (1.5 marks):** The adhesive penetrates into pores, crevices, and surface roughness of the substrate and hardens, forming a mechanical lock. Higher surface roughness generally improves bond strength. Effective for porous adherends like wood, concrete, and textiles. 2. **Adsorption / Wetting Theory (1.5 marks):** When the adhesive wets the substrate (contact angle < 90°), intimate molecular contact enables secondary forces — van der Waals, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bonds — to develop across the interface. This theory is dominant for polymer-metal joints. 3. **Chemical Bonding Theory (1 mark):** Primary chemical bonds (covalent, ionic, metallic) form between adhesive and substrate functional groups. This produces the strongest, most durable bonds (e.g., silane coupling agents bonding glass to polymers). 4. **Diffusion Theory (1 mark):** For polymer-polymer bonding, chain segments of the adhesive and substrate interdiffuse across the interface, forming an interphase. Requires chain mobility and is temperature-dependent (autohesion of rubber, solvent welding of plastics). 5. **Electrostatic Theory (1 mark):** Charge transfer across the adhesive-substrate interface creates an electrical double layer, generating electrostatic attraction. Predominant in metal-polymer contacts. > (Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for schematic support.) --- **(b)** A lap joint of bond area $400 \text{ mm}^2$ fails at a tensile load of $9600 \text{ N}$. Calculate the lap shear strength. Comment on whether the joint is suitable for a **structural application** (structural criterion: $\tau \geq 7 \text{ MPa}$). **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Given:** $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2$, $F = 9600 \text{ N}$ **Step 1 — Apply the lap shear strength formula:** $$ \tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{9600 \text{ N}}{400 \text{ mm}^2} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate:** $$ \tau = 24 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Compare with structural criterion:** Since $\tau = 24 \text{ MPa} \;\geq\; 7 \text{ MPa}$, the joint **exceeds** the structural threshold. **Step 4 — Conclusion (2 marks):** The adhesive is structurally suitable for load-bearing applications such as aerospace panel bonding or automotive structural joints. Typical structural adhesives (epoxy, phenolic) exhibit 15–35 MPa lap shear strength, so this joint falls in the expected range. --- **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1B] Q.B. (a)** Explain the **mechanism of formation of an adhesive bond** step-by-step. State the role of surface preparation. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** The formation of an adhesive bond proceeds through the following sequential stages: 1. **Surface Preparation (1.5 marks):** The adherend surface is cleaned (solvent degreasing), abraded (mechanical roughening), and sometimes chemically treated (e.g., chromate conversion on aluminum, silane primers on glass). This removes contaminants, increases surface energy, and promotes wetting. Without proper preparation, even the best adhesive will fail. 2. **Wetting / Adhesive Application (1.5 marks):** The liquid adhesive is applied and must flow over the substrate. Good wetting is indicated by a low contact angle ($\theta < 90°$), driven by a high surface energy substrate and a matched adhesive surface tension. 3. **Adhesion Development (1.5 marks):** Once intimate contact is established, interfacial forces — van der Waals, hydrogen bonds, and sometimes covalent bonds — lock the adhesive to the substrate. 4. **Curing / Solidification (1.5 marks):** The adhesive hardens via chemical crosslinking (thermoset), cooling (thermoplastic), or solvent evaporation. Internal cohesion builds. 5. **Final Bonded Assembly (1 mark):** The cured joint is ready for service; load transfer now occurs through the bonded interface. > *Role of surface preparation:* Removes weak boundary layers (oxides, oils, dust), increases surface area, raises surface energy, and exposes reactive groups for chemical bonding. --- **(b)** Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** for a liquid drop on a solid substrate. Given $\gamma_{LV} = 42 \text{ mN/m}$ and $\theta = 30^{\circ}$, calculate the work of adhesion. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Derivation (5 marks):** [See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps:] - Force balance: $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ - Young's equation: $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ - Dupré: $W_A = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - \gamma_{SL}$ - Combining with $\gamma_S \approx \gamma_{SV}$: $$ W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) $$ **Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** **Given:** $\gamma_{LV} = 42 \text{ mN/m} = 0.042 \text{ N/m}$, $\theta = 30^{\circ}$ **Step 1:** Calculate $\cos 30^{\circ} = 0.866$ **Step 2:** $$ W_A = 0.042 \times (1 + 0.866) $$ **Step 3:** $$ W_A = 0.042 \times 1.866 = 0.07837 \text{ J/m}^2 \approx 78.4 \text{ mJ/m}^2 $$ **Result:** The work of adhesion is **78.4 mJ/m²** — indicative of good interfacial bonding. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Confusing adhesion with cohesion** is the most common mistake in 3-mark definition questions. *Adhesion = adhesive to substrate; cohesion = within the adhesive itself.* > 2. **Forgetting to convert units** in lap shear problems: bond area in $\text{mm}^2$ and load in N gives $\tau$ in MPa directly. Do not divide by $10^6$ unless converting $\text{mm}^2$ to $\text{m}^2$. > 3. **Omitting surface preparation** in mechanism-of-bonding answers costs 1.5 marks. Examiners treat it as the most critical step. > 4. **In derivation questions, writing only the final equation** without force balance or Dupré substitution steps loses 3–4 marks. Always show equilibrium + Dupré substitution. > 5. **Listing theories without examples** is incomplete. Tag each theory with a real adherend (e.g., silane for chemical bonding theory, rubber-rubber for diffusion theory). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Adhesive (ASTM D 907):** Non-metallic substance that holds materials together by surface attachment. - **Adhesion:** Interfacial force between adhesive and substrate. - **Cohesion:** Internal force within the adhesive itself. - **Adherend:** The material being bonded (synonym: substrate). - **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — fundamental wetting relation. - **Young–Dupré:** $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — work of adhesion in terms of measurable contact angle. - **Wetting Criterion:** $\theta < 90^{\circ}$ for good bonding; $\theta > 90^{\circ}$ for poor bonding. - **Lap Shear Strength:** $\tau = F/A$ in MPa — primary structural joint test (ASTM D 1002). - **Five Theories of Adhesion:** Mechanical interlocking, Adsorption/wetting, Chemical bonding, Diffusion, Electrostatic. - **Failure Modes:** Adhesive failure (interface), Cohesive failure (within adhesive), Substrate failure (within adherend). - **Surface preparation is mandatory** — most critical step in adhesive bonding practice. - **Structural threshold:** $\tau \geq 7$ MPa for load-bearing applications. - **Standard test methods to remember:** ASTM D 1002 (lap shear), ASTM D 907 (terminology), ASTM D 903 (peel). - **High-Yield Constants to Memorize:** $\cos 30^{\circ} = 0.866$, $\cos 60^{\circ} = 0.5$, $\cos 0^{\circ} = 1$.

topics.title: Functions of Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions of Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Adhesive Science and Technology** (as prescribed under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT861), the **Functions of Adhesives** refer to the diverse range of *primary, secondary, and tertiary engineering roles* that a polymeric adhesive formulation is engineered to perform within a bonded joint or assembly, beyond the simplistic notion of merely "sticking two surfaces together."

According to established polymer engineering references aligned with KTU board expectations, an adhesive is a non-metallic substance capable of joining materials through **surface attachment (adhesion)** and **internal strength (cohesion)**, and its *functions* span mechanical load transfer, environmental sealing, thermal management, electrical isolation, vibration damping, and aesthetic finishing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 1 of PEPOT861 explicitly expects students to identify and classify the *multifunctional* roles of adhesives in modern engineering practice. The board examiner will not award full credit if you restrict your answer to "joining two surfaces" — credit is reserved for **classification + engineering application examples**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine adhesives as **multi-tool Swiss Army knives** rather than mere glue sticks. Just as a Swiss Army knife contains a blade, scissors, screwdriver, and file all in one compact device, an adhesive joint simultaneously serves as:

- A **structural bridge** (load-bearing skeleton),
- A **weather-proof shield** (sealing gasket),
- A **heat-spreader** (thermal manager),
- A **sound-absorber** (damping cushion), and
- A **galvanic insulator** (corrosion barrier).

A simple real-world analogy: when you tape a cracked smartphone screen, the tape is doing **three jobs at once** — (1) bonding the broken glass, (2) sealing the crack against dust/moisture, and (3) slightly dampening vibrations from the phone's speaker. This *multifunctionality* is exactly what distinguishes engineering adhesives from household glues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> *Adhesion* is the force that holds the adhesive to the substrate, whereas *function* is the engineering purpose the cured adhesive line serves in the application. A student who confuses these two terms will lose marks.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Adhesive Functions

The following standardized performance parameters are used universally in adhesive engineering:

- **Lap Shear Strength:** typically **0.5 – 40 MPa** depending on adhesive class.
- **Peel Strength:** typically **0.5 – 20 N/mm**.
- **Service Temperature Range:** **−60 °C to +300 °C** (for high-performance silicones and epoxies).
- **Dielectric Strength:** **15 – 40 kV/mm** (for electrical-grade epoxy resins).
- **Thermal Conductivity:** **0.1 – 2.0 W/(m·K)** (for thermally conductive filled systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Adhesive Functions

The functions of adhesives are systematically classified into the following seven engineering categories. This classification is the **highest-yield framework** for KTU ESE questions on this topic.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical / Structural Functions
- **Load Transfer:** Transmission of static and dynamic mechanical loads between adherends.
- **Stress Distribution:** Spreading concentrated stress over a wider bonded area, reducing peak stress at any single point (avoids stress risers).
- **Gap Filling:** Accommodation of manufacturing tolerances and irregular surface geometries.
- **Vibration Damping:** Conversion of mechanical vibration energy into heat through viscoelastic deformation of the adhesive layer.
- **Fatigue Resistance:** Absorption of cyclic loading through elastic recovery of the polymer matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind It:** Traditional mechanical fasteners (rivets, bolts, welds) create *point* or *line* stress concentrations. Adhesives distribute stress over the *entire* bond area, often increasing the fatigue life of an assembly by **5× to 10×** compared to spot welds in sheet metal joints.

### 2.1.2 Sealing / Barrier Functions
- **Fluid Sealing:** Prevention of liquid penetration (water, oil, fuel) through the joint interface.
- **Gas Sealing:** Hermetic sealing against gases (air, refrigerants, combustion gases).
- **Dust/Particulate Barrier:** Exclusion of environmental contaminants.
- **Chemical Resistance:** Resistance to acids, alkalis, solvents, and fuels that may contact the bond line.

### 2.1.3 Thermal Functions
- **Thermal Insulation:** Low thermal conductivity adhesive formulations (e.g., aerospace composites) reduce heat transfer.
- **Thermal Conduction:** Thermally-conductive filled adhesives (e.g., epoxy + Al₂O₃ or BN fillers) dissipate heat from electronic components.
- **Thermal Cycling Endurance:** Ability to survive repeated −40 °C to +150 °C cycles without bond-line failure.

### 2.1.4 Electrical Functions
- **Electrical Insulation:** Dielectric adhesives isolate conductors and prevent short circuits (e.g., epoxy potting compounds in transformers).
- **Electrical Conduction:** Silver-filled or carbon-filled adhesives form conductive paths (e.g., die-attach in semiconductor packaging, EMI shielding gaskets).
- **Static Dissipation:** Surface resistivity in the range **10⁶ – 10⁹ Ω/sq** for anti-static applications.

### 2.1.5 Chemical / Corrosion Functions
- **Galvanic Isolation:** Prevention of dissimilar-metal contact corrosion when bonding aluminum to copper, for example.
- **Corrosion Inhibition:** Some adhesive primers contain corrosion inhibitors (chromates, zinc chromate, etc.) that protect the bond-line metal interface.

### 2.1.6 Optical / Aesthetic Functions
- **Transparent Bonding:** Optical-grade UV-cure acrylics for glass-to-glass lamination.
- **Color Coding / Identification:** Differently pigmented structural adhesives for quality traceability.
- **Surface Finishing:** Filling surface imperfections and providing a cosmetically smooth joint.

### 2.1.7 Process / Assembly Functions
- **Curing-on-Demand:** Heat-activated or moisture-cure systems allow staged assembly.
- **Repositionability:** Pressure-sensitive adhesives permit temporary fixing.
- **Self-Indicating Cure:** Color-change indicators confirm cure completion.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Since "Functions of Adhesives" is a **classification-dominant topic**, the formulas below are *engineering evaluation formulas* — the kind KTU may give as numerical/data-interpretation sub-questions.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Relationship | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------|------------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Lap Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{F}{w \cdot L_{ov}}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Load-transfer function evaluation |
| 2 | Peel Force per Width | $p = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | N/mm | Flexible-bonding function evaluation |
| 3 | Stress Concentration Reduction Factor | $K_{t,adhesive} \approx 0.4\text{ to }0.6$ vs. $K_{t,mechanical} \approx 2.5\text{ to }4.0$ | dimensionless | Justifies adhesive over riveting |
| 4 | Thermal Conductivity (filled system) | $k_{comp} \approx k_{m} \cdot \left(1 - V_{f}\right) + k_{f} \cdot V_{f}$ (rule of mixtures, parallel model) | W/(m·K) | Heat-dissipation adhesive design |
| 5 | Volume Resistivity | $\rho = R \cdot \dfrac{A}{l}$ | Ω·cm | Electrical-insulation function |
| 6 | Dielectric Strength | $E_{br} = \dfrac{V_{br}}{t}$ | kV/mm | High-voltage insulation function |
| 7 | Vibration Damping Loss Factor | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$ | dimensionless | Viscoelastic damping function |
| 8 | Cure Conversion (DSC) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta H_{t}}{\Delta H_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Process / cure function control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In your formula sheet answers, always state the **assumptions** (e.g., for the thermal conductivity rule of mixtures, the *parallel* model assumes continuous conductive pathways; the *series* model assumes layered thermal barriers). Examiners explicitly award the assumption mark.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Dominant Adhesive Function | Example Application |
|------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **Aerospace** | Structural + Thermal + Sealing | Bonded aluminum honeycomb panels in aircraft fuselage |
| **Automotive** | Structural + Vibration Damping + Sealing | Hem-flange bonding with crash-durable epoxy |
| **Electronics** | Thermal + Electrical + Sealing | Die-attach with silver-filled epoxy in LED modules |
| **Medical Devices** | Biocompatible Bonding + Sealing | Catheter assembly with UV-cure acrylic |
| **Packaging** | Sealing + Repositionable Bonding | Pressure-sensitive labels on PET bottles |
| **Construction** | Structural + Gap Filling | Tile adhesives with polymer-modified cement |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since "Functions of Adhesives" is fundamentally a **classification + engineering-application** topic rather than a pure-mathematics derivation, the analytical depth in this section is delivered through (a) derivation of the *lap shear governing equation* (the most likely numerical question on this module), and (b) a structured **Function-vs-Property analytical matrix** that KTU examiners commonly test.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The Single-Lap-Shear Governing Equation

The lap shear test (ASTM D1002) is the most common experiment to evaluate the **mechanical load-transfer function** of an adhesive. The derivation below shows how a KTU Part-B numerical sub-question would be constructed.

### Given
- A single-lap-shear joint between two adherends.
- Bond area: $A = w \cdot L_{ov}$ (mm²), where $w$ is width and $L_{ov}$ is overlap length.
- Applied tensile load at failure: $F_{max}$ (N).
- Adherend thickness: $t_{sub}$ (mm); Adherend Young's modulus: $E_{sub}$ (MPa); Adhesive shear modulus: $G_a$ (MPa); Adhesive thickness: $t_a$ (mm).

### Step 1: Define the Average Shear Stress in the Bond Line

By definition of shear stress, the average shear stress $\tau_{avg}$ transmitted through the adhesive layer is the failure load divided by the bonded area:

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{F_{max}}{A} = \frac{F_{max}}{w \cdot L_{ov}}
$$

**Logic:** This assumes uniform stress distribution across the bond line — an idealization that is only strictly true for very short overlap lengths and very thin adhesive films.

### Step 2: Account for the Bending-Moment / Differential-Strain Effect (Volkersen Correction)

In a real lap joint, the adherends do not strain identically because the load is transferred progressively through the adhesive. The shear stress is therefore *non-uniform*, peaking at the ends of the overlap. **Volkersen's correction factor** $\omega$ modifies the simple average:

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G_a \cdot L_{ov}^2}{2 \cdot E_{sub} \cdot t_{sub} \cdot t_a}}
$$

**Logic:** A higher adhesive shear modulus $G_a$, a longer overlap $L_{ov}$, or a thinner substrate $t_{sub}$ increases $\omega$, which means more non-uniform stress and thus a *lower effective* average stress capacity for the same failure load.

### Step 3: Corrected Maximum / Effective Shear Stress

$$
\tau_{eff} = \frac{\tau_{avg}}{\omega} = \frac{F_{max}}{w \cdot L_{ov} \cdot \omega}
$$

**Logic:** Dividing by $\omega$ reflects the fact that, for the same applied load, the *peak* local stress is higher than the average — so the joint fails at a lower global load than a perfectly uniform-stress model would predict.

### Step 4: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given numerical values (typical KTU Part-B sub-question):**
- $F_{max} = 8000$ N
- $w = 25$ mm
- $L_{ov} = 12.5$ mm
- $G_a = 200$ MPa
- $E_{sub} = 70000$ MPa (aluminum)
- $t_{sub} = 1.6$ mm
- $t_a = 0.2$ mm

**Sub-step 4.1 — Bond area:**

$$
A = w \cdot L_{ov} = 25 \times 12.5 = 312.5 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Sub-step 4.2 — Average shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{F_{max}}{A} = \frac{8000}{312.5} = 25.6 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Sub-step 4.3 — Volkersen omega factor:**

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times (12.5)^2}{2 \times 70000 \times 1.6 \times 0.2}}
$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$
\text{Numerator} = 200 \times 156.25 = 31250
$$

Expanding the denominator:

$$
\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 70000 \times 1.6 \times 0.2 = 2 \times 70000 \times 0.32 = 44800
$$

Therefore:

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{31250}{44800}} = \sqrt{0.6975} = 0.835
$$

**Sub-step 4.4 — Effective (peak-corrected) shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{eff} = \frac{25.6}{0.835} = 30.66 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The peak local shear stress is approximately **30.66 MPa**, which is about **20 % higher** than the average 25.6 MPa assumed by the simple model. This is the **stress-distribution function** of the adhesive in action — a key concept the KTU examiner tests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever you present this derivation, explicitly state which assumption you are using (uniform stress vs. Volkersen). The board examiner awards 1–2 marks *solely* for stating the governing assumption.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Function-to-Adhesive-Family Mapping

This matrix is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question format** ("Match the function to the most suitable adhesive family").

| Engineering Function | Most Suitable Adhesive Family | Reason |
|----------------------|-------------------------------|--------|
| High structural load transfer | **Epoxy** (modified, toughened) | High cohesive strength, >30 MPa lap shear |
| High-temperature sealing | **Silicone** | Continuous service up to **+250 °C** |
| Optical-transparent bonding | **UV-cure acrylic** | Refractive index match, no yellowing |
| Thermal management in electronics | **Epoxy + Al₂O₃/BN filler** | Thermal conductivity **1 – 5 W/(m·K)** |
| Flexible / peelable bonding | **Pressure-sensitive acrylic** | High peel strength, low cohesive strength |
| Hermetic microelectronic sealing | **Epoxy + anhydride hardener** | Low CTE, low outgassing |
| Biocompatible medical bonding | **Cyanoacrylate (medical grade)** | Rapid cure, ISO 10993 biocompatible |
| Chemical-resistant tank lining | **Vinyl ester / Furane resin** | Excellent acid/alkali resistance |
| Cryogenic sealing | **PTFE / Epoxy-cryogenic grade** | Retains flexibility at −196 °C |
| Conductive die-attach | **Silver-filled epoxy** | Volume resistivity **< 10⁻⁴ Ω·cm** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"List any six engineering functions that adhesives perform in modern assemblies. Give one application example for each function."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (6 functions × 0.5 mark each, in tabular format):**

| Function | Application Example |
|----------|---------------------|
| 1. Load transfer | Bonded aluminum fuselage panels in aircraft |
| 2. Vibration damping | Crash-durable epoxy in automotive B-pillars |
| 3. Sealing (fluid) | Anaerobic thread sealant in pipe fittings |
| 4. Thermal conduction | Silver-filled epoxy in LED die-attach |
| 5. Electrical insulation | Epoxy potting of transformer windings |
| 6. Gap filling | Polyurethane windshield adhesive in car body |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Distinguish between the *adhesion* function and the *cohesion* function of an adhesive. Why are both essential for joint performance?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Adhesion (1 mark):** The molecular/chemical and mechanical interlocking forces that develop *between* the cured adhesive and the substrate surface. It governs how well the adhesive "sticks" to the adherend.
- **Cohesion (1 mark):** The internal strength of the cured adhesive bulk — i.e., the strength of the polymer matrix *within itself*. It governs how well the adhesive layer resists being torn apart internally.
- **Why both are essential (1 mark):** A joint fails either by *adhesive failure* (separation at the interface) or by *cohesive failure* (rupture within the adhesive or within the substrate). For optimal joint performance, cohesive strength of the adhesive should be **at least equal to or greater than the adhesion strength**, otherwise the joint is interface-limited and under-utilizes the bulk material.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option A)

### **Question A:**
**(a)** Classify the various functions of adhesives in modern engineering practice. Discuss in detail the *mechanical* and *thermal* functions with suitable adhesive chemistry and application examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A single-lap-shear aluminum joint is bonded with an epoxy adhesive of shear modulus $G_a = 250$ MPa and bond-line thickness $t_a = 0.15$ mm. The aluminum substrate is 1.2 mm thick with Young's modulus $E_{sub} = 70000$ MPa. The overlap is 15 mm wide and 20 mm long. The joint fails at a tensile load of 9.2 kN.
- (i) Calculate the average shear stress in the bond line. *(2 marks)*
- (ii) Calculate the Volkersen correction factor $\omega$ and the effective peak shear stress. *(3 marks)*
- (iii) Comment on the dominant failure mode if the effective peak stress exceeds 32 MPa. *(2 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of adhesive functions (7 marks):**

A systematic 7-category classification is presented:

1. **Mechanical functions (1.5 marks):** Load transfer, stress distribution, gap filling, vibration damping, fatigue resistance. Discussed with examples — e.g., toughened epoxy used in automotive structural bonding achieves lap shear strengths of **25–35 MPa** while reducing peak stress concentration by **40–60 %** compared to spot welds.

2. **Thermal functions (1.5 marks):** Thermal insulation (silicone / aerogel-filled systems, $k < 0.1$ W/(m·K)), thermal conduction (epoxy + BN or Al₂O₃, $k = 1$–$5$ W/(m·K)), and thermal cycling endurance. Example: thermally-conductive epoxy used in LED modules dissipates **>5 W** of heat per square centimeter.

3. **Sealing functions (1 mark):** Fluid and gas sealing with silicone and polyurethane in pipeline and construction applications.

4. **Electrical functions (1 mark):** Insulation (epoxy potting) vs. conduction (silver-filled epoxy in semiconductor packaging).

5. **Chemical/corrosion functions (0.5 mark):** Galvanic isolation using epoxy in aluminum–copper joints.

6. **Optical/aesthetic functions (0.5 mark):** UV-cure acrylics for transparent display lamination.

7. **Process/assembly functions (0.5 mark):** Cure-on-demand B-staged epoxy films used in aerospace prepreg layup.

(Each category must include at least one named adhesive family + one engineering application.)

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Average shear stress (2 marks):**

$$
A = w \cdot L_{ov} = 15 \times 20 = 300 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{F_{max}}{A} = \frac{9200 \text{ N}}{300 \text{ mm}^2} = 30.67 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Volkersen correction and effective peak stress (3 marks):**

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G_a \cdot L_{ov}^2}{2 \cdot E_{sub} \cdot t_{sub} \cdot t_a}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{250 \times (20)^2}{2 \times 70000 \times 1.2 \times 0.15}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
250 \times 400 = 100000
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times 70000 \times 1.2 \times 0.15 = 25200
$$

Therefore:

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{100000}{25200}} = \sqrt{3.968} = 1.992
$$

Effective peak stress:

$$
\tau_{eff} = \frac{\tau_{avg}}{\omega} = \frac{30.67}{1.992} = 15.40 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Note for examiner:** In Volkersen's formulation as presented here, $\omega > 1$ indicates that the simple average stress *overestimates* the joint capacity — the *effective* average stress that the joint can sustain is reduced. The actual *peak* stress at the overlap ends is higher than the average; this is captured by the more advanced Goland–Reissner analysis.

**(iii) Failure-mode commentary (2 marks):**

Since the calculated $\tau_{eff} = 15.40$ MPa is well below 32 MPa, the joint is *not* limited by the epoxy's cohesive strength. The failure is therefore most likely **adhesive (interfacial)** or **substrate (thin-sheet yielding) failure**, not cohesive failure of the adhesive. If the calculated effective stress *had* exceeded 32 MPa, the failure mode would be predominantly **cohesive within the epoxy** (provided the substrate is thick enough to avoid substrate yield).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option B)

### **Question B:**
**(a)** Discuss the role of adhesives as **sealing barriers** and as **electrical insulators/conductors**, with at least two examples for each sub-function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a labeled block diagram, explain how a bonded joint simultaneously performs *load-transfer*, *vibration-damping*, and *thermal-conduction* functions. Discuss how the choice of adhesive filler and viscoelastic properties enables these three functions to be tuned independently. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sealing and electrical functions (7 marks):**

**Sealing functions (3.5 marks):**
- **Fluid sealing:** Anaerobic thread sealants and RTV silicones are used in pipeline flanges and engine gaskets. The cured elastomer fills micro-gaps in the flange face, providing a continuous leak-proof barrier up to **~10 bar** internal pressure.
- **Gas sealing:** Epoxy and epoxy-phenolic formulations hermetically seal microelectronic packages against moisture ingress (per **IP68** rating), with water-vapour transmission rates **< 0.1 g/m²/day**.
- **Chemical resistance:** Vinyl ester and furane resins resist **> 95 % H₂SO₄** and **> 30 % NaOH**, used in chemical-tank linings.
- **Sealing + bonding combined:** Polyurethane windshield adhesives bond *and* seal automotive glass, eliminating the need for separate gaskets.

**Electrical functions (3.5 marks):**
- **Insulation:** Epoxy potting compounds encapsulate transformer windings; dielectric strength typically **20 – 40 kV/mm**, volume resistivity **> 10¹⁴ Ω·cm**.
- **Conduction:** Silver-filled epoxy in semiconductor die-attach has volume resistivity **~ 10⁻⁴ Ω·cm**, comparable to solder, but with a low cure temperature **< 200 °C** that protects temperature-sensitive devices.
- **Static dissipation:** Anti-static epoxy with controlled surface resistivity **10⁶ – 10⁹ Ω/sq** is used in clean-room flooring and IC-tray coatings.
- **EMI shielding:** Conductive adhesive gaskets filled with silver-coated nickel particles provide **> 60 dB** EMI attenuation in enclosure joints.

---

**(b) Multifunctional bonded joint block diagram (7 marks):**

A schematic block diagram of the bonded joint (refer to SECTION 4.3, "Functional Layer Architecture of a Bonded Joint") should be drawn showing:

1. **Top adherend**
2. **Surface preparation layer** (degrease + primer)
3. **Adhesive bulk layer** (cured filled polymer)
4. **Interphase region** (chemical bonding zone)
5. **Bottom adherend**

The adhesive bulk layer is then shown to perform the three functions simultaneously:

- **Load-transfer function (2 marks):** Achieved by the *cohesive strength* of the cured matrix. Increasing the cross-link density (e.g., higher epoxy/hardener ratio) raises cohesive strength and load capacity, but **reduces** damping.
- **Vibration-damping function (2 marks):** Achieved by the *viscoelastic loss factor* $\tan \delta$ of the polymer near its glass-transition temperature. A rubber-toughened epoxy (e.g., CTBN-modified) can achieve $\tan \delta \approx 0.3$ at operating frequency, dissipating vibration as heat. *Trade-off:* higher damping usually lowers cohesive strength.
- **Thermal-conduction function (3 marks):** Achieved by incorporating thermally-conductive but electrically-insulating fillers such as **boron nitride (BN)**, **alumina (Al₂O₃)**, or **aluminum nitride (AlN)**. A typical loading of **40–60 vol %** BN in epoxy raises thermal conductivity from **~0.2 W/(m·K)** (unfilled) to **~3–5 W/(m·K)**, while keeping the system electrically insulating.

**Independent tuning (final sentence for full credit):** By independently selecting (i) the base polymer chemistry (epoxy vs. silicone vs. polyurethane) for the *mechanical/viscoelastic* balance, and (ii) the type, shape, and volume fraction of the filler (BN for thermal, silica for rheology, CTBN rubber for toughness), the engineer can independently tune **load-transfer**, **damping**, and **thermal conduction** in a single adhesive formulation. This is the essence of the multifunctional design philosophy that makes modern adhesives superior to mechanical fasteners.

---

## 5.4 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points (KTU Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **"Functions of adhesives" ≠ "adhesion theories."** Many students mistakenly write the *mechanical theory of adhesion* or *adsorption theory of adhesion* when the question asks for the *functions*. The functions are the **engineering roles** the adhesive performs, not the mechanisms by which it sticks.
>
> 2. **Single-function answers lose 2–3 marks.** A response that lists only "joining" or "bonding" without covering sealing, thermal, electrical, and chemical roles will be capped at around 50–60 % of the marks.
>
> 3. **No application example = 0.5–1 mark lost per function.** Every function stated must be paired with a named application, otherwise the examiner treats it as a half-answer.
>
> 4. **In numerical sub-questions, always state the assumption.** A lap-shear calculation *must* mention whether the uniform-stress or Volkersen model is being used. Skipping this loses 1 mark even if the arithmetic is correct.
>
> 5. **Unit errors are heavily penalized.** $\tau$ in **MPa** (N/mm²) is the correct unit for adhesive stresses. Writing "N/mm" instead of "N/mm²" is a common slip and costs 0.5 mark.
>
> 6. **In diagram questions, use boxes, arrows, and labels — not paragraphs.** A block diagram or flowchart is mandatory for the 7-mark multifunction question; descriptive text alone will not earn full marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Primary function of any adhesive is mechanical load transfer**, evaluated by **lap shear stress** $\tau = F / (w \cdot L_{ov})$ with **Volkersen correction** for non-uniform stress distribution.
- **Seven major functional categories** to memorize: Mechanical, Sealing/Barrier, Thermal, Electrical, Chemical/Corrosion, Optical/Aesthetic, Process/Assembly.
- **Vibration damping** is governed by the viscoelastic loss factor $\tan \delta = E''/E'$; trade-off exists with cohesive strength.
- **Thermal conduction** in filled adhesives follows a **rule-of-mixtures** approximation, with fillers like **BN, Al₂O₃, AlN** delivering $k$ values of **1–5 W/(m·K)** while maintaining electrical insulation.
- **Electrical conduction** requires metallic fillers (typically **silver flakes**) at **> 25 vol %** loading; volume resistivity falls to **~10⁻⁴ Ω·cm**.
- **Dielectric strength** of insulating epoxies is typically **20–40 kV/mm**; remember the unit conversion $E_{br} = V_{br} / t$.
- **Galvanic isolation** of dissimilar metals (e.g., Al–Cu) is a unique chemical function achievable only with adhesives — no mechanical fastener can do it.
- **Multifunctionality** is the design philosophy: one adhesive layer can simultaneously bond, seal, conduct heat, dampen vibration, and insulate electrically — independent tuning is achieved by base-polymer selection + filler engineering.
- **Always pair each function with:** (i) a named adhesive family and (ii) a real-world application example — this is the KTU examiner's expectation.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHOCIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Module 1: Classification of Adhesives

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> **Adhesives** are non-metallic substances — applied as thin interlayers between two solid substrates — capable of holding materials together by **surface attachment (adhesion)** and **internal cohesive strength (cohesion)**. Their classification is the systematic taxonomy of bonding agents based on **chemical origin, physical form, mechanical performance, curing chemistry, and end-use application**.

In the **KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus (PEPOT861)**, the classification of adhesives is treated as the foundational framework upon which all subsequent modules (surface preparation, joint design, mechanical testing) are built. A typical modern industrial adhesive is rarely a single molecule — it is a formulated system containing a **base polymer (binder)**, **curing agents / hardeners**, **solvents**, **fillers**, **plasticizers**, **tackifiers**, and **stabilizers**.

### 🔑 Two Foundational Forces Every Adhesive Engineer Must Distinguish

| Force | Definition | Where It Acts |
|---|---|---|
| **Adhesion** | Force of attraction between the **adhesive layer and the substrate** | At the **interface** (bond line boundary) |
| **Cohesion** | Internal strength of the **adhesive material itself** | Within the **bulk** of the adhesive layer |

> [!NOTE]
> A good adhesive joint is one where **Cohesive Strength of Adhesive ≈ Adhesive Strength at Interface ≈ Strength of Substrate**. Failure occurring *inside* the adhesive = **Cohesive Failure** (desirable). Failure at the interface = **Adhesive Failure** (undesirable — indicates poor surface prep).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Molecular Velcro Story"

Imagine you are stitching two pieces of cloth together. You have two completely different choices:

1. **Sewing (Mechanical Fastening):** You puncture the cloth with a needle and thread. The joint is strong because the thread physically interlocks with the fabric.
2. **Gluing (Adhesive Bonding):** You apply a thin film of sticky polymer between the two cloths. After it dries/cures, the molecules of the glue form **chemical bridges**, **hydrogen bonds**, and **van der Waals attractions** with the fabric fibers.

An adhesive is essentially **"molecular stitching without needles."** Just like threads are classified as cotton, silk, polyester, or nylon, adhesives are classified based on:
- **What they're made of** (chemistry)
- **How they're applied** (physical form)
- **How they harden** (curing mechanism)
- **How much load they can carry** (structural vs non-structural)

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters (KTU Industrial Relevance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to KTU Module 1 learning outcomes, a polymer/chemical engineer must be able to **select an appropriate adhesive class** for a given substrate combination (metal-metal, metal-polymer, polymer-composite, etc.). Misclassification leads to **joint failure**, **delamination in composites**, and catastrophic losses in **aerospace, automotive, electronics, and packaging** industries.

---

## 1.4 Master Classification Framework

Adhesives are classified using **five independent classification axes**. An adhesive can simultaneously belong to one category in each axis. For example, **Epoxy** is *synthetic + thermosetting + liquid + reactive + structural*.

| Classification Axis | Major Categories |
|---|---|
| **1. Origin / Source** | Natural, Synthetic |
| **2. Chemical Base (Polymer Type)** | Thermosetting, Thermoplastic, Elastomeric, Alloys/Blends |
| **3. Physical Form** | Liquid, Paste, Film, Powder, Tape, Hot-melt |
| **4. Curing Mechanism** | Reactive (chemical cure), Non-reactive (solvent loss / hot-melt), Pressure-sensitive |
| **5. Performance Class** | Structural, Non-structural, Semi-structural |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Module 1: Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Classification by Origin — Detailed Breakdown

### 🟢 2.1.1 Natural Adhesives

Natural adhesives are derived from **animal, vegetable, or mineral sources** and have been used for over **6,000 years** (Egyptian pharaohs used animal glue for furniture). They are **biodegradable, renewable, low-cost, and eco-friendly**, but have **poor water/chemical resistance** and **low bond strength**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Frequently Asked:** *Give two examples of natural adhesives and state their applications.*

| Sub-class | Source | Key Component | Modern Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Animal Glues** | Bones, hides, fish | Collagen / Gelatin | Book binding, wood joints, gummed tape |
| **Casein Glue** | Milk protein | Casein | Wood labeling (wine bottles), packaging |
| **Starch / Dextrin** | Corn, potato, tapioca | Polysaccharides | Corrugated cardboard, paper bags, wallpaper paste |
| **Cellulosics** | Cotton, wood pulp | Cellulose derivatives (CMC, HEC) | Wallpaper, school glue (white glue) |
| **Natural Rubber** | Latex of *Hevea brasiliensis* | Polyisoprene | Surgical tape, pressure-sensitive labels |
| **Bitumen / Asphalt** | Petroleum residue | Hydrocarbon mixture | Roofing, waterproofing, pavement |
| **Shellac** | Lac bug secretions | Polyester of hydroxy fatty acids | Sealing wax, electrical insulation (vintage) |

### 🔵 2.1.2 Synthetic Adhesives

Synthetic adhesives are **engineered polymers** synthesized via polymerization reactions. They dominate **~85% of the global adhesive market** and offer tailored mechanical, thermal, and chemical properties.

| Sub-class | Chemistry | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermoplastic resins** | Linear/rebranched polymers that soften on heating | Polyvinyl acetate (PVA), Acrylics, Polyamides, Cellulose acetate |
| **Thermosetting resins** | Cross-linked 3D networks — irreversible cure | Epoxy, Phenolic, Urea-formaldehyde, Melamine, Polyurethane |
| **Elastomeric adhesives** | Rubbery, high elongation polymers | Silicones, Polysulfide, Neoprene, SBR |
| **Alloy/Blend adhesives** | Hybrid systems combining 2+ chemistries | Epoxy-phenolic, Nitrile-phenolic, Vinyl-phenolic |

---

## 2.2 Classification by Chemical Base (Polymer Type)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most critical classification axis** in the KTU syllabus because it dictates **service temperature, chemical resistance, flexibility, and load-bearing capacity**.

### 🧪 A. Thermoplastic Adhesives

Linear chain polymers that **soften on heating and re-solidify on cooling** (reversible process).

**Mechanism:** Bonds form via **physical interlocking and van der Waals forces** as the solvent evaporates or the polymer cools.

| Adhesive | Key Properties | Typical Uses |
|---|---|---|
| **PVA (Polyvinyl Acetate)** | Water-based, non-toxic, easy cleanup | Wood glue (Elmer's), book binding |
| **Polyamide (Nylon)** | High strength, abrasion resistance | Shoe soles, textile lamination |
| **Acrylic (Solution/Solvent)** | Clear, UV-resistant | Sign boards, plastic-to-plastic bonding |
| **Cellulose Acetate** | Transparent, flexible | Tape, photographic film lamination |

### 🧪 B. Thermosetting Adhesives

**Cross-linked 3D networks** formed by **irreversible chemical reaction** (cure). Once cured, they **cannot be re-melted or re-shaped**.

**Mechanism:** Cure via **addition polymerization, condensation, or catalytic cross-linking**. Heat, pressure, and/or catalysts initiate the reaction.

| Adhesive | Cure Mechanism | Tg (°C) | Key Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | Amine or anhydride hardener (2-part) | 80–200 | Excellent adhesion, chemical resistance, low shrinkage |
| **Phenolic (Phenol-Formaldehyde)** | Heat + pressure condensation | 150+ | High temp resistance, water resistance (BRAB ply) |
| **Urea-Formaldehyde** | Acid catalyst + heat | 80–100 | Rigid, used in plywood and particle board |
| **Melamine-Formaldehyde** | Heat + pressure | 110+ | Water-resistant, scratch-resistant laminates |
| **Polyurethane (PU)** | Isocyanate + polyol | −50 to 150 | Flexible, tough, excellent for shoes and automotive |
| **Cyanoacrylate** | Moisture (anionic) | 80 | Instant "super glue" — rapid room-temp cure |
| **Acrylic (anaerobic)** | Absence of oxygen + metal ions | 100 | Thread-locking, flange sealing |
| **Silicone** | Moisture (condensation) | −60 to 200 | Flexible, extreme temperature range, electrical |

### 🧪 C. Elastomeric Adhesives

Rubbery polymers with **very high elongation (200–1000%)**, **low elastic modulus**, and **excellent flexibility**. Used where **movement, vibration, or thermal expansion** is expected.

| Adhesive | Base Polymer | Key Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber** | Polyisoprene | Tapes, contact adhesives |
| **Neoprene (Polychloroprene)** | Chloroprene | Shoe industry, automotive, contact bonding |
| **SBR (Styrene-Butadiene Rubber)** | SBR copolymer | Carpet backing, tire bonding |
| **Butyl Rubber** | Isobutylene-isoprene | Sealants, double-glazed windows |
| **Polysulfide** | Thiokol | Aircraft fuel tank sealant, construction |
| **Silicone Rubber** | Polydimethylsiloxane | Gaskets, medical implants, bath sealants |

### 🧪 D. Alloy / Blend Adhesives

Engineered hybrids that combine the **best properties of two polymer classes** to overcome individual weaknesses. These are heavily used in **aerospace structural bonding**.

| Blend | Why It's Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy-Phenolic** | High temp epoxy + flexibility phenolic | Aircraft brakes, missile components |
| **Nitrile-Phenolic** | Oil resistance nitrile + high temp phenolic | Automotive brake linings, oil filters |
| **Vinyl-Phenolic** | Toughness of vinyl + high temp phenolic | Aerospace structural panels |
| **Epoxy-Polyurethane** | Strength of epoxy + flexibility of PU | Automotive structural panels |

---

## 2.3 Classification by Physical Form

> [!NOTE]
> Physical form determines the **application method, equipment required, and production line speed**.

| Form | Description | Examples | Application Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liquid (solvent-borne)** | Polymer dissolved in organic solvent | Contact cement (neoprene) | Brush, roller, spray |
| **Water-based Emulsion** | Polymer dispersed in water | PVA wood glue, latex | Brush, roller |
| **Paste** | High-viscosity thixotropic | Epoxy putty, silicone sealant | Caulking gun, trowel |
| **Film / Tape (B-stage)** | Solid film with partial cure | Epoxy film, phenolic film | Lay-up + autoclave |
| **Powder** | Solid polymer particles | Polyamide powder, epoxy powder coating | Electrostatic spray, fluidized bed |
| **Hot-melt** | Solid at room temp, melts on heating | EVA (ethylene-vinyl acetate), PUR hot-melt | Heated extrusion gun |
| **Foam / Gasket** | Pre-formed shapes | Acrylic foam tape (3M VHB) | Pressure application |

---

## 2.4 Classification by Curing Mechanism

This is the **engineer's most practical classification** because it dictates **cycle time, equipment, and process design**.

### ⚗️ 2.4.1 Reactive Adhesives (Chemical Cure)

Curing occurs via **irreversible chemical reaction**.

$$\text{Resin + Hardener} \xrightarrow{\text{heat / catalyst / moisture}} \text{Cross-linked Network} + \text{By-product (if any)}$$

| Sub-type | Trigger | Cure Time | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Two-part room-temp cure** | Mixing resin + hardener | Minutes to hours | Epoxy, PU, silicone (RTV) |
| **Heat-cure (one-part)** | Heat activation (often with latent hardener) | 30 min – 4 hrs at 120–180 °C | Epoxy film, phenolic |
| **Moisture-cure** | Atmospheric humidity | Hours to days | Silicone, PU, cyanoacrylate |
| **Anaerobic cure** | Absence of O₂ + metal ions | Minutes | Thread-lockers (Loctite) |
| **UV / Light-cure** | UV or visible light | Seconds | Acrylics, epoxies (UV) in dentistry, electronics |
| **Cyanoacrylate (instant)** | Trace moisture | Seconds | Super glue |

### ⚗️ 2.4.2 Non-Reactive Adhesives (Physical Cure)

Bond formation by **physical change only** — no chemical reaction.

$$\text{Solution} \xrightarrow{\text{solvent evaporation}} \text{Solid Film (Bond)}$$

$$\text{Solid Polymer} \xrightarrow{\text{heat}} \text{Liquid} \xrightarrow{\text{cool}} \text{Solid Film (Bond)}$$

| Sub-type | Process | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Solvent-based (drying)** | Solvent evaporation | Vinyl, acrylic, rubber cement |
| **Water-based (drying)** | Water evaporation | PVA emulsion, dextrin |
| **Hot-melt** | Melt + cool | EVA, polyamide, PUR hot-melt |
| **Pressure-sensitive (PSA)** | Permanent tack at room temp | Tape, labels (no cure required) |

---

## 2.5 Classification by Performance (Structural vs Non-Structural)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Defining Question:** *Differentiate between structural and non-structural adhesives.*

$$\text{Structural Adhesive: Shear Strength} \geq 7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Structural adhesives** are designed to **carry significant sustained loads** over the design life of the structure. They are **load-bearing** and replace mechanical fasteners (rivets, bolts, welds) in critical applications.

**Non-structural adhesives** are used for **temporary or low-load holding** — packaging, labels, sealing.

| Feature | Structural | Non-structural |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Strength** | $\geq 7$ MPa (often 20–40 MPa) | $< 7$ MPa |
| **Joint Design** | Lap shear, scarf, butt (engineered) | Simple overlap, spot |
| **Examples** | Epoxy, PU, Phenolic, Acrylic | PVA, dextrin, gum arabic |
| **Applications** | Aircraft fuselage, automotive chassis, wind turbine blades, bridge retrofit | Envelopes, labels, book binding, food packaging |
| **Failure Mode** | Cohesive (within adhesive) | Adhesive (at interface) — by design |

A **semi-structural** class sits between — e.g., **silicone and PU sealants** in automotive windshields (load-bearing but primarily for sealing).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the fundamental quantitative relationships governing adhesive selection and performance.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use in Engineering |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lap-Shear Strength** | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ where $F$ = load, $A$ = bond area | Compare adhesives |
| 2 | **Stress Distribution Factor** | $n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 2t/E \cdot \sqrt{G/E_a}}}$ | Joint design optimization |
| 3 | **Thermal Expansion Mismatch** | $\sigma_T = E_a \cdot \Delta T \cdot (\alpha_s - \alpha_a)$ | Predict joint thermal failure |
| 4 | **Adhesive Joint Classification Rule** | If $\tau \geq 7 \text{ MPa} \Rightarrow$ Structural | KTU board benchmark |
| 5 | **Cure Conversion (DSC)** | $\alpha(t) = \dfrac{\Delta H(t)}{\Delta H_{\text{total}}}$ | Cure monitoring |
| 6 | **Glass Transition Shift Rule (Fox)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \dfrac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$ | Predict blend Tg |
| 7 | **Peel Strength** | $P = \dfrac{F_{\text{peel}}}{w}$ (force per unit width) | Tape/film selection |
| 8 | **Volume Shrinkage in Cure** | $S_{\text{vol}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liq}} - \rho_{\text{solid}}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}} \times 100\%$ | Predict internal stresses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention for KTU Exams:** Always express strength in **MPa** (N/mm²) and area in mm². **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa.**

---

## 2.7 Decision-Making Heuristic for Adhesive Selection

When a KTU question asks *"Select an adhesive for X"*, use this five-step logic:

1. **Substrate compatibility** — e.g., rubber demands flexible elastomeric adhesive
2. **Service environment** — temperature, chemicals, UV, moisture
3. **Required mechanical strength** — structural vs non-structural
4. **Production process** — cure time, equipment, cycle time
5. **Regulatory/safety** — food contact (FDA), aerospace (BMS, MMM-A-132), automotive

# 🔬 Module 1: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the KTU Structural Adhesive Threshold Rule

> [!NOTE]
> The **7 MPa threshold** is not arbitrary. It is derived from the **criterion that the bonded joint must withstand typical sustained shear loads in engineered assemblies**.

### Step 1 — Start with the basic lap-shear strength equation

The fundamental stress in a single lap-shear joint (per ASTM D1002) is given by:

$$\tau = \frac{F_{\max}}{A_{\text{bond}}}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = shear strength of adhesive (MPa)
- $F_{\max}$ = maximum tensile load at failure (N)
- $A_{\text{bond}}$ = bonded overlap area (mm²)

### Step 2 — Convert units to MPa (KTU standard)

Since $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$, we have:

$$\tau_{\text{MPa}} = \frac{F_{\max}(\text{N})}{L \times w \, (\text{mm}^2)}$$

where $L$ = overlap length (mm), $w$ = joint width (mm).

### Step 3 — Apply the empirical structural benchmark

Experimental studies on aerospace and automotive lap-shear specimens (Boeing BSS 7202, ASTM D5868) have established the threshold:

$$\tau_{\text{structural}} \geq 7 \text{ MPa}$$

This threshold corresponds to the **load-bearing capacity equivalent to a riveted joint of similar footprint**.

### Step 4 — Derive the minimum overlap length for a given load

Rearranging the lap-shear equation to solve for the overlap length $L_{\min}$:

$$L_{\min} = \frac{F_{\max}}{\tau_{\min} \cdot w}$$

For a given load $F_{\max} = 5000 \text{ N}$, bond width $w = 25 \text{ mm}$, and $\tau_{\min} = 7 \text{ MPa}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\min} &= \frac{F_{\max}}{\tau_{\min} \cdot w} \\
&= \frac{5000 \text{ N}}{7 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 25 \text{ mm}} \\
&= \frac{5000}{175} \\
&= 28.57 \text{ mm} \approx 30 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **minimum required overlap length** for a 5 kN load using a structural adhesive is **30 mm** for a 25 mm wide joint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Mismatch Stress in Bonded Joints

When two dissimilar materials (substrate and adhesive) are bonded and subjected to a temperature change $\Delta T$, differential expansion generates internal stress.

### Step 1 — Free thermal expansion of each material

The free strain for the substrate and adhesive respectively:

$$\varepsilon_s = \alpha_s \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\varepsilon_a = \alpha_a \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Mismatch strain (excess strain causing stress)

$$\varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}} = (\alpha_s - \alpha_a) \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 3 — Constrained strain energy and resulting stress

If the adhesive is constrained by the (much stiffer) substrate, the elastic stress generated in the adhesive is:

$$\sigma_T = E_a \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{mismatch}}$$

$$\sigma_T = E_a \cdot (\alpha_s - \alpha_a) \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 4 — Numerical example

**Given:** Aluminum substrate bonded with epoxy adhesive.
- $E_a = 3.0 \text{ GPa} = 3000 \text{ MPa}$ (epoxy modulus)
- $\alpha_s = 23 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}$ (aluminum)
- $\alpha_a = 65 \times 10^{-6} \, /^\circ\text{C}$ (epoxy)
- $\Delta T = -50 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ (cooling from cure temp 80°C to 30°C)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= E_a \cdot (\alpha_s - \alpha_a) \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 3000 \times (23 - 65) \times 10^{-6} \times (-50) \\
&= 3000 \times (-42 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-50) \\
&= 3000 \times 2100 \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 6.3 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare with adhesive strength

If epoxy tensile strength $\sigma_{\text{UTS}} = 60 \text{ MPa}$, the safety factor is:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{UTS}}}{\sigma_T} = \frac{60}{6.3} \approx 9.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This explains why **adhesive selection must consider thermal expansion compatibility** — particularly in aerospace and cryogenic applications.

---

## 3.3 Fox Equation Derivation for Predicting Tg of Blends (Used in Adhesive Alloy Design)

When formulating alloy/blend adhesives (e.g., epoxy-PU hybrid), predicting the final **glass transition temperature (Tg)** is critical.

### Step 1 — Empirical Fox equation

The Fox equation (1965) describes Tg of a copolymer or blend as:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \sum_{i} \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$$

where:
- $T_g$ = blend glass transition (Kelvin)
- $w_i$ = weight fraction of component $i$
- $T_{g,i}$ = glass transition of pure component $i$ (Kelvin)

### Step 2 — For a binary blend (2 components)

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical example

**Given:** 60% Epoxy (Tg = 100°C = 373 K) + 40% Polyurethane (Tg = −40°C = 233 K).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{T_g} &= \frac{0.60}{373} + \frac{0.40}{233} \\
&= 1.608 \times 10^{-3} + 1.716 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 3.324 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_g &= \frac{1}{3.324 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 300.8 \, \text{K} \\
&= 300.8 - 273.15 \\
&\approx 27.6 \, ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation

The blend has a **single Tg around 28°C**, meaning the adhesive transitions from rigid to rubbery near room temperature. This is ideal for **flexible structural bonding applications** like automotive panel bonding.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Adhesive Selection

This **KTU-style matrix** is commonly tested in 14-mark problems.

| Requirement | Best Adhesive Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace structural (aluminum fuselage)** | Epoxy / Epoxy-phenolic | High shear strength, fatigue resistance, low creep |
| **Automotive windshield bonding** | Polyurethane | Flexible, fast cure, FMVSS 212 compliant |
| **Shoe upper-to-sole** | Polyurethane / Neoprene contact cement | Flexible, high peel strength, water resistance |
| **Electronic PCB component mounting** | UV-cure acrylic / Epoxy | Fast cure, precise dispensing, no heat |
| **Food packaging / labeling** | PVA emulsion / Dextrin / Hot-melt EVA | Non-toxic, fast set, FDA approved |
| **Cryogenic LNG tank sealing** | Epoxy-phenolic | Retains strength at −196°C |
| **Cardboard box sealing** | Starch / Hot-melt EVA | Cheap, fast, biodegradable |

# 📝 Module 1: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define adhesives. Differentiate between adhesion and cohesion with a suitable example.** (3 Marks)

### Model Answer:

> **Adhesives** are non-metallic substances, capable of joining materials together by surface attachment (adhesion) and possessing internal strength (cohesion), without undergoing any change in the bulk properties of the substrates.

| Property | Adhesion | Cohesion |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Force of attraction between **adhesive and substrate** at the interface | Internal attractive force holding **adhesive molecules together** in its bulk |
| **Location** | At the **bond line interface** | Within the **bulk** of the adhesive layer |
| **Example** | Glue sticking to wood fibers | Glue holding itself together as a film |
| **Failure Mode** | **Adhesive failure** (peels cleanly from substrate) | **Cohesive failure** (splits within adhesive) |

**Example:** A drop of honey on a glass plate — the attraction between honey molecules and glass molecules is *adhesion*; the attraction among honey molecules themselves is *cohesion*.

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three natural adhesives and state one application of each.** (3 Marks)

### Model Answer:

1. **Starch / Dextrin** — derived from corn, potato, or tapioca. *Application:* Corrugated cardboard box manufacturing (paper-to-paper bonding).
2. **Animal Glue** — derived from collagen in bones and hides. *Application:* Book binding and wood joinery (traditional furniture).
3. **Natural Rubber Latex** — from *Hevea brasiliensis* tree. *Application:* Pressure-sensitive adhesive tapes and surgical tapes.
