topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermodynamics of Adhesion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thermodynamics of Adhesion** is the branch of interfacial science that quantitatively describes the energy changes associated with the formation and rupture of an adhesive bond at an interface, without invoking the specific molecular mechanisms (which fall under "adhesion mechanics"). It treats adhesion as a reversible, equilibrium process governed by the minimization of the **Gibbs free energy** of the system, where the driving force is the reduction in **interfacial free energy** when an adhesive wets a substrate.

The subject is built upon three fundamental pillars:

1. **Surface free energy (γ)** — the excess Gibbs free energy per unit area at a surface (units: **mJ/m²** or **dynes/cm**).
2. **Interfacial free energy (γ₁₂)** — the excess free energy at the boundary between two condensed phases.
3. **Contact angle (θ)** — the macroscopic geometric observable that bridges thermodynamics with experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Adhesion, in thermodynamic terms, is a **spontaneous process** only if the total free energy of the system *decreases*. Therefore, the *work of adhesion* ($W_a$) must exceed the *work of cohesion* ($W_c$) for spontaneous wetting and intimate contact — the foundational criterion for adhesive joint design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have two identical drops of water: one placed on a freshly cleaned glass slide, and another on a greasy Teflon pan.

- On **glass**, the droplet flattens out and spreads. Why? Because the water molecules *prefer* to interact with the silica (Si–O–H) groups on the glass surface more strongly than with each other. The system lowers its energy by maximizing the contact area.
- On **Teflon**, the droplet beads up into a near-perfect sphere. The water molecules *prefer* to stay amongst themselves rather than touch the fluorocarbon surface. The system lowers its energy by minimizing the contact area.

**This "preference for whom to touch" is precisely what thermodynamics of adhesion quantifies.** It converts the macroscopic bead shape into numbers (contact angle → work of adhesion → surface free energies) that engineers use to **predict** whether a real adhesive will wet, spread, and stick to a given substrate.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of surface free energy (γ) as the *social preference score* of a material: a high γ means the material is **"needy"** (it wants to bond with anything nearby), while a low γ means it is **"independent"** (perfectly happy with itself, like a Teflon-coated pan).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module

| Quantity | Symbol | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface free energy | $\gamma$ | mJ/m² | Excess energy per unit area of surface |
| Interfacial free energy | $\gamma_{SL}, \gamma_{LA}, \gamma_{SA}$ | mJ/m² | Energy at solid–liquid, liquid–air, solid–air interfaces |
| Contact angle | $\theta$ | degrees (°) | Geometric observable of wetting |
| Work of adhesion | $W_a$ | mJ/m² | Energy required to separate unit area of interface |
| Work of cohesion | $W_c$ | mJ/m² | Energy required to separate unit area of a bulk phase |
| Spreading coefficient | $S$ | mJ/m² | Predicts whether liquid spontaneously spreads |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Interfacial Energies at a Three-Phase Contact Line

When a liquid droplet (L) sits on a solid substrate (S) in equilibrium with its vapour (A = ambient/air), a **three-phase contact line** is formed. At this line, three interfaces meet, each carrying its own free energy per unit area:

- $\gamma_{SA}$ — solid–ambient (vapour) interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{SL}$ — solid–liquid interfacial energy
- \gamma_{LA} — liquid–ambient interfacial energy (commonly written as \gamma_L)

## 2.2 Young's Equation (1805) — The Foundation Stone

**Statement:** At mechanical and thermodynamic equilibrium at the three-phase contact line, the horizontal components of the three interfacial tensions must balance.

$$\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the **bedrock of wetting thermodynamics** and is derivable from either a force balance or a virtual displacement of the contact line (both shown in Section 3).

## 2.3 Dupré's Equation (1869) — Work of Adhesion

The **reversible work of adhesion** ($W_a$) is the free energy decrease when unit area of two distinct phases (1 and 2) form an interface, minus the free energy of the two original surfaces.

$$W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$$

When phases 1 and 2 are condensed (solid and liquid), this gives the energy released upon formation of the solid–liquid interface:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - \gamma_{SL}$$

## 2.4 Work of Cohesion ($W_c$)

For a single phase splitting into two identical surfaces:

$$W_c = 2\gamma$$

> For a pure liquid: $W_c = 2\gamma_L$.

## 2.5 Young–Dupré Equation (Combining 2.2 and 2.3)

By eliminating $\gamma_{SL}$ from Young's and Dupré's equations:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LA}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Equation**: This is the *most tested* relation in Module 1, as it directly connects a **measurable quantity** (contact angle $\theta$) to a **thermodynamic quantity** ($W_a$).

## 2.6 Spreading Coefficient (Harkins, 1929)

Defines whether a liquid will **completely wet** (spread into a thin film) on a solid surface:

$$S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LA}$$

Or equivalently:

$$S = \gamma_{SA} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA})$$

- $S > 0$ → complete wetting (spreading)
- $S < 0$ → partial wetting (droplet forms, $\theta > 0$)
- $S = 0$ → critical wetting condition (θ = 0°)

## 2.7 Critical Surface Tension of Wetting ($\gamma_c$) — Zisman (1964)

Zisman empirically observed that for many homologous liquids on a given solid, $\cos\theta$ varies linearly with $\gamma_L$. Extrapolating $\cos\theta = 1$ (i.e., $\theta = 0$) gives the **critical surface tension**:

$$\cos\theta = 1 - \beta(\gamma_L - \gamma_c)$$

Liquids with $\gamma_L \leq \gamma_c$ will completely wet the solid.

## 2.8 Fowkes' Approach (1964) — Component Decomposition of γ

Fowkes proposed that the total surface free energy is the sum of independent contributions from different intermolecular forces:

$$\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p + \gamma^h + \gamma^{\pi} + \gamma^i + \cdots$$

where superscripts denote: $d$ = dispersion (London), $p$ = polar, $h$ = hydrogen bonding, $\pi$ = π-bonding, $i$ = induction.

A common simplified form (Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble, OWRK):

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$$

> This geometric-mean approximation is widely used to determine **solid surface energy** from contact-angle measurements with probe liquids of known $\gamma_L^d$ and $\gamma_L^p$.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$ | Young's equation | Wetting geometry |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$ | Dupré's equation | Adhesion strength (thermodynamic) |
| 3 | $W_c = 2\gamma$ | Work of cohesion | Cohesive strength baseline |
| 4 | $W_a = \gamma_{LA}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Young–Dupré equation | $W_a$ from contact angle |
| 5 | $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LA}$ | Spreading coefficient | Predict complete wetting |
| 6 | $\cos\theta = 1 - \beta(\gamma_L - \gamma_c)$ | Zisman's empirical | Find critical $\gamma_c$ of solid |
| 7 | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p})$ | Owens–Wendt (OWRK) | Determine $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$ |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Adhesive formulation**: Predict which resin will wet a given plastic or metal substrate.
- **Surface treatment verification**: Quantify the effect of plasma, corona, or chemical etching on polymer surface energy.
- **Coating industry**: Determine whether a paint or ink will wet and spread on a substrate without retreating (beading).
- **Biomedical implants**: Control protein/cell adhesion on titanium or polymer surfaces by tuning $\gamma$.
- **Microfluidics & lab-on-chip**: Design channel surface energies for controlled droplet motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Young's Equation from Force Balance

**Setup:** Consider a sessile liquid droplet on a flat, smooth, homogeneous, rigid solid substrate in equilibrium with its vapour.

Let the three-phase contact line have length $\ell$ (per unit length = 1). At this line, three interfacial tensions act tangentially:

- $\gamma_{SA}$ pulls the solid surface *outward* (away from the droplet) along the substrate plane.
- $\gamma_{SL}$ pulls the solid–liquid interface *inward* (under the droplet).
- $\gamma_{LA}$ acts along the liquid surface tangent, making angle $\theta$ with the solid surface.

**Horizontal force balance** (taking "outward from droplet" as positive):

$$\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

**Rearranging:**

$$\gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

> *Geometric Note:* Only the horizontal component of $\gamma_{LA}$ enters the balance because the vertical components are supported by the substrate (normal stress) and play no role in the *horizontal* equilibrium condition for an undeformable solid.

**Equivalence with energy approach:** Imagine an infinitesimal virtual displacement $dx$ of the contact line outward (the liquid advances by $dx$, covering fresh solid; vapour recedes). The change in total surface free energy must be zero at equilibrium:

$$dG = (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SA})\,dx + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta\,dx = 0$$

Solving:

$$\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

Both routes give the **identical result** — confirming thermodynamic self-consistency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Dupré's Work of Adhesion

**Setup:** Two condensed phases (1) and (2), each with surface free energies $\gamma_1$ and $\gamma_2$, initially have surfaces exposed to vacuum (or their own vapour). They are brought into contact to form an interface of energy $\gamma_{12}$ per unit area.

**Initial state energy (per unit area):**

$$G_i = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2$$

**Final state energy (per unit area):**

$$G_f = \gamma_{12}$$

**Free energy change:**

$$\Delta G = G_f - G_i = \gamma_{12} - \gamma_1 - \gamma_2$$

**Reversible work of adhesion** is the negative of this free-energy change (work done *by* the system on bringing surfaces together):

$$W_a = -\Delta G = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$$

This is **Dupré's equation** (1869).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Young–Dupré Equation

**Goal:** Eliminate $\gamma_{SL}$ between Young's and Dupré's equations to obtain $W_a$ in terms of measurable $\theta$.

**Start with Dupré's equation for a solid–liquid pair (with ambient as phase 3):**

$$W_a = \gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - \gamma_{SL}$$

**Substitute $\gamma_{SL}$ from Young's equation:**

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

**Therefore:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - (\gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta)$$

$$W_a = \cancel{\gamma_{SA}} + \gamma_{LA} - \cancel{\gamma_{SA}} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{LA}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

> *Engineering Insight:* Since $\theta$ is **directly measurable** with a goniometer (one drop, one photograph) and $\gamma_{LA}$ is **tabulated** for almost all common liquids (water, diiodomethane, glycerol, formamide), $W_a$ is the *first thermodynamic quantity* an adhesive scientist can obtain experimentally.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Spreading Coefficient

**Definition:** Harkins defined the spreading coefficient as the difference between work of adhesion and work of cohesion of the liquid:

$$S = W_a - W_c$$

For a liquid spreading on a solid substrate:

$$W_a = \gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - \gamma_{SL}$$

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LA}$$

Therefore:

$$S = (\gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - \gamma_{SL}) - 2\gamma_{LA}$$

$$\boxed{S = \gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LA}}$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- If $S > 0$: the energy released by adhesion exceeds the cohesive energy "spent" by spreading — **complete wetting** ($\theta = 0$).
- If $S < 0$: spreading costs more energy than it releases — **partial wetting** ($\theta > 0$).
- At the critical condition $S = 0$: $\theta \to 0$, the borderline of total wetting.

---

## 3.5 Derivation V — Critical Surface Tension (Zisman Plot)

Zisman found empirically that for a homologous series of liquids on a given solid:

$$\cos\theta = 1 - \beta(\gamma_L - \gamma_c)$$

**Derivation logic (dimensional/empirical):**

At $\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$, complete wetting occurs, implying the liquid's surface tension is at or below the solid's "critical" surface tension:

$$\gamma_L \le \gamma_c \implies \theta = 0°$$

The straight-line fit of $\cos\theta$ vs $\gamma_L$ extrapolated to $\cos\theta = 1$ gives $\gamma_c$ on the abscissa.

**Example numerical use:**
Suppose on PTFE (Teflon), the following data are obtained:

| Liquid | $\gamma_L$ (mJ/m²) | $\cos\theta$ |
|---|---|---|
| n-Hexane | 18.4 | 0.95 |
| n-Octane | 21.8 | 0.82 |
| n-Decane | 23.9 | 0.71 |
| n-Tetradecane | 26.7 | 0.55 |

Linear regression of $\cos\theta$ vs $\gamma_L$ gives intercept at $\cos\theta = 1$ corresponding to $\gamma_c \approx 18$ mJ/m² for PTFE — confirming its well-known low surface energy.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for Adhesion Prediction

| Step | Question | Tool/Equation | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is the substrate clean and smooth? | Contact-angle measurement | If yes → proceed; else surface treatment |
| 2 | What is $\gamma_{LA}$ of the adhesive? | Tabulated / Wilhelmy plate | Compare with substrate |
| 3 | What is the contact angle $\theta$? | Goniometer | Compute $W_a$ via Young–Dupré |
| 4 | Does $W_a > W_c$? | Compare $W_a$ with $2\gamma_{LA}$ | If yes → spontaneous spreading ($S > 0$) |
| 5 | What is the substrate surface energy? | OWRK with probe liquids | Quantify $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$ |
| 6 | Will the joint survive service? | $W_a$ vs measured peel strength | Thermodynamic ceiling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thermodynamics of Wetting at a Contact Line

This block diagram maps the conceptual flow of thermodynamic analysis, from molecular interactions to measurable contact angle, in a single cohesive architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PHASE 1 Molecular Interactions London Dispersion Polar Acid-Base H-Bonding"] --> B["PHASE 2 Interfacial Energies gamma_S gamma_L gamma_SL"]
    B --> C["PHASE 3 Three Phase Contact Line Solid Liquid Ambient"]
    C --> D["PHASE 4 Force Balance Horizontal Resolution"]
    D --> E["Youngs Equation gamma_SA equals gamma_SL plus gamma_LA cos theta"]
    E --> F["PHASE 5 Dupre Equation W_a equals gamma_SA plus gamma_LA minus gamma_SL"]
    F --> G["PHASE 6 Eliminate gamma_SL Algebraic Substitution"]
    G --> H["Young Dupre Equation W_a equals gamma_LA times 1 plus cos theta"]
    H --> I["PHASE 7 Wetting Criteria W_a greater than W_c Spreading Coefficient S"]
    I --> J{"Decision S greater than 0"}
    J -- Yes --> K["Complete Wetting theta equals 0 Thin Film Formation"]
    J -- No --> L["Partial Wetting Droplet Forms Finite Contact Angle"]
    L --> M["Quantify via Zisman Plot or OWRK Method"]
    M --> N["Predict Adhesive Joint Performance"]
```

## 4.2 Free-Energy Decision Tree — Adhesion vs Cohesion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Start Adhesive Candidate L on Substrate S"] --> P2["Measure Contact Angle theta"]
    P2 --> P3["Look up gamma_LA from Tables"]
    P3 --> P4["Apply Young Dupre W_a equals gamma_LA times 1 plus cos theta"]
    P4 --> P5["Compute W_c equals 2 times gamma_LA"]
    P5 --> P6{"W_a versus W_c"}
    P6 -- W_a greater than W_c --> P7["Spontaneous Wetting Good Adhesive Candidate"]
    P6 -- W_a less than W_c --> P8["Poor Wetting Cohesion Dominates Modify Surface or Resin"]
    P8 --> P9["Apply Surface Treatment Plasma Corona Chemical Etching"]
    P9 --> P2
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Three-Phase Contact Line (Conceptual Block Representation)

```mermaid

    subgraph left["Solid Substrate Region"]
        S1["Solid Surface Energy gamma_SA"]
    end
    subgraph center["Contact Line Region"]
        I1["Interfacial Energy gamma_SL"]
        I2["Liquid Vapour Energy gamma_LA"]
        I3["Contact Angle theta"]
    end
    subgraph right["Liquid Droplet Region"]
        L1["Liquid Bulk gamma_LA"]

    S1 --- I1
    I1 --- I3
    I3 --- I2
    I2 --- L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block-level representation replaces an actual sessile-droplet drawing while preserving the **functional interconnections** between the three interfacial energies and the contact angle $\theta$ at the triple line.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *thermodynamic work of adhesion* and state Dupré's equation. How is it experimentally determined for a solid–liquid system?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **thermodynamic work of adhesion** ($W_a$) is the reversible free energy decrease (per unit area) when two distinct condensed phases form an interface. **Dupré's equation (1869):**

$$W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$$

For a solid–liquid system with ambient as phase 3: $W_a = \gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - \gamma_{SL}$.

**Experimental determination:** Combine Young's equation with Dupré's equation to eliminate $\gamma_{SL}$, yielding the **Young–Dupré equation:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{LA}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

Both $\gamma_{LA}$ (tabulated for the liquid) and $\theta$ (measured by goniometer) are known, so $W_a$ is obtained without measuring $\gamma_{SL}$ directly.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **work of adhesion** and **work of cohesion**. State the criterion for spontaneous spreading of a liquid on a solid surface.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Work of Adhesion ($W_a$) | Work of Cohesion ($W_c$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Energy to separate unit area of a **dissimilar** interface | Energy to separate unit area of a **like** interface |
| Equation | $W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$ | $W_c = 2\gamma$ |
| When $W_a > W_c$ | Adhesion dominates | Cohesion dominates |

**Criterion for spontaneous spreading (complete wetting):**

$$W_a > W_c \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad S = W_a - W_c > 0$$

The spreading coefficient $S > 0$ implies $\theta = 0°$ and the liquid forms a continuous film on the substrate.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks with KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Starting from the force balance at the three-phase contact line, **derive Young's equation** and discuss the assumptions inherent in it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Young–Dupré equation** and using the data below, calculate the **thermodynamic work of adhesion** of water on a cleaned aluminium surface at 25 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Given:** $\gamma_{LA}(\text{water}, 25\,°\text{C}) = 72.8$ mJ/m², measured contact angle $\theta = 65°$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a sessile liquid droplet (L) on a flat, smooth, homogeneous, rigid solid (S) surrounded by its vapour (A = ambient). At the three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act: $\gamma_{SA}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LA}$.

**Step 2 — Force balance (horizontal component, outward from droplet = positive):**

$$\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent energy derivation:** Virtual displacement $dx$ of the contact line outward:

$$dG = (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SA})dx + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta\,dx = 0$$

gives the same result.

**Step 4 — Assumptions:**
1. Solid surface is **smooth**, **rigid**, **homogeneous**, and **isotropic**.
2. Solid is **not deformed** by the liquid (no spreading pressure).
3. Line tension is negligible compared to interfacial forces.
4. The droplet is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** (quasi-static).
5. **No adsorption** of vapour onto the solid beyond the equilibrium vapour pressure.

**Step 5 — Limit:** Valid only for ideal, non-reactive systems; on rough/chemically heterogeneous surfaces, the measured $\theta$ becomes the *apparent* contact angle (Wenzel's or Cassie's regime — covered in higher modules).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — State Young–Dupré equation:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{LA}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$\cos(65°) = 0.4226$$

$$W_a = 72.8\,(1 + 0.4226)$$

$$W_a = 72.8 \times 1.4226$$

$$\boxed{W_a \approx 103.6 \text{ mJ/m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** This is the upper-bound *thermodynamic* work of adhesion. The actual *practical* peel or shear strength will be lower due to viscoelastic, dissipative, and rate-dependent losses (mechanical adhesion considerations, Module 2).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is the **spreading coefficient**? Derive it from thermodynamic principles. A liquid with $\gamma_{LA} = 45$ mJ/m² forms a contact angle of 30° on a solid. The interfacial energy $\gamma_{SL} = 25$ mJ/m². Determine whether the liquid will **completely wet** the solid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the **OWRK (Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble) equation**, explain how the **dispersive** and **polar** components of a solid's surface free energy can be determined experimentally using two probe liquids. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition:** The spreading coefficient $S$ is the difference between the work of adhesion and the work of cohesion:

$$S = W_a - W_c$$

**Step 2 — Derivation:**

$$S = (\gamma_{SA} + \gamma_{LA} - \gamma_{SL}) - 2\gamma_{LA}$$

$$\boxed{S = \gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LA}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical computation using Young's equation:**

$$\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta = 25 + 45\cos(30°)$$

$$\gamma_{SA} = 25 + 45 \times 0.8660 = 25 + 38.97 = 63.97 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

$$S = 63.97 - 25 - 45 = -6.03 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** Since $S < 0$, the liquid will **NOT completely wet** the solid; a finite contact angle will persist (partial wetting regime, as observed $\theta = 30°$).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — OWRK equation:**

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Combine with Young–Dupré:**

$$W_a = \gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_S^d\gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p\gamma_L^p}\right)$$

$$\gamma_L(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d}\sqrt{\gamma_L^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p}\sqrt{\gamma_L^p}$$

**Step 3 — Experimental procedure:**
- Use **two probe liquids** with very different polarities (e.g., **diiodomethane** — purely dispersive, $\gamma_L^p = 0$, and **water** — both dispersive and polar).
- Measure the contact angle $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ on the unknown solid.
- Write the OWRK equation for each liquid — two equations, two unknowns ($\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$).
- Solve the simultaneous linear system:

$$\gamma_{L1}(1 + \cos\theta_1) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d}\sqrt{\gamma_{L1}^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p}\sqrt{\gamma_{L1}^p}$$

$$\gamma_{L2}(1 + \cos\theta_2) = 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d}\sqrt{\gamma_{L2}^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^p}\sqrt{\gamma_{L2}^p}$$

**Step 4 — Total solid surface energy:** $\gamma_S = \gamma_S^d + \gamma_S^p$

> *Why two liquids?* A single equation in two unknowns ($\gamma_S^d$, $\gamma_S^p$) is unsolvable. Two probe liquids of contrasting polarity provide the second independent equation needed to resolve both components uniquely.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes (Module 1 — Thermodynamics of Adhesion):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1 + \cos\theta)$ factor** in Young–Dupré. Many students write $W_a = \gamma_L\cos\theta$ alone — this is a **2-mark penalty** per occurrence in ESE.
> 2. **Confusing work of adhesion with work of cohesion.** $W_a$ involves *dissimilar* phases; $W_c$ involves a *single* phase splitting. Mixing these up in spreading-coefficient problems yields wrong sign of $S$.
> 3. **Using $\gamma_L$ (liquid surface tension) in place of $\gamma_{LA}$.** For most common liquids in air at 25 °C, these are numerically identical, but examiners in KTU strict-marking schemes *expect* the explicit notation $\gamma_{LA}$.
> 4. **Omitting assumptions in Young's equation derivation.** Listing only "smooth surface" loses marks; examiners expect all five: smooth, rigid, homogeneous, isotropic, no line tension.
> 5. **Sign errors in spreading-coefficient calculation.** If you compute $S$ and get a positive value when the given $\theta > 0°$, **recheck** — partial wetting always implies $S < 0$.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Three interfacial energies** ($\gamma_{SA}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LA}$) coexist at the contact line.
- ☐ **Young's equation** ($\gamma_{SA} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LA}\cos\theta$) is the geometric foundation; valid only on ideal surfaces.
- ☐ **Dupré's equation** ($W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$) is the thermodynamic foundation.
- ☐ **Young–Dupré equation** $W_a = \gamma_{LA}(1 + \cos\theta)$ is the **most-used** formula in this module — commit it to memory.
- ☐ **Work of cohesion** $W_c = 2\gamma$ applies to a single pure phase.
- ☐ **Spreading coefficient** $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SA} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LA}$:
  - $S > 0$ → complete wetting
  - $S < 0$ → partial wetting (droplet forms)
- ☐ **Zisman's $\gamma_c$** is obtained by extrapolating $\cos\theta$ vs $\gamma_L$ to $\cos\theta = 1$.
- ☐ **OWRK / Fowkes** decompose $\gamma$ into dispersive ($\gamma^d$) and polar ($\gamma^p$) parts; need **two probe liquids** of contrasting polarity to solve.
- ☐ **Thermodynamic $W_a$** is the *upper bound* on practical adhesive strength — real joints are weaker due to viscoelastic dissipation.
- ☐ **Units throughout**: mJ/m² (SI for surface energy); contact angle in degrees; $\cos\theta$ is dimensionless.

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — Thermodynamics of Adhesion**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PEPOT861 — Adhesive Science & Technology*

topics.title: Contact Angle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Contact Angle # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Contact Angle ($\theta$)**: The quantitative measure of the angle formed at the **three-phase contact line** (also called the *triple point* or *wetting line*) where the **liquid**, **solid substrate**, and **vapour/gas phase** intersect. It is conventionally measured through the denser liquid phase, bounded by the liquid–vapour interface and the tangent to the solid surface at the point of contact. In the context of **Adhesive Science & Technology**, the contact angle is the *fundamental thermodynamic indicator of wettability*. It directly quantifies the ability of a liquid adhesive (or molten polymer) to spread over and intimate contact with a solid adherend surface — the very first prerequisite for adhesion. **Standard Metric Units:** - Angle: **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)** - Most commonly reported between **0°** (perfect wetting) and **180°** (complete non-wetting). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Picture**: Imagine placing a droplet of water on a freshly waxed car body versus on a clean glass slide. > - On the **waxed car**: The drop beads up like a tiny dome — it does **not want to spread**. The contact angle is **large** (>90°). > - On the **glass slide**: The drop flattens out and spreads aggressively — it **wants to wet** the surface. The contact angle is **small** (<90°). > > The contact angle is therefore a direct visual "thermometer" of how friendly a liquid is to a solid surface. A small angle = good wetting = good adhesive bond precursor. A large angle = poor wetting = bad adhesion. **Why this matters for adhesives:** Before any chemical or mechanical bonding can occur between an adhesive and a substrate, the adhesive must first *physically flow* over every micro-roughness and *intimately contact* every atom of the substrate surface. If the contact angle is high, the adhesive leaves gaps, voids, and air pockets — catastrophically reducing bond strength. This is the **thermodynamic work of adhesion**, and it begins with the contact angle. ## 1.3 The Three Wetting Regimes (KTU High-Yield Classification) | Regime | Contact Angle Range | Adhesion Implication | |---|---|---| | **Complete Wetting** | $\theta = 0°$ | Total spreading — adhesive forms a continuous film | | **Partial Wetting** | $0° < \theta < 90°$ | Good wetting — adhesion is favourable | | **High Contact Angle** | $90° < \theta < 180°$ | Poor wetting — adhesion is poor, beading occurs | | **Complete Non-Wetting** | $\theta = 180°$ | Theoretical limit — drop sits as perfect sphere | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: Module 1 establishes contact angle as the *first gateway concept* linking surface energetics to bond formation. The student must master the link: **interfacial energies → Young's equation → contact angle → wettability → adhesive bond quality.** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Interfacial Tensions at the Triple Line At the three-phase boundary point, **three interfacial tensions** act simultaneously, each trying to minimize the system's free energy: 1. **$\gamma_{SV}$** — Solid–Vapour interfacial tension (energy per unit area of the solid–air interface). 2. **$\gamma_{SL}$** — Solid–Liquid interfacial tension (energy per unit area of the solid–liquid interface). 3. **$\gamma_{LV}$** — Liquid–Vapour interfacial tension (commonly just called the *surface tension* of the liquid). > [!IMPORTANT] > All $\gamma$ values carry the **standard unit of mN/m** (millinewtons per metre), which is dimensionally equivalent to **mJ/m²** (millijoules per square metre) — the unit of surface free energy. ## 2.2 Horizontal Force Balance at the Contact Line Consider a tiny segment of the three-phase contact line. Three forces per unit length pull along the interfaces: - $\gamma_{SV}$ pulls **outward along the solid surface** (away from the liquid). - $\gamma_{SL}$ pulls **along the solid surface, inward** (toward the liquid bulk). - $\gamma_{LV}$ pulls **along the liquid–vapour interface** at angle $\theta$ from the solid surface. Resolving the horizontal components and equating for mechanical equilibrium gives the **famous Young's Equation (1805)**. ## 2.3 Young's Equation — The Cornerstone of Wetting Science $$ \gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ This is the **single most important equation** in all of wetting and adhesion science. Rearranged: $$ \cos\theta \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}} $$ ## 2.4 Young–Dupré Equation — Work of Adhesion The **thermodynamic work of adhesion** $W_a$ (energy released per unit area when a liquid–solid interface is created from separate liquid and solid surfaces) is given by the **Young–Dupré equation**: $$ W_{a} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) \;=\; \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} $$ ## 2.5 Work of Cohesion (for completeness in wetting analysis) For a pure liquid, the work of cohesion (energy to separate a unit area of liquid into two liquid–vapour interfaces) is: $$ W_{c} \;=\; 2\gamma_{LV} $$ ## 2.6 Spreading Coefficient $S$ The condition for **complete spontaneous spreading** of a liquid over a solid: $$ S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV} \;=\; W_{a} - W_{c} $$ > If $S \geq 0$ → complete wetting ($\theta = 0°$). > If $S < 0$ → partial wetting (finite $\theta$). ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Use / Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Valid on **smooth, rigid, homogeneous, isotropic** solid surfaces | | 2 | Cosine form | $\cos\theta = \dfrac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Used to compute $\theta$ from measured/interfacial tensions | | 3 | Young–Dupré | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Direct measure of **adhesion strength** at the molecular level | | 4 | Work of Cohesion | $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | For pure liquid–liquid interface | | 5 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | Predicts whether spreading is spontaneous | | 6 | Critical Surface Tension (Zisman) | $\gamma_c \approx \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \rightarrow 1$ | Empirical — wets if $\gamma_{LV} \leq \gamma_c$ | | 7 | Contact Angle Hysteresis | $\Delta\theta = \theta_{adv} - \theta_{rec}$ | Surface roughness/chemical heterogeneity indicator | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Adhesive Bonding**: Selecting primers/surface treatments to lower $\theta$ before applying structural adhesives in aerospace and automotive joints. - **Coatings & Paints**: Formulating paints that wet low-energy polymer substrates (PE, PP) — requires surface energy matching. - **Biomedical Implants**: Designing hydrophobic vs. hydrophilic surfaces for stents, catheters, and drug-eluting coatings. - **Microfluidics**: Engineering droplet motion in lab-on-chip devices using controlled $\theta$ gradients. - **Fibre–Matrix Composites**: Ensuring proper fibre wetting by the resin is the single most critical factor determining composite strength. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Young's Equation We consider a sessile liquid droplet resting on an ideal solid substrate in a vapour environment. We assume: - Solid is smooth, rigid, chemically homogeneous, and isotropic. - System is at **thermodynamic equilibrium**. - Drop is small enough that **gravity can be neglected** (capillary length regime). ### Step 1 — Identify the Three Surface Free Energies At the triple line, three distinct interfaces meet: 1. Solid–Vapour interface with free energy per unit area $\gamma_{SV}$. 2. Solid–Liquid interface with free energy per unit area $\gamma_{SL}$. 3. Liquid–Vapour interface with free energy per unit area $\gamma_{LV}$. ### Step 2 — Express the Total Surface Free Energy $G$ For a drop of base radius $r$ on the solid, the total interfacial area contributions are: - Solid–Vapour region not covered by drop: area $= A_{solid} - \pi r^2$. - Solid–Liquid region: area $= \pi r^2$. - Liquid–Vapour interface: area $= 2\pi r^2$ (for a hemispherical cap, ignoring numerical prefactors). The total surface free energy: $$ G \;=\; \gamma_{SV}(A_{solid} - \pi r^2) + \gamma_{SL}(\pi r^2) + \gamma_{LV}(A_{LV}) $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Virtual Displacement of the Contact Line Allow the contact line to advance by an infinitesimal displacement $dr$. The droplet spreads so that the base radius increases by $dr$. Since the drop volume is conserved, the change in the liquid–vapour area must account for the geometry change. For a small displacement along the solid plane: $$ dG \;=\; (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV}) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \cdot dA_{LV} $$ ### Step 4 — Geometry of the Liquid–Vapour Surface Change When the base expands by $2\pi r \, dr$ (i.e., perimeter times displacement), the liquid–vapour area changes. By projecting the $LV$ interface along the horizontal, the vertical component contributes nothing, while the horizontal projection of the $LV$ area equals the contact line displacement. The change in liquid–vapour area is therefore: $$ dA_{LV} \;=\; -2\pi r \cos\theta \, dr $$ The negative sign indicates that as the drop spreads outward, the *vertical projection* of the LV interface contracts horizontally. ### Step 5 — Equilibrium Condition: $dG = 0$ At equilibrium, the total free energy is minimized, so $dG = 0$: $$ (\gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV}) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \cdot (-2\pi r \cos\theta \, dr) \;=\; 0 $$ Divide both sides by $2\pi r \, dr$: $$ \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;=\; 0 $$ ### Step 6 — Rearrange to Standard Young's Equation Form $$ \gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ Or equivalently: $$ \cos\theta \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation**: $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ is the **driving force for spreading** (how much the solid "prefers" being covered by liquid). $\gamma_{LV}$ is the **resisting force** (the liquid's surface tension trying to pull itself into a sphere). The cosine of $\theta$ is the ratio of these two competing tendencies. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation for Work of Adhesion The **Dupré equation** (1869) defines the work of adhesion as the reversible work required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface into separate solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces: $$ W_a \;=\; \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} $$ Substituting Young's equation $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ into the Dupré equation: $$ W_a \;=\; \underbrace{(\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)}_{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} $$ The $\gamma_{SL}$ terms cancel exactly: $$ W_a \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta + \gamma_{LV} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{W_a \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight**: Since $W_a$ is a measurable thermodynamic quantity that directly reflects the **thermodynamic intrinsic adhesion** (excluding mechanical interlocking and chemical bonding contributions), it serves as the upper theoretical limit of adhesion strength achievable purely from interfacial molecular contact. Higher $\theta$ → lower $W_a$ → weaker adhesive joint. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style) **Problem**: A polyethylene substrate has $\gamma_{SV} = 31.0$ mN/m. A molten adhesive drop with $\gamma_{LV} = 25.0$ mN/m gives an interfacial tension of $\gamma_{SL} = 8.5$ mN/m at the operating temperature. Calculate: (a) the contact angle, and (b) the thermodynamic work of adhesion. **Solution**: ### Part (a): Contact Angle Using Young's equation: $$ \cos\theta \;=\; \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}} \;=\; \frac{31.0 - 8.5}{25.0} \;=\; \frac{22.5}{25.0} \;=\; 0.900 $$ Therefore: $$ \theta \;=\; \cos^{-1}(0.900) \;\approx\; 25.84° $$ ### Part (b): Work of Adhesion $$ W_a \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) \;=\; 25.0 \times (1 + 0.900) \;=\; 25.0 \times 1.900 $$ $$ W_a \;=\; 47.5 \; \text{mJ/m}^2 $$ > **Engineering verdict**: $\theta \approx 26°$ is well below 90°, indicating **good wetting**, consistent with a useful $W_a$ of 47.5 mJ/m² for a polymer–polymer interface. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Three-Phase Contact Line Force Balance ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Solid Substrate
Surface Energy gammaSV"] nodeB["Sessile Liquid Droplet
Surface Tension gammaLV"] nodeC["Vapour Phase
Air Environment"] nodeD["Triple Contact Line
3-Phase Boundary Point"] nodeE["Contact Angle Theta
Measured Through Liquid"] nodeF["gammaSV Pulls Outward Along Solid"] nodeG["gammaSL Pulls Inward Along Solid"] nodeH["gammaLV Pulls Along LV Interface at Angle Theta"] nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeA --> nodeF nodeA --> nodeG nodeB --> nodeH ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — Wetting Regime Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Liquid Drop Placed on Solid Substrate"] measure["Measure Contact Angle Theta"] complete["Complete Wetting
Theta equals 0 degree
Spreading Coefficient S greater than 0"] good["Partial Wetting Favourable
0 less than Theta less than 90 degree
Good Adhesion Expected"] poor["Partial Wetting Unfavourable
90 less than Theta less than 180 degree
Poor Adhesion Beading Occurs"] nonwet["Complete Non Wetting
Theta equals 180 degree
Theoretical Limit Drop is Perfect Sphere"] start --> measure measure --> complete measure --> good measure --> poor measure --> nonwet ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Linkage from Surface Energies to Adhesion Strength ```mermaid flowchart LR inp1["Interfacial Tensions
gammaSV gammaSL gammaLV"] inp2["Surface Chemistry
Substrate and Adhesive"] inp3["Surface Roughness
Heterogeneity"] proc1["Young Equation
cos Theta equals gammaSV minus gammaSL over gammaLV"] proc2["Young Dupre Equation
Wa equals gammaLV times 1 plus cos Theta"] proc3["Spreading Coefficient
S equals gammaSV minus gammaSL minus gammaLV"] out1["Contact Angle Theta
Wettability Indicator"] out2["Work of Adhesion Wa
Thermodynamic Bond Strength"] out3["Spontaneous Spread or Bead
Practical Bond Quality"] inp1 --> proc1 inp2 --> inp1 inp3 --> proc1 proc1 --> out1 proc1 --> proc2 proc2 --> out2 proc1 --> proc3 proc3 --> out3 out2 --> out3 ``` ## 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (For Sketch in Exam) ``` γ_LV ↗ / / θ /___________ γ_SV (→) |←———— γ_SL ————→| ────────████████████████████ ← Solid Substrate ~~~~~~~~~ ← Liquid Drop (sessile) ~~~~~~~~~~~ ← Vapour above ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Reading**: The angle $\theta$ is measured inside the liquid, between the tangent to the liquid–vapour interface and the solid surface. $\gamma_{SV}$ acts along the solid surface outward; $\gamma_{SL}$ acts along the solid surface inward; $\gamma_{LV}$ acts along the curved liquid–vapour interface at angle $\theta$ from the horizontal. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define contact angle. State Young's equation and explain the physical significance of each term.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Definition (1 mark)**: The contact angle $\theta$ is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line where the solid, liquid, and vapour phases meet, measured through the liquid phase between the tangent to the liquid–vapour interface and the solid surface. > **Young's Equation (1 mark)**: $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ > **Physical Significance (1 mark)**: > - $\gamma_{SV}$ — Solid–vapour interfacial tension; represents the surface energy of the bare substrate. > - $\gamma_{SL}$ — Solid–liquid interfacial tension; represents the energy at the adhesive–substrate contact. > - $\gamma_{LV}$ — Liquid–vapour surface tension of the adhesive; represents the cohesive pull of the adhesive molecules. > - The equation balances horizontal forces at the triple line; $\theta$ quantifies wettability. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Distinguish between advancing and receding contact angles. What is contact angle hysteresis and what does it indicate?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Advancing contact angle $\theta_A$ (1 mark)**: The contact angle measured when the liquid front is actively advancing over a fresh solid surface. > **Receding contact angle $\theta_R$ (1 mark)**: The contact angle measured when the liquid front is retreating, exposing previously wetted solid surface. > **Hysteresis (1 mark)**: $\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$. It arises from **surface roughness** and **chemical heterogeneity** of the substrate. A large hysteresis indicates a rough, chemically non-uniform surface — often correlated with enhanced *practical* adhesion due to mechanical interlocking at microscopic asperities. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Derive Young's equation for the contact angle of a liquid drop on a smooth solid surface starting from the principle of minimization of total surface free energy. **Model Solution (7 Marks):** 1. **(2 marks)** Define the three interfacial tensions $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$ at the triple line. State the assumption of an ideal smooth, rigid, homogeneous solid. 2. **(2 marks)** Write the total surface free energy expression for a sessile drop of base radius $r$ on a solid of total area $A_{solid}$: $$ G \;=\; \gamma_{SV}(A_{solid} - \pi r^2) \;+\; \gamma_{SL}(\pi r^2) \;+\; \gamma_{LV}(A_{LV}) $$ 3. **(2 marks)** Apply virtual displacement $dr$ along the contact line, use the geometric relation $dA_{LV} = -2\pi r \cos\theta \, dr$, and set $dG = 0$ for equilibrium. 4. **(1 mark)** Rearrange to obtain the final form: $$ \gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta $$ --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]** — A structural epoxy adhesive at 60°C has a surface tension of $\gamma_{LV} = 42.5$ mN/m. On an aluminium substrate, the contact angle is measured as 38°. Calculate: (i) the work of adhesion $W_a$, (ii) the work of cohesion $W_c$, and (iii) the spreading coefficient $S$. Comment on whether this adhesive will wet the aluminium surface effectively. **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Work of Adhesion (2 marks)**: $$ W_a \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) \;=\; 42.5 \times (1 + \cos 38°) $$ $$ \cos 38° \;\approx\; 0.7880 $$ $$ W_a \;=\; 42.5 \times 1.7880 \;\approx\; 75.99 \;\text{mJ/m}^2 $$ **(ii) Work of Cohesion (1 mark)**: $$ W_c \;=\; 2\gamma_{LV} \;=\; 2 \times 42.5 \;=\; 85.0 \;\text{mJ/m}^2 $$ **(iii) Spreading Coefficient (3 marks)**: First, find $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ from Young's equation: $$ \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;=\; 42.5 \times 0.7880 \;\approx\; 33.49 \;\text{mN/m} $$ Then: $$ S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV} \;=\; 33.49 - 42.5 \;\approx\; -9.01 \;\text{mN/m} $$ **Comment (1 mark)**: Since $S < 0$, the adhesive will **not** spread completely, but since $\theta = 38° < 90°$, **partial wetting** occurs which is sufficient for effective adhesion. The $W_a \approx 76$ mJ/m² indicates **good thermodynamic adhesion** on aluminium — making this a usable structural joint. --- ### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice] **Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the Young–Dupré equation. A liquid adhesive has a surface tension of 30 mN/m and forms a contact angle of 45° on a polymer substrate. Calculate: (i) the work of adhesion, and (ii) the work of spreading. **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **(i) Young–Dupré Equation (3 marks)**: The thermodynamic work of adhesion is the reversible energy required to separate a unit area of solid–liquid interface into separate solid–vapour and liquid–vapour interfaces: $$ W_a \;=\; \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) $$ The **work of spreading** $W_s$ (also called the spreading coefficient with sign convention reversed in some texts): $$ W_s \;=\; W_a - W_c \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) - 2\gamma_{LV} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) $$ **(ii) Numerical Calculation (4 marks)**: Given: $\gamma_{LV} = 30$ mN/m, $\theta = 45°$, so $\cos 45° \approx 0.7071$. Work of adhesion: $$ W_a \;=\; 30 \times (1 + 0.7071) \;=\; 30 \times 1.7071 \;\approx\; 51.21 \;\text{mJ/m}^2 $$ Work of cohesion: $$ W_c \;=\; 2 \times 30 \;=\; 60 \;\text{mJ/m}^2 $$ Work of spreading: $$ W_s \;=\; 51.21 - 60 \;=\; -8.79 \;\text{mJ/m}^2 $$ **Interpretation**: $W_s < 0$ confirms **partial wetting**, consistent with $\theta = 45°$. The adhesive wets the substrate but does not spontaneously spread as a film. --- **Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Discuss the effects of surface roughness and surface heterogeneity on contact angle measurement. How do these factors influence the practical adhesive bond strength? **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **1. Surface Roughness (3 marks)**: According to the **Wenzel equation**, on a rough surface with roughness factor $r$ (ratio of actual to projected area): $$ \cos\theta_{Wenzel} \;=\; r \cos\theta_{smooth} $$ Since $r \geq 1$ for a rough surface: - If $\theta_{smooth} < 90°$: roughening **decreases** the apparent angle (enhances wetting). - If $\theta_{smooth} > 90°$: roughening **increases** the apparent angle (enhances non-wetting). In adhesion: microscale roughness can **enhance mechanical interlocking** and improve practical bond strength, *provided* the adhesive can penetrate into the asperities (good initial wetting). **2. Surface Heterogeneity (Cassie–Baxter Model) (2 marks)**: For a composite surface of fraction $f_1$ of chemistry-A and $(1-f_1)$ of chemistry-B: $$ \cos\theta_{CB} \;=\; f_1 \cos\theta_1 + (1 - f_1)\cos\theta_2 $$ Heterogeneity creates **contact angle hysteresis** ($\theta_A \neq \theta_R$), which is often exploited in industrial adhesives to provide energy dissipation during crack propagation. **3. Practical Influence on Bond Strength (2 marks)**: - **Enhances mechanical interlocking** of the adhesive into the substrate micro-topography. - **Increases effective contact area** beyond the geometric projection. - However, *excessive* roughness traps air, prevents complete wetting, and creates **stress concentrators** that initiate premature failure. - Optimal surface preparation (e.g., grit blasting, plasma treatment) balances these effects. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks)**: Confusing **mN/m** (force per length, interfacial tension) with **mJ/m²** (energy per area, surface free energy). Although dimensionally equal, context matters. Always write the unit explicitly. > > 2. **Angle Measurement Direction (–1 mark)**: Forgetting to measure $\theta$ **through the liquid** (not the vapour). If drawn wrong, full marks may be denied in descriptive answers. > > 3. **Sign Error in Young's Equation (–2 marks)**: Writing $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — this is **wrong**. The correct form is $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. > > 4. **Forgetting Equilibrium Condition (–2 marks)**: In derivations, students often skip the explicit statement "$dG = 0$ at equilibrium" — this is mandatory for full marks in Part B derivations. > > 5. **Confusing Work of Adhesion with Work of Spreading (–1 mark)**: $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ is **always positive**. $W_s = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1)$ is **always negative or zero** for any real liquid. > > 6. **Skipping the Geometric Relation $dA_{LV}$ (–2 marks)**: The step where $dA_{LV} = -2\pi r \cos\theta \, dr$ is derived must be shown — simply writing "by geometry" earns only partial credit. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Contact angle $\theta$** is measured through the liquid at the three-phase line. - [x] **Three interfacial tensions** govern wetting: $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$. - [x] **Young's Equation**: $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — the foundational equation of wetting. - [x] **Young–Dupré**: $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — the thermodynamic work of adhesion. - [x] **Work of Cohesion**: $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$. - [x] **Spreading Coefficient**: $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$. If $S \geq 0$, complete spreading; if $S < 0$, partial wetting with finite $\theta$. - [x] **Wetting regimes**: $\theta < 90°$ (good wetting, adhesion); $\theta > 90°$ (poor wetting, non-adhesive behaviour). - [x] **Hysteresis** $\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$ indicates surface roughness and chemical heterogeneity. - [x] **Wenzel and Cassie–Baxter** models modify Young's equation for rough and heterogeneous surfaces. - [x] **Engineering rule**: For effective adhesive bonding, always aim for $\theta < 90°$ and high $W_a$. - [x] **Units**: $\gamma$ values use **mN/m** or **mJ/m²**; angles use **degrees** or **radians**; $W$ values use **mJ/m²**. - [x] **Always** state equilibrium condition ($dG = 0$) explicitly in derivations for full credit.

topics.title: Work of Adhesion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Work of Adhesion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Work of Adhesion ($W_a$ or $W_{ad}$)** is defined as the **reversible thermodynamic work** (per unit area, expressed in $\text{J/m}^2$ or equivalently $\text{N/m}$) required to **separate two dissimilar phases** (or two condensed phases) at their common interface, thereby creating two new interfaces, under conditions of constant temperature ($T$), pressure ($P$), and chemical potential ($\mu$).

In the specific context of adhesion science, when a liquid ($L$) is in contact with a solid ($S$), the work of adhesion is the energy required to pull the liquid away from the solid, creating a solid–vapor interface and a liquid–vapor interface from what was originally a solid–liquid interface.

The general Dupré form is expressed as:

$$W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$$

where $\gamma_1$, $\gamma_2$ are the surface free energies of the two phases, and $\gamma_{12}$ is the interfacial free energy between them.

For the **solid–liquid system** (the most important case in adhesive technology):

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

Here:
- $\gamma_{sv}$ = Solid–Vapor interfacial free energy
- $\gamma_{lv}$ = Liquid–Vapor interfacial free energy (surface tension of liquid)
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = Solid–Liquid interfacial free energy

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Think of it like peeling two perfectly stuck-together adhesive tapes apart.** The energy your hands must supply to slowly pull them apart (without tearing the backing) — measured as force times distance per unit area — is exactly the **Work of Adhesion**. It is a *thermodynamic* number, meaning it represents the **minimum energy required** to break the interface if done infinitely slowly and reversibly (i.e., without dissipating energy as heat or sound).

A simpler analogy: imagine two wet glass plates stuck together by a thin film of water. To pull them apart, you must overcome the surface tension of the water at the air–water interface and the energy at the water–glass interface. The total energy required per unit area of contact equals the work of adhesion.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Adhesive Bonding

> [!TIP]  
> A **high** Work of Adhesion ($W_a$) implies:
> - Strong thermodynamic "wetting" tendency of the adhesive on the substrate.
> - Greater energy dissipation needed to separate the joint.
> - Better intrinsic adhesion strength (before considering mechanical interlocking or chemical bonding).

Conversely, a **low** $W_a$ indicates poor interfacial contact and a tendency toward spontaneous dewetting — which is undesirable for adhesives.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Unit (SI):** $\text{J/m}^2$ or $\text{N/m}$ (since $1\,\text{J} = 1\,\text{N}\cdot\text{m}$, dividing by $\text{m}^2$ yields $\text{N/m}$)
- **Typical magnitude (polymer–polymer):** $30 - 70\,\text{mJ/m}^2$
- **Typical magnitude (metal–polymer):** $100 - 500\,\text{mJ/m}^2$
- **Surface tension of water (reference liquid):** $\gamma_{lv} \approx 72.8\,\text{mN/m}$ at $20^\circ\text{C}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The Work of Adhesion is rooted in the **thermodynamics of interfaces**. Every interface stores excess free energy (surface free energy), and creating or destroying interfaces always costs or releases energy.

### 2.1.1 Free Energy of the System

Consider a solid–liquid system in equilibrium with its vapor. The total interfacial free energy of the system (per unit area of solid–liquid contact) is:

$$G_{total} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}$$

If the liquid is completely separated from the solid (no contact), the energy is:

$$G_{separated} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}$$

The difference (energy released upon contact) is the work of adhesion:

$$W_a = G_{separated} - G_{contact} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

Wait — this is the work of immersion. The full Dupré expression arises when we account for the **liquid–vapor interface disappearing** during wetting. The reversible work of adhesion is therefore:

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Each Term

- **$\gamma_{sv}$:** Energy needed to create a new solid surface (as the liquid detaches).
- **$\gamma_{lv}$:** Energy needed to create a new liquid surface (as the liquid detaches).
- **$\gamma_{sl}$:** Energy *gained back* (subtracted) because the solid–liquid interface is destroyed.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU Part B questions.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Description / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dupré's Equation (general) | $W_a = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - \gamma_{12}$ | Work to separate phases 1 and 2 |
| 2 | Dupré's Equation (solid–liquid) | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | Work to detach liquid from solid |
| 3 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | Mechanical equilibrium at contact line |
| 4 | Young–Dupré Equation | $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Combines Young + Dupré; **most used** |
| 5 | Work of Cohesion (liquid) | $W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}$ | Work to split a liquid into two parts |
| 6 | Spreading Coefficient ($S$) | $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}$ | Predicts spontaneous spreading if $S \geq 0$ |
| 7 | Adhesion Tension | $\tau_a = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | Vector form of adhesion driving force |
| 8 | Fowkes Geometric Mean | $\gamma_{12} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - 2\sqrt{\gamma_1^d \gamma_2^d}$ | Dispersive component only |
| 9 | Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble | $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_s + \gamma_l - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_s^d\gamma_l^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p\gamma_l^p}\right)$ | Dispersive + polar components |

> [!NOTE]  
> In KTU exams, you will most often use **Equation 4** (Young–Dupré) since contact angle $\theta$ is the most easily measured quantity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Work of Adhesion concept is **the foundational design parameter** for:

1. **Adhesive formulation** — choosing a resin whose surface energy is lower than the substrate's critical surface energy ensures $W_a$ is maximized.
2. **Surface treatment design** — corona, plasma, or chemical etching modifies $\gamma_{sv}$ to raise $W_a$.
3. **Coating and printing industries** — predicting whether an ink/coat will wet a substrate.
4. **Biomedical implants** — controlling protein/cell adhesion to titanium or polymer surfaces.
5. **Microfluidics and lab-on-chip** — designing channel surfaces with controlled $W_a$ for fluid transport.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Dupré's Equation for a Solid–Liquid Interface

### Setup

We consider a solid substrate $S$ in contact with a liquid drop $L$, both immersed in vapor $V$. We want to find the reversible work required to **separate** the liquid completely from the solid, exposing fresh solid–vapor and liquid–vapor interfaces.

### Step 1: Define Initial State Energy

The initial system has one interface: the solid–liquid interface ($S$–$L$).

$$E_{initial} = \gamma_{sl} \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the area of contact. We set $A = 1\,\text{m}^2$ for unit-area work, giving:

$$E_{initial} = \gamma_{sl}$$

### Step 2: Define Final State Energy

After separation, we have two interfaces: solid–vapor ($S$–$V$) and liquid–vapor ($L$–$V$).

$$E_{final} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}$$

### Step 3: Apply the First Law (Reversible Process)

For a reversible process at constant $T$ and $P$, the work done *on the system* equals the change in Gibbs free energy of the surfaces:

$$W_{rev} = E_{final} - E_{initial}$$

Substituting the energies:

$$W_{rev} = (\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}) - \gamma_{sl}$$

### Step 4: Identify the Result as Work of Adhesion

By definition, this reversible work is the **Work of Adhesion**:

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}}$$

This is **Dupré's Equation** (1869). It holds for any pair of condensed phases (liquid–liquid, solid–liquid, solid–solid).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation

### Step 1: Start with Young's Equation

At the three-phase contact line, horizontal force balance gives:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

Rearranging:

$$\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Dupré's Equation

Starting from:

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

We rearrange:

$$W_a = (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}) + \gamma_{lv}$$

### Step 3: Replace the Bracket Using Young's Equation

Substituting $\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$:

$$W_a = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta + \gamma_{lv}$$

Factor out $\gamma_{lv}$:

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré Equation** — extremely useful because $\gamma_{lv}$ and $\theta$ are directly measurable.

## 3.3 Derivation of Work of Cohesion

### Step 1: Consider a Liquid Column Split into Two Halves

A liquid of surface tension $\gamma_{lv}$ has molecules held together by cohesive forces. To split the liquid, we must create **two new liquid–vapor interfaces** (each with energy $\gamma_{lv}$).

### Step 2: Apply Energy Balance

For unit area:

$$E_{initial} = 0 \quad \text{(no new interface)}$$

$$E_{final} = 2\gamma_{lv}$$

### Step 3: Identify as Work of Cohesion

$$\boxed{W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Spreading Coefficient

The spreading coefficient compares adhesion to cohesion:

$$S = W_a - W_c$$

Substituting:

$$S = (\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}) - 2\gamma_{lv}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}}$$

### Physical Interpretation

- If $S > 0$: liquid **spreads completely** on the solid (complete wetting, $\theta = 0$).
- If $S < 0$: liquid forms a **finite contact angle** (partial wetting).
- If $S = 0$: critical condition (wetting just begins).

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Problem:** A liquid with surface tension $\gamma_{lv} = 45\,\text{mN/m}$ forms a contact angle of $30^\circ$ on a polymer substrate. Calculate (a) the work of adhesion, (b) the work of cohesion, and (c) the spreading coefficient.

**Given:**
- $\gamma_{lv} = 45\,\text{mN/m} = 0.045\,\text{N/m}$
- $\theta = 30^\circ \Rightarrow \cos 30^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.8660$

### Part (a): Work of Adhesion

Using the Young–Dupré equation:

$$W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

Substituting values:

$$W_a = 45\,(1 + 0.8660)\,\text{mN/m}$$

$$W_a = 45 \times 1.8660\,\text{mN/m}$$

$$\boxed{W_a \approx 83.97\,\text{mJ/m}^2}$$

### Part (b): Work of Cohesion

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{lv} = 2 \times 45 = 90\,\text{mN/m}$$

$$\boxed{W_c = 90\,\text{mJ/m}^2}$$

### Part (c): Spreading Coefficient

$$S = W_a - W_c = 83.97 - 90 = -6.03\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{S \approx -6.03\,\text{mJ/m}^2}$$

**Interpretation:** Since $S < 0$, the liquid does **not** spread completely; it forms a finite contact angle (consistent with $\theta = 30^\circ > 0$). Adhesion is weaker than cohesion, indicating the polymer surface has relatively low surface energy compared to the liquid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Flow During Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INITIAL STATE: Solid-Liquid Interface in Equilibrium"] --> B["Apply Reversible Separation Force"]
    B --> C["Energy Required to Create Solid-Vapor Interface"]
    B --> D["Energy Required to Create Liquid-Vapor Interface"]
    B --> E["Energy Released by Destroying Solid-Liquid Interface"]
    C --> F["Net Reversible Work = Work of Adhesion Wa"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{"Comparison with Work of Cohesion Wc"}
    G -->|Wa > Wc| H["Adhesive Failure Dominates: Liquid wets substrate strongly"]
    G -->|Wa < Wc| I["Cohesive Failure Dominates: Liquid beads up, partial wetting"]
    G -->|Wa = Wc| J["Critical Wetting Condition: Contact angle theta = 0"]
```

## 4.2 Contact Angle Geometry Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SY["SUBSTRATE PHASE (Solid)"]
        SD["Solid surface with energy gamma_sv"]
    end
    subgraph IY["INTERFACIAL REGION"]
        SL["Solid-Liquid interface with energy gamma_sl"]
        CL["Three-phase contact line"]
    end
    subgraph LY["LIQUID DROP"]
        LD["Liquid with surface tension gamma_lv"]
        CA["Contact angle theta measured through the liquid"]
    end
    subgraph VY["VAPOR PHASE (Air)"]
        VP["Surrounding vapor medium"]
    end
    SD --- SL
    SL --- LD
    LD --- CA
    CA --- CL
    CL --- SD
    VY -. surrounds .-> LD
    VY -. surrounds .-> CA
```

## 4.3 Vector Force Balance at the Contact Line

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Three-Phase Contact Line Equilibrium"] --> B["Vector gamma_sv: pulls along solid surface toward vapor"]
    A --> C["Vector gamma_sl: pulls along solid surface toward liquid"]
    A --> D["Vector gamma_lv: pulls along liquid surface at angle theta from solid"]
    B --> E["Horizontal Component Balance: gamma_sv = gamma_sl + gamma_lv times cos theta"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["Young Equation Solved for Adhesion Tension: gamma_lv cos theta = gamma_sv minus gamma_sl"]
    F --> G["Substituted into Duple Equation to yield Young-Duple: Wa = gamma_lv times 1 plus cos theta"]
```

## 4.4 Decision Logic: Predicting Wetting Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST["Start: Measure contact angle theta and liquid surface tension gamma_lv"] --> CL1{"Compute Wa = gamma_lv times 1 plus cos theta"}
    CL1 --> CL2{"Compare Wa with Wc = 2 times gamma_lv"}
    CL2 -->|Wa greater than Wc| R1["Spreading coefficient S greater than 0: COMPLETE WETTING theta equals 0"]
    CL2 -->|Wa equal to Wc| R2["Critical condition S equals 0: theta equals 0 borderline"]
    CL2 -->|Wa less than Wc| R3["Partial wetting 0 less than theta less than 180 degrees"]
    CL3["Compute spreading coefficient S = Wa minus Wc"] --> CL1
```
