# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Q1.** Define the term **Work of Adhesion**. Mention its SI unit.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Work of Adhesion** is defined as the reversible work required per unit area to separate two condensed phases (such as a liquid and a solid) at their interface, producing two new surfaces under constant temperature, pressure, and chemical potential.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ (Dupré's equation)
>
> **SI unit:** $\text{J/m}^2$ (equivalent to $\text{N/m}$ or $\text{mJ/m}^2$ in cgs scale)

**(Mark split:** Definition — 1, Equation — 1, Unit — 1)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]`

**Q2.** Distinguish between **Work of Adhesion** and **Work of Cohesion**.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Work of Adhesion ($W_a$) | Work of Cohesion ($W_c$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Work to separate two *dissimilar* phases | Work to separate two *similar* phases |
| Equation | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | $W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}$ |
| Nature | Interfacial property | Bulk property of liquid |
| Wetting criterion | If $W_a > W_c$, spreading occurs | Always defined for a single liquid |

**(Mark split:** Definition difference — 1, Equations — 1, Comparison — 1)

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.A.** **(a)** Derive **Dupré's Equation** for the work of adhesion between a liquid and a solid. Discuss the physical significance of each term in the equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Young–Dupré Equation** and explain how it is used to experimentally determine the work of adhesion. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Statement of the problem (1 mark)**
Consider a solid–liquid interface of unit area in equilibrium with vapor. We want to find the reversible work to completely separate them.

**Step 2: Initial state energy (1 mark)**
The system initially has only the solid–liquid interface:
$$G_i = \gamma_{sl}$$

**Step 3: Final state energy (2 marks)**
After complete separation, the system has a solid–vapor interface and a liquid–vapor interface:
$$G_f = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}$$

**Step 4: Energy balance and identification (2 marks)**
$$W_a = G_f - G_i = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

**Step 5: Physical significance (1 mark)**

- $\gamma_{sv}$ — energy needed to create new solid surface.
- $\gamma_{lv}$ — energy needed to create new liquid surface.
- $\gamma_{sl}$ — energy recovered by destroying the original interface.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Write Young's Equation (1 mark)**
Mechanical equilibrium of horizontal forces at the contact line:
$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$
$$\Rightarrow \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 2: Rewrite Dupré's Equation (1 mark)**
$$W_a = (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}) + \gamma_{lv}$$

**Step 3: Substitute (2 marks)**
$$W_a = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta + \gamma_{lv}$$

**Step 4: Final form (1 mark)**
$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

**Step 5: Experimental application (2 marks)**
- The surface tension $\gamma_{lv}$ of the liquid is measured using a Wilhelmy plate or du Noüy ring.
- The contact angle $\theta$ is measured using a goniometer (sessile drop method).
- $W_a$ is then calculated from the above equation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B.** **(a)** Define **Spreading Coefficient** and derive its mathematical expression. What condition must be satisfied for complete wetting of a liquid on a solid surface? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water at $20^\circ\text{C}$ ($\gamma_{lv} = 72.8\,\text{mN/m}$) has a contact angle of $50^\circ$ on a cleaned polyethylene surface. Calculate (i) the work of adhesion, (ii) the work of cohesion, and (iii) the spreading coefficient. Comment on the wetting behavior. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition (2 marks)**
The **Spreading Coefficient** ($S$) is defined as the difference between the work of adhesion and the work of cohesion:
$$S = W_a - W_c$$

It predicts whether a liquid will spontaneously spread on a solid surface.

**Step 2: Substitute Dupré and Cohesion equations (2 marks)**
$$S = (\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}) - 2\gamma_{lv}$$
$$S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}$$

**Step 3: Derive complete wetting condition (2 marks)**

Using Young's equation $\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$:

$$S = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta - \gamma_{lv} = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)$$

For complete wetting, $S \geq 0$:
$$\gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta \geq 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 0^\circ$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**
Complete wetting occurs only when the contact angle $\theta = 0^\circ$ (Young's condition).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $\gamma_{lv} = 72.8\,\text{mN/m}$, $\theta = 50^\circ$, $\cos 50^\circ \approx 0.6428$

**(i) Work of Adhesion (3 marks):**
$$W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 72.8 \times (1 + 0.6428)$$
$$W_a = 72.8 \times 1.6428 \approx 119.6\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**(ii) Work of Cohesion (2 marks):**
$$W_c = 2\gamma_{lv} = 2 \times 72.8 = 145.6\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**(iii) Spreading Coefficient (2 marks):**
$$S = W_a - W_c = 119.6 - 145.6 = -26.0\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Comment (included in part iii marks):**
Since $S < 0$, water does **not** spread completely on polyethylene. The contact angle is finite ($50^\circ$), confirming partial wetting. This is why polyethylene is considered hydrophobic — its low surface energy makes $W_a < W_c$. Surface treatment (e.g., corona discharge) is needed to improve wetting.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Many students write $\text{N/m}$ when the energy form is $\text{J/m}^2$ — these are dimensionally equal but KTU evaluators expect consistency. **Always state both** with a clarifying note.
>
> 2. **Sign error in spreading coefficient:** Forgetting that $S = W_a - W_c$ (not the reverse). Reversing gives wrong physical conclusions.
>
> 3. **Missing "reversible" qualifier:** Work of adhesion is defined only for *reversible* processes. Real adhesive bonds always require **more** energy due to viscoelastic/plastic dissipation. Mention this if asked "compare $W_a$ with practical peel strength."
>
> 4. **Forgetting the vapor-phase assumption:** Dupré's equation assumes equilibrium with vapor. For solid–solid adhesion (no vapor), the formulation differs.
>
> 5. **Numerical errors in cosine values:** Especially $\cos 30^\circ = 0.866$, $\cos 45^\circ = 0.707$, $\cos 60^\circ = 0.500$ — these are repeatedly tested.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Work of Adhesion**

- ✅ **Definition:** Reversible work per unit area to separate two phases at their interface.
- ✅ **Dupré's Equation:** $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ — the master equation.
- ✅ **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ — horizontal force balance.
- ✅ **Young–Dupré Equation:** $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — most useful experimentally.
- ✅ **Work of Cohesion:** $W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}$ — energy to split a homogeneous liquid.
- ✅ **Spreading Coefficient:** $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}$.
- ✅ **Complete wetting condition:** $S \geq 0$ $\Longleftrightarrow$ $\theta = 0^\circ$.
- ✅ **Higher $W_a$** $\Rightarrow$ stronger adhesion, better wetting.
- ✅ **SI Unit:** $\text{J/m}^2$ (numerically equal to $\text{N/m}$).
- ✅ **Typical values:** $W_a$ for polymer–polymer $\approx 30$–$70\,\text{mJ/m}^2$; metal–polymer $\approx 100$–$500\,\text{mJ/m}^2$.
- ✅ **Always mention "reversible"** when defining $W_a$ — it sets the theoretical lower bound.
- ✅ **For KTU numericals:** always show full substitution with units to avoid partial-mark deductions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wetting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wetting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Wetting

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Adhesive Science & Technology (PEPOT861, KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **Wetting** is defined as the ability of a liquid adhesive to make intimate molecular contact with a solid substrate (adherend) by displacing air or other contaminants at the interface. From a thermodynamic standpoint, wetting is the process by which the solid–vapor interface is replaced by a solid–liquid interface, governed by interfacial free energies.

The quantitative measure of wetting is the **Contact Angle (θ)**, which is the angle measured through the liquid phase between the tangent to the liquid–vapor interface at the three-phase contact line and the solid surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Module 1):**
> Wetting is the spontaneous spreading of a liquid over a solid surface, determined by the balance of three interfacial tensions acting at the three-phase contact line: solid–vapor (γsv), solid–liquid (γsl), and liquid–vapor (γlv).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you place a single drop of water on two different surfaces:

- **On a clean glass slide:** the drop flattens and spreads out — the water "wets" the glass. The contact angle is small (less than 90°).
- **On a freshly waxed car body (or a lotus leaf):** the drop beads up into a near-perfect sphere and rolls off easily. The contact angle is large (greater than 90°).

In both cases, **the same water** is interacting with **two different solids**. The difference lies entirely in the balance of intermolecular forces (cohesive forces within the water vs. adhesive forces between the water and the solid). When adhesive forces dominate, the drop spreads (good wetting). When cohesive forces dominate, the drop beads up (poor wetting).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Wetting Matters in Adhesive Bonding:**
> An adhesive cannot form a strong bond unless it **first wets** the substrate. A non-wetting adhesive will leave voids, micro-bubbles, and weak interfacial boundaries — leading to premature joint failure. This is why surface preparation (cleaning, plasma treatment, primer application) is the **single most important step** in any bonding operation.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Standard unit of interfacial / surface tension:** **mN/m** (millinewtons per meter) — equivalent to **dyne/cm** in CGS, and numerically equal to **mJ/m²** (millijoules per square meter) when expressed as surface free energy.
- **Young's equation** is the foundational mathematical statement of wetting equilibrium.
- The condition **θ = 0°** corresponds to **complete wetting** (perfect spreading).
- The condition **θ > 0°** corresponds to **partial wetting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Syllabus Outcome CO1, Module 1):**
> The student must be able to (i) define contact angle, (ii) state Young's equation, (iii) classify wetting regimes, and (iv) relate wetting to work of adhesion, work of cohesion, and the spreading coefficient.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Interfacial Tensions Acting at the Contact Line

At the **three-phase contact line** (where solid, liquid, and vapor meet), three interfacial tensions act simultaneously:

| Symbol | Interfacial Tension | Acts On | Tends To |
|---|---|---|---|
| γsv | Solid–vapor | Solid surface | Pulls the drop to spread |
| γsl | Solid–liquid | New solid/liquid boundary | Pulls the drop to contract |
| γlv | Liquid–vapor | Liquid surface | Pulls the drop to contract |

These three vector forces are in mechanical equilibrium at the contact line, leading to the famous **Young's Equation (1805)**.

## 2.2 Young's Equation — The Cornerstone of Wetting Theory

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

**Why** this equation is so powerful: it relates a measurable geometric quantity (the contact angle θ) to three thermodynamic surface properties. By measuring θ alone, one can compute γsl if γsv and γlv are known.

## 2.3 Three Wetting Regimes Based on Contact Angle

- **Complete Wetting (θ = 0°):** cos θ = 1, liquid spreads spontaneously to form a thin film. Condition: γsv ≥ γsl + γlv.
- **Partial Wetting — Wetting Regime (0° < θ < 90°):** cos θ > 0. The liquid forms a flattened droplet; the substrate is "wettable" by the liquid. Good for adhesion.
- **Partial Wetting — Non-Wetting Regime (90° < θ < 180°):** cos θ < 0. The liquid beads up; the substrate is "non-wettable". Poor adhesion without surface treatment.
- **Perfect Non-Wetting (θ = 180°):** Theoretical limit, never achieved in practice.

## 2.4 Work of Adhesion (Wa) — Dupré's Equation

The **Work of Adhesion** is the reversible thermodynamic work required to separate a unit area of liquid from a solid at the interface, creating two new surfaces (solid–vapor and liquid–vapor).

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

Combining with Young's equation gives the **Young–Dupré Equation**:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

This is the single most important equation in adhesion science because it links **macroscopic wetting** to **thermodynamic adhesion strength**.

## 2.5 Work of Cohesion (Wc)

The **Work of Cohesion** is the reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid into two fresh liquid–vapor surfaces.

$$
W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}
$$

## 2.6 Spreading Coefficient (S) — Harkins' Criterion

The **Spreading Coefficient** determines whether a liquid will spontaneously spread on a solid:

$$
S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}
$$

- **S > 0 (positive):** Complete spreading; θ → 0°.
- **S < 0 (negative):** Partial wetting; finite contact angle.
- **S = 0:** Critical condition; θ is at its equilibrium value.

## 2.7 Three Thermodynamic Modes of Wetting (Van Oss Classification)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Mandate:** Students must be able to identify the three modes of wetting and write the corresponding work expressions.

1. **Adhesional Wetting:** A solid is brought into contact with a liquid from outside (no change in liquid area). Work = W_a = γlv(1 + cos θ).
2. **Immersional Wetting:** A solid is completely immersed in the liquid (solid–vapor interface replaced by solid–liquid). Work = γsv − γsl = γlv cos θ.
3. **Spreading Wetting (Coating):** A liquid spreads over the solid to form a continuous film (solid–vapor replaced AND new liquid–vapor created). Work = γsv − γsl − γlv = S (spreading coefficient).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Young's Equation | γsv = γsl + γlv cos θ | γ in mN/m; θ in degrees/radians |
| 2 | Young–Dupré Equation | Wa = γlv (1 + cos θ) | Wa in mJ/m² |
| 3 | Work of Adhesion | Wa = γsv + γlv − γsl | Wa in mJ/m² |
| 4 | Work of Cohesion | Wc = 2 γlv | Wc in mJ/m² |
| 5 | Spreading Coefficient | S = γsv − γsl − γlv | S in mN/m |
| 6 | Adhesional Wetting Work | Wa = γlv (1 + cos θ) | mJ/m² |
| 7 | Immersional Wetting Work | Wim = γlv cos θ | mJ/m² |
| 8 | Spreading Wetting Work | Ws = γlv (cos θ − 1) = S | mJ/m² |
| 9 | Critical Surface Tension | γc = limit of γlv as θ → 0 | mN/m |
| 10 | Fowkes Equation | γ12 = γ1 + γ2 − 2 (γ1^d · γ2^d)^½ | Superscript d = dispersive |

## 2.9 Engineering Utility of Wetting Theory

- **Adhesive Formulation:** Designing polymers and resins with optimal surface energy (typically 38–44 mN/m for structural adhesives).
- **Surface Treatment Verification:** Quantifying the effectiveness of plasma, corona, or chemical etching by measuring water contact angle reduction.
- **Quality Control:** Contact angle goniometry is used in PCB manufacturing, aerospace composite layup, and medical device bonding.
- **Predicting Bond Strength:** Higher Wa directly correlates with higher thermodynamic work of adhesion and (generally) improved practical bond strength.
- **Selecting Primers and Coupling Agents:** A primer must wet the substrate; otherwise it acts as a release layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Rule of Thumb:**
> For an adhesive to wet a substrate, the adhesive's surface tension must be **less than or equal to** the substrate's critical surface tension (γlv ≤ γc of the solid). This is the basis for Zisman's empirical plots used industrially.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation from Force Balance at the Three-Phase Contact Line

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
At the three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act as horizontal force vectors pulling along the respective interfaces:
- γsv pulls along the solid surface outward (away from the liquid drop).
- γsl pulls along the solid surface inward (under the drop).
- γlv pulls along the liquid–vapor interface, making angle θ with the solid surface (in the horizontal plane, its horizontal component is γlv cos θ).

**Step 2 — Apply horizontal force equilibrium.**
For the contact line to be stationary, the sum of horizontal forces must equal zero:

$$
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain Young's Equation.**
Moving the γsl and γlv cos θ terms to the right-hand side:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

This is the **Young Equation (1805)**, the foundation of all wetting thermodynamics.

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
- If γsv is large (high-energy solid), cos θ is large → θ is small → good wetting.
- If γsl is large (poor interaction between solid and liquid), cos θ decreases → θ increases → poor wetting.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation (Work of Adhesion in Terms of Contact Angle)

**Step 1 — Start from Dupré's general work of adhesion.**
The reversible work per unit area required to separate a solid–liquid interface is:

$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate γsl.**

$$
\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - W_a
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute this expression for γsl into Young's Equation.**

$$
\gamma_{sv} = (\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - W_a) + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify (γsv cancels from both sides).**

$$
0 = \gamma_{lv} - W_a + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Wa.**

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

This is the **Young–Dupré Equation**. It is enormously useful because contact angle θ and surface tension γlv are easily measured, allowing Wa to be calculated directly.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Spreading Coefficient

**Step 1 — Start with the definitions.**

$$
S = W_a - W_c
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Dupré's expression for Wa and Wc = 2γlv.**

$$
S = (\gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}) - 2\gamma_{lv}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}
$$

**Step 4 — Use Young's equation to eliminate γsl.**

Since γsv = γsl + γlv cos θ, we get γsl = γsv − γlv cos θ. Substituting:

$$
S = \gamma_{sv} - (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta) - \gamma_{lv}
$$

**Step 5 — Final simplified form.**

$$
S = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1)
$$

This beautiful result shows that S is **negative for all partial wetting** (since cos θ ≤ 1, with equality only at θ = 0°). Complete spontaneous spreading requires S ≥ 0.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A structural epoxy adhesive has surface tension γlv = **42.5 mN/m**. When placed on a cleaned aluminium substrate, the measured contact angle is **28°**. Calculate:
(a) Work of adhesion (Wa)
(b) Work of cohesion (Wc)
(c) Spreading coefficient (S)
(d) Comment on whether the adhesive will wet the substrate effectively.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Work of Adhesion:**

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 42.5 \times (1 + \cos 28°)
$$

Compute cos 28° = **0.8829**.

$$
W_a = 42.5 \times (1 + 0.8829) = 42.5 \times 1.8829 = 80.02 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

**Part (b) — Work of Cohesion:**

$$
W_c = 2\gamma_{lv} = 2 \times 42.5 = 85.0 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

**Part (c) — Spreading Coefficient:**

$$
S = W_a - W_c = 80.02 - 85.0 = -4.98 \text{ mN/m}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
S = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1) = 42.5 \times (0.8829 - 1) = 42.5 \times (-0.1171) = -4.98 \text{ mN/m}
$$

**Part (d) — Interpretation:**
Since S is **negative** (and θ = 28° > 0°), the adhesive undergoes **partial wetting**. However, θ = 28° is well below 90°, indicating a wettable, high-energy surface — typical of properly prepared aluminium. The high Wa = 80.02 mJ/m² relative to Wc = 85.0 mJ/m² indicates good thermodynamic potential for adhesion, though **spontaneous spreading will not occur**. Application of pressure or a thinner bondline is recommended in practice.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Three Modes of Wetting

| Property | Adhesional Wetting | Immersional Wetting | Spreading Wetting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometric operation | Solid contacts liquid at surface | Solid fully submerged | Liquid spreads over solid |
| Solid–vapor interface created? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Solid–liquid interface destroyed? | Yes | No (already created) | Yes |
| Liquid–vapor interface created? | No | No | Yes |
| Work expression | γlv (1 + cos θ) | γlv cos θ | γlv (cos θ − 1) |
| Equals | Wa | γsv − γsl | S |
| Sign for partial wetting | Always positive | Positive if θ < 90° | Always negative |
| Practical relevance | Sessile drop, contact angle goniometry | Dip coating, immersion tests | Coating, painting, printing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Force Balance at the Three-Phase Contact Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["SOLID SUBSTRATE WITH HIGH SURFACE ENERGY"] --> nodeB["THREE PHASE CONTACT LINE FORMS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["THREE INTERFACIAL TENSIONS ACT"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["GAMMA SV PULLS SOLID SURFACE OUTWARD"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["GAMMA SL PULLS SOLID LIQUID BOUNDARY INWARD"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["GAMMA LV PULLS AT CONTACT ANGLE THETA"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["HORIZONTAL FORCE EQUILIBRIUM"]
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["YOUNG EQUATION GAMMA SV EQUALS GAMMA SL PLUS GAMMA LV COS THETA"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["SOLVE FOR THETA CONTACT ANGLE"]

    nodeI --> nodeK["THETA GREATER THAN 90 DEGREE NON WETTING REGIME"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["THETA EQUALS ZERO COMPLETE SPREADING"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["GOOD ADHESION STRONG BOND"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["POOR ADHESION SURFACE TREATMENT NEEDED"]

```

```mermaid
    nodeZ --> nodeAE["THETA EQUALS 90"] --> nodeAF["NEUTRAL WETTING BOUNDARY"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEPOT861]**

**Q.A1. Define contact angle and state Young's equation. How is contact angle used to classify wetting?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The contact angle (θ) is the angle measured through the liquid phase between the tangent to the liquid–vapor interface at the three-phase contact line and the solid substrate. Young's equation is γsv = γsl + γlv cos θ, which relates the contact angle to the three interfacial tensions (solid–vapor γsv, solid–liquid γsl, liquid–vapor γlv) acting at the contact line.

Classification of wetting by contact angle:
1. **θ = 0°:** Complete wetting; spontaneous film formation.
2. **0° < θ < 90°:** Partial wetting (wetting regime); good adhesion; drop spreads partially.
3. **θ > 90°:** Partial wetting (non-wetting regime); drop beads up; poor adhesion.
4. **θ → 180°:** Perfect non-wetting; theoretical limit.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEPOT861]**

**Q.A2. Distinguish between work of adhesion and work of cohesion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Work of Adhesion (Wa) | Work of Cohesion (Wc) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Energy needed to separate a unit area of solid–liquid interface into separate solid–vapor and liquid–vapor interfaces | Energy needed to separate a unit area of liquid into two fresh liquid–vapor interfaces |
| Equation | Wa = γsv + γlv − γsl = γlv (1 + cos θ) | Wc = 2 γlv |
| Significance | Quantifies adhesive bonding strength at the interface | Quantifies the internal cohesive strength of the bulk liquid adhesive |
| Comparison | If Wa > Wc, the liquid wets the solid (adhesion preferred) | If Wc > Wa, the liquid prefers to remain as a drop (cohesion preferred) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ INTERNAL CHOICE — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / PEPOT861, Module 1]**

**Q.A.(a) Derive Young's equation for contact angle. Discuss the three wetting regimes with the help of a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**
At the three-phase contact line where solid, liquid, and vapor meet, three interfacial tensions act horizontally: γsv along the solid surface outward, γsl along the solid–liquid interface inward, and γlv along the liquid–vapor interface at angle θ to the solid surface. For mechanical equilibrium of the contact line:

$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

This is **Young's Equation (1805)**.

**Three Wetting Regimes (3 marks):**

1. **Complete Wetting (θ = 0°):** The liquid spreads spontaneously to form a thin film. Condition: γsv ≥ γsl + γlv. Example: pure water on freshly cleaned glass.

2. **Partial Wetting — Wetting Regime (0° < θ < 90°):** cos θ > 0. The drop is flattened. Adhesive forces dominate partially. Example: epoxy on cleaned aluminium (θ ≈ 25°–35°).

3. **Partial Wetting — Non-Wetting Regime (90° < θ < 180°):** cos θ < 0. The drop beads up. Cohesive forces within the liquid dominate. Example: water on PTFE (θ ≈ 110°).

The diagram should show a sessile drop on a solid substrate with the contact angle marked at the three-phase contact line and the three interfacial tensions acting as tangent vectors.

---

**Q.A.(b) A polyurethane adhesive has surface tension γlv = 38.0 mN/m and forms a contact angle of 35° on a treated polyethylene substrate. Calculate: (i) Work of adhesion, (ii) Work of cohesion, (iii) Spreading coefficient. Comment on the wetting behaviour. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** γlv = 38.0 mN/m, θ = 35°.

**Step 1 — Compute cos 35°:**

$$
\cos 35° = 0.8192
$$

**(i) Work of Adhesion (2 marks):**

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 38.0 \times (1 + 0.8192) = 38.0 \times 1.8192 = 69.13 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

**(ii) Work of Cohesion (2 marks):**

$$
W_c = 2\gamma_{lv} = 2 \times 38.0 = 76.0 \text{ mJ/m}^2
$$

**(iii) Spreading Coefficient (2 marks):**

$$
S = W_a - W_c = 69.13 - 76.0 = -6.87 \text{ mN/m}
$$

Alternatively:

$$
S = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1) = 38.0 \times (0.8192 - 1) = -6.87 \text{ mN/m}
$$

**Comment on wetting behaviour (1 mark):**
Since S < 0 and θ = 35° (between 0° and 90°), the adhesive exhibits **partial wetting** in the **wetting regime**. The substrate has been effectively treated (likely by corona or plasma treatment, which raises the surface energy of polyethylene from ~31 mN/m to ~38–44 mN/m). Good practical adhesion is expected despite the negative spreading coefficient, because (a) Wa/Wc = 69.13/76.0 ≈ 0.91 is close to 1, and (b) applied pressure during cure will overcome the small negative spreading coefficient.

---

### ⭐ INTERNAL CHOICE — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEPOT861, Module 1]**

**Q.B.(a) Explain the three thermodynamic modes of wetting with suitable expressions for the work of wetting and one industrial example for each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The wetting of a solid by a liquid can occur in three geometrically distinct thermodynamic modes:

**1. Adhesional Wetting (2.5 marks):**
- **Process:** A solid (originally in vapor) is brought into contact with a liquid surface from outside, without immersion. New solid–liquid interface is created, replacing solid–vapor interface. Liquid–vapor interface is **not** changed.
- **Work of Adhesional Wetting:**

$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

- **Industrial example:** Sessile drop goniometry used in laboratories to measure contact angle on flat substrates; fundamental to adhesive drop tests.

**2. Immersional Wetting (2.5 marks):**
- **Process:** A solid is completely immersed into a liquid, completely replacing the solid–vapor interface with solid–liquid. The liquid–vapor interface area does **not** change.
- **Work of Immersional Wetting:**

$$
W_{im} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta
$$

- **Industrial example:** **Wilhelmy plate technique** for measuring contact angle; dip coating of metal parts; powder impregnation.

**3. Spreading Wetting (Coating) (2 marks):**
- **Process:** A liquid drop spreads over a solid to form a continuous film, replacing solid–vapor and creating new liquid–vapor interface.
- **Work of Spreading Wetting:**

$$
W_s = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv} = \gamma_{lv}(\cos\theta - 1) = S
$$

- **Industrial example:** Paint application, printing ink spreading on paper, adhesive film formation, lubrication film spreading on bearings.

---

**Q.B.(b) Explain Fowkes' theory for surface free energy of solids and the Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble method for determining the dispersive and polar components of surface energy. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Fowkes' Theory (3.5 marks):**
Fowkes (1964) proposed that the total interfacial free energy γ of a phase is the sum of independent components arising from different types of intermolecular forces:

$$
\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p + \gamma^{ab} + \gamma^{i} + \ldots
$$

where the superscripts denote dispersive (London), polar (Keesom), acid–base (hydrogen bonding), and induced components. Fowkes argued that only components of **similar type** can interact across an interface. For two non-polar phases (1 and 2) interacting only through dispersive (London) forces, the interfacial energy is given by the **geometric mean** of the dispersive components:

$$
\gamma_{12} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - 2\sqrt{\gamma_1^d \cdot \gamma_2^d}
$$

This is the **Fowkes geometric mean equation** and is foundational for computing work of adhesion:

$$
W_a^{12} = 2\sqrt{\gamma_1^d \cdot \gamma_2^d}
$$

**Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble (OWRK) Method (3.5 marks):**
Owens and Wendt extended Fowkes by assuming surface energy has only two components: dispersive (γ^d) and polar (γ^p):

$$
\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p
$$

The interfacial energy between two phases 1 and 2 becomes:

$$
\gamma_{12} = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_1^d \cdot \gamma_2^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_1^p \cdot \gamma_2^p}\right)
$$

Combining with the Young–Dupré equation and rearranging yields the **OWRK working equation**:

$$
\frac{\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)}{2\sqrt{\gamma_{lv}^d}} = \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^p} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_{lv}^p}{\gamma_{lv}^d}}
$$

This is the equation of a straight line (y = mx + c). By plotting the LHS against the x-axis variable for several probe liquids of known γlv^d and γlv^p, the slope and intercept give √γsv^p and √γsv^d respectively, allowing both components of the solid's surface energy to be determined experimentally. This method is widely used industrially to characterize polymer, metal, and ceramic surfaces for adhesive bonding qualification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the contact angle definition:** Many students write "contact angle is the angle between solid and liquid" without specifying it is measured **through the liquid phase** at the **three-phase contact line**. (−1 mark)
> 2. **Wrong substitution in Young's equation:** Writing γlv = γsv − γsl − γsv cos θ instead of γsv = γsl + γlv cos θ. (−2 marks)
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mN/m and mJ/m² without noting they are dimensionally identical for surface/interfacial quantities. (−0.5 mark)
> 4. **Confusing Wa and Wc:** Saying "work of adhesion = 2γlv" (which is actually Wc). (−2 marks)
> 5. **Sign error in spreading coefficient:** Forgetting that S < 0 for partial wetting. (−1 mark)
> 6. **No diagram:** Part B questions on wetting regimes **must** include a labeled sessile drop diagram with contact angle and three interfacial tensions marked. A textual answer without a diagram loses at least 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Skipping Step 4 derivation steps:** Examiners allocate marks for intermediate rearrangement steps in Young–Dupré derivation. Skipping directly from Dupré to final Wa equation costs up to 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Wetting** = ability of liquid adhesive to make molecular contact with solid, displacing air/contaminants.
- [ ] **Contact angle θ** is measured **through the liquid** at the **three-phase contact line**.
- [ ] **Young's Equation:** γsv = γsl + γlv cos θ — the foundation equation.
- [ ] **Complete wetting:** θ = 0°, liquid spreads as a film.
- [ ] **Wetting regime:** 0° < θ < 90°, partial wetting, good adhesion.
- [ ] **Non-wetting regime:** 90° < θ < 180°, drop beads up, poor adhesion.
- [ ] **Work of Adhesion Wa = γlv (1 + cos θ)** — Young–Dupré equation; key link to adhesion.
- [ ] **Work of Cohesion Wc = 2γlv.**
- [ ] **If Wa > Wc** → liquid wets solid (good adhesion).
- [ ] **Spreading Coefficient S = γsv − γsl − γlv = γlv (cos θ − 1).**
- [ ] **S > 0** → complete spreading; **S < 0** → partial wetting.
- [ ] **Three modes of wetting:** Adhesional (Wa), Immersional (γlv cos θ), Spreading (S).
- [ ] **Units:** Surface tension in **mN/m** = surface energy in **mJ/m²** (numerically equivalent).
- [ ] **Adhesive rule:** γlv of adhesive must be ≤ γc (critical surface tension) of substrate.
- [ ] **Fowkes geometric mean equation:** γ12 = γ1 + γ2 − 2√(γ1^d · γ2^d).
- [ ] **OWRK method:** Two-component model γ = γ^d + γ^p; plot for experimental determination.
- [ ] **Higher Wa** correlates with stronger thermodynamic adhesion (subject to practical factors).
- [ ] **Surface preparation** is essential because contaminants dramatically increase θ.
- [ ] **Wilhelmy plate** = industrial technique for measuring contact angle via immersional wetting.
- [ ] Always state the **regime of wetting** when quoting a contact angle in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of Adhesion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strength of Adhesion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strength of Adhesion (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The *Strength of Adhesion* is defined as the **force per unit area (or per unit width)** required to rupture the bond formed between an adhesive and a substrate. It is a quantitative measure of the load-bearing capacity of an adhesively bonded joint at the **interface (adhesive failure)** or within the **adhesive bulk (cohesive failure)** under a defined loading mode.

Mathematically, it is the engineering stress at the moment of joint failure, expressed in units of **MPa (N/mm²)** for area-based loading or **N/mm (N/cm)** for linear (peel) loading.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tape on a Wall"

Imagine sticking a piece of cellophane tape on a freshly painted wall.

- If the paint peels *off the wall* with the tape, the tape is stronger than the paint's grip on the wall → **Adhesive (interfacial) failure**.
- If the tape itself splits and half remains stuck, half comes away → **Cohesive (bulk) failure** of the tape.
- If the wall plaster cracks → **Substrate failure**.

The "strength of adhesion" therefore is not a single material property but a **system property** — it depends on the adhesive, the substrate, the interface, the geometry, the loading mode, and the environment. A good engineer optimizes this system to ensure failure occurs in the *most predictable and energy-absorbing* location.

### 1.3 Two Fundamental Strengths

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction KTU Examiners Love to Test:**

| Term | Where Failure Occurs | What It Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Adhesive Strength** | At the adhesive–substrate interface | Quality of interfacial bonding |
| **Cohesive Strength** | Inside the adhesive (or inside the substrate) | Strength of bulk material itself |

A well-designed joint is one where the adhesive strength at the interface **exceeds** the cohesive strength of the adhesive — so the joint fails *cohesively*, indicating the interface is the strongest link.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

In aerospace (bonded aluminium skins), automotive (hem-flange replacement by structural bonding), and electronics (die-attach), the strength of adhesion dictates:
- **Load transfer efficiency** between adherends
- **Fatigue life** under cyclic loading
- **Safety factor** in crash/impact scenarios
- **Long-term durability** under humidity, heat, and UV

A typical structural adhesive bond in an aircraft achieves lap-shear strengths of **30–40 MPa**, while a high-performance aerospace epoxy can reach **>70 MPa**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Loading on Adhesive Joints

The measured strength depends *critically* on how the load is applied. KTU examiners routinely ask students to identify the loading mode from a diagram.

1. **Tensile (Pull-off) Loading** — Force perpendicular to the bond plane. Pure tension, minimal stress concentration.
2. **Shear Loading** — Force parallel to the bond plane. The most common industrial mode (lap shear).
3. **Peel Loading** — Force concentrated at a line, acting to progressively separate flexible adherends.
4. **Cleavage Loading** — Force at one end of a rigid joint, prying the adherends apart.
5. **Impact / Fatigue Loading** — Dynamic or cyclic loading.

### 2.2 The Mechanics of Joint Failure — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1: Stress Concentration.** Real joints (e.g., single-lap) have **non-uniform stress distributions** due to geometry. Peak stresses occur at the overlap ends (Volkersen's analysis).
- **Step 2: Crack Initiation.** Under sufficient load, micro-voids or interfacial flaws begin to grow at the high-stress regions.
- **Step 3: Crack Propagation.** The crack propagates either along the interface (adhesive failure) or kinks into the adhesive (cohesive failure).
- **Step 4: Final Fracture.** The joint separates. The locus of failure is mapped using **fractography** (SEM, XPS) to identify whether failure was adhesive, cohesive, or mixed.

### 2.3 Factors Controlling Strength of Adhesion

| S.No | Factor | Mechanism | Effect on Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Surface Preparation** | Removes weak boundary layers, increases surface energy | ↑ Strength (often 2–10×) |
| 2 | **Surface Energy / Wetting** | Good contact via low contact angle (Young's eq.) | ↑ Strength (thermodynamic adhesion) |
| 3 | **Interfacial Chemical Bonding** | Covalent / ionic / H-bonds across interface | ↑↑ Strength (highest contribution) |
| 4 | **Mechanical Interlocking** | Adhesive flows into pores / roughness | ↑ Strength (especially on porous substrates) |
| 5 | **Interdiffusion / Interpenetration** | Polymer chains diffuse across interface | ↑ Strength (esp. for polymers) |
| 6 | **Adhesive Thickness** | Too thin → stress peaks; too thick → bending strain | Optimal at ~0.1–0.2 mm |
| 7 | **Cure Conditions** | Degree of cure governs Tg and crosslink density | ↑ Strength up to full cure |
| 8 | **Environmental Factors** | Moisture, temperature, UV, chemicals | ↓ Strength (plasticization, hydrolysis) |
| 9 | **Joint Geometry & Overlap Length** | Longer overlap reduces average shear stress | ↑ Strength (up to a limit) |
| 10 | **Loading Rate (Strain Rate)** | Higher rate → brittle response | ↑ Peak stress, ↓ strain-to-failure |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question paper on this module.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L}$ | Average shear stress in lap joint (P = load, b = width, L = overlap) | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Tensile (pull-off) stress (P = load, A = bonded area) | MPa |
| 3 | $P/L = f(w, E_1, E_2, t_a)$ | Peel force per unit width (function of adherend bending stiffness $EI$) | N/mm |
| 4 | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | **Young's Equation** — contact angle of liquid on solid | J/m² |
| 5 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | **Work of Adhesion** (Dupré) | J/m² |
| 6 | $W_c = 2\gamma_a$ | **Work of Cohesion** of adhesive (a = liquid adhesive surface energy) | J/m² |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{A}$ | Joint strength (peak load ÷ bonded area) | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{b}\sqrt{\dfrac{G_a}{E_a \cdot t_a \cdot t_s}}$ | **Volkersen peak shear stress** at overlap end | MPa |
| 9 | $G_{Ic} = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial A}$ | **Strain Energy Release Rate** (fracture mechanics) | J/m² |
| 10 | $K_{Ic} = \sqrt{G_{Ic} \cdot E^{\prime}}$ | **Fracture Toughness** (E' = plane-strain modulus) | MPa·m^(1/2) |

> **Note:** Use `\vert` for absolute values inside text — never the literal pipe `|` in tables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Single-lap shear strength values feed into *Boeing* and *Airbus* bonded-joint design allowables. A shift from cohesive to adhesive failure is a **red flag** signalling contamination.
- **Automotive crashworthiness:** Crash-impact simulations (LS-DYNA, ABAQUS) use cohesive-zone models with $G_{Ic}$ to predict bondline failure.
- **Microelectronics:** Die-shear strength (per MIL-STD-883) uses $\tau = P/A$ on a silicon die attached to a leadframe.
- **Medical devices:** Peel strength of medical tapes is governed by N/cm measurements using **180° peel tests**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Work of Adhesion from Young's Equation (Dupré's Relation)

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark derivation question.**

We start with **Young's Equation** describing the equilibrium of a liquid drop on a solid substrate under three interfacial tensions:

$$\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 1 — Identify the system.**
- $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapour interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy
- $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapour (surface tension of liquid adhesive)
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $\gamma_{sl}$.**

$$\gamma_{sl} \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;-\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply the thermodynamic definition of Work of Adhesion.**
The work required to separate a unit area of solid–liquid interface into two free surfaces (solid–vapour and liquid–vapour) is:

$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; \gamma_{sl}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the rearranged Young's equation into the $W_a$ expression.**

$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv} \;-\; (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta)$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\gamma_{sv}$.**

$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $\gamma_{lv}$.**

$$\boxed{\,W_a \;=\; \gamma_{lv}\,(1 + \cos\theta)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - If $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting), $W_a = 2\gamma_{lv}$ — equal to the **work of cohesion**, $W_c$.
> - If $\theta = 180°$ (no wetting), $W_a = 0$ — no adhesion.
> - A small contact angle therefore predicts **high thermodynamic adhesion**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Shear Stress in a Single-Lap Joint

Consider two rigid adherends overlapped over a length $L$ and width $b$, loaded in tension by force $P$.

**Step 1 — Define the bonded (overlap) area.**

$$A \;=\; b \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of average shear stress** (force parallel to the bond plane divided by area):

$$\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the area expression:**

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L}\,}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A single-lap joint has $b = 25$ mm, $L = 12.5$ mm, and fails at $P = 6250$ N.

$$\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{6250 \text{ N}}{25 \text{ mm} \times 12.5 \text{ mm}} \;=\; \dfrac{6250}{312.5} \;=\; 20 \text{ MPa}$$

This is the typical value reported for an epoxy–aluminium structural bond.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Volkersen's Peak Shear Stress at Overlap End

In a real single-lap joint, the adherends stretch non-uniformly because each carries only a fraction of the load. This causes **differential strain** and produces a **shear-lag** stress concentration at the overlap ends.

**Step 1 — Assume elastic adherends, linear elastic adhesive, small deformations.**

**Step 2 — The governing differential equation for adhesive shear strain is:**

$$\dfrac{d^2 \tau}{dx^2} \;-\; \lambda^2 \,\tau \;=\; 0$$

where:

$$\lambda^2 \;=\; \dfrac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — The general solution is:**

$$\tau(x) \;=\; A\cosh(\lambda x) + B\sinh(\lambda x)$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions** $\tau = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $\tau = \tau_0$ at $x = L$ (symmetric overlap):

$$\tau(x) \;=\; \tau_{avg} \,\dfrac{\lambda L / 2}{\sinh(\lambda L / 2)} \cosh(\lambda x)$$

**Step 5 — Peak shear stress occurs at $x = L/2$:**

$$\boxed{\,\tau_{\max} \;=\; \tau_{avg} \,\dfrac{\lambda L / 2}{\tanh(\lambda L / 2)}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

- For $\lambda L \gg 1$, $\tanh(\lambda L/2) \to 1$ → $\tau_{\max}$ rises significantly above $\tau_{avg}$ (stress concentration).
- For $\lambda L \ll 1$, $\tau_{\max} \approx \tau_{avg}$ (uniform distribution — long, thin, flexible adherends).
- This is why **longer overlaps** do not proportionally increase strength — the peak stress grows too.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Test

| Loading Condition | Recommended Test | Standard | Property Measured | Failure Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tension on small disc | Pull-off adhesion | ASTM D4541 | $\sigma_t$ (MPa) | MPa |
| Lap shear of flat coupons | Single-lap shear | ASTM D1002 | $\tau$ (MPa) | MPa |
| Flexible adherend peel | 90° / 180° peel | ASTM D903 / D1876 | $P/w$ (N/mm) | N/mm |
| Rigid adherend end-pull | Cleavage | ASTM D1062 | $P/A$ (MPa) | MPa |
| Bulk adhesive strength | Tensile dog-bone | ASTM D638 | $\sigma_{uts}$ (MPa) | MPa |
| Fracture toughness of bond | DCB / TDCB | ISO 25217 | $G_{Ic}$ (J/m²) | J/m² |
| Die attach in microelectronics | Die-shear | MIL-STD-883 | $\tau$ (MPa) | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always report strength with both the **test standard** *and* the **failure mode** (e.g., "20 MPa, 100% cohesive failure in adhesive"). Examiners award full credit only when both are stated.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Factors Affecting Strength of Adhesion


### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Modes of Joint Failure Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Applied Load Increases"] --> B{"Where Does Crack Initiate?"}
    B -- "At adhesive-substrate interface" --> C["Adhesive Failure"]
    B -- "Inside adhesive bulk" --> D["Cohesive Failure in Adhesive"]
    B -- "Inside substrate bulk" --> E["Cohesive Failure in Substrate"]
    B -- "Multiple locations" --> F["Mixed Mode Failure"]

    C --> G["Surface Energy too low"]
    C --> H["Contamination or weak boundary layer"]
    D --> I["Adhesive under-cured"]
    D --> J["Cohesive strength less than interfacial strength"]

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Stress Distribution in a Single-Lap Joint (Volkersen Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Left Adherend: Carries Load P/2 at Far End"] --> B["Overlap Region: Length L, Width b"]
    B --> C["Right Adherend: Carries Load P/2 at Far End"]
    B --> D{"Stress Along Overlap"}
    D -- "x = 0" --> E["Shear Stress tau = 0"]
    D -- "x = L/2" --> F["Shear Stress tau = tau MAX (Peak)"]
    D -- "x = L" --> G["Shear Stress tau = 0"]

    E --> H["Volkersen Non-uniform Profile"]
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I["Average tau avg = P divided by b times L"]
    I --> J["tau MAX greater than tau avg by factor depending on lambda L"]

    classDef region fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#000;
    classDef result fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#385723,color:#000;

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between adhesive failure and cohesive failure in a bonded joint. How can the failure mode be identified experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Adhesive failure (1 mark):** Separation occurs at the interface between the adhesive and the substrate. Both fracture surfaces are smooth; one shows bare substrate, the other shows a clean adhesive face with no substrate residue.
- **Cohesive failure (1 mark):** Separation occurs within the bulk of the adhesive (or substrate). The adhesive residue is visible on *both* adherends.
- **Identification (1 mark):** Visual / optical microscopy, SEM, or XPS analysis of the fracture surfaces to detect chemical residues of the substrate on the adhesive side (or vice versa).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
**State Young's equation and explain the significance of the contact angle in determining the strength of adhesion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\gamma_{sv} \;=\; \gamma_{sl} \;+\; \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

- $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$, $\gamma_{lv}$ are interfacial energies of solid–vapour, solid–liquid, and liquid–vapour interfaces.
- $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle (1 mark).
- **Significance (1 mark):** A **smaller $\theta$** indicates better wetting of the substrate by the adhesive, leading to higher thermodynamic work of adhesion $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$. For $\theta \to 0$, $W_a$ is maximised; for $\theta \to 180°$, $W_a \to 0$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **single-lap shear test** (ASTM D1002). Derive the expression for **average shear strength** and explain why the actual failure load is lower than predicted by this average value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Work of Adhesion ($W_a$)** and **Work of Cohesion ($W_c$)**. Starting from Young's equation, derive the Dupré relation for $W_a$. A liquid adhesive has surface tension $\gamma_{lv} = 42$ mJ/m² and a contact angle of $\theta = 35°$ on a cleaned steel substrate. Calculate the work of adhesion. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Single-Lap Shear Test & Average Shear Strength (7 marks)

- **Schematic (2 marks):** Two rectangular adherends (typically 100 × 25 mm, 1.6 mm thick aluminium) overlapped over a length $L$ (typically 12.5 mm). The assembly is gripped in a tensile testing machine and pulled at a constant crosshead speed (e.g., 1.3 mm/min) until failure. The peak load $P_{\max}$ is recorded.

- **Derivation of average shear strength (3 marks):**
  Bonded (overlap) area:

$$A \;=\; b \cdot L$$

Average shear stress at failure:

$$\tau_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\max}}{b \cdot L}$$

- **Why the actual failure load is lower than predicted (2 marks):**
  - The adhesive layer and adherends deform differentially, producing a **non-uniform shear stress distribution** along the overlap (Volkersen's analysis).
  - **Peak shear stress** at the overlap ends is much greater than $\tau_{avg}$, initiating failure prematurely.
  - Additional stress concentrations arise from **bending moments** (single-lap geometry is eccentric) and from **defects** at the bondline.

#### Part (b) — Work of Adhesion, Dupré Relation, Numerical (7 marks)

- **Definitions (2 marks):**
  - $W_a$ = reversible work required to separate a unit area of the adhesive–substrate interface into two free surfaces (solid–vapour and liquid–vapour).
  - $W_c$ = reversible work required to separate a unit area of the bulk adhesive into two new liquid surfaces → $W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}$.

- **Derivation of Dupré relation (3 marks):**

$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

From Young's equation, $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$. Substituting:

$$W_a \;=\; \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta) \;=\; \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

- **Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

$$W_a \;=\; 42 \, \text{mJ/m}^2 \times (1 + \cos 35°)$$

$$\cos 35° \;=\; 0.8192$$

$$W_a \;=\; 42 \times 1.8192 \;=\; 76.4 \, \text{mJ/m}^2$$

> **Conclusion:** The thermodynamic work of adhesion is **76.4 mJ/m²**, indicating a moderately strong interfacial bond.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the **Volkersen shear-lag analysis** for a single-lap joint. State the assumption, write the governing differential equation, and give the expression for the **peak shear stress**. Discuss how the joint geometry influences the peak-to-average stress ratio. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **standard modes of failure** observed in adhesive joints? With the help of a table, list at least **four standard mechanical tests** (with their ASTM designations) used to evaluate the strength of adhesion, and specify the property each test measures. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Volkersen Shear-Lag Analysis (7 marks)

- **Assumption (1 mark):** The adhesive layer is linear elastic; the adherends are elastic, isotropic, and experience only uniaxial tension; plane sections remain plane; bending and transverse stresses are neglected.

- **Governing differential equation (2 marks):**

$$\dfrac{d^2 \tau}{dx^2} \;-\; \lambda^2 \,\tau \;=\; 0$$

where:

$$\lambda^2 \;=\; \dfrac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \dfrac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)$$

- **Peak shear stress expression (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{\max} \;=\; \tau_{avg} \,\dfrac{\lambda L / 2}{\tanh(\lambda L / 2)}$$

- **Influence of joint geometry (2 marks):**
  - **Longer overlap $L$** → higher $\lambda L$ → peak-to-average ratio grows (more severe stress concentration).
  - **Stiffer adherends** (larger $E_i t_i$) → smaller $\lambda$ → stress distribution becomes more uniform.
  - **Thinner bondline $t_a$** → larger $\lambda$ → higher stress concentration.
  - **Practical implication:** A *longer* lap does not proportionally increase joint strength beyond a certain length because of the escalating stress concentration.

#### Part (b) — Failure Modes & Standard Tests (7 marks)

- **Modes of failure (2 marks):**
  - **Adhesive failure** — at the interface.
  - **Cohesive failure** — in adhesive or substrate.
  - **Mixed failure** — combination.
  - **Substrate failure** — substrate itself fractures.

- **Standard tests table (5 marks):**

| S.No | Test Name | ASTM / ISO Standard | Property Measured | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Single-Lap Shear | ASTM D1002 | Average shear strength $\tau_{avg}$ | MPa |
| 2 | T-Peel | ASTM D1876 | Peel force per unit width | N/mm |
| 3 | 90° Peel | ASTM D903 | Peel force per unit width | N/mm |
| 4 | Pull-Off Adhesion | ASTM D4541 | Tensile bond strength | MPa |
| 5 | Butt-Joint Tension | ASTM D897 | Tensile strength | MPa |
| 6 | Cleavage | ASTM D1062 | Cleavage force / area | MPa |
| 7 | Double-Cantilever Beam | ISO 25217 | Fracture toughness $G_{Ic}$ | J/m² |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** quote a "strength" without specifying the **test standard** and the **failure mode** — both are mandatory. Marks are deducted even if your numerical answer is correct.
> - **Do not** confuse $\tau_{avg}$ (engineering mean stress) with $\tau_{\max}$ (Volkersen peak). Examiners specifically test whether students know the *difference*.
> - **Do not** write $W_a = \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$. The correct Dupré form is $\gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$. Losing the "+1" costs **1 full mark** in derivations.
> - **Do not** use the bare pipe `|` for absolute value inside markdown tables — it breaks the table layout. Use `\vert`.
> - **Always** state units (MPa, N/mm, J/m²) for every numerical answer. A correct number without a unit is treated as **incomplete**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Strength of Adhesion**

- ☐ **Adhesive vs Cohesive Strength:** Adhesive = interface; Cohesive = bulk. Always report the failure mode.
- ☐ **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ — equilibrium of a sessile drop.
- ☐ **Dupré's Relation:** $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ — thermodynamic work of adhesion.
- ☐ **Work of Cohesion:** $W_c = 2\gamma_{lv}$ — for a pure liquid adhesive.
- ☐ **Spreading Criterion:** Adhesion is favoured when $W_a > W_c$ (i.e., the liquid "wets" the solid).
- ☐ **Volkersen Equation:** $\tau_{\max} = \tau_{avg} \cdot (\lambda L/2)/\tanh(\lambda L/2)$ — explains why long laps are inefficient.
- ☐ **Five Loading Modes:** Tensile, Shear, Peel, Cleavage, Impact/Fatigue.
- ☐ **Optimal Bondline Thickness:** $\sim$0.1–0.2 mm (varies by adhesive modulus).
- ☐ **Surface Preparation Multiplier:** Proper surface prep can improve joint strength by **2–10×**.
- ☐ **Key Standards to Memorise:** ASTM D1002 (lap shear), D1876 (T-peel), D903 (90° peel), D4541 (pull-off), D897 (butt joint), D1062 (cleavage), ISO 25217 (DCB).
- ☐ **Three Primary Theories of Adhesion:** Mechanical interlocking, Adsorption (wetting), Chemical bonding. (Diffusion, electrostatic, weak-boundary-layer are supplementary.)
- ☐ **Fracture Mechanics View:** Joint strength $\propto \sqrt{E \cdot G_{Ic}}$; cohesive-zone modelling captures progressive damage.
- ☐ **Always Report:** Load, area (or width), strength with units, test standard, and failure mode — together as one complete datum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Treatments for Metals and Plastics
Solvent Cleaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solvent Cleaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solvent Cleaning** is a *physico-chemical surface preparation process* in which one or more organic (or semi-organic) liquid solvents are brought into intimate contact with a substrate in order to **dissolve, displace, or emulsify organic contaminants** — such as machining oils, grease, mold-release agents, fingerprints (sebum), waxes, lapping compounds, and loosely-bound shop dirt — from the surface prior to adhesive bonding, coating, or painting operations.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, solvent cleaning is classified as a **non-abrasive, non-mechanical primary surface treatment** that depends entirely on the **solvency power, surface tension, evaporation rate, and chemical compatibility** of the chosen fluid with both the *contaminant* and the *substrate*. It is governed by the classical **"like dissolves like"** principle rooted in the **Hildebrand–Hansen solubility parameter theory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Solvent cleaning is the **first and most universally applied** step in any adhesive bonding pre-treatment sequence. No matter how sophisticated the subsequent primer, plasma, or chemical etch treatment may be, its performance is *severely compromised* if the substrate has not been properly solvent-degreased. The adhesive joint strength is bounded above by the *cleanliness* of the adherend surface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are cooking and your stainless-steel pan is covered with burnt grease. You cannot apply a non-stick coating or a new layer of seasoning unless that grease is **completely removed**. What do you do?

You reach for a cleaning liquid — perhaps dish-soap, perhaps vinegar — because **the chemistry of the cleaner is matched to the chemistry of the grime**. The cleaning fluid *penetrates under* the grease, *weakens* the adhesion of the contaminant to the metal, and *carries it away* when you wipe it off.

**Solvent cleaning in adhesive technology is exactly this process, but engineered with scientific precision:**

- The "pan" is the **metal, polymer, or composite substrate** that you intend to bond.
- The "burnt grease" is the **organic contamination** (oils, release agents, fingerprints) sitting on the surface.
- The "dish-soap" is the **engineered industrial solvent** (acetone, MEK, toluene, isopropanol, or a formulated blend) chosen for its solvency toward the specific contaminant.
- The "wiping action" is the **mechanical assistance** (agitation, brushing, ultrasonic cavitation) provided to accelerate the dissolution and to physically transport the dissolved film away from the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Solvent cleaning does **not** remove the *native oxide layer* or *chemically alter* the substrate. It only removes the *loosely-bound organic overlayer*. For stronger, durable bonds, solvent cleaning is therefore almost always followed by a *secondary* treatment such as abrasion, grit-blasting, chemical etching, or plasma/corona treatment.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following standard metrics are universally referenced in KTU-level and industrial solvent-cleaning evaluations:

- **Surface tension of clean water at 25 °C:** **γ_H2O = 72.0 mN/m** (reference benchmark for "wetting").
- **Standard reference solvent for Relative Evaporation Rate (RER):** **n-Butyl acetate** (assigned RER = 1.0 by ASTM D3539).
- **Hildebrand total solubility parameter of a "good" cleaning solvent:** typically **δ_t ≈ 18 – 26 MPa^(1/2)**.
- **Kauri-Butanol (Kb) value of a strong solvent:** **Kb > 100** (e.g., methylene chloride ≈ 136, toluene ≈ 105).
- **Standard flash-point classification temperature:** **< 38 °C = "Flammable"**; **38 – 93 °C = "Combustible"** (per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106).
- **Recommended maximum contact angle on a "clean" metallic surface:** **θ < 10°** (indicating complete wetting by the subsequent adhesive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these metrics matter:** They are the *numerical fingerprints* by which a formulator or a quality-control engineer *selects, justifies, and audits* a solvent-cleaning operation in a real production environment. KTU questions frequently test the *numerical interpretation* of these quantities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — The Six Sequential Steps

Solvent cleaning is *not* a single event but a **six-stage physico-chemical transport process**. Understanding each stage is essential because each one introduces a potential failure mode that the process engineer must control.

1. **Wetting of the Contaminated Surface (Stage 1 — Kinetic):**
   The solvent liquid must *spread* over the contaminated substrate, displacing the air at the solid–contaminant interface. The driving force is the **difference in surface energies**. If γ_solvent < γ_contaminant, complete wetting (θ → 0) is spontaneous. If wetting fails, the solvent beads up and the cleaning action is *localized to microscopic spots only*.

2. **Penetration & Diffusion into the Contaminant Layer (Stage 2 — Mass Transfer):**
   The solvent molecules diffuse *into* the bulk of the grease/oil film. This follows **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion**:

   $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_{\text{solvent-in-contaminant}} \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

   The diffusion coefficient $D$ is highly dependent on the **viscosity of the contaminant** and the **molar volume of the solvent**.

3. **Solvency / Dissolution of the Contaminant (Stage 3 — Thermodynamic):**
   This is the *heart* of the cleaning process. The contaminant dissolves into the solvent bulk when the **Hildebrand (or Hansen) solubility parameters** of the two are closely matched. The closer $\delta_{\text{solvent}} \approx \delta_{\text{contaminant}}$, the greater the thermodynamic driving force for dissolution.

4. **Displacement of the Contaminant from the Substrate (Stage 4 — Adhesive Failure):**
   Once the contaminant is swollen and partially dissolved, the **adhesive force** between the contaminant and the substrate is weakened. The solvent can then *undercut* and *lift* the contaminant film. This stage is enhanced by **mechanical agitation** (ultrasonic cavitation, brushing, spray impingement).

5. **Transport of Dissolved Contaminant into the Bulk Solvent (Stage 5 — Convective):**
   The dissolved contaminant is carried away from the interface into the bulk solvent by **convective diffusion** (agitation, spray jets, or fresh-solvent rinsing). Without this step, the interface becomes *saturated* and cleaning stalls — a phenomenon called **"solvent exhaustion"** or **"equilibrium stop"**.

6. **Evaporation of Residual Solvent (Stage 6 — Drying):**
   After the cleaning fluid has done its job, it must leave the surface completely. The **evaporation rate** must be matched to the production line speed. Too slow → bottleneck; too fast → non-uniform drying, streaking, and condensation of moisture from humid air onto the cold solvent-wetted surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' behind the steps:** The student should remember that a solvent that has *high solvency* but *low wetting ability* (high surface tension) will sit on the contaminant as droplets and clean only locally. Conversely, a solvent with *excellent wetting* but *poor solvency* will spread beautifully and remove *nothing*. The engineering art is to **balance both properties simultaneously**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Engineering Use | Typical Units |
|---|--------------------|--------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Hildebrand total solubility parameter | $\delta_{t} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta E_{v}}{V_{m}}}$ | Quantifies cohesive energy density; "like dissolves like" matching | $\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ |
| 2 | Hansen 3-parameter decomposition | $\delta_{t}^{2} = \delta_{d}^{2} + \delta_{p}^{2} + \delta_{h}^{2}$ | Separates dispersive, polar, and H-bonding contributions | $\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ |
| 3 | Hansen solubility sphere radius | $R_{0} = \sqrt{4(\delta_{d,s}-\delta_{d,c})^{2} + (\delta_{p,s}-\delta_{p,c})^{2} + (\delta_{h,s}-\delta_{h,c})^{2}}$ | Determines if a polymer is soluble in a given solvent | $\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | Relative Evaporation Rate (RER) | $\text{RER} = \dfrac{t_{90\%,\text{ref}}}{t_{90\%,\text{solvent}}}$ | ASTM D3539; n-BuOAc = 1.0 baseline | dimensionless |
| 5 | Young's equation (wetting) | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$ | Predicts contact angle from interfacial energies | $\text{mN/m}$ |
| 6 | Work of adhesion (Young–Dupré) | $W_{a} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv}(1+\cos\theta)$ | Energy released per unit area when adhesive wets surface | $\text{mJ/m}^{2}$ |
| 7 | Kauri-Butanol value | $K_{b} = \dfrac{\text{mL of solvent to titrate}}{\text{correlated to Kb scale}}$ | Empirical solvency strength (ASTM D1133) | dimensionless |
| 8 | Cleaning efficiency (gravimetric) | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{\text{initial}} - m_{\text{final}}}{m_{\text{initial}}} \times 100\%$ | Quantifies % contaminant removed | % |
| 9 | Diffusion-limited cleaning time | $t_{\text{clean}} \approx \dfrac{L^{2}}{2 D_{\text{eff}}}$ | Time for solvent to penetrate a contaminant film of thickness $L$ | seconds |
| 10 | Vapor degrease boiling point criterion | $T_{b,\text{solvent}} < 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ ideally | Determines energy cost and safety class | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In written examinations, students frequently confuse $\delta_{t}$ (total Hildebrand parameter) with $\delta_{d}$, $\delta_{p}$, $\delta_{h}$ (the *three* Hansen components). Always remember: $\delta_{t}$ is a *single scalar*; Hansen parameters are a *triplet* that you must *sum in quadrature* to retrieve the total.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solvent cleaning is the **silent workhorse** of every high-performance adhesive-bonded assembly in the modern world:

- **Aerospace:** Aluminum wing-skin panels are solvent-cleaned (often with *non-chlorinated* alkaline + solvent blends) immediately before primer application. A contaminated surface here is a *catastrophic safety risk*.
- **Automotive:** Sheet-metal body panels are wiped with *hydrocarbon solvent* on the e-coat line to remove stamping oils and ensure paint adhesion.
- **Electronics:** PCB surfaces are cleaned with *isopropanol or fluorinated solvents* to remove flux residues prior to underfill or conformal-coat application.
- **Medical devices:** Surgical instruments and implants are solvent-cleaned before sterilization, leaving *no toxic residue* on the bonding surface.
- **Footwear & composites:** Release-agent residues on rubber outsoles are wiped with MEK/toluene blends before cyanoacrylate bonding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Trend (NEP-2020 Sustainability Context):** Traditional chlorinated solvents (e.g., trichloroethylene, perchloroethylene) are being **rapidly phased out** under the *Montreal Protocol* and *REACH* regulations because of their *ozone-depletion potential* (ODP) and *carcinogenicity*. Modern KTU-aware engineers must therefore be familiar with *aqueous*, *bio-based (terpene)*, and *hydrofluoroether (HFE)* alternatives.
