# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a primer in adhesive bonding. State any two of its primary functions. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **primer** is a low-viscosity, film-forming coating applied to a substrate surface prior to the application of the main adhesive, in order to improve wettability, chemical compatibility, and bond durability at the interface.

**Two primary functions:**
1. **Surface energy enhancement:** It raises the critical surface energy of low-energy substrates (such as polyolefins), enabling the adhesive to spread uniformly and form a continuous contact.
2. **Chemical bridging:** Reactive groups in the primer (e.g., silanol, isocyanate, epoxy) form covalent bonds with both the substrate and the adhesive, creating a stable interfacial bridge.

> **Marking Note:** Definition = 1 mark; Two functions with brief explanation = 1 mark each. Vague answers without naming specific functions lose marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a primer and a coupling agent. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primer | Coupling Agent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Film thickness** | $2$ to $25\ \mu m$ (visible film) | Monomolecular layer (invisible) |
| **Composition** | Polymer + reactive groups + solvents | Pure bifunctional molecule (e.g., silane) |
| **Primary role** | Wetting, sealing, corrosion protection | Chemical bridging across interface |
| **Application** | Spray, brush, dip, wipe | Dilute solution, often by wipe-on/wipe-off |

Coupling agents are often **incorporated into primers** but are not identical to them. A primer is a complete coating system; a coupling agent is a molecular additive.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates a **Module Internal Choice** in Part B. Two fully independent alternative questions are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Primer Mechanisms and Substrate Compatibility

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain with suitable diagrams the four primary mechanisms by which a primer improves adhesion. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The four mechanisms, in order of operational sequence, are:

**1. Surface Energy Modification (Wettability):**
The primer deposits polar functional groups on the substrate surface. These groups raise the surface energy $\gamma_{SV}$ of the substrate above the surface tension $\gamma_{LV}$ of the adhesive. Per the Young-Dupré equation:

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

A higher $\gamma_{SV}$ lowers the contact angle $\theta$, improving spreading.

**2. Chemical Bonding (Coupling):**
The primer contains bifunctional molecules (e.g., silanes, isocyanates). One end bonds to the substrate (e.g., $-\text{Si}-\text{O}-\text{Al}-$ on aluminum) and the other end reacts with the adhesive (e.g., with $-\text{OH}$ or $-\text{NH}_2$ of an epoxy). This forms a continuous covalent network.

**3. Mechanical Keying (Micro-roughening):**
The solvent in the primer swells or partially dissolves the top $5$ to $50\ \text{nm}$ of the substrate. After flash-off, the surface is left micro-roughened. The adhesive flows into these micro-cavities, locking in place upon cure.

**4. Barrier / Passivation Function:**
On metals, the primer forms a dense, cross-linked film that prevents oxygen and moisture from reaching the metal-oxide interface. This blocks the formation of a **weak boundary layer (WBL)** of hydrated oxide that would otherwise cause premature joint failure.

> *Marks breakdown: Naming + brief description of each mechanism = 1.5 marks × 4 = 6 marks; Diagram/equation reference = 1 mark.*

---

#### (b) Recommend a suitable primer system for bonding (i) polypropylene to an epoxy adhesive, and (ii) aluminum to a phenolic adhesive. Justify each choice. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Polypropylene to Epoxy Adhesive — Chlorinated Polyolefin (CPO) Primer**

Polypropylene is a non-polar, low-surface-energy polymer ($\gamma_c \approx 30\ \text{mJ/m}^2$). Epoxy adhesives are polar and will not wet untreated PP.

**Recommendation:** A chlorinated polyolefin (CPO) primer, applied as a thin $5$ to $10\ \mu m$ film.

**Justification:**
- The CPO primer **partially dissolves and re-precipitates** into the top layer of PP, forming an interpenetrating network.
- It also deposits polar $-\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ and $-\text{C}=\text{O}$ groups, raising surface energy to $\sim 42\ \text{mJ/m}^2$.
- This enables the epoxy to wet, and the $-\text{OH}$ groups of cured epoxy can form hydrogen bonds with residual polar sites on the primed PP.

**(ii) Aluminum to Phenolic Adhesive — Silane + Epoxy Primer System**

Aluminum has a native amphoteric oxide layer $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ that is hydrophilic and reactive but mechanically weak if hydrated.

**Recommendation:** A two-stage system: (1) silane primer (e.g., 3-glycidyloxypropyl trimethoxysilane, 3-GPS), followed by (2) an epoxy-phenolic tie-coat.

**Justification:**
- The silane **chemisorbs** onto $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, forming covalent $\text{Al}-\text{O}-\text{Si}$ bonds.
- The epoxy end of the silane reacts with the phenolic adhesive during cure at $150\ ^\circ C$ to $180\ ^\circ C$, producing a thermally stable aromatic network.
- This is the **standard aerospace-grade system** (per Boeing/Airbus specifications) for high-temperature structural joints.

> *Marks breakdown: Substrate identification = 1 mark; Primer recommendation = 1.5 marks; Justification (mechanism + properties) = 1.5 marks per system. Total = 7 marks.*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Primer Formulation, Application, and Numerical Computation

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Describe the step-by-step procedure for applying a primer to a metal substrate prior to adhesive bonding. Mention critical parameters and their typical values. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The standard primer application workflow on metal is:

**Step 1 — Substrate Cleaning (Degreasing):**
Remove oils and greases using acetone, MEK, or alkaline degreaser. Verify cleanness via the **water-break test** (water should form a continuous film, not beads).

**Step 2 — Surface Treatment (Abrasion / Chemical Etching):**
- Mechanical: Grit-blast with $80$-mesh $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ to achieve $R_a = 1.5$ to $3.0\ \mu m$.
- Chemical: Acid etch (e.g., chromic-sulfuric acid for aluminum) or conversion coating (e.g., chromate or anodize).

**Step 3 — Primer Mixing:**
For two-part primers, mix resin and hardener in the exact stoichiometric ratio (typically $4:1$ by volume). Mix slowly to avoid air entrapment. **Pot life = 4 to 8 hours** at $25\ ^\circ C$.

**Step 4 — Application:**
Apply by **spray (HVLP), brush, dip, or roller** to a wet film thickness (WFT) of $25$ to $75\ \mu m$ (corresponding to a DFT of $5$ to $25\ \mu m$).

**Step 5 — Flash-Off:**
Allow the primer to air-dry for **10 to 30 minutes** at RT. Solvent vapors must escape fully to prevent bubble formation in the adhesive layer.

**Step 6 — Adhesive Application:**
Apply adhesive over the primer within the **recoat window** (typically 1 to 24 hours). Beyond this window, primer activity decays.

**Step 7 — Cure and Bond:**
Cure the joint per adhesive manufacturer's schedule (heat + pressure as specified).

> *Marks breakdown: 7 steps × 1 mark each = 7 marks (with emphasis on flash-off time, DFT, and pot life being the critical parameters).*

---

#### (b) A steel plate of area $2.5\ \text{m}^2$ is to be coated with a primer of solids content $35\%$ (by volume) and density $1.10\ \text{g/cm}^3$. The required dry film thickness is $15\ \mu m$. Calculate the volume and mass of wet primer required. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute volume of dry film:**

$$V_{dry} = A \cdot t_d = 2.5\ \text{m}^2 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{dry} = 3.75 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute wet primer volume using solids fraction:**

$$V_{wet} = \dfrac{V_{dry}}{S_v} = \dfrac{3.75 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^3}{0.35}$$

$$V_{wet} = 1.071 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to liters:**

$$V_{wet} = 1.071 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.1071\ \text{L} \approx 107.1\ \text{mL}$$

**Step 4 — Compute mass:**

$$m_{wet} = V_{wet} \cdot \rho = 1.071 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 \times 1100\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$m_{wet} = 0.1178\ \text{kg} = 117.8\ \text{g}$$

> **Final Answer:** $V_{wet} \approx 107\ \text{mL}$ and $m_{wet} \approx 118\ \text{g}$ of wet primer are required.

> *Marks breakdown: Step 1 = 1.5 marks; Step 2 = 1.5 marks; Step 3 = 1.5 marks; Step 4 = 1.5 marks; Final boxed answer = 1 mark.*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN KTU 2024 EXAMS (PRIMERS MODULE):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "primer" with "adhesion promoter / coupling agent."** These are related but not identical. State the distinction explicitly for full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Numerical answers without units (mL, g, $\mu m$) typically lose 0.5 mark per question.
> 3. **Skipping the substrate preparation step.** KTU evaluators deduct 1 to 2 marks if the answer jumps directly to primer application without mentioning surface cleaning/treatment.
> 4. **Wrong primer for non-polar plastics.** Stating "use epoxy primer on polypropylene" is a textbook error. Always recommend CPO or similar chlorinated systems for PP/PE.
> 5. **Ignoring pot life and flash-off time.** These are favorite short-answer targets. Memorize the ranges: pot life $\mathbf{4-8\ hours}$, flash-off $\mathbf{10-30\ minutes}$.
> 6. **Writing paragraphs without structure.** KTU 2024 Scheme rewards bullet points, sub-headings, and equation references. Wall-of-text answers cap at $\sim 70\%$ of allotted marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Primers" — KTU PEPOT861 Module 2**

- **Definition:** A low-viscosity pre-coat applied to a substrate before the main adhesive, to enhance wetting, bonding, and durability.
- **Typical DFT:** $\mathbf{2 \text{ to } 25\ \mu m}$ (much thinner than adhesive or paint).
- **Core Functions:** Wetting, chemical bridging, mechanical keying, barrier, corrosion protection, plasticizer blocking.
- **Mechanisms:** (i) Surface energy modification; (ii) Chemical bonding; (iii) Mechanical keying; (iv) Barrier/passivation.
- **Key Equation:** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — work of adhesion by Young-Dupré.
- **Classification Axes:** By chemistry (silanes, epoxy, PU, acrylic, phenolic, CPO); by function (adhesion, anti-corrosion, barrier, tie-coat); by form (solvent, water, 100% solids, hot-melt).
- **Substrate Pairings to Memorize:**
    - PP / PE → **Chlorinated Polyolefin (CPO)** primer.
    - Aluminum / Glass → **Silane** primer (e.g., 3-GPS).
    - Steel + Rubber → **Isocyanate-based cover-coat system** (e.g., Chemlok 205 + 220).
    - Wood / Concrete → **Dilute acrylic latex** (pore sealing).
    - PVC → **Polyurethane barrier** primer (blocks plasticizer migration).
- **Application Workflow:** Clean → Treat → Mix → Apply → Flash-off → Adhesive → Cure.
- **Critical Parameters:** Pot life ($\mathbf{4-8\ h}$), flash-off time ($\mathbf{10-30\ min}$), recoat window ($\mathbf{1-24\ h}$).
- **Volume Estimation Formula:** $V_{wet} = \dfrac{A \cdot t_d}{S_v}$ — must convert to liters for practical use.
- **Common Failure Cause:** Skipping primer on a difficult substrate → **adhesive failure at interface** (vs. **cohesive failure** when primer is correctly used).
- **PTFE Rule:** No commercial primer works on virgin PTFE; **chemical etching (e.g., sodium naphthalenide)** is mandatory first.
- **Aerospace Benchmark:** Boeing/Airbus standards require silane + epoxy-phenolic primer systems for structural aluminum joints.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plasma Treatment** is a non-thermal, surface-specific modification process in which a low-temperature ionized gas (the "fourth state of matter") is brought into contact with a polymeric or metallic substrate to alter its surface energy, chemical functionality, and topography without modifying the bulk properties of the material.

In the context of **adhesive bonding technology**, plasma treatment is classified as a **physical-chemical surface preparation method** used to enhance **wettability**, increase **surface free energy**, introduce **polar functional groups**, and improve the **adhesive joint strength** between the adherend and the adhesive.

The plasma state is formally defined as a partially ionized gas consisting of:
- **Free electrons**
- **Positive and negative ions**
- **Neutral atoms and molecules**
- **Photons (UV radiation)**
- **Radicals and excited species**

When classified by thermal equilibrium, plasma is divided into two principal categories:

| Plasma Type | Temperature Range | Electron Temp. | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Plasma (Equilibrium)** | ~10,000–20,000 K | ≈ Ion Temp. | Metallurgy, welding, cutting |
| **Non-Thermal Plasma (Cold/Non-equilibrium)** | **300–500 K (room T)** | 10,000–50,000 K | **Surface treatment for adhesion** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Adhesion Science primarily deals with **Cold Plasma (Non-thermal plasma)** at near-ambient substrate temperatures, making it ideal for polymer and composite adherends that would otherwise degrade under elevated heat.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Molecular Sandblasting" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Rain on a Dusty Window:**
> Imagine a glass window that has been exposed to city pollution for years. Water droplets (the plasma) cannot spread on the dusty surface; they bead up and roll off — analogous to a low surface energy polymer resisting adhesive wetting.
> 
> Now imagine spraying **fine, electrically charged water vapor (plasma)** onto the window. The energetic particles not only **clean the dust (etching/ablation)** but also **chemically "stamp" new polar groups** onto the glass surface. Suddenly, the next raindrop spreads perfectly into a thin, uniform film — analogous to an adhesive now achieving full wetting and spreading on the treated adherend.
> 
> **Plasma = A combination of "molecular cleaning + chemical stamping"**, performed at room temperature.

### Real-World Industrial Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In industries such as **automotive (bonded aluminum body panels)**, **aerospace (CFRP composite joints)**, **medical devices (PTFE catheter bonding)**, and **packaging (PE/PP film lamination)**, plasma treatment has replaced traditional solvent-based or wet-chemical primers because it is:
> - **Environmentally friendly** (no VOC emissions)
> - **Dry, fast (seconds)**, and **continuous in-line processable**
> - **Substrate-selective** — bulk properties remain unchanged

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dielectric breakdown voltage of air** | $V_b$ | **3.0 × 10⁶** | V/m |
| **Standard atmospheric pressure** | $p_0$ | **1.013 × 10⁵** | Pa |
| **Plasma treatment exposure time (typical)** | $t$ | **1 – 300** | seconds |
| **Operating pressure (low-pressure plasma)** | $p$ | **1 – 1000** | Pa (mTorr range) |
| **Operating power** | $P$ | **10 – 1000** | W |
| **Surface energy increase (PE film)** | $\Delta \gamma_s$ | **20 – 40** | mJ/m² |
| **Frequency of operation (RF plasma)** | $f$ | **13.56** | MHz (ISM standard) |

---

## 1.4 Classification of Plasma in Adhesive Surface Engineering

Plasma treatments are categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Pressure Regime:**
   - **Low-Pressure Plasma (LPP)** — vacuum chamber, 1–1000 Pa
   - **Atmospheric Pressure Plasma (APP)** — open air, corona & dielectric barrier discharge

2. **By Discharge Mechanism:**
   - **Corona Discharge**
   - **Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD)**
   - **Glow Discharge (DC/RF/Microwave)**
   - **Plasma Jet / Torch**

3. **By Process Gas Chemistry:**
   - **Inert Gases:** Ar, He, N₂ (cleaning + crosslinking)
   - **Reactive Gases:** O₂, Air, H₂O vapor (oxidation, polar group introduction)
   - **Fluorinated Gases:** CF₄, SF₆ (hydrophobic surfaces — used inversely for release layers)
   - **Polymer-forming Gases:** HMDSO, C₂H₄ (plasma polymerization / primer deposition)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Mechanisms of Plasma–Surface Interaction

When plasma contacts a substrate, four simultaneous processes occur. The **net surface modification** is the algebraic sum of these competing effects.

### Mechanism 1 — Surface Cleaning (Ablation / Etching)

Plasma ions, electrons, and UV photons bombard the substrate surface. Organic contaminants, weak boundary layers, and adsorbed hydrocarbons are volatilized and pumped away.

$$\text{Contaminant (solid)} \xrightarrow{\text{UV + ions}} \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} \uparrow + \text{volatile fragments} \uparrow$$

> **Why this matters:** Adhesive failure often initiates at the **weak boundary layer** (WBL). Plasma ablates this layer, exposing the true substrate chemistry — directly improving **mechanical interlocking**.

### Mechanism 2 — Surface Activation (Functional Group Insertion)

Reactive species (radicals, atomic oxygen, hydroxyl groups) react with the substrate surface to insert **polar functional groups**.

For a polyethylene (PE) surface treated with O₂ plasma:

$$-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}_2- + \text{O}^\bullet \rightarrow -\text{CH}_2-\overset{\text{O}}{\underset{\|}{\text{C}}}- + -\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{OH})-$$

> **Common functional groups introduced:** –C=O (carbonyl), –COOH (carboxyl), –OH (hydroxyl), –NH₂ (amino, from N₂/NH₃ plasma), –SO₃H (sulfonate, from SO₂ plasma).

### Mechanism 3 — Crosslinking (CASING — Crosslinking via Activated Species of INert Gases)

Discovered by Schonhorn & Hansen (1967), inert gas plasma (He, Ar) creates a **densely cross-linked, oxygen-impermeable skin** on polymer surfaces.

$$\text{Polymer chains} \xrightarrow{\text{He}^+, \text{Ar}^+, \text{UV}} \text{Free radicals} \rightarrow \text{C–C crosslinks (3D network)}$$

> **CASING effect:** Improves cohesive strength of the surface layer, suppresses the migration of low-molecular-weight species to the interface, and increases joint durability under humid aging.

### Mechanism 4 — Plasma Polymerization (Grafting / Primer Deposition)

Monomeric gas precursors (e.g., HMDSO, acrylic acid, allylamine) are fragmented in the plasma and deposited as a thin, pinhole-free **polymer-like film** that acts as a **chemically tailored primer layer**.

$$\text{Monomer (gas)} \xrightarrow{\text{plasma}} \text{Radicals/ions} \xrightarrow{\text{deposition}} \text{Thin (10–100 nm) functional coating}$$

> **Industrial Relevance:** Used in bonding **PTFE, PEEK, and silicone rubber** — substrates that are otherwise nearly impossible to bond without primers.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **authoritative reference** for solving numerical and analytical questions on Plasma Treatments in KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Paschen's Law (Breakdown Voltage)** | $V_b = \dfrac{B \cdot p \cdot d}{\ln(A \cdot p \cdot d) - \ln[\ln(1 + 1/\gamma_{se})]}$ | $V_b$ = breakdown voltage (V); $p$ = pressure (Pa); $d$ = electrode gap (m); $A, B$ = gas constants; $\gamma_{se}$ = secondary emission coefficient | Predicts minimum ignition voltage for LPP |
| 2 | **Youngs Dupré Work of Adhesion** | $W_a = \gamma_s + \gamma_l - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_l (1 + \cos\theta)$ | $\gamma_s, \gamma_l, \gamma_{sl}$ = surface/interfacial energies (mJ/m²); $\theta$ = contact angle (°) | **Directly links** plasma-induced $\gamma_s$ change to bond strength |
| 3 | **Fowkes Surface Energy Decomposition** | $\gamma^{total} = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$ | $\gamma^d$ = dispersive, $\gamma^p$ = polar component (mJ/m²) | Distinguishes polar vs. dispersive gains post-plasma |
| 4 | **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Käble (OWRK) Equation** | $\gamma_l (1+\cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p \gamma_l^p}\right)$ | $\theta$ = measured contact angle; subscripts $s, l, d, p$ | Used to calculate $\gamma_s^d, \gamma_s^p$ from contact angle of ≥2 probe liquids |
| 5 | **Degree of Treatment (DoT) — Surface Energy Gain** | $\text{DoT}\ (\%) = \dfrac{\gamma_s^{treated} - \gamma_s^{untreated}}{\gamma_s^{target} - \gamma_s^{untreated}} \times 100$ | $\gamma_s$ in mJ/m² | Quality control metric in industrial lines |
| 6 | **Contact Angle Hysteresis** | $\Delta\theta = \theta_{advancing} - \theta_{receding}$ | All angles in ° | Indicates surface heterogeneity & chemical functionalization |
| 7 | **Plasma Sheath Thickness (Debye Length)** | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_0 k_B T_e}{n_e e^2}}$ | $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity; $k_B$ = Boltzmann const.; $T_e$ = electron temp.; $n_e$ = electron density; $e$ = electron charge | Characteristic dimension of plasma–surface interaction |
| 8 | **Sputtering Yield** | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Number of ejected atoms}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies ablation rate; depends on ion mass & energy |
| 9 | **Treatment Decay (Hydrophobic Recovery)** | $\gamma_s(t) = \gamma_s^{initial} + (\gamma_s^{max} - \gamma_s^{initial}) e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau$ = decay time constant (hours) | Models aging of plasma-treated polymer surfaces |
| 10 | **Average Electron Energy in Cold Plasma** | $\bar{\varepsilon}_e = \dfrac{3}{2} k_B T_e$ | $T_e$ in K, $\bar{\varepsilon}_e$ in eV | Typical cold plasma: $\bar{\varepsilon}_e \approx$ 1–10 eV |

> **Critical Note on Units:** Surface energy values for polymers are typically expressed in **mJ/m²** (= ergs/cm² × 1). The OWRK equation's square roots must be performed on values in **mJ/m²**, NOT converted to J/m² mid-equation.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Plasma Treatment in Adhesive Bonding

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - Replaces **wet-chemical primers** (chromic-sulfuric acid etch for polyolefins) — eliminating hazardous waste.
> - Enables bonding of **low-surface-energy polymers** (PE: $\gamma_s \approx 31$ mJ/m²; PP: $\gamma_s \approx 30$ mJ/m²; PTFE: $\gamma_s \approx 18$ mJ/m²) — all below the **critical wetting threshold** of typical adhesives ($\gamma_l \approx 38$–45 mJ/m²).
> - **Post-plasma PE surface energy** rises to **50–70 mJ/m²** — exceeding adhesive surface tension and guaranteeing complete wetting.
> - **Locus-of-failure shift:** Untreated PE fails *adhesively* at the interface. Plasma-treated PE fails *cohesively* within the adhesive — the gold-standard indicator of a strong, durable joint.

**Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A substrate is **bondable** when $\gamma_s \geq \gamma_l$. A safety margin of **$\gamma_s - \gamma_l \geq$ 10 mJ/m²** is desired for durable, void-free wetting on slightly rough industrial surfaces.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking Plasma-Induced Surface Energy Gain to Joint Strength

### Problem Setup
A **polypropylene (PP) adherend** is treated with **oxygen plasma for 90 seconds**. Before treatment, contact angle with deionized water is $\theta_1 = 95°$. After treatment, the contact angle drops to $\theta_2 = 35°$.

**Given probe liquid (water):** $\gamma_l^{total} = 72.8$ mJ/m², $\gamma_l^d = 21.8$ mJ/m², $\gamma_l^p = 51.0$ mJ/m².

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate the surface energy of PP before and after plasma treatment.
2. Determine the polar and dispersive components using the **OWRK method**.
3. Estimate the percentage increase in work of adhesion.

---

### Step 1 — Apply the Young–Dupré Equation (Pre-Treatment)

The Young–Dupré equation relates the measurable contact angle to surface energies:

$$W_a = \gamma_l (1 + \cos\theta)$$

For untreated PP with $\theta_1 = 95°$:

$$W_{a,1} = 72.8 \times \left(1 + \cos 95°\right)$$

We know:
$$\cos 95° = \cos(90° + 5°) = -\sin 5° = -0.0872$$

Therefore:

$$W_{a,1} = 72.8 \times (1 - 0.0872) = 72.8 \times 0.9128 = 66.45 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

---

### Step 2 — Calculate Total Surface Energy Pre-Treatment (Single-Liquid Approximation)

If we assume the dispersive component of PP $\gamma_s^d \approx 30$ mJ/m² (literature value for untreated PP), then:

$$W_{a,1} = 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \cdot \gamma_l^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^p \cdot \gamma_l^p}$$

Substituting:

$$66.45 = 2\sqrt{30 \times 21.8} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^p \times 51.0}$$

$$66.45 = 2\sqrt{654} + 2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p}$$

$$66.45 = 2(25.57) + 2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p}$$

$$66.45 = 51.14 + 2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p}$$

$$2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p} = 15.31$$

$$\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p} = 7.66$$

$$51.0 \gamma_s^p = 58.68$$

$$\gamma_s^p = 1.15 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

Total pre-treatment surface energy:

$$\gamma_s^{total,1} = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p = 30 + 1.15 = 31.15 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

> **This matches the well-known PP surface energy of ~30 mJ/m²** — verifying our calculation.

---

### Step 3 — Apply OWRK to Post-Treatment Case ($\theta_2 = 35°$)

For treated PP:

$$W_{a,2} = 72.8 \times (1 + \cos 35°) = 72.8 \times (1 + 0.8192) = 72.8 \times 1.8192 = 132.44 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

We assume plasma does **not** significantly alter the dispersive component ($\gamma_s^d$ remains ~30 mJ/m²; polar groups are inserted on top). Apply OWRK:

$$132.44 = 2\sqrt{30 \times 21.8} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^{p,2} \times 51.0}$$

$$132.44 = 51.14 + 2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^{p,2}}$$

$$2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^{p,2}} = 81.30$$

$$\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^{p,2}} = 40.65$$

$$51.0 \gamma_s^{p,2} = 1652.42$$

$$\gamma_s^{p,2} = 32.40 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

Total post-treatment surface energy:

$$\gamma_s^{total,2} = 30 + 32.40 = 62.40 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

---

### Step 4 — Percentage Increase in Work of Adhesion

$$\% \text{ Increase} = \dfrac{W_{a,2} - W_{a,1}}{W_{a,1}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Increase} = \dfrac{132.44 - 66.45}{66.45} \times 100 = \dfrac{65.99}{66.45} \times 100 \approx 99.3\%$$

> **Interpretation:** Oxygen plasma treatment of PP **nearly doubles** the thermodynamic work of adhesion with water (and analogously with water-borne adhesives). This directly translates into stronger, more durable bonded joints.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydrophobic Recovery Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** Plasma-treated polymers lose their activation over time due to **reorientation of polar groups into the bulk** and **diffusion of low-molecular-weight oxidized species (LMWOS) to the surface**. This is called **hydrophobic recovery** or **aging**.

The decay is modeled as **first-order kinetics**:

$$\gamma_s(t) = \gamma_s^{\infty} + \left(\gamma_s^{max} - \gamma_s^{\infty}\right) e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Variables:**
- $\gamma_s^{max}$: Surface energy immediately after plasma
- $\gamma_s^{\infty}$: Long-term (equilibrium) surface energy
- $\tau$: Time constant of decay
- $t$: Time elapsed since treatment

**Numerical Example:**
Given $\gamma_s^{max} = 65$ mJ/m², $\gamma_s^{\infty} = 38$ mJ/m², $\tau = 168$ hours (1 week), find $\gamma_s$ after **24 hours**:

$$\gamma_s(24) = 38 + (65 - 38) \cdot e^{-24/168}$$

$$= 38 + 27 \cdot e^{-0.1429}$$

$$= 38 + 27 \times 0.8669$$

$$= 38 + 23.41 = 61.41 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

After **1 week (168 h):**

$$\gamma_s(168) = 38 + 27 \cdot e^{-1} = 38 + 27 \times 0.368 = 38 + 9.93 = 47.93 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

> **Engineering Implication:** Plasma-treated parts should be bonded **within 24–48 hours** of treatment to retain maximum surface energy. Industrial lines are designed with **in-line plasma + immediate adhesive application** to bypass hydrophobic recovery entirely.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Calculating Plasma-Activated Bond Strength via OWRK + Failure Mode Shift

**Setup:** An epoxy adhesive ($\gamma_l = 45$ mJ/m², $\gamma_l^d = 38$, $\gamma_l^p = 7$) is to be applied to PP adherend.

**Without plasma:** $\gamma_s^{PP} = 31.15$ mJ/m² → $\gamma_s < \gamma_l$ → **Incomplete wetting**; expected failure: **adhesive (interfacial)**.

**After plasma:** $\gamma_s^{PP*} = 62.40$ mJ/m² → $\gamma_s \gg \gamma_l$ → **Complete spreading wetting**; expected failure: **cohesive within adhesive**.

**Verification of Wetting Criterion (Spreading Coefficient, $S$):**

$$S = \gamma_s - \gamma_l - \gamma_{sl} \approx \gamma_s - 2\gamma_l \quad (\text{for low } \gamma_{sl})$$

$$S_{untreated} = 31.15 - 90 = -58.85 \text{ mJ/m}^2 \quad (\text{NEGATIVE} \Rightarrow \text{no spontaneous spreading})$$

$$S_{treated} = 62.40 - 90 = -27.60 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

> **Note:** Even post-plasma, the spreading coefficient remains slightly negative — this is normal. **Complete wetting** (Young's condition satisfied) and **strong bond** are still achieved because the contact angle is below the critical $90°$ threshold. Spontaneous spreading ($S > 0$) is rare in real adhesive systems but is the ideal ceiling.

**Critical Contact Angle Check:**

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{W_a}{\gamma_l} - 1$$

For treated PP:

$$\cos\theta = \dfrac{132.44}{72.8} - 1 = 1.819 - 1 = 0.819 \Rightarrow \theta = 35°$$

> Since $\theta < 90°$, the adhesive **completely wets** the treated surface. Joint strength will be governed by **cohesive failure of the adhesive**, not interfacial debonding.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define cold plasma and explain why it is preferred over thermal plasma for treating polymer substrates in adhesive bonding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cold plasma, also called non-thermal or non-equilibrium plasma, is a partially ionized gas in which the electron temperature ($T_e \approx$ 10,000–50,000 K) is much higher than the heavy-species (ions, neutrals) temperature, which remains near room temperature (~300–500 K).

**Preferred over thermal plasma (2 marks):**
1. **Substrate temperature preservation:** Polymers (PE, PP, PTFE, PET) have low melting/glass transition temperatures and would **deform, degrade, or oxidize in bulk** under thermal plasma. Cold plasma keeps the substrate near ambient temperature while delivering energetic electrons and radicals to the surface only.
2. **Selective surface modification:** The energetic electrons dissociate process gas molecules (O₂, N₂) to create radicals that react with the top few nanometers of the polymer, increasing surface energy without altering bulk mechanical properties.
3. **Compatibility with continuous industrial processing:** Cold plasma enables in-line atmospheric pressure treatment (corona, DBD, plasma jet) on continuous webs and 3D parts without vacuum chambers.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List the four mechanisms by which plasma modifies polymer surfaces. State the role of CASING in adhesive bonding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Four Mechanisms (2 marks):**
1. **Ablation / Cleaning** — removal of organic contaminants and weak boundary layers by ion/UV bombardment.
2. **Activation** — insertion of polar functional groups (–OH, –C=O, –COOH) via reactions with plasma radicals.
3. **CASING (Crosslinking via Activated Species of INert Gases)** — He/Ar plasma-induced crosslinking of surface polymer chains.
4. **Plasma Polymerization** — deposition of a thin, pinhole-free functional primer layer from monomeric gas precursors.

**Role of CASING (1 mark):** CASING produces a **densely cross-linked surface skin** that improves the **cohesive strength of the surface layer**, suppresses the diffusion of low-molecular-weight species (LMWOS) to the interface, and enhances the durability of the adhesive joint under humid and thermal aging.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**(a)** With neat schematic sketches, describe the construction and working of **(i) Corona Discharge treatment system** and **(ii) Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD) system** for atmospheric pressure plasma treatment of polymer films. Compare their suitability for continuous in-line processing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-density polyethylene (LDPE) film has a measured water contact angle of $97°$ before treatment and $38°$ after oxygen plasma treatment for 60 seconds. Using the OWRK method (probe liquid: water; $\gamma_l = 72.8$ mJ/m², $\gamma_l^d = 21.8$ mJ/m², $\gamma_l^p = 51.0$ mJ/m²), determine:
1. The work of adhesion before and after treatment.
2. The polar and total surface energy of LDPE after plasma treatment (assume $\gamma_s^d = 30$ mJ/m² remains unchanged).
3. Comment on the expected shift in locus of failure for an epoxy adhesive joint. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a):**

**Corona Discharge System (3.5 marks):**

A corona discharge system consists of:
- **High-voltage, high-frequency (15–30 kHz) power supply**
- **Stationary metal electrode** (knife-edge or bar)
- **Grounded, rotating metallic roller** (or counter-electrode drum)
- **Dielectric backing** on the roller to localize the discharge
- **Film/substrate** passing between the electrodes in the air gap (~1–3 mm)

When high voltage is applied, the **non-uniform electric field** at the sharp electrode ionizes the surrounding air, creating a **localized plasma of ions, electrons, and ozone**. The substrate is exposed to this plasma for milliseconds as it passes through the gap, producing surface oxidation and polar group insertion. Corona is **continuous, atmospheric, and ideal for thin polymer films** (PE, PP, PET, PVC).

**DBD System (3.5 marks):**

A dielectric barrier discharge consists of:
- **Two parallel metallic electrodes** (one or both covered with a **dielectric barrier** — ceramic, glass, or polymer)
- **AC high-voltage source (1–100 kHz, 5–20 kV)**
- **Gas gap (1–5 mm)** between the electrodes where the substrate passes
- **Process gas flow** (air, O₂, N₂, He) through the gap

The dielectric barrier **limits current** and prevents arcing. The discharge consists of **numerous micro-discharge filaments** (nanoseconds long) that uniformly cover the electrode area. DBD produces a **more homogeneous plasma** than corona, with better control over gas chemistry, and is preferred for **3D parts, thicker sheets, and selective area treatment**.

**Comparison Table for Continuous In-Line Processing:**

| Feature | Corona | DBD |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode geometry | Knife edge | Parallel plane |
| Plasma uniformity | Moderate | High |
| 3D part treatment | Limited (films) | Excellent |
| Process gas control | Air only | Air / O₂ / N₂ / He |
| Power efficiency | Lower | Higher |
| Treatment speed | Up to 500 m/min | Up to 100 m/min |
| **Best for** | **Films & foils** | **Sheets, molded parts, textiles** |

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- $\theta_1 = 97°$ (untreated LDPE)
- $\theta_2 = 38°$ (plasma-treated LDPE)
- Probe liquid (water): $\gamma_l = 72.8$, $\gamma_l^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_l^p = 51.0$ mJ/m²
- Assume $\gamma_s^d = 30$ mJ/m² (constant)

**Step 1 — Work of adhesion BEFORE treatment (Young–Dupré):**

$$W_{a,1} = \gamma_l (1 + \cos\theta_1) = 72.8 \times (1 + \cos 97°)$$

$$\cos 97° = -\sin 7° = -0.1219$$

$$W_{a,1} = 72.8 \times (1 - 0.1219) = 72.8 \times 0.8781 = 63.93 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Work of adhesion AFTER treatment:**

$$W_{a,2} = \gamma_l (1 + \cos\theta_2) = 72.8 \times (1 + \cos 38°)$$

$$\cos 38° = 0.7880$$

$$W_{a,2} = 72.8 \times (1 + 0.7880) = 72.8 \times 1.7880 = 130.17 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply OWRK equation post-treatment:**

$$W_{a,2} = 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^p \gamma_l^p}$$

$$130.17 = 2\sqrt{30 \times 21.8} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_s^p \times 51.0}$$

$$130.17 = 2(25.57) + 2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p}$$

$$130.17 = 51.14 + 2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p}$$

$$2\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p} = 79.03$$

$$\sqrt{51.0 \gamma_s^p} = 39.51$$

$$51.0 \gamma_s^p = 1561.20$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_s^p = 30.61 \text{ mJ/m}^2}$$

**Total surface energy after plasma:**

$$\boxed{\gamma_s^{total,2} = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p = 30 + 30.61 = 60.61 \text{ mJ/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on locus of failure shift (3 marks reasoning):**

| Property | Before Plasma | After Plasma |
|---|---|---|
| Surface energy | ~31 mJ/m² | 60.61 mJ/m² |
| Water contact angle | 97° (>90°, poor wetting) | 38° (<90°, complete wetting) |
| Polar functional groups | Negligible | High (–OH, –C=O, –COOH) |
| Expected failure mode | **Adhesive (interfacial)** | **Cohesive (within adhesive)** |
| Joint durability | Poor (WBL failure) | High |

> **Conclusion:** The plasma treatment has **doubled the surface energy** and introduced abundant polar groups. The work of adhesion has increased by **104%** (from 63.93 → 130.17 mJ/m²). The epoxy adhesive ($\gamma_l \approx 45$ mJ/m²) will now fully wet the LDPE surface, and the bond strength will be governed by the cohesive strength of the epoxy itself — not by interfacial debonding. The **locus of failure shifts from interfacial to cohesive**, the gold-standard indicator of an optimal adhesive joint.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **"hydrophobic recovery"** in plasma-treated polymers. Derive the first-order kinetic equation governing the decay of surface energy with time and discuss its practical implications for industrial adhesive bonding lines. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **CASING (Crosslinking via Activated Species of INert Gases)** mechanism in detail. How does it differ from oxygen plasma activation? Justify why helium and argon plasmas can also enhance adhesion despite being chemically inert. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a):**

**Definition of Hydrophobic Recovery (1.5 marks):**

Hydrophobic recovery (also called "aging" of plasma-treated polymers) is the **gradual loss of surface activation** over time. After plasma treatment, the surface energy $\gamma_s$ slowly decays back toward its original low value (or an intermediate equilibrium value), even when the treated part is stored in clean, dry conditions. The rate depends on polymer type, storage temperature, and treatment intensity.

**Three Driving Mechanisms (1.5 marks):**

1. **Reorientation of polar groups:** Plasma-inserted polar functional groups (–OH, –C=O) are surface-mobile. To minimize interfacial free energy with air, they **rotate inward** into the bulk, exposing non-polar chains to the surface.
2. **Diffusion of Low-Molecular-Weight Oxidized Species (LMWOS):** Plasma scission produces short, oxidized polymer chains that are mobile. These migrate **into the bulk** over time, lowering the surface concentration of polar groups.
3. **Post-plasma oxidation vs. contamination:** If stored in air, the surface may re-contaminate with airborne hydrocarbons, lowering surface energy.

**Derivation of the First-Order Decay Equation (3 marks):**

The rate of decay of surface energy excess above equilibrium is assumed to be **first order**:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\gamma_s(t) - \gamma_s^{\infty}\right] = -\dfrac{1}{\tau}\left[\gamma_s(t) - \gamma_s^{\infty}\right]$$

Let $y(t) = \gamma_s(t) - \gamma_s^{\infty}$. The ODE becomes:

$$\dfrac{dy}{dt} = -\dfrac{y}{\tau}$$

Separate variables:

$$\dfrac{dy}{y} = -\dfrac{dt}{\tau}$$

Integrate from $t = 0$ to $t$:

$$\int_{y_0}^{y(t)} \dfrac{dy}{y} = -\int_0^t \dfrac{dt}{\tau}$$

$$\ln y(t) - \ln y_0 = -\dfrac{t}{\tau}$$

$$y(t) = y_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{\gamma_s(t) - \gamma_s^{\infty} = \left(\gamma_s^{max} - \gamma_s^{\infty}\right) e^{-t/\tau}}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_s(t) = \gamma_s^{\infty} + \left(\gamma_s^{max} - \gamma_s^{\infty}\right) e^{-t/\tau}}$$

Where:
- $\gamma_s^{max}$ = surface energy immediately post-plasma
- $\gamma_s^{\infty}$ = long-term equilibrium surface energy
- $\tau$ = time constant (typically 50–500 hours depending on polymer)
- $t$ = elapsed time since treatment

**Practical Industrial Implications (1 mark):**

> **Bond immediately after plasma treatment.**
> - Industrial adhesive bonding lines integrate **in-line plasma treatment stations** immediately upstream of the adhesive applicator.
> - Typical allowable delay: **< 24 hours** for polyolefins; **< 1 hour** for highly crystalline polymers like PTFE.
> - For storage > 24 h, parts must be wrapped in clean aluminum foil and stored in climate-controlled environments — but re-treatment is often preferred.
> - The shorter the **$\tau$**, the more urgent the bonding schedule; $\tau$ decreases with higher storage temperature.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b):**

**CASING Mechanism in Detail (3.5 marks):**

CASING (Crosslinking via Activated Species of INert Gases) was discovered by **Schonhorn and Hansen (1967)**. In this process, **chemically inert gases** (He, Ar, Ne) — which do not chemically react with the polymer — are used to generate plasma. Despite being inert, the energetic ions ($He^+$, $Ar^+$) and vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV) photons still interact with the polymer surface.

**Mechanism:**
1. **UV photolysis & ion bombardment** create free radicals on polymer chains (C–H bond scission).
2. Adjacent radical sites on neighboring chains **recombine**, forming new **C–C covalent crosslinks**.
3. This creates a **densely cross-linked, three-dimensional network** restricted to the top ~10–100 nm of the polymer surface.
4. The cross-linked skin is **rigid, less mobile**, and **less permeable** to oxygen and low-molecular-weight species.

**Effect on adhesive bonding:**
- The cross-linked layer has **higher cohesive strength** than the underlying bulk polymer.
- It acts as a **barrier to LMWOS migration** to the interface.
- The surface is "locked" and less prone to hydrophobic recovery — joints show **superior aging resistance**.
- Particularly valuable for **HDPE, PP, and fluoropolymers** where weak boundary layers are chronic failure initiators.

**Comparison with Oxygen Plasma Activation (2 marks):**

| Feature | CASING (He/Ar plasma) | Oxygen Plasma Activation |
|---|---|---|
| **Process gas** | Inert (He, Ar) | Reactive (O₂, air) |
| **Primary mechanism** | Crosslinking via radicals | Chemical functionalization |
| **Functional groups added** | None (C–C bonds only) | –OH, –C=O, –COOH |
| **Change in surface energy** | Small (mostly $\gamma^d$) | Large (mostly $\gamma^p$) |
| **Hydrophobic recovery rate** | Very slow | Faster |
| **Best for** | Joints under thermal/humid aging | Quick wettability boost |

**Why Inert Gases Enhance Adhesion Despite Being Chemically Inert (1.5 marks):**

Although He and Ar are **chemically inert**, their plasma states are **highly energetic**:
1. **Ionic kinetic energy:** $He^+$ ions accelerated through the plasma sheath (~10–100 eV per ion) deliver sufficient momentum to break C–H and C–C bonds on impact.
2. **VUV photons:** Inert gas plasmas emit intense vacuum-ultraviolet radiation (e.g., He resonance line at 58.4 nm = 21.2 eV) that exceeds the bond dissociation energies of most organic bonds (C–H = 4.3 eV, C–C = 3.6 eV), causing **photolytic chain scission**.
3. **Crosslink formation:** The resultant surface radicals combine to form a stable, mechanically robust cross-linked layer — **without** requiring reactive gas chemistry.

> **Bottom line:** The "inertness" of He/Ar refers to **chemical reactions with the polymer**, not the absence of physical energy transfer. The energetic ions and VUV photons transfer sufficient energy to restructure the polymer surface into a tougher, more durable, and more adhesive-friendly layer.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Plasma Treatment Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $\gamma_s$ with $\gamma_l$:** Students often apply Young's equation with the **wrong side** (e.g., using substrate surface energy instead of liquid surface tension). Always verify: $\gamma_l (1+\cos\theta)$ uses **probe liquid** properties on the LHS.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting unit consistency in OWRK:** Mixing mJ/m² with J/m² mid-calculation produces wrong answers by a factor of 1000. Stay in mJ/m² throughout.
> 
> 3. **Neglecting $\gamma_s^d$ assumption in OWRK:** For single-probe-liquid problems, the polar component cannot be uniquely determined without assuming $\gamma_s^d$. Always state this assumption explicitly — examiners mark for it.
> 
> 4. **Mistaking CASING for activation:** Students often write "He plasma increases surface energy dramatically." It does NOT — it increases **cohesive strength** and **durability**. The surface energy gain from CASING alone is modest; its value is in **long-term joint stability**.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring hydrophobic recovery in industrial context:** When asked "why is plasma done in-line?", students describe wettability without mentioning **hydrophobic recovery**. Examiners specifically look for this 1-mark mention.
> 
> 6. **Wrong figure for PTFE surface energy:** PTFE $\gamma_s \approx 18$ mJ/m² — among the lowest of all solids. Many students quote 30–35 mJ/m². Memorize this value.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Plasma Treatments**

- ☐ **Plasma = 4th state of matter** (ionized gas with electrons, ions, neutrals, photons).
- ☐ **Cold plasma** keeps substrate at ~300 K while electrons reach 10,000–50,000 K — ideal for polymers.
- ☐ **Four mechanisms:** Cleaning (ablation), Activation (polar groups), CASING (crosslinking), Plasma Polymerization (primer).
- ☐ **Surface energy increases** dramatically after plasma: PE 31 → 50–70 mJ/m²; PP 30 → 50–70 mJ/m²; PTFE 18 → 45–55 mJ/m².
- ☐ **Contact angle decreases** after plasma (e.g., PE from 97° → 38°) — direct measure of improved wettability.
- ☐ **OWRK equation** is the gold standard to determine $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$ from contact angles of at least 2 probe liquids.
- ☐ **Work of adhesion** $W_a = \gamma_l (1 + \cos\theta)$ doubles or triples after plasma treatment.
- ☐ **Failure mode shifts** from **adhesive (interfacial)** to **cohesive (within adhesive)** after plasma — the indicator of a strong joint.
- ☐ **Atmospheric Pressure Plasma (APP)** types: Corona, DBD, Plasma Jet — for continuous in-line processing.
- ☐ **Low Pressure Plasma (LPP)** types: RF (13.56 MHz), Microwave — for batch processing and aggressive treatments (PTFE).
- ☐ **CASING (He/Ar plasma)** = crosslinking without chemical functionalization; improves durability and aging resistance.
- ☐ **Plasma polymerization** with HMDSO, acrylic acid, allylamine = thin (10–100 nm) primer layers for bonding difficult substrates.
- ☐ **Hydrophobic recovery** = first-order decay $\gamma_s(t) = \gamma_s^{\infty} + (\gamma_s^{max} - \gamma_s^{\infty}) e^{-t/\tau}$ — bond within 24 h.
- ☐ **Bonding rule of thumb:** $\gamma_s \geq \gamma_l + 10$ mJ/m² safety margin.
- ☐ **Process gases:** O₂ → polar groups; Ar/He → crosslinking; N₂ → amine groups; CF₄ → hydrophobic surface (release).
- ☐ **PTFE, silicone, and fluoropolymers** require LPP plasma (RF, 60–300 s) or APP plasma jet; corona/DBD alone is insufficient.
- ☐ **Critical constant:** Atmospheric plasma power supply operates at **13.56 MHz** (ISM band) to avoid radio interference.
- ☐ **Debye length** $\lambda_D$ characterizes the plasma sheath — the active zone of surface modification.
- ☐ **Paschen's Law** $V_b = f(pd)$ governs plasma ignition voltage — minimum at $(pd)_{min}$ depends on gas.
- ☐ **Industrial advantage:** Plasma is **dry, fast (seconds), solvent-free, and continuous** — replacing hazardous wet-chemistry primers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods for Evaluating the Effectiveness of Surface Preparation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Methods for Evaluating the Effectiveness of Surface Preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Preparation Evaluation** is the systematic, qualitative and quantitative assessment of physico-chemical modifications applied to an adherend surface prior to adhesive bonding. The objective is to verify that the surface possesses the requisite **surface free energy**, **chemical functionality**, **topographical features**, and **absence of contaminants** to promote thermodynamic wetting, mechanical interlocking, and durable interfacial adhesion.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT861)**, the topic falls under **Module 2 – Surface Preparation of Adherends**, and the effectiveness is gauged by the **change in surface properties** relative to the as-received (untreated) baseline. Effective surface preparation is reflected by:

- An **increase in polar functional groups** (verified by XPS/FTIR).
- A **decrease in contact angle** (verified by goniometry).
- An **increase in surface roughness** within an optimum window (verified by profilometry/AFM).
- **Reduction in organic contaminants** (verified by XPS, FTIR, or simple water-break test).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Double-Sided Tape on a Dusty Wall**
> Imagine trying to stick a poster onto a freshly painted wall that has just a thin layer of dust. The poster falls off almost immediately because the **dust layer acts as a weak boundary**, preventing intimate molecular contact between the tape adhesive and the wall surface. Similarly, in adhesive bonding, the "dust" can be **oxidation layers, oils, release agents, adsorbed moisture, or weak boundary layers**. Surface preparation evaluation asks the question: *"How clean, how reactive, and how rough is the surface right now?"* The answer determines whether the adhesive will form a **durable bond** or a **premature failure**.

A clean, high-surface-energy substrate will allow the liquid adhesive droplet to **spread spontaneously** (low contact angle), maximizing the contact area and enabling the formation of primary (chemical) and secondary (van der Waals, hydrogen) bonds across the interface.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| **Metric** | **Symbol** | **Typical Range / Target** |
|---|---|---|
| Contact Angle | $\theta$ | **0° < θ < 90°** (wetting) ; ideally < 30° |
| Surface Free Energy | $\gamma_s$ | **20 – 70 mJ/m²** |
| Polar Component | $\gamma_s^p$ | Increases after treatment |
| Dispersive Component | $\gamma_s^d$ | Relatively stable |
| Arithmetic Roughness | $R_a$ | **0.2 – 5.0 µm** (optimum for bonding) |
| Critical Surface Tension | $\gamma_c$ | Should exceed adhesive's $\gamma_{lv}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Single Most Important Heuristic** in KTU-style problems: **"A properly prepared surface will exhibit a contact angle less than 30° when tested with distilled water, and a dyne level ≥ 56 dynes/cm."**

### 1.4 Classification of Evaluation Methods

The methods are broadly classified into **three families**:

1. **Wettability-based methods** — Contact angle, dyne test, water-break test, capillary rise.
2. **Chemical/Compositional methods** — XPS (ESCA), Auger Electron Spectroscopy (AES), FTIR, ATR-FTIR, EDX.
3. **Topographical/Physical methods** — Stylus profilometry, Optical profilometry, SEM, AFM, optical microscopy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Surface preparation evaluation rests on **three pillars**:

#### **Pillar 1 — Thermodynamic Wettability (Adhesion Science Foundation)**

The driving force for an adhesive to spread on a substrate is governed by **Young's Equation (1805)**, which balances the three interfacial tensions at the three-phase contact line:

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$

where:
- $\gamma_{sv}$ = solid–vapor interfacial tension (≈ surface free energy of solid $\gamma_s$)
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solid–liquid interfacial tension
- $\gamma_{lv}$ = liquid–vapor interfacial tension (surface tension of liquid)
- $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle (Young's angle)

For complete spontaneous wetting, **θ → 0°** is desired. Surface preparation must therefore **maximize $\gamma_{sv}$** and **minimize $\gamma_{sl}$**.

#### **Pillar 2 — Work of Adhesion (Dupré Equation)**

The thermodynamic work of adhesion per unit area is given by **Dupré (1869)**:

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

Combining with Young's Equation yields the **Young–Dupré Equation**:

$$W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** The work of adhesion quantifies the energy required to separate the adhesive from the substrate in a reversible (thermodynamic) sense. A *larger* $W_a$ means *stronger intrinsic adhesion* — exactly what surface preparation aims to enhance.

#### **Pillar 3 — Surface Energy Decomposition (Fowkes / Owens–Wendt)**

Real surfaces exhibit both **dispersive (London) interactions** and **polar (acid–base, hydrogen bonding, dipole–dipole) interactions**. The **Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble (OWRK) Method (1969)** expresses the interfacial tension as:

$$\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\right)$$

Combined with Young's Equation, this yields:

$$\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\right)$$

By measuring contact angles with **at least two liquids of known $\gamma_l^d$ and $\gamma_l^p$** (typically water + diiodomethane), $\gamma_{sv}^d$ and $\gamma_{sv}^p$ can be solved simultaneously.

#### **Pillar 4 — Critical Surface Tension of Wetting (Zisman, 1964)**

For a homologous series of liquids, $\cos\theta$ plotted against $\gamma_{lv}$ is approximately linear. The extrapolation to **$\cos\theta = 1$** (i.e., $\theta = 0°$) defines the **critical surface tension $\gamma_c$**:

$$\cos\theta = 1 - b(\gamma_c - \gamma_{lv})$$

For wetting, the adhesive's $\gamma_{lv}$ must **not exceed** the substrate's $\gamma_c$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Each Method

#### **Method A — Sessile Drop Goniometry (ASTM D7334, D5946)**

1. Place a calibrated droplet (3–5 µL) of test liquid on the prepared surface.
2. Image the droplet profile from the side using a CCD camera.
3. Fit the profile to the **Young–Laplace equation** to extract $\theta$.
4. Repeat at multiple locations to ensure statistical validity (n ≥ 5).
5. Compare $\theta_{\text{prepared}}$ with $\theta_{\text{untreated}}$ — a reduction confirms effectiveness.

#### **Method B — Wilhelmy Plate Method (ASTM D3825)**

1. Suspend a thin plate of the substrate vertically from a microbalance.
2. Immerse it into a liquid of known $\gamma_{lv}$ at a controlled velocity.
3. Record the force experienced:

$$F = \gamma_{lv} L \cos\theta - m g + \rho g L t x$$

   where $L$ is the perimeter, $t$ the plate thickness, $x$ the immersion depth.
4. At $x = 0$ (zero immersion), the equilibrium is:

$$F = \gamma_{lv} L \cos\theta$$

5. Solve for $\theta$. The advantage is **dynamic contact angle** measurement (advancing and receding).

#### **Method C — Capillary Rise Method**

For porous substrates (textiles, papers, porous ceramics), the **Washburn equation** governs liquid penetration:

$$h^2 = \frac{r \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta}{2\eta} t$$

where $r$ = effective capillary radius, $\eta$ = liquid viscosity, $t$ = time.

A plot of $h^2$ vs. $t$ yields the slope from which $\cos\theta$ (and thus $\theta$) is extracted.

#### **Method D — Dyne Test (ASTM D2578)**

1. Apply a dyne ink (calibrated surface tension solution) to the surface using a cotton swab.
2. Observe the behavior for 2–4 seconds.
3. **Result interpretation**:
   - Continuous film for 2 s → surface energy ≥ that dyne level.
   - Beading up or retraction → surface energy < that dyne level.
4. Bracketing with successive dyne levels (typically 30, 36, 38, 40, 44, 56, 60 dynes/cm) determines the dyne level precisely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dyne test is a **rapid, qualitative field test** — invaluable in industrial QA but lacks the precision of goniometry. KTU often asks this in **Part A (3 marks)**.

#### **Method E — Water-Break Test (Qualitative)**

Dip the prepared part into clean water and observe:
- **Continuous unbroken water film** → surface is clean and hydrophilic (PASS).
- **Film retracts into droplets** → contaminated or low-energy surface (FAIL).

This test is extremely **simple and inexpensive**, suitable for field inspection of large components (e.g., aircraft skins before priming).

#### **Method F — X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS / ESCA)**

1. Irradiate the surface with monochromatic X-rays (typically Al K$\alpha$ at **1486.6 eV**).
2. Measure the kinetic energy of ejected photoelectrons:

$$E_{binding} = h\nu - E_{kinetic} - \phi_{spectrometer}$$

3. The binding energy identifies the element and its chemical state.
4. Quantify **C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Si 2p** peaks to compute **O/C atomic ratio** — a powerful metric of surface oxidation/cleaning effectiveness.

> **Industry Rule-of-Thumb:** An effective surface treatment yields **O/C > 0.3** for polymers, **O/C > 0.5** for metals.

#### **Method G — Attenuated Total Reflectance FTIR (ATR-FTIR)**

1. Place the sample in optical contact with a high-refractive-index crystal (Ge, ZnSe, Diamond).
2. The IR beam undergoes multiple internal reflections, sampling a depth of **0.5 – 2.0 µm**.
3. Functional group analysis:
   - **C=O stretch (~1700 cm⁻¹)** → carboxyl, carbonyl groups.
   - **O–H stretch (3200–3600 cm⁻¹)** → hydroxyl groups.
   - **C–H stretch (2850–2960 cm⁻¹)** → aliphatic contaminants (a *negative* indicator after cleaning).

#### **Method H — Stylus Profilometry (ISO 4287, 4288)**

1. Drag a diamond stylus (tip radius **2 µm or 10 µm**) across the surface at constant speed.
2. Record vertical displacement vs. lateral position.
3. Compute roughness parameters:
   - **Arithmetic Mean Roughness**: $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\displaystyle\int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$
   - **Root-Mean-Square Roughness**: $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\displaystyle\int_0^L z^2(x) \, dx}$
   - **Ten-Point Mean Roughness**: $R_z = \dfrac{1}{5}\left(\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{5} Z_i^{peak} + \sum_{i=1}^{5} Z_i^{valley}\right)$

> [!NOTE]
> For adhesive bonding, an $R_a$ of **1.5 – 3.0 µm** is typically optimal — too smooth provides poor mechanical interlocking, too rough traps air pockets.

#### **Method I — Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)**

1. Coat sample with a thin conductive layer (Au, Pt, or C).
2. Scan with focused electron beam (1–30 keV).
3. Detect **secondary electrons (SE)** for topography, **back-scattered electrons (BSE)** for compositional contrast.
4. Magnification up to **100,000×** reveals crack patterns, pits, residues, and ablation textures from laser/abrasive treatment.

#### **Method J — Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)**

1. Scan a sharp tip (radius **~10 nm**) across the surface using a cantilever.
2. Maintain constant force (contact mode) or constant oscillation amplitude (tapping mode).
3. Generate 3D topographical maps at **sub-nanometer vertical resolution**.
4. Compute $R_a$, $R_q$, and **skewness** ($R_{sk}$) — skewness differentiates peaks vs. valleys dominant morphologies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical** — memorize for Part A and Part B derivations.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Application / Method** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | Young's Equation — contact angle | mJ/m² |
| 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | Dupré Work of Adhesion | mJ/m² |
| 3 | $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Young–Dupré Equation | mJ/m² |
| 4 | $\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\right)$ | Owens–Wendt OWRK | mJ/m² |
| 5 | $\cos\theta = 1 - b(\gamma_c - \gamma_{lv})$ | Zisman Critical Surface Tension | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F = \gamma_{lv} L \cos\theta$ | Wilhelmy Plate Force | N |
| 7 | $h^2 = \dfrac{r \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta}{2\eta} t$ | Washburn Capillary Rise | m² |
| 8 | $E_{binding} = h\nu - E_{kinetic} - \phi$ | XPS Binding Energy | eV |
| 9 | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | Arithmetic Roughness | µm |
| 10 | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{L}\int_0^L z^2(x) \, dx}$ | RMS Roughness | µm |

> **Units Convention:** $1 \, \text{mJ/m}^2 = 1 \, \text{dyne/cm}$. Many older textbooks (and some industrial standards) still use **dynes/cm** — be alert during problem statements.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry Sector** | **Critical Evaluation Method** | **Why It Matters** |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace (composite bonding) | XPS, Contact angle, Lap shear | Failure = catastrophic; FAA mandates tight QA |
| Automotive (structural adhesives) | Dyne test, FTIR | High-throughput production lines |
| Electronics (PCB conformal coating) | Contact angle, Dyne | Adhesion of solder masks, encapsulants |
| Medical devices (catheter bonding) | Contact angle, XPS | Biocompatibility requires clean surfaces |
| Marine (anti-fouling coatings) | Contact angle, Profilometry | Wetted surface life depends on adhesion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation (Force Balance Approach)

Young's Equation can be rigorously derived by minimizing the **total surface free energy** of a sessile droplet at constant volume.

**Step 1 — Define the total interfacial energy $G$:**

$$G = \gamma_{sl} A_{sl} + \gamma_{sv} A_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} A_{lv}$$

where $A_{sl}$, $A_{sv}$, $A_{lv}$ are the corresponding interfacial areas.

**Step 2 — Apply a small virtual displacement $dx$** along the three-phase contact line at constant droplet volume.

$$\frac{dG}{dx} = 0 \quad \text{(equilibrium condition)}$$

The geometric relationships yield:

$$\frac{dA_{sl}}{dx} = -\cos\phi, \quad \frac{dA_{sv}}{dx} = +\cos\phi - \cos\psi, \quad \frac{dA_{lv}}{dx} = 0$$

where $\phi$ and $\psi$ are the angles the solid surface makes at the contact line.

**Step 3 — Substituting and setting $dG/dx = 0$:**

$$-\gamma_{sl} \cos\phi + \gamma_{sv}(\cos\phi - \cos\psi) = 0$$

$$\gamma_{sv} \cos\psi = \gamma_{sl} \cos\phi - \gamma_{sv}\cos\phi + \gamma_{sv}\cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — At thermodynamic equilibrium, the contact angle** $\theta = \psi = \phi$ **(Young's angle)**. Simplifying:

$$\gamma_{sv} \cos\theta = \gamma_{sl} \cos\theta - \gamma_{sv}\cos\theta + \gamma_{sv}\cos\theta$$

which reduces to:

$$\boxed{\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}$$

This is **Young's Equation** — the foundational relation of wettability science.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Equation

**Step 1 — Recall the Dupré Equation:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$$

This expresses the work of adhesion as the energy gained when an adhesive–substrate interface forms from two separate surfaces.

**Step 2 — Substitute** $\gamma_{sl}$ from Young's Equation:

$$\gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Plug into Dupré:**

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta)$$

$$W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\gamma_{sv}$:**

$$\boxed{W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)}$$

This is the **Young–Dupré Equation**. It explicitly shows that as $\theta \to 0°$, $W_a \to 2\gamma_{lv}$ (maximum work of adhesion).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Owens–Wendt Two-Liquid Method

> **Problem:** A metal adherend has been solvent wiped and grit-blasted. Contact angle measurements were taken with two probe liquids:

| **Liquid** | $\gamma_{lv}$ (mJ/m²) | $\gamma_{lv}^{d}$ (mJ/m²) | $\gamma_{lv}^{p}$ (mJ/m²) | **Measured** $\theta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water | 72.8 | 21.8 | 51.0 | 35° |
| Diiodomethane | 50.8 | 50.8 | 0 | 28° |

**Find:** Total surface free energy $\gamma_{sv}$ and its dispersive and polar components.

---

**Solution — Step 1: Apply the OWRK equation** for each liquid:

For **Water** ($\theta = 35°$):

$$\gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}\gamma_{lv}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}\gamma_{lv}^{p}}\right)$$

$$72.8(1 + \cos 35°) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}(21.8)} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}(51.0)}\right)$$

$$72.8(1 + 0.8192) = 2\left(\sqrt{21.8\gamma_{sv}^{d}} + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p}}\right)$$

$$72.8 \times 1.8192 = 132.438$$

**Equation (W):**

$$\sqrt{21.8\gamma_{sv}^{d}} + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p}} = 66.219$$

---

For **Diiodomethane** ($\theta = 28°$):

$$50.8(1 + \cos 28°) = 2\left(\sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{d}(50.8)} + \sqrt{\gamma_{sv}^{p}(0)}\right)$$

$$50.8(1 + 0.8829) = 2\sqrt{50.8\gamma_{sv}^{d}}$$

$$50.8 \times 1.8829 = 95.654 = 2\sqrt{50.8\gamma_{sv}^{d}}$$

$$\sqrt{50.8\gamma_{sv}^{d}} = 47.827$$

$$50.8\gamma_{sv}^{d} = 2287.42$$

$$\gamma_{sv}^{d} = 45.028 \;\; \text{mJ/m}^2$$

---

**Step 2 — Substitute back into Equation (W)** to find $\gamma_{sv}^{p}$:

$$\sqrt{21.8 \times 45.028} + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p}} = 66.219$$

$$\sqrt{981.61} + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p}} = 66.219$$

$$31.330 + \sqrt{51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p}} = 66.219$$

$$\sqrt{51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p}} = 34.889$$

$$51.0\gamma_{sv}^{p} = 1217.27$$

$$\gamma_{sv}^{p} = 23.868 \;\; \text{mJ/m}^2$$

---

**Step 3 — Total Surface Free Energy:**

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sv}^{d} + \gamma_{sv}^{p} = 45.028 + 23.868$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{sv} \approx 68.90 \;\; \text{mJ/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** This $\gamma_{sv}$ value is significantly higher than untreated aluminum (~33 mJ/m²), confirming **highly effective surface preparation**. Most structural epoxy adhesives ($\gamma_{lv} \approx 40$ mJ/m²) will readily wet this surface.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| **Decision Criterion** | **Sessile Drop** | **Wilhelmy** | **Dyne Test** | **XPS** | **Profilometry** | **FTIR** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quantitative | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ (semi) | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ |
| Qualitative | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Speed | Med | Med | **Fast** | Slow | Med | Med |
| Cost | Med | Med | **Low** | **High** | Med | Med |
| Sample prep needed | None | Coating | None | None | None | None |
| Detects chemistry | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ |
| Detects topography | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✗ |
| Detects wetting | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Best for field QA | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

**Legend:** ✓ = suitable ; ✓✓ = good ; ✓✓✓ = excellent ; ✗ = not suitable

---

### 3.5 Worked Example — Zisman Plot Construction

> **Problem:** Contact angles of n-alkane liquids on a PTFE surface were measured:

| **Liquid** | $\gamma_{lv}$ (mJ/m²) | $\theta$ |
|---|---|---|
| Hexane | 18.4 | 12° |
| Octane | 21.8 | 24° |
| Decane | 23.9 | 35° |
| Dodecane | 25.4 | 46° |
| Tetradecane | 26.7 | 57° |

**Find:** Critical surface tension $\gamma_c$.

**Solution:**

| $\gamma_{lv}$ (mJ/m²) | $\cos\theta$ |
|---|---|
| 18.4 | 0.9781 |
| 21.8 | 0.9135 |
| 23.9 | 0.8192 |
| 25.4 | 0.6947 |
| 26.7 | 0.5446 |

Linear regression of $\cos\theta$ vs. $\gamma_{lv}$ yields:

$$\cos\theta = m \cdot \gamma_{lv} + c$$

With $m = -0.0415$, $c = 1.7419$, set $\cos\theta = 1$:

$$1 = -0.0415\gamma_c + 1.7419$$

$$0.0415\gamma_c = 0.7419$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_c \approx 17.88 \;\; \text{mJ/m}^2}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the well-known low surface energy of PTFE. Adhesives with $\gamma_{lv} < 17.88$ mJ/m² will wet spontaneously — a very narrow selection, hence the notorious difficulty of bonding PTFE without special surface treatments (e.g., sodium etching, plasma).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Surface Preparation Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AsReceived Substrate] --> B[Surface Preparation Method]
    B --> B1[Mechanical e.g. Abrasion Grit Blasting]
    B --> B2[Chemical e.g. Solvent Pickling]
    B --> B3[Physical e.g. Plasma Corona Laser]
    B1 --> C[Prepared Surface]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D{Wettability Tests}
    D --> D1[Sessile Drop Goniometry]
    D --> D2[Wilhelmy Plate]
    D --> D3[Dyne Pen Test]
    D --> D4[Water Break Test]
    C --> E{Chemical Tests}
    E --> E1[XPS ESCA]
    E --> E2[ATR FTIR]
    E --> E3[Auger Electron Spectroscopy]
    C --> F{Topographical Tests}
    F --> F1[Stylus Profilometer]
    F --> F2[Optical Profilometer]
    F --> F3[AFM]
    F --> F4[SEM]
    D1 --> G[Quantitative Surface Energy]
    D2 --> G
    E1 --> H[Surface Chemistry Profile]
    E2 --> H
    F1 --> I[Roughness Profile Ra Rq]
    F3 --> I
    G --> J{Acceptance Criteria}
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> J1[Theta less than 30 degrees AND gamma sv greater than 40 mJ per sq m AND O to C ratio greater than 0.3]
    J --> J2[Approved for Bonding]
    J --> J3[Rejected for Re Preparation]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Contact Angle Interpretation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Contact Angle Measurement] --> B0{Angle Theta}
    B0 --> C1[Theta between 0 and 30 degrees]
    B0 --> C2[Theta between 30 and 90 degrees]
    B0 --> C3[Theta between 90 and 150 degrees]
    B0 --> C4[Theta greater than 150 degrees]
    C1 --> D1[Complete Wetting Excellent Adhesion]
    C2 --> D2[Partial Wetting Acceptable for Bonding]
    C3 --> D3[Poor Wetting Surface Treatment Required]
    C4 --> D4[Superhydrophobic Severe Contamination or Low Energy Material]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — XPS Photoelectric Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[X ray Source Al K alpha 1486.6 eV] --> B1[Photon h nu]
    B1 --> C1[Surface Atom Electron]
    C1 --> D1[Photoelectron ejected with kinetic energy E k]
    D1 --> E1[Electron Analyzer]
    E1 --> F1[Binding Energy Computation]
    F1 --> G1[Elemental Identification C O N Si]
    G1 --> H1[Chemical State Oxidation Functional Group]
    H1 --> I1[O to C Atomic Ratio]
    I1 --> J1[Effectiveness Verdict]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Owens–Wendt Solution Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start] --> S2[Measure theta with two liquids]
    S2 --> S3[Liquid 1 typically water gamma lv 72.8]
    S2 --> S4[Liquid 2 typically diiodomethane gamma lv 50.8]
    S3 --> S5[Compute gamma lv times 1 plus cos theta]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Set up two simultaneous OWRK equations]
    S6 --> S7[Solve for gamma sv to the d]
    S7 --> S8[Solve for gamma sv to the p]
    S8 --> S9[Sum to obtain gamma sv total]
    S9 --> S10[Compare against acceptance threshold]
    S10 --> S11[Report wettability verdict]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Schematic — Method Selection Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[What do you want to measure] --> K2{Property}
    K2 --> K3[Surface Energy]
    K2 --> K4[Surface Chemistry]
    K2 --> K5[Surface Roughness]
    K2 --> K6[Quick Field Check]
    K3 --> K31[Sessile Drop or Wilhelmy]
    K4 --> K41[XPS for elements or FTIR for functional groups]
    K5 --> K51[Stylus or Optical Profilometer]

    K51 --> K7

```
