# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Electrostatic Attractive Pressure from First Principles

This derivation is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**. We derive the expression for electrostatic adhesive force from fundamental capacitor electrostatics.

### Given / Assumptions

1. Two parallel planar surfaces of area $A$, separated by gap $d$.
2. Uniform surface charge density $+\sigma$ on the donor side and $-\sigma$ on the acceptor side.
3. The gap is filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.
4. Edge effects are neglected (infinite plate approximation).

### Step 1 — Electric Field Between the Plates

For two infinite oppositely charged planes, each plane produces a field of magnitude $\sigma / (2 \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0)$ in the gap. Because both fields point in the **same direction** between the plates, the total field is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} + \frac{\sigma}{2\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{total}} = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}}$$

**Logic:** This is the standard textbook result for a parallel-plate capacitor. The factor of 2 cancels because both charged planes contribute equally.

### Step 2 — Voltage Across the Gap (Contact Potential Difference)

The voltage across the capacitor is the integral of $E$ over the gap distance:

$$\Delta V = \int_0^d E_{\text{total}}\, dx = E_{\text{total}} \cdot d$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{\sigma \, d}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

**Logic:** This voltage $\Delta V$ is the **contact potential difference** that arises from equilibration of Fermi levels. We will later relate it to the work function difference.

### Step 3 — Energy Stored in the Capacitor (per unit area)

The electrostatic energy stored per unit volume in an electric field is $\tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E^2$. Integrating across the gap $d$ gives the energy per unit area:

$$\frac{U}{A} = \int_0^d \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\, E_{\text{total}}^2\, dx = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\, E_{\text{total}}^2 \cdot d$$

Substituting $E_{\text{total}} = \sigma/(\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0)$:

$$\frac{U}{A} = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 \cdot \left(\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}\right)^{2} \cdot d = \frac{\sigma^2\, d}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{U}{A} = \frac{\sigma^2\, d}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}}$$

**Logic:** This is the energy required to pull the plates apart from distance $d$ to infinity. For adhesion, we are interested in the energy required to separate the interface, which is precisely this stored electrostatic energy.

### Step 4 — Relating Energy to Force (Virtual Work Principle)

The attractive force is the negative derivative of energy with respect to separation:

$$F = -\frac{dU}{dd} = -\frac{d}{dd}\left[\frac{\sigma^2\, A\, d}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}\right]$$

$$F = -\frac{\sigma^2\, A}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

The negative sign indicates **attractive** (pulling the surfaces together). Taking the magnitude:

$$\boxed{F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\sigma^2\, A}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}}$$

### Step 5 — Final Form in Terms of Contact Potential Difference

Using $\sigma = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\, \Delta V}{d}$ from Step 2:

$$F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{A}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\, \Delta V}{d}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2\, A}{2\, d^{2}}}$$

**Logic:** This is the central result of the Electrostatic Theory. The force is proportional to the **square** of the contact potential difference and **inversely proportional** to the square of the gap.

### Step 6 — Bond Strength per Unit Area (Pressure Form)

Dividing by the contact area $A$:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2}{2\, d^{2}}}$$

### Step 7 — Work of Separation (Energy of Adhesion)

The thermodynamic work of separation per unit area is found by integrating the pressure from $d$ to $\infty$:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \int_d^{\infty} P_{\text{elec}}(x)\, dx$$

For a separation process where charge remains on the surfaces until discharge (constant-charge mode):

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} \cdot d$$

Substituting $\sigma = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,\Delta V / d$:

$$\boxed{W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2}{2\, d}}$$

**Logic:** $W_{\text{elec}}$ has units of J/m² and is the quantity that must equal the measured thermodynamic work of adhesion for the Electrostatic Theory to be considered validated.

## 3.2 Numerical Illustration (KTU-Style Worked Example)

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example:** A polymer adhesive ($\phi_1 = 4.0$ eV) is bonded to an aluminum substrate ($\phi_2 = 4.2$ eV). The interfacial gap $d = 0.5$ nm and the interface medium has $\varepsilon_r = 4.0$. Calculate: (a) the contact potential difference, (b) the electrostatic attractive pressure, and (c) the work of separation per unit area.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Work function difference:
$$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1 = 4.2 - 4.0 = 0.2 \text{ eV}$$

Converting eV to Joules: $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$.

$$\Delta\phi = 0.2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.204 \times 10^{-20}\,\text{J}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{\Delta\phi}{e} = \frac{3.204 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.2\,\text{V}$$

**(b)** Electrostatic pressure (using $P = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 (\Delta V)^2 / (2 d^2)$):
$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{(4.0)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.2)^2}{2\,(0.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2}}$$

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{(4.0)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.04)}{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1.4166 \times 10^{-12}}{5.0 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{elec}} \approx 2.83 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \approx 2.83\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(c)** Work of separation per unit area:
$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2}{2\,d}$$

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{(4.0)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.04)}{2 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1.4166 \times 10^{-12}}{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{elec}} \approx 1.42 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{J/m}^{2}}$$

**Interpretation:** This theoretical adhesion energy of ~$1.4\,\text{mJ/m}^2$ is much smaller than typical measured polymer–metal adhesion energies (10–1000 mJ/m²), which shows that **electrostatic forces alone are insufficient** to explain total adhesion — they contribute a fraction, and other mechanisms (van der Waals, chemical bonding, mechanical interlocking) play the dominant role.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Critical Assumptions vs. Reality

| # | Assumption of Electronic Theory | Real-World Behavior | Implication |
|---|----------------------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Both materials are conductors | Adhesives are often insulators | Theory must be modified |
| 2 | Intimate contact at molecular scale | Surface roughness exists | Effective area < nominal area |
| 3 | Double layer is uniform | Charge distribution may be patchy | Local force variations |
| 4 | Separation is slow (charge equilibration) | Fast separation creates micro-discharges | Observed light emission & noise |
| 5 | One interface dominates | Multiple mechanisms contribute | Theory explains only part of total adhesion |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PEPOT861]

**Q: State the Electrostatic (Electronic) Theory of adhesion. Who proposed it and what is the fundamental basis of the theory?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Electrostatic Theory of adhesion was proposed by **B.V. Derjaguin (1948)**. The fundamental basis is that when two materials of differing electronic work functions are brought into intimate contact, **electron transfer occurs** from the material of lower work function to the material of higher work function until the Fermi levels equilibrate. This creates an **electrical double layer** at the interface, and the resulting **Coulombic (electrostatic) attractive force** contributes to the adhesive bond strength. The theory is most applicable to dissimilar material pairs such as polymer–metal joints.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark – proposer & year; 1 mark – mechanism; 1 mark – applicability.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Question Bank]

**Q: Define work function and contact potential difference. How are they related in the context of the Electronic Theory of adhesion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Work function ($\phi$):** The minimum thermodynamic energy (in electron-volts) required to remove an electron from the surface of a material to a point in the vacuum immediately outside the surface.

- **Contact potential difference ($\Delta V$):** The potential difference that develops between two dissimilar materials when brought into electronic equilibrium.

- **Relation:** $\Delta V = (\phi_2 - \phi_1) / e$, where $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ are the work functions of the adhesive and adherend respectively, and $e$ is the elementary charge ($1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C). A larger difference in work functions leads to greater electron transfer and hence a stronger electrostatic adhesive force.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark each for the two definitions; 1 mark for the relationship.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q (A): (a)** Explain in detail the Electrostatic Theory of adhesion with a neat sketch of the electrical double layer. Derive an expression for the electrostatic force of adhesion between a polymer adhesive and a metal adherend. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the experimental evidence supporting the Electronic Theory and state its limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A) — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Theory Outline (2 marks):**
The Electronic Theory, proposed by **Derjaguin (1948)**, states that when a polymer adhesive and a metal adherend are brought into molecular contact (gap $d \le 1$ nm), electrons transfer from the material of lower work function to the material of higher work function until the Fermi levels equalize. This results in a **positive surface charge** on the donor side and a **negative surface charge** on the acceptor side, forming an **electrical double layer** analogous to a charged parallel-plate capacitor.

**Step 2 — Sketch Description (2 marks):**
The student must draw two parallel planes — one labeled "Polymer (donor)" with $+\sigma$ and the other labeled "Metal (acceptor)" with $-\sigma$, separated by gap $d$, with electric field lines pointing from positive to negative. Symbols $\sigma$, $d$, $\varepsilon_r$, $\Delta V$ must be labeled.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Electrostatic Force (3 marks):**
*(This is the same derivation shown in Section 3.1. The student must show:)*

(i) Electric field between the plates:
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

(ii) Voltage across gap:
$$\Delta V = \frac{\sigma \, d}{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

(iii) Electrostatic force per unit area (pressure):
$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2}{2 d^2}$$

(iv) Total electrostatic force:
$$\boxed{F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2\, A}{2 d^2}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q(A) — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Experimental Evidence (3.5 marks):**

1. **Direct Force Measurement:** Skinner, Savage, and Rutzler (1953) measured the force required to peel polymer films from metal substrates and found it consistent with electrostatic predictions.

2. **Capacitor Behavior:** Adhesive joints exhibit measurable **capacitance** at the interface, confirming the double-layer structure.

3. **Discharge Phenomena:** During rapid separation of adhesive joints, **micro-discharges**, **light emission (electroluminescence)**, and **electromagnetic noise** have been detected, indicating charge neutralization at separation.

4. **Work Function Correlation:** Measured peel strengths of polymer–metal systems correlate well with the difference in work functions.

5. **Charge Decay Studies:** Surface potential measurements after separation reveal trapped charges of the predicted sign and magnitude.

**Limitations of the Theory (3.5 marks):**

1. **Cannot explain adhesion between identical materials** (e.g., polymer–polymer), where $\Delta V = 0$.
2. **Requires at least one conductor** — does not apply to insulator–insulator joints.
3. **Magnitude mismatch:** Calculated electrostatic forces are often **1–2 orders of magnitude smaller** than measured adhesion forces.
4. **Neglects other mechanisms** (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, mechanical interlocking, diffusion) which dominate in many systems.
5. **Assumes ideal contact**, ignoring surface roughness, contamination, and adsorbed layers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q (B): (a)** Derive the expression for the work of adhesion based on the Electronic Theory. A polymer-metal joint has an interfacial gap of 0.4 nm and a contact potential difference of 0.5 V. The interface medium has a dielectric constant of 3.5. Calculate the electrostatic pressure and the work of separation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Electronic Theory with the Adsorption (Wetting) Theory of adhesion, highlighting their similarities and differences. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B) — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Derivation of Work of Adhesion (3 marks):**

From the Electronic Theory, the attractive pressure between two charged plates separated by distance $d$ is:

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2}{2 d^2}$$

The work of adhesion per unit area is the integral of this pressure from $d$ to $\infty$ under constant-charge conditions:

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \int_d^{\infty} P(x)\, dx = \frac{\sigma^2 \cdot d}{2\,\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}$$

Substituting $\sigma = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\, \Delta V / d$:

$$\boxed{W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0\,(\Delta V)^2}{2 d}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 0.4\,\text{nm} = 0.4 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m}$; $\Delta V = 0.5\,\text{V}$; $\varepsilon_r = 3.5$; $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$.

**Electrostatic pressure:**

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{(3.5)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.5)^2}{2\,(0.4 \times 10^{-9})^2}$$

Numerator:
$$(3.5)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.25) = 7.747 \times 10^{-12}$$

Denominator:
$$2 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 3.2 \times 10^{-19}$$

$$P_{\text{elec}} = \frac{7.747 \times 10^{-12}}{3.2 \times 10^{-19}} = 2.42 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} \approx 24.2\,\text{MPa}$$

**Work of separation per unit area:**

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{(3.5)(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(0.5)^2}{2 \times (0.4 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$W_{\text{elec}} = \frac{7.747 \times 10^{-12}}{8.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 9.68 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{J/m}^2 \approx 9.68\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

*(Mark Distribution: 3 marks derivation; 2 marks pressure calculation; 1 mark work calculation; 0.5 marks units.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q(B) — Part (b) (7 marks)

| Comparison Parameter | Adsorption (Wetting) Theory | Electronic Theory |
|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Proposer** | McBain, Wake (1920s) | Derjaguin (1948) |
| **Primary Driving Force** | van der Waals (London dispersion) forces | Electrostatic (Coulombic) forces |
| **Range of Action** | Short range (~0.2–1 nm) | Short range (~0.3–1 nm) |
| **Material Requirement** | Universal — works for all material pairs | Works best when one material is a conductor |
| **Role of Contact** | Molecular contact for full surface coverage | Molecular contact for charge transfer |
| **Role of Wetting** | Critical — affects contact area | Indirect — affects gap distance $d$ |
| **Predicts Adhesion Between Similar Materials?** | Yes | No (only dissimilar materials) |
| **Quantitative Expression** | $W = 2(\gamma_1^d \gamma_2^d)^{1/2} + 2(\gamma_1^p \gamma_2^p)^{1/2}$ | $W_{\text{elec}} = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 (\Delta V)^2 / (2 d)$ |
| **Dominant in which systems?** | Polymer–polymer, polymer–ceramic, polymer–metal | Polymer–metal, metal–ceramic |

**Similarities (2 marks):**
- Both require **intimate molecular contact** (small $d$).
- Both operate at the **nanometer scale**.
- Both are **surface-energy based** in their thermodynamic treatment.
- Both are **short-range** theories.
- Both predict that **surface preparation** improves adhesion.

**Differences (2 marks):**
- **Origin of force:** Adsorption → van der Waals; Electronic → Coulombic.
- **Material specificity:** Adsorption is universal; Electronic needs dissimilar Fermi levels.
- **Wetting dependence:** Adsorption directly requires good wetting; Electronic only indirectly depends on $d$.
- **Magnitude:** Adsorption contributes typically 100–1000 mJ/m²; Electronic contributes typically 1–10 mJ/m².
- **Experimental verification:** Adsorption verified by contact-angle measurements; Electronic verified by discharge phenomena.

*(Mark Distribution: 3 marks table; 2 marks similarities; 2 marks differences.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator** of $P_{\text{elec}} = \sigma^2 / (2 \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0)$ — this is the single most common error and costs **1–2 marks** every time.
> 2. **Confusing $\varepsilon_0$ (free space) with $\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ (medium permittivity)** — examiners specifically look for the correct placement of $\varepsilon_r$.
> 3. **Missing the capacitor analogy** — when asked for the schematic, students often draw random charges without showing the **double-layer capacitor structure**. This costs up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Stating that Electronic Theory explains adhesion between identical materials** — this is FALSE and will be penalized. Always state: *the theory works only for dissimilar material pairs*.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors** in numerical problems — particularly eV ↔ J conversion and nm ↔ m conversion. Always write units explicitly.
> 6. **Not citing Derjaguin (1948)** — name-dropping the proposer is mandatory for full marks in KTU ESE theory questions.
> 7. **Skipping the assumption of constant charge vs. constant potential** — a high-value distinction that examiners love to test.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrostatic (Electronic) Theory**

- **Proposer:** B.V. Derjaguin (1948); extended by Skinner, Savage, Rutzler (1953).
- **Core Idea:** Electron transfer at the interface of dissimilar materials creates an **electrical double layer** → Coulombic attraction → adhesion.
- **Trigger Condition:** $\phi_1 \neq \phi_2$ (different work functions). If equal → no electron transfer → no electrostatic adhesion.
- **Fermi Level Equilibration:** All electron transfer stops when $E_{F,\text{adhesive}} = E_{F,\text{adherent}}$.
- **Key Equation (Force):** $F_{\text{elec}} = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 (\Delta V)^2 A / (2 d^2)$.
- **Key Equation (Pressure):** $P_{\text{elec}} = \sigma^2 / (2 \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0)$.
- **Key Equation (Work of Separation):** $W_{\text{elec}} = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 (\Delta V)^2 / (2 d)$.
- **Constants to Memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$; $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$; $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$.
- **Contact Potential Difference:** $\Delta V = (\phi_2 - \phi_1)/e$ in Volts.
- **Best Applicability:** Polymer–metal, metal–ceramic, rubber–metal joints.
- **Experimental Evidence:** Capacitance measurement, micro-discharges during separation, light emission, correlation of peel strength with $\Delta\phi$.
- **Limitations:** Cannot explain similar-material adhesion; needs at least one conductor; magnitude often smaller than measured adhesion; ignores other mechanisms.
- **Engineering Examples:** Tire rubber–brass adhesion, metallized film lamination, die-attach in microelectronics, aluminum–epoxy aerospace joints.
- **Cross-Link with Other Theories:** The gap $d$ in the Electronic Theory is the same as the contact distance in the Adsorption Theory → a smaller gap strengthens *both* mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wetting Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wetting Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Wetting** is the ability of a liquid adhesive to spread over and make intimate molecular contact with a solid substrate surface, governed by the thermodynamic balance of interfacial free energies at the three-phase contact line. It is the *preliminary, indispensable, and rate-controlling step* in adhesion — without adequate wetting, no adhesive joint can develop its full interfacial strength.

The quantitative measure of wetting is the **contact angle (θ)**, which is the angle formed between the tangent to the liquid–vapor interface and the solid surface at the three-phase contact line, measured through the liquid phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO2 / Bloom Level: Understand):**
> The student must distinguish between **wetting** (a thermodynamic / equilibrium phenomenon) and **adhesion** (a mechanical / practical strength phenomenon). A perfectly wetting system (θ = 0°) does NOT automatically guarantee a strong adhesive joint; it only guarantees full interfacial molecular contact, which is the *prerequisite* for subsequent interfacial bonding (chemical, mechanical interlocking, diffusion).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy (Water Droplet on Different Surfaces):**
- Place a water droplet on a **clean glass slide** — it spreads out almost into a thin film (θ ≈ 0° to 20°). This is *complete wetting*. Glass is "happy" to be in contact with water.
- Place the same droplet on a **freshly waxed car body** — it sits as a perfect bead, almost spherical (θ ≈ 110°). This is *non-wetting*. The wax "rejects" the water.
- The same liquid, the same gravity, the same temperature — the only difference is the **substrate surface energy**.

**Wetting is, simply put, the surface energy tug-of-war:**
- The **solid** wants the liquid to spread (it lowers the high-energy solid surface).
- The **liquid** wants to pull itself into a droplet (cohesion / surface tension).
- The **final droplet shape** is the equilibrium of this contest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The contact angle θ is measured in **degrees (°)** and the equilibrium value is governed by **Young's Equation (1805)**, which remains the foundational equation of wetting science.

### 1.3 Why Wetting is the Foundation of Adhesion

The Adhesion Science field universally accepts the **wetting → bonding → strength** sequence:

1. **Step 1 — Wetting:** Liquid adhesive flows and conforms to the micro-roughness of the substrate.
2. **Step 2 — Interfacial Bonding:** Molecular forces (van der Waals, hydrogen bonding, acid-base, covalent) develop across the interface.
3. **Step 3 — Joint Strength:** After solidification, the load-bearing capacity of the joint is established.

Failure of Step 1 (poor wetting) causes **adhesive failure** at the interface; failure of Step 2 causes **cohesive failure** within the adhesive. The wetting theory topic belongs to Step 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Interfacial Tensions

Consider a sessile liquid drop resting on a flat, smooth, homogeneous, rigid, non-deformable solid in a controlled atmosphere. Three interfaces exist:

| Interface | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Solid–Vapor | $\gamma_{SV}$ | Free energy per unit area of the solid exposed to vapor |
| Solid–Liquid | $\gamma_{SL}$ | Free energy per unit area of the solid–liquid interface |
| Liquid–Vapor | $\gamma_{LV}$ | Free energy per unit area of the liquid–vapor (surface tension of the liquid) |

Units throughout: **mN/m** (equivalent to **dyn/cm**, with 1 mN/m = 1 dyn/cm × 1, numerically 1 mN/m = 1 dyn/cm numerically since both are in mN/m = erg/cm²).

### 2.2 Young's Equation — The Master Equation

At the three-phase contact line, horizontal force balance yields:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$

where θ is the **equilibrium (Young's) contact angle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention — Critical for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - $\theta = 0°$ → **Complete Wetting** (liquid spreads to a film)
> - $0° < \theta < 90°$ → **Partial Wetting / Wetting** (good adhesive behavior)
> - $90° < \theta < 180°$ → **Non-wetting** (poor adhesive behavior)
> - $\theta = 180°$ → **Complete Non-wetting** (theoretical, never observed)

### 2.3 Work of Adhesion — Young–Dupré Equation

The **Work of Adhesion (W_a)** is the reversible thermodynamic work required to separate a unit area of the solid–liquid interface into a unit area each of solid–vapor and liquid–vapor surfaces. Deriving from a free-energy cycle (see SECTION 3):

$$W_{a} \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

Combining with Young's equation, eliminating $\gamma_{SL}$:

$$W_{a} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$

This is the **Young–Dupré Equation** (1869) — the single most important equation in adhesion thermodynamics.

### 2.4 Work of Cohesion

The **Work of Cohesion (W_c)** is the work required to separate a unit area of liquid into two liquid–vapor surfaces:

$$W_{c} \;=\; 2\,\gamma_{LV}$$

### 2.5 Spreading Coefficient

The **Spreading Coefficient (S)** predicts whether a liquid will spontaneously spread on a solid:

$$S \;=\; W_{a} - W_{c} \;=\; \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

| Condition | Meaning | KTU Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $S > 0$ | Complete spreading ($\theta = 0°$) | Liquid will form a film |
| $S < 0$ | Partial wetting ($\theta > 0°$) | Liquid forms a finite-contact-angle droplet |

### 2.6 Components of Surface Free Energy (Fowkes / Owens–Wendt)

Real surfaces are not dispersion-only. **Fowkes (1964)** proposed that total surface free energy is the sum of independent components:

$$\gamma_{S} \;=\; \gamma_{S}^{d} \;+\; \gamma_{S}^{p}$$

where $d$ = dispersion (London) component, $p$ = polar (Keesom + Debye + H-bonding + acid-base) component.

**Fowkes Geometric Mean Equation** for the dispersion contribution across an interface:

$$\gamma_{SL}^{d} \;=\; \gamma_{S} \;+\; \gamma_{L} \;-\; 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\,\gamma_{L}^{d}}$$

**Owens–Wendt Extension (1969)** — the most widely used practical method:

$$\frac{\gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\,\gamma_{L}^{d}} \;+\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\,\gamma_{L}^{p}}$$

By measuring θ with **two probe liquids of known $\gamma_L^d$ and $\gamma_L^p$** (typically diiodomethane — purely dispersive, and water — both components), the unknowns $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$ of the solid can be solved simultaneously.

### 2.7 Zisman Critical Surface Tension

Plotting $\cos\theta$ versus $\gamma_{LV}$ for a homologous series of liquids on a given solid and extrapolating to $\cos\theta = 1$ ($\theta = 0°$) gives the **Critical Surface Tension of Wetting ($\gamma_c$)** — a single-number empirical descriptor of substrate wettability.

### 2.8 Contact Angle Hysteresis

On real (heterogeneous, rough) surfaces, the observed contact angle is not single-valued:

$$\theta_{R} \;\le\; \theta_{observed} \;\le\; \theta_{A}$$

- $\theta_A$ = Advancing contact angle (liquid front pushing onto dry surface)
- $\theta_R$ = Receding contact angle (liquid front retreating from wet surface)
- $\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$ = Hysteresis (a measure of surface heterogeneity / roughness)

**KTU 2024 Relevance:** For adhesive bonding, the **advancing contact angle $\theta_A$** is the relevant parameter, because the adhesive advances onto a dry substrate.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Find unknown $\gamma$ or θ |
| 2 | Young–Dupré | $W_{a} = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Work of adhesion from contact angle |
| 3 | Work of Cohesion | $W_{c} = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | Internal cohesion of liquid |
| 4 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | Predict spreading vs beading |
| 5 | Fowkes Geometric Mean | $\gamma_{SL}^{d} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}}$ | Dispersion contribution to $\gamma_{SL}$ |
| 6 | Owens–Wendt | $\dfrac{\gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)}{2} = \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}}$ | Solve solid SFE components |
| 7 | Young from SFE components | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}}$ | Used in Young's eq. for advanced calculations |
| 8 | Critical Surface Tension | $\cos\theta = 1$ at $\gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{c}$ | Zisman plot intercept |
| 9 | Hysteresis | $\Delta\theta = \theta_{A} - \theta_{R}$ | Surface roughness / heterogeneity indicator |
| 10 | Adhesion Tension | $A = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Wetting tension / capillary driving force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention for KTU Numericals:** All surface free energies in **mJ/m²** (numerically equal to mN/m). Contact angles in **degrees (°)** unless explicitly stated in radians. Work of adhesion has the same units as SFE.

### 2.10 Engineering Utility of Wetting Theory

- **Adhesive Formulation:** Predicting which adhesive will wet a given substrate (metal, polymer, ceramic).
- **Surface Treatment Verification:** Quantifying the improvement in wettability after plasma, corona, or chemical treatment of polymers.
- **Coating & Painting:** Ensuring inks and paints spread uniformly on substrates.
- **Composite Manufacturing:** Fiber–matrix wetting in carbon-fiber / glass-fiber reinforced composites.
- **Biomedical Adhesives:** Wetting of tooth enamel by dental resins, wetting of bone by bone cements.
- **Printability:** Ink wetting on paper / polymer films in packaging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Young's Equation (Force Balance on the Three-Phase Contact Line)

Consider a sessile liquid drop of base radius $r$ resting on a smooth, flat, horizontal solid. The three interfacial tensions act along the contact line.

**Step 1 — Identify the three tangential forces per unit length of contact line:**

The horizontal (x-direction) forces on the contact line are:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ acting outward (pulling the solid surface away from the drop).
- $\gamma_{SL}$ acting inward (pulling the solid–liquid interface toward the drop center).
- $\gamma_{LV}$ acting at the liquid–vapor surface, tangent to the drop profile, with horizontal component $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ pulling inward (toward the bulk of the drop, when measured along the surface from vapor side).

**Step 2 — Apply static equilibrium (no net horizontal force):**

For the contact line to be stationary, the sum of horizontal forces must equal zero:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL} \;-\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;=\; 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to obtain Young's Equation:**

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \quad\blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Conditions for Young's Equation (essential for KTU 14-mark answers):**
> (a) Solid surface is smooth, flat, homogeneous, isotropic, and rigid.
> (b) Solid is not deformed by the liquid (no swelling).
> (c) Liquid does not penetrate the solid (no absorption).
> (d) Three-phase line is in true thermodynamic equilibrium.
> (e) The vapor phase behaves ideally (no adsorption modifies $\gamma_{SV}$).
> These are the conditions under which $\gamma_{SV}$ is the equilibrium value; on real surfaces we use $\gamma_{SV} - \pi_e$ (with $\pi_e$ = equilibrium spreading pressure of adsorbed vapor film).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young–Dupré Equation (Work of Adhesion from Contact Angle)

**Step 1 — Consider the thermodynamic cycle of separating a unit area of solid–liquid interface.**

Create a unit area of S–L interface starting from clean S–V and L–V interfaces:

$$\text{Process: } (S\text{–}V) + (L\text{–}V) \;\longrightarrow\; (S\text{–}L) \;+\; (\text{released energy})$$

The free-energy change (which is negative, since the process is spontaneous when $W_a > 0$) is:

$$\Delta G \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;-\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \gamma_{LV}$$

**Step 2 — Define Work of Adhesion as the negative of ΔG:**

$$W_{a} \;=\; -\Delta G \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Young's Equation to eliminate $\gamma_{SL}$:**

From Young's: $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{a} &= \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \left(\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta\right) \\
&= \cancel{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV} - \cancel{\gamma_{SV}} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \\
&= \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \\
&= \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta) \quad\blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spreading Coefficient Expression

**Step 1 — Define the spreading coefficient S as the free-energy change of a unit area of dry solid being covered by a film of liquid (i.e., replacing S–V interface with S–L interface, and creating new L–V interface where the S–V used to be):**

$$S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; \left(\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\right)$$

**Step 2 — From Young's Equation, substitute $\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$:**

$$S \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \;-\; \gamma_{LV} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1)$$

Since $\cos\theta \le 1$ always, $S \le 0$ for any finite contact angle $\theta \ge 0°$. Equality $S = 0$ occurs at $\theta = 0°$ (complete spreading).

**Step 3 — Express S in terms of work of adhesion and work of cohesion:**

$$S \;=\; W_{a} - W_{c} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) - 2\gamma_{LV} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) \quad\blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Owens–Wendt Geometric Mean Equation

**Step 1 — Begin with the Fowkes hypothesis that the total interfacial energy between phases i and j is the sum of geometric-mean contributions of like components:**

$$\gamma_{ij} \;=\; \gamma_{i} + \gamma_{j} - 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_{i}^{d}\gamma_{j}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{i}^{p}\gamma_{j}^{p}}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply this to the S–L interface:**

$$\gamma_{SL} \;=\; \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}}$$

**Step 3 — Insert into the Young–Dupré expression $W_{a} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - \gamma_{SL}$:**

$$W_{a} \;=\; 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} \;+\; 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Young–Dupré from contact angle $W_{a} = \gamma_{L}(1 + \cos\theta)$:**

$$\frac{\gamma_{L}(1 + \cos\theta)}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} \;+\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Practical Procedure:**
- Measure θ with **Liquid 1** (e.g., diiodomethane, $\gamma_L^d = 50.8$, $\gamma_L^p \approx 0$) → yields $\gamma_S^d$.
- Measure θ with **Liquid 2** (e.g., water, $\gamma_L^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0$) → using $\gamma_S^d$ from Step 5a, solve for $\gamma_S^p$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Style)

---

**Example 1: Work of Adhesion Calculation [3-Mark KTU Style]**

A polyethylene surface has $\gamma_S = 31$ mJ/m². A droplet of water ($\gamma_L = 72.8$ mJ/m²) shows a contact angle of 94° on the surface. Calculate the work of adhesion.

**Solution:**

Given: $\gamma_{LV} = 72.8$ mJ/m², $\theta = 94°$, $\cos(94°) = -0.0698$.

Apply Young–Dupré equation:

$$W_{a} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$

$$W_{a} \;=\; 72.8 \times (1 + (-0.0698))$$

$$W_{a} \;=\; 72.8 \times 0.9302$$

$$W_{a} \;=\; 67.72 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Conclusion:** $W_a = 67.72$ mJ/m². (For comparison, $W_c$ of water = $2 \times 72.8 = 145.6$ mJ/m², so $S < 0$ and water does **not** spontaneously spread on polyethylene — consistent with θ > 90°.)

---

**Example 2: Spreading Coefficient Analysis [7-Mark KTU Style]**

An epoxy adhesive has $\gamma_{LV} = 45$ mJ/m², and on an aluminum substrate the contact angle is 32°. The surface free energy of cleaned aluminum is $\gamma_{SV} = 840$ mJ/m². Calculate:
(a) The work of adhesion.
(b) The work of cohesion of the epoxy.
(c) The spreading coefficient — will the adhesive spread?

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Work of Adhesion**
$\cos(32°) = 0.8480$.

$$W_{a} \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta) \;=\; 45 \times (1 + 0.8480) \;=\; 45 \times 1.8480$$

$$W_{a} \;=\; 83.16 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Part (b): Work of Cohesion**
$$W_{c} \;=\; 2\,\gamma_{LV} \;=\; 2 \times 45 \;=\; 90 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Part (c): Spreading Coefficient**
$$S \;=\; W_{a} - W_{c} \;=\; 83.16 - 90 \;=\; -6.84 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

Since $S < 0$, the adhesive will **NOT spontaneously spread** completely; it will form a finite contact angle droplet of θ = 32° on aluminum. Although wetting is not complete, the angle is well below 90° — the adhesive adequately wets aluminum for a strong joint.

---

**Example 3: Owens–Wendt Determination of Solid SFE Components [14-Mark KTU Style]**

The contact angles of two probe liquids on a polymer substrate are:
- Diiodomethane: $\theta_1 = 38°$, with $\gamma_{L1}^{d} = 50.8$ mJ/m², $\gamma_{L1}^{p} = 0$ mJ/m².
- Water: $\theta_2 = 71°$, with $\gamma_{L2}^{d} = 21.8$ mJ/m², $\gamma_{L2}^{p} = 51.0$ mJ/m², $\gamma_{L2} = 72.8$ mJ/m².

Determine $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$ of the polymer, and its total surface free energy.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Owens–Wendt for Diiodomethane (Liquid 1):**
$\cos(38°) = 0.7880$.

$$\frac{72.8 \cdot \text{(NOT this one — we need }\gamma_{L1}\text{)}}{\text{Use } \gamma_{L1} = 50.8 \text{ mJ/m}^2:}$$

$$\frac{50.8 \times (1 + 0.7880)}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d} \times 50.8} \;+\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p} \times 0}$$

$$\frac{50.8 \times 1.7880}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_{S}^{d}}$$

$$\frac{90.83}{2} \;=\; 45.42 \;=\; \sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_{S}^{d}}$$

$$50.8\,\gamma_{S}^{d} \;=\; 45.42^2 \;=\; 2062.97$$

$$\gamma_{S}^{d} \;=\; 40.61 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Owens–Wendt for Water (Liquid 2):**
$\cos(71°) = 0.3256$.

$$\frac{72.8 \times (1 + 0.3256)}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d} \times 21.8} \;+\; \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p} \times 51.0}$$

$$\frac{72.8 \times 1.3256}{2} \;=\; \sqrt{40.61 \times 21.8} \;+\; \sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p}}$$

$$\frac{96.50}{2} \;=\; 48.25 \;=\; \sqrt{885.30} \;+\; \sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p}}$$

$$48.25 \;=\; 29.75 \;+\; \sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p}}$$

$$\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p}} \;=\; 48.25 - 29.75 \;=\; 18.50$$

$$51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p} \;=\; 18.50^2 \;=\; 342.25$$

$$\gamma_{S}^{p} \;=\; 6.71 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total Surface Free Energy:**
$$\gamma_{S} \;=\; \gamma_{S}^{d} + \gamma_{S}^{p} \;=\; 40.61 + 6.71 \;=\; 47.32 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Result:** The polymer is a **low-energy, predominantly dispersion-character** surface ($\gamma_S^d \gg \gamma_S^p$).

---

**Example 4: Critical Surface Tension via Zisman Plot [Conceptual Numerical]**

The following data are obtained for n-alkanes on a fluoropolymer surface:

| Liquid | $\gamma_{LV}$ (mJ/m²) | cos θ |
|---|---|---|
| n-Hexane | 18.4 | 0.62 |
| n-Octane | 21.6 | 0.45 |
| n-Decane | 23.8 | 0.28 |
| n-Dodecane | 25.4 | 0.12 |
| n-Tetradecane | 26.6 | -0.05 |

Plot cos θ vs $\gamma_{LV}$, extrapolate to cos θ = 1, and read $\gamma_c$.

**Solution Sketch (for KTU):**
- Linear regression gives slope m and intercept b.
- Setting cos θ = 1: $\gamma_{LV} = (1 - b)/m$.
- For the above data, performing a least-squares fit yields $\gamma_c \approx 16$ mJ/m² — characteristic of PTFE-type fluoropolymers.

(For exam answer, the student may approximate: trend shows cos θ = 1 would occur at $\gamma_{LV} \approx 15$–17 mJ/m².)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Sessile Drop with Interfacial Tension Vectors (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three-Phase Contact Line] --> B[Force gamma_SV: Outward pull on solid surface]
    A --> C[Force gamma_SL: Inward pull on solid-liquid interface]
    A --> D[Force gamma_LV cos theta: Inward pull on liquid-vapor interface]
    B --> E{Horizontal Force Balance}
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Young's Equation: gamma_SV equals gamma_SL plus gamma_LV cos theta]
    F --> G{Contact Angle Theta}
    G -->|theta less than 90 deg| H[Wetting regime: Good adhesive contact]
    G -->|theta greater than 90 deg| I[Non-wetting regime: Poor adhesive contact]
    G -->|theta equals 0 deg| J[Complete wetting: Film formation]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Owens–Wendt Method Procedural Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Solid Substrate of Unknown SFE] --> P2[Measure theta with Liquid A: Diiodomethane]
    P1 --> P3[Measure theta with Liquid B: Water]
    P2 --> P4[Compute Left-Hand Side for Liquid A]
    P3 --> P5[Compute Left-Hand Side for Liquid B]
    P4 --> P6[Solve for gamma_S_d: dispersion component]
    P6 --> P7[Substitute gamma_S_d into Liquid B equation]
    P5 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Solve for gamma_S_p: polar component]
    P6 --> P9[Compute Total SFE: gamma_S equals gamma_S_d plus gamma_S_p]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[End: Report gamma_S_d, gamma_S_p, gamma_S]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Wetting Regimes Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Sessile Drop on Substrate] --> Q2{Measure Contact Angle theta}
    Q2 -->|theta equals 0 deg| Q3[Complete Wetting: Spreading Coefficient S greater than 0]
    Q2 -->|0 deg less than theta less than 90 deg| Q4[Partial Wetting: S less than 0, Work of Adhesion greater than Work of Cohesion half]
    Q2 -->|theta equals 90 deg| Q5[Boundary: Wetting equals Non-wetting]
    Q2 -->|90 deg less than theta less than 180 deg| Q6[Non-wetting: Hydrophobic or Low Energy Substrate]
    Q3 --> Q7[KTU Implication: Maximum Interfacial Contact]
    Q4 --> Q7
    Q6 --> Q8[KTU Implication: Adhesive Failure Likely at Interface]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — SFE Component Partition (Fowkes-Owens-Wendt Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Total Solid SFE gamma_S] --> R2[Dispersion Component gamma_S_d: London forces]
    R1 --> R3[Polar Component gamma_S_p: Keesom plus Debye plus H-bond plus Acid-Base]
    R2 --> R4[Geometric Mean Coupling with Liquid]
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Interfacial Tension gamma_SL]
    R5 --> R6[Young-Dupre: Work of Adhesion equals gamma_LV times 1 plus cos theta]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Experimental Determination Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Wetting Characterization Goal] --> S2{Zisman Critical Surface Tension: Single-parameter empirical}
    S1 --> S3[Owens-Wendt: Two probe liquids, gives d and p components]
    S1 --> S4[van Oss Acid-Base: Three probe liquids, gives Lifshitz-van der Waals plus acid plus base]
    S1 --> S5[Wu Harmonic Mean: Suitable for polymer-polymer systems]
    S2 --> T1[Use for: Quality control, substrate ranking]
    S3 --> T2[Use for: Adhesive design, predicting acid-base interactions]
    S4 --> T3[Use for: Polar substrates, biological surfaces]
    S5 --> T4[Use for: Polymer blends, coatings]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Note:** Physical drawings of droplet geometry with vector arrows are beyond the Mermaid textual-flow schema, so the diagrams above present the **functional architecture** of wetting analysis — mapping how the equations, forces, and components interact to determine adhesive wettability.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
Define the contact angle and explain the significance of Young's equation in adhesion science. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
The **contact angle (θ)** is the angle formed at the three-phase (solid–liquid–vapor) contact line, measured through the liquid phase, between the tangent to the liquid–vapor interface and the solid surface. It is a direct measure of the wettability of a substrate by a liquid adhesive.

**Young's equation** is the foundational thermodynamic relation governing equilibrium contact angle:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

**Significance in Adhesion:**
1. It quantifies wetting, the prerequisite for adhesion.
2. It enables the calculation of **work of adhesion** via the Young–Dupré equation: $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$.
3. It bridges measurable quantities (θ, $\gamma_{LV}$) with fundamental interfacial energetics ($\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$), enabling rational adhesive selection for a given substrate.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]**
Distinguish between **work of adhesion** and **work of cohesion**. How is the spreading coefficient related to these? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Work of Adhesion ($W_a$) | Work of Cohesion ($W_c$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Reversible work to separate S–L interface into S–V and L–V | Reversible work to separate L–L into two L–V surfaces |
| Equation | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ |
| Nature | Interfacial (solid–liquid) | Internal (liquid–liquid) |

**Spreading Coefficient:**

$$S \;=\; W_{a} - W_{c} \;=\; \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$$

- $S > 0$: Liquid completely spreads (θ = 0°).
- $S < 0$: Liquid forms a finite contact angle droplet.

**Adhesive Interpretation:** If $W_a > W_c$, the adhesive wets the substrate more strongly than it coheres, favoring spreading and good interfacial contact.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question Pattern]

**(a)** State and derive **Young's equation** for the equilibrium contact angle of a liquid drop on a solid surface. Clearly state the assumptions and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A liquid adhesive of surface tension 38 mN/m forms a contact angle of 28° on a clean steel substrate. Calculate:
  (i) Work of adhesion
  (ii) Work of cohesion
  (iii) Spreading coefficient
  (iv) Interfacial tension between adhesive and steel (use $\gamma_{SV} = 460$ mN/m²) **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Statement and Derivation:**

**Statement:** Young's equation relates the three interfacial tensions and the equilibrium contact angle at the three-phase contact line of a sessile liquid drop on a smooth, flat, homogeneous, rigid, and non-porous solid.

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

**Derivation (Force Balance Method):**
1. At the three-phase contact line, three interfacial tensions act tangentially along the contact line: $\gamma_{SV}$ (along solid surface, outward from drop), $\gamma_{SL}$ (along solid surface, inward toward drop), and $\gamma_{LV}$ (along liquid–vapor surface, at angle θ from the solid surface).
2. Resolving the liquid–vapor tension into horizontal and vertical components: horizontal = $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ (inward), vertical = $\gamma_{LV}\sin\theta$ (upward, balanced by normal stress on the solid).
3. Horizontal equilibrium (no net horizontal force on the contact line):

$$\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = 0$$

4. Rearranging:

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta \quad\blacksquare$$

**Assumptions:**
- Solid is smooth, flat, homogeneous, isotropic, rigid, and chemically inert.
- Solid is non-deformable and not penetrated by the liquid.
- No adsorption of vapor on the solid surface (or $\gamma_{SV}$ is corrected for spreading pressure $\pi_e$).
- System is at true thermodynamic equilibrium.

**Limitations:**
- Real surfaces have roughness (causes hysteresis).
- Real surfaces have chemical heterogeneity.
- Deformable solids violate the rigid-substrate assumption.
- The equation does not predict the absolute magnitude of joint strength.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $\gamma_{LV} = 38$ mN/m, $\theta = 28°$, $\gamma_{SV} = 460$ mN/m².
$\cos(28°) = 0.8829$.

**(i) Work of Adhesion:**

$$W_{a} = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = 38 \times (1 + 0.8829) = 38 \times 1.8829 = 71.55 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**(ii) Work of Cohesion:**

$$W_{c} = 2\gamma_{LV} = 2 \times 38 = 76 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**(iii) Spreading Coefficient:**

$$S = W_{a} - W_{c} = 71.55 - 76 = -4.45 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

Since $S < 0$, the adhesive will NOT spontaneously form a film but will form a finite contact angle droplet of θ = 28° — adequate for bonding.

**(iv) Interfacial Tension (using Young's Equation):**

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = 460 - 38 \times 0.8829 = 460 - 33.55 = 426.45 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Alternative — KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Question Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **Fowkes** and **Owens–Wendt** approaches to determining the surface free energy components of a solid. Derive the Owens–Wendt equation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The contact angle of diiodomethane ($\gamma_L = 50.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^d = 50.8$, $\gamma_L^p = 0$) on a polymer is 35°. The contact angle of water ($\gamma_L = 72.8$ mN/m, $\gamma_L^d = 21.8$, $\gamma_L^p = 51.0$) on the same polymer is 75°. Calculate $\gamma_S^d$, $\gamma_S^p$, and total SFE. Comment on the polar/dispersive character of the polymer. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fowkes and Owens–Wendt Approaches:**

**Fowkes Hypothesis (1964):** The total surface free energy of a phase is the sum of independent, non-interacting components, each arising from a distinct type of intermolecular force. For non-polar systems, only the **Lifshitz–van der Waals (dispersion)** component contributes:

$$\gamma = \gamma^{d} \quad\text{(non-polar phase)}$$

For a non-polar solid–non-polar liquid interface:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}}$$

This is the **Fowkes geometric mean equation**, valid when only dispersion forces operate across the interface.

**Owens–Wendt Extension (1969):** Extended Fowkes to polar systems by partitioning SFE into **dispersion ($d$)** and **polar ($p$, lumping all non-dispersion interactions: dipole, H-bonding, acid–base)** components:

$$\gamma = \gamma^{d} + \gamma^{p}$$

Assuming geometric mean coupling for each component across the interface:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - 2\left[\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}}\right]$$

**Derivation of Owens–Wendt Equation:**
1. Work of adhesion from interfacial energies:

$$W_{a} = \gamma_{S} + \gamma_{L} - \gamma_{SL}$$

2. Substitute the Owens–Wendt expression for $\gamma_{SL}$:

$$W_{a} = 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}}$$

3. Equate to the Young–Dupré form $W_{a} = \gamma_{L}(1 + \cos\theta)$:

$$\frac{\gamma_{L}(1 + \cos\theta)}{2} = \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d}\gamma_{L}^{d}} + \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p}\gamma_{L}^{p}} \quad\blacksquare$$

**Practical Use:** Measure θ with at least two probe liquids (one purely dispersive — diiodomethane; one with both components — water or formamide), solve the two equations simultaneously for $\gamma_S^d$ and $\gamma_S^p$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diiodomethane: $\theta_1 = 35°$, $\gamma_{L1} = 50.8$, $\gamma_{L1}^{d} = 50.8$, $\gamma_{L1}^{p} = 0$.
- Water: $\theta_2 = 75°$, $\gamma_{L2} = 72.8$, $\gamma_{L2}^{d} = 21.8$, $\gamma_{L2}^{p} = 51.0$.
- $\cos(35°) = 0.8192$; $\cos(75°) = 0.2588$.

**Step 1 — Owens–Wendt for Diiodomethane (Liquid 1):**
Since $\gamma_{L1}^{p} = 0$, the equation reduces to:

$$\frac{50.8 \times (1 + 0.8192)}{2} = \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{d} \times 50.8}$$

$$\frac{50.8 \times 1.8192}{2} = \frac{92.42}{2} = 46.21 = \sqrt{50.8\,\gamma_{S}^{d}}$$

$$50.8\,\gamma_{S}^{d} = 46.21^2 = 2135.36$$

$$\gamma_{S}^{d} = 42.04 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Owens–Wendt for Water (Liquid 2):**

$$\frac{72.8 \times (1 + 0.2588)}{2} = \sqrt{42.04 \times 21.8} + \sqrt{\gamma_{S}^{p} \times 51.0}$$

$$\frac{72.8 \times 1.2588}{2} = \frac{91.64}{2} = 45.82$$

$$\sqrt{42.04 \times 21.8} = \sqrt{916.47} = 30.27$$

$$45.82 = 30.27 + \sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p}}$$

$$\sqrt{51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p}} = 45.82 - 30.27 = 15.55$$

$$51.0\,\gamma_{S}^{p} = 15.55^2 = 241.80$$

$$\gamma_{S}^{p} = 4.74 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Total SFE:**

$$\gamma_{S} = \gamma_{S}^{d} + \gamma_{S}^{p} = 42.04 + 4.74 = 46.78 \;\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**Comment:** The polymer is **strongly dispersion-dominated** ($\gamma_S^d / \gamma_S \approx 89.9\%$), with only a small polar contribution. This is typical of polyolefins (PE, PP) and other low-surface-energy polymers, consistent with their known poor adhesion behavior and need for surface treatment (corona, plasma, chemical etching) before bonding.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Board Evaluation (Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $\cos\theta$ in Young–Dupré:** For θ > 90°, $\cos\theta$ is negative. Many students write $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 - \cos\theta)$ — this is a critical error losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mN/m and mJ/m² — though numerically equal, students should explicitly state the unit. Markers deduct for sloppy notation.
>
> 3. **Misidentification of Probe Liquids:** In Owens–Wendt, students often use two polar liquids or two non-polar liquids. The method **requires one purely dispersive (diiodomethane)** and **one with both components (water)**.
>
> 4. **Confusing $\gamma_S$ and $\gamma_{SV}$:** On a clean, dry surface in ambient air, $\gamma_{SV} \approx \gamma_S$ (vapor adsorption is small). On high-energy surfaces exposed to high-humidity air, $\gamma_{SV}$ may be reduced by the spreading pressure $\pi_e$. Marks are lost for not acknowledging this.
>
> 5. **Algebraic Errors in Simultaneous Equations:** Solve step-by-step. Do not skip the intermediate substitution of $\gamma_S^d$ into the second equation.
>
> 6. **Spreading Coefficient Sign Interpretation:** $S < 0$ does NOT mean the adhesive fails — it means the adhesive will not form a film, but it can still adequately wet the surface (θ < 90°).
>
> 7. **Neglecting Assumptions of Young's Equation:** Always list the assumptions (smooth, flat, rigid, homogeneous, etc.) in 7-mark derivations. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Wetting Theory (KTU 2024 PEPOT861 Module 2):**

- **Wetting** = the spreading of a liquid on a solid to form intimate molecular contact; prerequisite for adhesion.
- **Contact angle θ** is the *single most important* descriptor of wettability.
- **Young's Equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — the foundational equation.
- **Young–Dupré Equation:** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — bridges measurable θ to adhesion energy.
- **Work of Cohesion:** $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ — internal liquid cohesion.
- **Spreading Coefficient:** $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) \le 0$.
- **Wetting regimes:** θ < 90° → wetting; θ > 90° → non-wetting; θ = 0° → complete spreading.
- **Fowkes SFE Partition:** $\gamma = \gamma^d + \gamma^p$ (dispersion + polar).
- **Owens–Wendt Equation:** $\frac{\gamma_L(1+\cos\theta)}{2} = \sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_S^p \gamma_L^p}$.
- **Practical Owens–Wendt:** Use **diiodomethane (non-polar probe)** + **water (polar probe)**.
- **Zisman's Critical Surface Tension ($\gamma_c$):** Empirical single-parameter substrate wettability descriptor from $\cos\theta$ vs $\gamma_{LV}$ plot extrapolation.
- **Contact Angle Hysteresis:** $\Delta\theta = \theta_A - \theta_R$ — measure of surface roughness/heterogeneity; $\theta_A$ is the relevant parameter for adhesive advancing front.
- **Assumptions of Young's Equation:** Smooth, flat, homogeneous, rigid, non-porous, non-deformable, non-swelling solid; ideal vapor.
- **Units:** All SFE in **mJ/m² = mN/m**; contact angles in **degrees (°)**.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A substrate needs $\gamma_S > \gamma_L$ (or at least $\gamma_S \ge \gamma_L$) for good wetting by a given liquid adhesive.
- **Surface Treatment Effect:** Plasma, corona, chemical etching increase $\gamma_S$ (especially $\gamma_S^p$) → improve wetting → improve adhesive joint strength.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** In numericals, always state the contact angle regime (wetting / non-wetting) explicitly after calculating θ.
- **Avoid Common Pitfalls:** Sign of $\cos\theta$ in Young–Dupré; unit consistency; correct identification of probe liquids for Owens–Wendt; proper assumptions in Young's equation derivation.

---

**End of Module 2 — Wetting Theory Notes (PEPOT861, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Chemical Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chemical Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Adhesive Science & Technology**, **Chemical Bonding** refers to the intra- and intermolecular attractive forces that hold atoms together within a molecule (primary bonds) and between molecules (secondary bonds). These bonds are the fundamental origin of all adhesion phenomena, dictating the cohesive strength of the adhesive itself and the adhesive forces that form at the adhesive–substrate interface.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Adhesive Science & Technology, the study of chemical bonding under Module 2 establishes the molecular foundation required to understand wetting, surface energy, adhesion mechanisms, and bond durability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Every adhesive joint failure or success can ultimately be traced back to the type, density, and energy of the chemical bonds present across the interface.

### 1.2 Types of Chemical Bonds (Overview)

Chemical bonds are classified into two broad categories:

1. **Primary Bonds (Intramolecular Forces):**
   - Ionic bonds
   - Covalent bonds
   - Metallic bonds

2. **Secondary Bonds (Intermolecular Forces):**
   - Hydrogen bonds
   - Van der Waals (London dispersion) forces
   - Dipole–dipole interactions

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building with Lego bricks:

- **Covalent bonds** are like two Lego bricks that have been **glued together (fused)** at the stud level — extremely strong, requires force to break.
- **Ionic bonds** are like a magnet stuck to a fridge — strong attraction but can be released with effort (e.g., in water).
- **Metallic bonds** are like a pool of shared electrons — like a crowd where everyone holds hands loosely but still moves together (e.g., copper, aluminum).
- **Hydrogen bonds** are like weak Velcro strips — easy to attach and detach, but numerous strips together hold great weight (e.g., water, nylon).
- **Van der Waals forces** are like the **static cling** between a balloon and a sweater — very weak individually but omnipresent.

In an adhesive joint, the adhesive molecules must "shake hands" (form bonds) with the substrate molecules. The strength of that handshake determines whether your tape sticks to the wall or falls off!

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reference:** Bond energies typically range from **0.5–5 kcal/mol** for secondary bonds to **50–200 kcal/mol** for primary covalent bonds.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Primary Chemical Bonds

#### 2.1.1 Ionic Bonding
- Formed by **complete transfer of electrons** from one atom to another.
- Result: formation of **cations (+)** and **anions (−)** held by **electrostatic (Coulombic) attraction**.
- Example: NaCl — sodium loses one electron to chlorine.

**Why it matters in adhesion:** Ionic substrates (e.g., glass, ceramics, metal oxides) form strong ionic interactions with polar adhesives like epoxies.

#### 2.1.2 Covalent Bonding
- Formed by **mutual sharing of electron pairs** between atoms.
- Highly directional bonds with specific bond angles (e.g., tetrahedral in carbon).
- Examples: C–C, C–H, C–O, Si–O in silicone adhesives.

**Why it matters in adhesion:** Covalent bonds provide the **highest adhesive strength**. Structural adhesives (epoxies, polyurethanes, cyanoacrylates) form covalent crosslinks with substrates through coupling agents (e.g., silanes).

#### 2.1.3 Metallic Bonding
- Metal atoms **share delocalized electrons** in an "electron sea."
- Provides ductility, conductivity, and high cohesive energy in metals.

**Why it matters in adhesion:** Metallic substrates (Al, Cu, steel) require surface treatments (anodizing, primers) to create oxide layers capable of covalent bonding with adhesives.

### 2.2 Secondary Chemical Bonds

#### 2.2.1 Hydrogen Bonding
- Special dipole–dipole interaction involving H bonded to **N, O, or F**.
- Bond energy: **5–10 kcal/mol**.
- Crucial in water-based adhesives, PVA, acrylics.

#### 2.2.2 Van der Waals (London Dispersion) Forces
- Arise from **temporary induced dipoles** due to electron cloud fluctuations.
- Universal — present in **all** materials.
- Bond energy: **0.5–2 kcal/mol**.

#### 2.2.3 Dipole–Dipole Interactions
- Occur between **permanently polar molecules**.
- Strength depends on dipole moment magnitude.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Bond Type | Energy (kcal/mol) | Energy (kJ/mol) | Range | Directionality | Example in Adhesion |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ionic | 100–200 | 420–840 | Long | Non-directional | Glass + epoxy |
| Covalent | 50–200 | 210–840 | Short (~0.1–0.2 nm) | Highly directional | Silane coupling on glass |
| Metallic | 50–150 | 210–630 | Long | Non-directional | Steel + structural adhesive |
| Hydrogen | 5–10 | 20–40 | Short (~0.2–0.3 nm) | Directional | PVA adhesive on wood |
| Dipole–dipole | 2–5 | 8–20 | Short | Moderate | Polar polymer adhesion |
| Van der Waals | 0.5–2 | 2–8 | Very short (~0.3–0.5 nm) | Non-directional | PTFE (low adhesion!) |

### 2.4 Core Governing Equations

**Coulomb's Law (Ionic Bonds):**
$$
E_{\text{ionic}} = -\frac{k \cdot q_1 \cdot q_2}{r}
$$

where $k = 8.99 \times 10^9 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m^2/C^2}$ is Coulomb's constant, $q_1, q_2$ are ionic charges, and $r$ is the interionic distance.

**Bond Dissociation Energy (Covalent):**
$$
\Delta H_{\text{bond}} = \sum E_{\text{bonds broken}} - \sum E_{\text{bonds formed}}
$$

**Lennard-Jones Potential (Van der Waals):**
$$
U(r) = 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the depth of the potential well (bond energy) and $\sigma$ is the distance at which the potential is zero.

**Work of Adhesion (Young–Dupré Equation):**
$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

where $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{lv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$ are the solid–vapor, liquid–vapor, and solid–liquid interfacial tensions, respectively.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Epoxy–aluminum joints rely on **covalent** silane bonding for strength at high altitudes.
- **Automotive:** Crash-resistant body panels use **structural polyurethane adhesives** with both covalent and hydrogen bonding.
- **Electronics:** PCB laminates use **epoxy–glass** covalent + ionic bonding for thermal stability.
- **Biomedical:** Tissue adhesives exploit **hydrogen bonding** with biological substrates for biocompatibility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** Adhesive selection begins with identifying the substrate's bonding capability (polarity, surface energy, functional groups) to predict which bond type will dominate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Lennard-Jones Potential and Minimum Bond Energy

The Lennard-Jones potential describes the **interaction energy between two non-bonding atoms or molecules** as a function of separation distance $r$.

**Step 1 — Repulsive Term (Born Repulsion):**
At very short distances, electron clouds overlap, generating strong repulsive forces modeled as:
$$
U_{\text{rep}}(r) = \frac{A}{r^{12}}
$$
where $A$ is a positive constant.

**Step 2 — Attractive Term (London Dispersion):**
At slightly larger distances, induced dipole–induced dipole interactions create attractive forces modeled as:
$$
U_{\text{att}}(r) = -\frac{B}{r^{6}}
$$
where $B$ is a positive constant representing dispersion interaction strength.

**Step 3 — Combine Both Terms:**
The total potential is the sum:
$$
U(r) = \frac{A}{r^{12}} - \frac{B}{r^{6}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in Lennard-Jones Form:**
Substitute $\sigma$ (zero-crossing distance) and $\epsilon$ (well depth):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \left( \frac{A}{B} \right)^{1/6} \\
\epsilon &= \frac{B^2}{4A}
\end{aligned}
$$

After substitution:
$$
U(r) = 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Find Equilibrium Distance (Minimum Energy):**
Differentiate with respect to $r$ and set to zero:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dU}{dr} &= 4\epsilon \left[ -12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right] = 0 \\
6 \frac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} &= 12 \frac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} \\
\frac{1}{r^{6}} &= \frac{2 \sigma^{6}}{r^{12}} \\
r_{\min}^{6} &= 2 \sigma^{6} \\
r_{\min} &= 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Energy Value:**
Substitute $r_{\min}$ back into $U(r)$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\min} &= 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{\sigma}{2^{1/6}\sigma} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{\sigma}{2^{1/6}\sigma} \right)^{6} \right] \\
&= 4\epsilon \left[ \left( \frac{1}{2^{1/6}} \right)^{12} - \left( \frac{1}{2^{1/6}} \right)^{6} \right] \\
&= 4\epsilon \left[ \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{2} \right] \\
&= 4\epsilon \left( -\frac{1}{4} \right) \\
U_{\min} &= -\epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms that $\epsilon$ is the magnitude of the attractive well depth — the **bond energy** for that interaction.

### 3.2 Derivation: Work of Adhesion from Interfacial Energies

The Young–Dupré equation gives the thermodynamic work of adhesion per unit area.

**Step 1 — Young's Equation for Contact Angle:**
At equilibrium, the horizontal force balance at the contact line:
$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta
$$
where $\theta$ is the contact angle.

**Step 2 — Solve for $\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}$:**
$$
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Define Work of Adhesion:**
By definition (Dupré, 1869):
$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine Equations:**
Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl} \\
&= (\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}) + \gamma_{lv} \\
&= \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta + \gamma_{lv} \\
W_a &= \gamma_{lv} (1 + \cos\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **Young–Dupré equation**, used universally in surface science and adhesive engineering.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bond Selection for Adhesive Applications

| Substrate | Dominant Surface Bonds | Recommended Adhesive Chemistry | Expected Bond Type at Interface |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glass / Ceramics | Ionic (Si–O–Si) | Silane-modified epoxy | Covalent (Si–O–Si) |
| Aluminum (anodized) | Ionic (Al₂O₃) | Epoxy + silane primer | Covalent + ionic |
| Steel (oxide) | Ionic + metallic | Phenolic or epoxy | Covalent + ionic |
| Wood (cellulose) | Hydrogen bonds | PVA, polyurethane | Hydrogen bonds |
| Polyethylene (PE) | Van der Waals only | Corona-treated + hot-melt | Van der Waals → covalent after treatment |
| PTFE | Very weak VdW | Surface-etched + epoxy | Weak covalent (post-etch) |
| Rubber | Crosslinked covalent | Cyanoacrylate, contact adhesive | Covalent + diffusion |
| Paper (cellulose) | Hydrogen bonds | Starch-based, PVA | Hydrogen bonds |

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Calculating Work of Adhesion

**Given:**
- Contact angle of water on an epoxy-coated surface: $\theta = 65°$
- Surface tension of water: $\gamma_{lv} = 72.8 \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2}$

**Find:** Work of adhesion $W_a$.

**Solution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= \gamma_{lv} (1 + \cos\theta) \\
&= 72.8 \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2} \times (1 + \cos 65°) \\
&= 72.8 \times (1 + 0.4226) \\
&= 72.8 \times 1.4226 \\
W_a &\approx 103.6 \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value represents the thermodynamic driving force for adhesion per unit interfacial area.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
