
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The silane coupling mechanism shown above is the single most important chemical bonding process taught in KTU Module 2 because it directly bridges the gap between **inorganic substrates (glass, metals)** and **organic polymer adhesives (epoxies)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Differentiate between primary and secondary chemical bonds with examples relevant to adhesion.

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Primary Bonds | Secondary Bonds |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Intramolecular | Intermolecular |
| Energy | 50–200 kcal/mol | 0.5–10 kcal/mol |
| Directionality | Highly directional | Non-directional (mostly) |
| Example | Covalent Si–O in silane coupling | Van der Waals in PTFE substrate |
| Role in adhesion | Strong durable interface | Initial wetting & contact |

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** Explain the role of hydrogen bonding in adhesive joints. Give two adhesive examples.

**Model Answer:**
Hydrogen bonds form when H is covalently bonded to highly electronegative atoms (N, O, F) and interacts with another electronegative atom. In adhesive joints, they are responsible for:
- Initial tack and wetting on polar surfaces.
- Cohesive strength in polar polymer adhesives.

**Examples:**
1. **Polyvinyl Acetate (PVA)** — wood glue, forms H-bonds with cellulose.
2. **Polyurethane** adhesives — form H-bonds with carbonyl and N–H groups on substrates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the formation and characteristics of **ionic, covalent, and metallic bonds**. Discuss which of these bonds plays the most dominant role in structural adhesive joints. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Lennard-Jones potential** and show that the equilibrium distance is $r_{\min} = 2^{1/6} \sigma$ with minimum energy $-\epsilon$. Mention its significance in adhesion science. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Ionic Bond:**
- Transfer of valence electron from metal to non-metal.
- Example: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ ; Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻ ; Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl.
- Held by **Coulombic attraction** $E = -\dfrac{kq_1q_2}{r}$.
- Energy: 100–200 kcal/mol.
- Non-directional.

**Covalent Bond:**
- Sharing of electron pair between two atoms.
- Example: H–H, C–C, C–O, Si–O.
- Highly directional (specific bond angles).
- Energy: 50–200 kcal/mol.
- **Most dominant** in structural adhesives because of high strength and chemical specificity.

**Metallic Bond:**
- Delocalized electron sea model.
- Bonds cations within a lattice via shared electrons.
- Found in metal substrates (steel, Al) but **not at the adhesive interface** unless oxide layers are present.

**Conclusion:** **Covalent bonding** dominates structural adhesive joints (e.g., epoxy + silane on glass) due to its high bond energy and chemical specificity.

##### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes. The derivation proceeds through 6 steps:
1. Repulsive term $A/r^{12}$
2. Attractive term $-B/r^6$
3. Sum gives total potential
4. Substitution using $\sigma$ and $\epsilon$
5. Differentiate to find $r_{\min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$
6. Substitute back to get $U_{\min} = -\epsilon$

**Significance in Adhesion:**
The Lennard-Jones potential models the **van der Waals interaction** between adhesive molecules and substrate atoms at very short range. It explains why adhesives must come into **intimate molecular contact** (wetting) for secondary bond formation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the importance of **hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals forces** in adhesive technology. Compare their bond energies and explain how surface treatments can convert Van der Waals interactions into covalent bonds (give one example). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from **Young's equation**, derive the **Young–Dupré equation** for work of adhesion. A polymer adhesive has a surface tension of 35 mN/m and forms a contact angle of 40° on a steel substrate. Calculate the work of adhesion. The surface tension of the adhesive is 35 mN/m. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

**Hydrogen Bonding:**
- Strongest secondary bond (5–10 kcal/mol).
- Directional — requires specific H-bond acceptor geometry.
- Critical in PVA, epoxy-amine curing, polyurethane adhesion, biological adhesives.

**Van der Waals Forces:**
- Universal but weak (0.5–2 kcal/mol).
- Arise from instantaneous dipoles.
- Important in initial wetting and contact of non-polar substrates like polyethylene and PTFE.

**Comparison:**
- Hydrogen bond energy is **5–10× stronger** than Van der Waals.
- Hydrogen bonds are directional; Van der Waals are not.

**Surface Treatment Example:**
- **Corona or plasma treatment of polyethylene** introduces polar –OH, –COOH groups on the surface. These can then react with functional groups in the adhesive, forming **covalent bonds** (e.g., C–O–C) instead of mere Van der Waals contact. This converts a weak adhesive joint into a strong, durable one.

##### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for derivation.

**Step 1:** Young's equation:
$$
\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange:
$$
\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} = \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Dupré's definition:
$$
W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}
$$

**Step 4:** Combine:
$$
W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $\gamma_{lv} = 35 \ \mathrm{mN/m}$, $\theta = 40°$
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= 35 \times (1 + \cos 40°) \\
&= 35 \times (1 + 0.7660) \\
&= 35 \times 1.7660 \\
W_a &= 61.81 \ \mathrm{mN/m} \ \text{or} \ \mathrm{mJ/m^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students forget that $\gamma_{lv}$ must be in mN/m and $W_a$ comes out in mJ/m² (numerically equal, but dimensionally different in SI base units). Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Trig error:** Forgetting that $\cos(40°) \approx 0.766$ not $\sin(40°)$. Calculator must be in **degree mode**.
> 3. **Sign error:** Reversing Young's equation gives wrong sign of $W_a$.
> 4. **Bond energy units:** Mixing kcal/mol with kJ/mol in tables loses 1 mark. Always state the unit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chemical bonds are the foundation of all adhesion phenomena** — every adhesive joint is governed by primary and/or secondary bonds at the molecular interface.
- **Primary bonds (ionic, covalent, metallic)** provide **high strength (50–200 kcal/mol)** and are responsible for structural adhesion.
- **Secondary bonds (H-bonds, VdW, dipole–dipole)** provide **moderate to weak strength (0.5–10 kcal/mol)** and dominate in pressure-sensitive and contact adhesives.
- **Covalent bonding via silane coupling agents** is the **gold standard** for joining inorganic substrates (glass, metals) with organic polymer adhesives.
- **Surface energy matching** (wetting) is a **prerequisite** for chemical bond formation — without intimate contact, even strong bonds cannot form.
- **Young–Dupré equation** $W_a = \gamma_{lv}(1 + \cos\theta)$ is the **master equation** linking contact angle measurement to adhesive bond strength.
- **Lennard-Jones potential** $U(r) = 4\epsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$ describes Van der Waals interactions and predicts $r_{\min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$ with bond energy $\epsilon$.
- **Surface treatments** (corona, plasma, silanization) can **upgrade weak Van der Waals adhesion to strong covalent adhesion** — a critical engineering strategy.
- **Hydrogen bonds** (5–10 kcal/mol) are particularly important in **polar polymer adhesives** such as PVA, polyurethanes, and epoxies.
- **Bond energy hierarchy (high to low):** Covalent ≈ Ionic ≈ Metallic > Hydrogen > Dipole–dipole > Van der Waals.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Select adhesive chemistry based on substrate functional groups, then engineer the surface to expose or create those functional groups for maximum bond formation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always (1) state the principle, (2) draw a labeled diagram, (3) show the math, (4) give a real adhesive example, and (5) conclude with engineering significance. This 5-step structure consistently scores full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weak Boundary Layer Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weak Boundary Layer Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Weak Boundary Layer (WBL) Theory** — A foundational adhesion theory proposed by **Jacob J. Bikerman (1961)** which postulates that a true, atomically clean interfacial failure between an adhesive and a substrate almost **never** occurs under practical conditions. Instead, failure takes place **cohesively within a thin, mechanically inferior layer** that resides at (or very close to) the geometric interface.

This mechanically inferior zone may originate from the adhesive itself (e.g., unreacted monomer, low-molecular-weight oligomer), from the substrate (e.g., oxide layer, contamination), or from the environment (e.g., moisture, oxidation products). Because the chain of matter always breaks at its **weakest link**, the measured "adhesive strength" of a joint is actually the **cohesive strength of this boundary layer**, not the true interfacial adhesion force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The WBL Theory is one of the six classical theories of adhesion (alongside Mechanical, Adsorption, Diffusion, Electrostatic, and Chemical Bonding theories). It is the **most cited explanation** for why surface preparation dramatically improves joint strength.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sandwich Board"

Imagine two wooden planks glued together by a smear of butter. If you try to pull them apart, the planks will not separate at the butter–wood interface. Instead, the butter layer itself will split cohesively in the middle. 

Now, replace the clean wood with **dusty wood** (covered in sawdust). The wood–butter interface is now interrupted by a thin **sawdust layer**. When you pull the planks apart, they separate *within* the sawdust, not within the butter and not at the butter–wood interface. The "adhesive" (butter) was never truly tested for its own strength — the sawdust became the **Weak Boundary Layer**.

In an engineering adhesive joint:
- **Plank** = Adherend (substrate, e.g., aluminum, steel)
- **Butter** = Adhesive (e.g., epoxy)
- **Sawdust** = Contamination, oxide layer, moisture, unreacted species → **Weak Boundary Layer**

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

- **Typical WBL thickness:** **1 nm to 1 µm** (depending on contamination mechanism)
- **Bikerman's reported range:** Most practical joints have a contaminated layer of **≥ 0.1 µm**
- **Strength of WBL vs. bulk adhesive:** Usually **< 30%** of bulk cohesive strength
- **Critical surface energy requirement for adhesion:** **γ_critical ≈ 40–45 mJ/m²** for structural adhesives

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bikerman's Postulate — Step-by-Step Logic

Bikerman's argument rests on four sequential logical steps:

1. **Thermodynamic Imperfection:** It is statistically impossible to bring two surfaces into perfect molecular contact without any entrained third phase. Even in high-vacuum conditions, **air pockets**, **moisture monolayers**, and **hydrocarbon contaminants** persist at the interface.
2. **Time-Dependent Contamination:** Given sufficient time, **low-molecular-weight species** (plasticizers, oligomers, unreacted monomers, processing oils) will *migrate* to the interface by diffusion, driven by thermodynamic compatibility with the substrate.
3. **Chemical Reaction Layer:** The adhesive and substrate may react at their interface, producing a **reaction-product layer** (e.g., metal oxides, metal-soap complexes) whose mechanical properties differ from either parent material.
4. **Failure Path Selection:** When external load is applied, the crack propagates through the path of **lowest resistance**. Since the WBL has the lowest cohesive strength, failure occurs *within* this layer, masquerading as "adhesive failure" but being fundamentally *cohesive in nature*.

### 2.2 Classification of Weak Boundary Layers

| Class | Origin | Typical Examples | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I — Adhesive-side WBL** | Migrating species from bulk adhesive | Plasticizer migration, low-MW oligomer, unreacted monomer | Cure schedule optimization, use of barrier primers |
| **Type II — Substrate-side WBL** | Loose oxide scales, friable surface layers | Aluminum oxide (Boehmite), mill scale on steel, anodized layers | Chemical etching, abrasion, primer application |
| **Type III — Environmental WBL** | External contamination | Moisture films, oils, fingerprints, oxide growth | Solvent cleaning, plasma treatment, vacuum handling |
| **Type IV — Reaction-product WBL** | Chemical interaction at interface | Metal-soap formation (Pb, Zn), oxide intergrowth, silane hydrolysis layer | Surface passivation, coupling agents, use of inhibitors |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The WBL Theory itself is mostly conceptual, but the *mechanical strength* of the weak layer can be expressed using fracture-mechanics and thermodynamic formulations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \, \gamma_s}{\pi \, a}} \quad &\text{(Griffith-Orowan fracture criterion)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{bond}} &= \eta \cdot \gamma_{LV} \quad &\text{(Bikerman's bond-strength postulate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{WBL}} &\approx \sqrt{D \cdot t_{\text{exp}}} \quad &\text{(Fickian diffusion layer growth)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_a &= 2 \sqrt{\gamma_1^{d} \cdot \gamma_2^{d}} + 2 \sqrt{\gamma_1^{p} \cdot \gamma_2^{p}} \quad &\text{(Fowkes / Owens-Wendt work of adhesion)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_f$ | Fracture stress of the WBL | MPa |
| $E$ | Young's modulus of the WBL | GPa |
| $\gamma_s$ | Surface energy (fracture) of WBL | mJ/m² |
| $a$ | Critical flaw size in the WBL | µm |
| $F_{\text{bond}}$ | Practical joint strength | MPa |
| $\eta$ | Plasticity / energy-dissipation factor (dimensionless) | — |
| $\gamma_{LV}$ | Liquid–vapor surface energy of adhesive | mJ/m² |
| $t_{\text{WBL}}$ | Diffusion-grown WBL thickness | m |
| $D$ | Diffusion coefficient of contaminant | m²/s |
| $t_{\text{exp}}$ | Exposure time before bonding | s |
| $W_a$ | Thermodynamic work of adhesion | mJ/m² |
| $\gamma^{d}, \gamma^{p}$ | Dispersive and polar components of surface energy | mJ/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** WBL Theory explains why the *cleanliness*, *roughness*, and *chemical nature* of the substrate surface dominate the strength of a real engineering joint — often more than the bulk properties of the adhesive itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Flaw Size in a Weak Boundary Layer

Consider a brittle weak boundary layer of thickness $t$ containing a penny-shaped crack of radius $a$. Using the **Griffith energy-balance criterion**, the system releases elastic strain energy as the crack extends by $\delta a$, balanced by the surface energy required to create two new crack faces.

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{\text{elastic}} &= -\dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} \quad &\text{(per unit thickness)} \\
U_{\text{surface}} &= + 2 \pi a \cdot \gamma_s \quad &\text{(per unit thickness)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The total potential energy $U_{total}$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{total} &= U_{\text{elastic}} + U_{\text{surface}} \\
&= -\dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} + 2 \pi a \cdot \gamma_s
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dU_{total}}{da} = 0$ at the critical (equilibrium) condition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dU_{total}}{da} &= -\dfrac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 2 \pi \gamma_s = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the critical applied stress $\sigma_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f^2 &= \dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{a} \\
\sigma_f &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{a}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** As the flaw size $a$ inside the WBL **increases**, the joint strength $\sigma_f$ **decreases** as $1/\sqrt{a}$. This is why contamination (which *is* the flaw) so dramatically weakens an adhesive joint — even a micrometre-scale contamination layer can cut joint strength by an order of magnitude.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Does the Failure Follow WBL?

| Observation in Failed Joint | Implies WBL? | Action Required |
|---|---|---|
| Failure is "apparently adhesive" but substrate shows ghosting / residue on **both** sides | **Yes** — failure inside the WBL | Improve surface cleaning |
| Failure reveals a **glassy, brittle, smooth** fracture surface | **Yes** — WBL is a thin glassy oligomer layer | Adjust cure cycle to drive off volatiles |
| Failure is truly **cohesive** inside the bulk adhesive | **No** — bulk adhesive is the weak link | Reformulate adhesive, increase crosslink density |
| Failure occurs in the **substrate** (e.g., fibre tear in composite, steel pull-out) | **No** — substrate is weakest | Acceptable failure mode (desirable) |
| Substrate surface appears **clean** but white residue is seen | **Yes** — Type II substrate WBL (oxide, mould release) | Apply primer / coupling agent |
| Joint strength improves with **solvent wipe + abrasion** | **Yes** — confirms WBL was present | Standardize surface prep protocol |

### 3.3 Mathematical Estimation of WBL Strength Loss

Suppose an epoxy adhesive has bulk cohesive strength $\sigma_{\text{bulk}} = 50$ MPa. The WBL has Young's modulus $E_{\text{WBL}} = 1.5$ GPa and fracture surface energy $\gamma_s = 0.5$ J/m². The WBL contains a contaminant-induced flaw of size $a = 5 \mu\text{m}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.5}{5 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^{-6}}} \\
&= \sqrt{3.0 \times 10^{14}} \\
&\approx 1.73 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 17.3 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the WBL fails at **~17.3 MPa**, while the bulk adhesive can sustain **~50 MPa** — a strength loss of **~65%** purely due to a micrometre-scale weak layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Weak Boundary Layer Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph bulkAdh["BULK ADHESIVE LAYER (Strength ~50 MPa)"]
        direction TB
        adh1["Crosslinked epoxy network"]
        adh2["Toughened polymer chains"]
        adh3["Filler particles"]
    end

    subgraph wblLayer["WEAK BOUNDARY LAYER (Thickness 10 nm - 1 um)"]
        direction TB
        wbl1["Unreacted monomer / oligomer"]
        wbl2["Moisture film"]
        wbl3["Trapped air pocket"]
        wbl4["Reaction product e.g. metal soap"]
        wbl5["Diffused plasticizer"]
    end

    subgraph prepSub["PREPARED SUBSTRATE SURFACE"]
        direction TB
        sub1["Chemical oxide layer"]
        sub2["Bulk adherend metal"]
    end

    load["APPLIED TENSILE LOAD"] --> adh1
    adh3 --> wblLayer
    wblLayer --> prepSub

    wbl1 -.->|"Crack nucleation"| fracture
    wbl2 -.->|"Stress concentration"| fracture
    wbl3 -.->|"Hydrolysis site"| fracture
    wbl4 -.->|"Brittle cleavage plane"| fracture
    wbl5 -.->|"Plasticization"| fracture

    fracture["COHESIVE FAILURE WITHIN WBL\n(~17 MPa, well below bulk strength)"]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Formation of WBL over Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Surface preparation"] --> B["Step 2: Time delay before bonding"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Adsorption of contaminants"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Diffusion of low-MW species"]
    D --> E["Step 5: WBL nucleates and grows"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Adhesive application over WBL"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Cure - WBL now sandwiched"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Failure in WBL under load"]
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — Clean Joint vs. WBL-Contaminated Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clean["CLEAN INTERFACE - STRONG JOINT"]
        c1["Bulk Adhesive (50 MPa)"] --- c2["Clean Substrate Surface"]
        c2 --- c3["Bulk Adherend"]
    end

    subgraph dirty["WBL CONTAMINATED INTERFACE - WEAK JOINT"]
        d1["Bulk Adhesive (50 MPa)"] --- d2["Weak Boundary Layer (~17 MPa)"]
        d2 --- d3["Substrate"]
        d3 --- d4["Bulk Adherend"]
    end

    clean -->|"Failure at ~48 MPa in bulk adhesive"| cf["Cohesive failure in adhesive - desirable"]
    dirty -->|"Failure at ~17 MPa inside WBL"| df["Apparent adhesive failure - undesirable"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *State the Weak Boundary Layer Theory of adhesion. Who proposed it?*

> **Model Answer:**
> The Weak Boundary Layer (WBL) Theory was proposed by **J. J. Bikerman in 1961**. It states that true interfacial failure between an adhesive and a substrate is virtually impossible under practical conditions. Instead, failure always occurs **cohesively within a thin, mechanically weak layer** that exists at or near the interface. This weak boundary layer may consist of contaminants, oxide layers, unreacted monomers, moisture, or reaction products. The measured "adhesive strength" is therefore the **cohesive strength of this weak layer**, not the true interfacial adhesion.
> *(3 marks — 1 mark for proposer + year, 2 marks for the statement)*

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** *List four types of weak boundary layers with one example of each.*

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Adhesive-side WBL** — e.g., migration of plasticizer or unreacted epoxy monomer to the interface.
> 2. **Substrate-side WBL** — e.g., a friable aluminium oxide (boehmite) layer.
> 3. **Environmental WBL** — e.g., moisture film or fingerprint oil contamination.
> 4. **Reaction-product WBL** — e.g., metal-soap formation between acidic adhesive species and metal substrate.
> *(3 marks — ½ mark each for type + ¼ mark each for example, properly enumerated)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Explain in detail the **Weak Boundary Layer Theory** of adhesion. Discuss the various **origins and types** of weak boundary layers that may form in a practical adhesive joint.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A structural adhesive joint fails apparently at the interface. Using the Griffith fracture criterion, **derive** an expression for the strength of the weak boundary layer and show how a contamination flaw of size **5 µm** reduces the joint strength from 50 MPa to approximately 17 MPa. Assume $E_{\text{WBL}} = 1.5$ GPa and $\gamma_s = 0.5$ J/m².

> **Model Answer for Part (a):**
>
> **Definition:** Bikerman (1961) proposed that true interfacial failure is practically non-existent. The locus of failure is always cohesive, occurring within the weakest layer present at or near the joint interface. This layer is called the **Weak Boundary Layer (WBL)**.
>
> **Origins of WBL:**
> 1. **Incomplete wetting / entrapped air:** Surface roughness traps air bubbles that prevent molecular contact.
> 2. **Migration of low-MW species:** Plasticizers, residual solvents, and oligomers diffuse to the interface over time, driven by thermodynamic compatibility.
> 3. **Substrate-side contamination:** Loose oxide scales, mill scale, release agents, oils, and dust.
> 4. **Environmental contamination:** Moisture, hydrocarbons from atmosphere, handling fingerprints.
> 5. **Reaction-product layer:** Chemical reaction between adhesive and substrate (e.g., metal soap, oxide intergrowth).
>
> **Types of WBL:**
> - **Type I — Adhesive-origin** (e.g., unreacted epoxy)
> - **Type II — Substrate-origin** (e.g., loose oxide)
> - **Type III — Environmental** (e.g., moisture, oil)
> - **Type IV — Reaction-product** (e.g., metal-soap)
>
> **Implication:** Surface preparation (cleaning, abrasion, chemical etching, priming) is critical because it removes or strengthens the WBL. *(7 marks)*

> **Model Answer for Part (b):**
>
> **Griffith fracture criterion derivation:**
>
> Strain energy released per unit crack advance:
> $$\begin{aligned} U_{\text{elastic}} &= -\dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} \end{aligned}$$
>
> New surface energy created:
> $$\begin{aligned} U_{\text{surface}} &= +2 \pi a \, \gamma_s \end{aligned}$$
>
> Total potential energy:
> $$\begin{aligned} U_{total} &= -\dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2}{E} + 2 \pi a \, \gamma_s \end{aligned}$$
>
> Equilibrium condition $\dfrac{dU_{total}}{da}=0$:
> $$\begin{aligned} -\dfrac{2 \pi a \sigma^2}{E} + 2 \pi \gamma_s &= 0 \end{aligned}$$
> $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_f &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \, \gamma_s}{a}} \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Numerical evaluation with $a = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $E = 1.5 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $\gamma_s = 0.5$ J/m²:**
> $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_f &= \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9} \times 0.5}{5 \times 10^{-6}}} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{-6}}} \\[4pt] &= \sqrt{3 \times 10^{14}} \\[4pt] &\approx 1.73 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\[4pt] &\approx 17.3 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$
>
> Thus the WBL strength (~17 MPa) is **~65 % lower** than the bulk adhesive (~50 MPa). The flaw in the WBL is responsible for the catastrophic loss in joint strength. *(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 3 for numerical evaluation and interpretation)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Discuss the **role of surface preparation** in the context of Weak Boundary Layer Theory. Explain why mechanical abrasion, solvent degreasing, and chemical etching are essential pre-bonding steps.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* A WBL of thickness 200 nm grows by Fickian diffusion of a low-MW contaminant into the bonding zone. The diffusion coefficient is $D = 1 \times 10^{-14}$ m²/s. Calculate the **exposure time** required for this WBL to form. Comment on the engineering implications.

> **Model Answer for Part (a):**
>
> **Why surface preparation matters (link to WBL Theory):**
> Since the WBL is the weakest link in the joint, removing or strengthening it directly improves joint performance. Bikerman emphasized that *the practical strength of an adhesive joint is determined by the cohesive strength of the weakest layer present at the interface — not by the intrinsic adhesive properties.*
>
> **Mechanical Abrasion:**
> - Removes **Type II WBL** (loose oxide scales, mill scale, rust).
> - Creates controlled micro-roughness that increases **mechanical interlocking** and improves wetting.
> - Exposes a fresh, chemically active substrate surface.
>
> **Solvent Degreasing:**
> - Removes **Type III WBL** (oils, greases, fingerprints, hydrocarbons).
> - Acetone, IPA, or MEK are commonly used in a **three-stage wipe** protocol.
> - Must precede abrasion to avoid driving contaminants into the surface.
>
> **Chemical Etching:**
> - Removes tenacious **Type IV WBL** (oxide layers, reaction products).
> - Creates a stable, reproducible oxide of known chemistry (e.g., chromic-sulfuric etch for Al alloys per **ASTM D2651**).
> - Promotes formation of **chemical bonds** (with primers / coupling agents) in addition to removing the weak layer.
>
> **Key takeaway:** The order of preparation — *degrease → abrade → re-degrease → prime* — is critical. Abrading a greasy surface only smears the WBL deeper into the substrate. *(7 marks)*

> **Model Answer for Part (b):**
>
> **Fickian diffusion layer growth:**
> $$\begin{aligned} t_{\text{WBL}} &\approx \sqrt{D \cdot t_{\text{exp}}} \end{aligned}$$
>
> Solving for $t_{\text{exp}}$ with $t_{\text{WBL}} = 200 \text{ nm} = 2 \times 10^{-7}$ m and $D = 1 \times 10^{-14}$ m²/s:
> $$\begin{aligned} t_{\text{exp}} &= \dfrac{t_{\text{WBL}}^{2}}{D} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-7})^{2}}{1 \times 10^{-14}} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-14}}{1 \times 10^{-14}} \\[4pt] &= 4 \text{ seconds} \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Comment:** The WBL reaches a thickness of 200 nm in only **4 seconds** of exposure! This means that any delay between surface preparation and adhesive application — even brief handling or storage in non-cleanroom conditions — can compromise the joint. In practice:
> - Bonding should occur **immediately** after surface preparation.
> - If delay is unavoidable, the substrate must be **re-cleaned** or stored under **dry, inert atmosphere**.
> - This is why aerospace and biomedical bonding specifications (e.g., **NASA-STD-5001**, **ASTM E864**) limit the time between preparation and bonding to **≤ 30 minutes** in cleanroom conditions.
> *(7 marks — 4 for derivation and calculation, 3 for engineering interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. *Mentioning Bikerman and the year (1961)* is **mandatory** — students frequently omit the proposer's name and lose ½ mark.
> 2. In numerical problems, *units must be carried explicitly through every line* — converting µm → m, GPa → Pa, etc. A 1-mark penalty applies if unit inconsistency is present.
> 3. The answer should clearly distinguish **interfacial (true adhesive) failure** from **cohesive failure within the WBL** — examiners test whether students understand the central *paradox* of the theory.
> 4. For derivation questions, **every algebraic step** is expected. Skipping directly from $U_{total}$ to $\sigma_f$ without differentiation loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **WBL Theory proposed by J. J. Bikerman (1961).**
- ✅ True interfacial failure is practically non-existent; failure is always **cohesive** in the weakest layer.
- ✅ **Four classes of WBL:** Adhesive-side, Substrate-side, Environmental, Reaction-product.
- ✅ **Bikerman's bond strength postulate:** $F_{\text{bond}} = \eta \cdot \gamma_{LV}$
- ✅ **Griffith fracture criterion:** $\sigma_f = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{a}}$ — flaw size $a$ in WBL is the dominant variable.
- ✅ **Fickian growth of WBL:** $t_{\text{WBL}} \approx \sqrt{D \cdot t_{\text{exp}}}$ — a 200 nm WBL forms in only seconds.
- ✅ Surface preparation (degrease → abrade → re-degrease → prime) is the **direct engineering response** to WBL Theory.
- ✅ Practical joints always have a WBL; *measured joint strength = cohesive strength of WBL*.
- ✅ WBL Theory explains why **primers, coupling agents, and surface treatments** are indispensable for structural bonding.
- ✅ Standard reference surfaces for testing: clean aluminium per **ASTM D2651**, steel per **ASTM D2541**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification of Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Classification of Adhesives** refers to the systematic arrangement of adhesive materials into distinct categories based on their chemical origin, physical form, structural behavior, setting/curing mechanism, and end-use application. This taxonomy is essential for proper adhesive selection in engineering design, since the choice of adhesive governs joint strength, durability, service temperature, and substrate compatibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Adhesives are broadly classified based on (a) origin (Natural & Synthetic), (b) chemical nature (Organic & Inorganic), (c) physical form (Liquid, Paste, Solid film, Powder), (d) setting mechanism (Drying, Chemical reaction, Cooling from melt, Pressure-sensitive), and (e) structural behavior (Thermoplastic, Thermosetting, Elastomeric).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toolbox Intuition"

Imagine a **doctor's medical kit**. Just as a doctor classifies medicines as *antibiotics*, *painkillers*, *antiseptics*, and *vaccines* based on **how they work and what they treat**, an adhesive engineer classifies adhesives based on **how they set, where they come from, and what they bond**.

- **Natural adhesives** are like *traditional home remedies* (turmeric paste, animal glue) — old, reliable, but limited in strength and durability.
- **Synthetic adhesives** are like *modern pharmaceuticals* — engineered in a lab, with precise control over strength, temperature resistance, and chemical resistance.
- **Hot-melt adhesives** are like *hot wax* — apply hot, it cools and sticks instantly.
- **Pressure-sensitive adhesives (PSA)** are like *reusable sticky notes* — they stick on light pressure and can be peeled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Every adhesive you will study in this course belongs to one (or more) of the classification buckets. Identifying the bucket is the **first step** in any adhesive selection problem.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Classification

The following physical and chemical metrics are used by KTU examiners to compare adhesive classes:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lap Shear Strength | $\tau$ | **MPa** (N/mm²) | Measures joint strength |
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ | **°C** or **K** | Defines service temperature limit |
| Viscosity | $\eta$ | **Pa·s** or **cP** | Indicates flow/application behavior |
| Solid Content | $S$ | **% by mass** | Amount of non-volatile matter |
| Curing Time | $t_c$ | **minutes / hours** | Production throughput |
| Service Temperature Range | $\Delta T$ | **°C** | Operating window |
| Pot Life | $t_p$ | **minutes** | Working time after mixing |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification Framework

Adhesives are classified along **five primary axes**. Each axis answers a different engineering question.

### 2.1.1 Classification by **Origin / Source**

**Step 1 — Natural Adhesives (Bio-based)**
- Sub-category: **Animal-based** — *Hide glue, casein, blood albumin*
- Sub-category: **Vegetable-based** — *Starch, dextrin, natural rubber, gum arabic*
- Sub-category: **Mineral-based** — *Asphalt, bitumen, sodium silicate (water glass)*

**Step 2 — Synthetic Adhesives (Engineered polymers)**
- Sub-category: **Thermoplastic** — soften on heating (e.g., PVA, EVA, polyamides)
- Sub-category: **Thermosetting** — cross-link irreversibly (e.g., epoxy, phenolic, urea-formaldehyde)
- Sub-category: **Elastomeric** — flexible, rubbery (e.g., neoprene, silicone, polyurethane)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A 3-mark question frequently asks *“Differentiate between natural and synthetic adhesives with examples.”* Always remember: natural = bio-derived, eco-friendly, low strength; synthetic = lab-made, high strength, tailorable.

### 2.1.2 Classification by **Chemical Nature**

- **Organic adhesives** — carbon-based polymers (most common: epoxies, acrylics, polyurethanes)
- **Inorganic adhesives** — non-carbon systems (sodium silicate, ceramic-based adhesives, phosphoric acid-based binders)

### 2.1.3 Classification by **Physical Form**

| Physical Form | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid (solvent-based) | Polymer dissolved in volatile solvent | Contact adhesives |
| Liquid (water-based) | Polymer emulsion in water | PVA emulsion |
| Paste / Mastic | High-viscosity, gap-filling | Epoxy paste |
| Powder | Solid resin, mixed with water before use | Urea-formaldehyde powder |
| Film / Tape | Pre-cast dry adhesive layer | Epoxy film, pressure-sensitive tape |
| Hot-melt stick | Solid, applied molten | EVA hot-melt glue sticks |
| Solventless (100% solids) | No volatiles, cures chemically | Two-part epoxies |

### 2.1.4 Classification by **Setting / Curing Mechanism**

This is the **most important classification** for KTU 2024 and carries the highest weightage in Part B questions.

**Mechanism 1 — Loss of Solvent (Drying)**
- Solvent evaporates → polymer film solidifies → bond forms
- Example: **PVA emulsion, contact adhesives, dextrin**

**Mechanism 2 — Loss of Water (Drying)**
- Water evaporates from dispersion → solid bond
- Example: **Starch paste, animal glue**

**Mechanism 3 — Chemical Reaction (Curing)**
- Two components react (or heat triggers cross-linking) → irreversible hardening
- Sub-types:
  - Polymerization (acrylics, unsaturated polyesters)
  - Polyaddition (epoxies, polyurethanes)
  - Polycondensation (phenolic, silicone — releases water)
- Example: **Two-part epoxy, phenolic resin**

**Mechanism 4 — Cooling from Melt (Hot-melt)**
- Apply molten → cool → solidify (no chemical change)
- Example: **EVA hot-melt, polyamide hot-melt**

**Mechanism 5 — Pressure-Sensitive (Tack-based)**
- Permanent tack at room temperature; bonds under light pressure, no chemical change
- Example: **Tape, labels, medical adhesives**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Rule of Thumb:** When asked to select an adhesive for a *fast production line*, choose **hot-melt** (Mechanism 4). For *high-strength structural joints*, choose **chemical reaction** (Mechanism 3). For *temporary/removable bonds*, choose **pressure-sensitive** (Mechanism 5).

### 2.1.5 Classification by **Structural / Load-Bearing Behavior**

- **Structural adhesives** — Designed to carry high sustained loads ($ \tau \geq 7\,\text{MPa} $ typically). Examples: epoxies, modified acrylics, polyurethanes.
- **Non-structural adhesives** — Low-load, temporary, or sealing applications. Examples: PVA, dextrin, pressure-sensitive tapes.
- **Semi-structural adhesives** — Intermediate. Examples: contact adhesives, some elastomeric systems.

### 2.1.6 Classification by **Application Temperature**

- **Room-temperature curing** — Epoxies (with hardener), PVA, contact adhesives
- **Heat-curing** — Phenolic, melamine-formaldehyde, some epoxies ($T = 120{-}180\,°C$)
- **Low-temperature curing** — Specialty acrylics

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Lap shear strength (force over bond area) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{shear}}}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Dynamic viscosity definition | $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| 3 | $T_g \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_m$ (Boyer-Beaman rule, K) | Approx. relation for symmetric polymers | $\text{K}$ |
| 4 | $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_0}{C}\right)$ | First-order curing kinetics | $\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $E_a = R \cdot \dfrac{\ln(t_{c2}/t_{c1})}{1/T_1 - 1/T_2}$ | Arrhenius-type activation energy of cure | $\text{J/mol}$ |
| 6 | $S_{\%} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$ | Solid content of adhesive | $\%$ |
| 7 | $W_a = 2\sqrt{\gamma_1^d \cdot \gamma_2^d} + 2\sqrt{\gamma_1^p \cdot \gamma_2^p}$ | Work of adhesion (Fowkes-Owens-Wendt) | $\text{mJ/m}^2$ |
| 8 | $\gamma_c = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young-Dupré wetting relation | $\text{mN/m}$ |
| 9 | $t_p \approx \dfrac{V_{\text{resin}}}{\dot{V}_{\text{reaction}}}$ | Pot life estimation | $\text{min}$ |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1-\nu}$ | Thermal residual stress in bonded joint | $\text{MPa}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equation 7 (Work of adhesion, Fowkes-Owens-Wendt) is **frequently asked in Part B**. Memorize the dispersive ($d$) and polar ($p$) components — they govern wettability and bond strength.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Preferred Adhesive Class | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Epoxy (thermosetting) | High strength-to-weight, fatigue resistance |
| Automotive | Structural acrylic, PU, hot-melt | Crash durability + fast assembly |
| Electronics | Epoxy, silicone, PSA | Electrical insulation, thermal conductivity |
| Packaging | EVA hot-melt, dextrin | Fast set, food-safe |
| Construction | Phenolic, epoxy, cement-based | Load-bearing, weather resistance |
| Footwear | Neoprene (contact), PU | Flexibility, water resistance |
| Medical | Silicone, PSA, cyanoacrylate | Biocompatibility, skin-safe |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Classification Decision Logic for Adhesive Selection

The following derivation builds the **Decision Matrix** that engineers use to classify (and subsequently select) an adhesive for a given application. Each criterion is weighted by its importance factor.

### Step 1 — Define the governing performance equation for a bonded joint

The average shear stress in a single-lap joint is:

$$
\bar{\tau} = \frac{F}{w \cdot L}
$$

where $F$ is the applied tensile force (N), $w$ is the bond width (mm), and $L$ is the bond overlap length (mm).

**Conversion logic:** The numerator is the load the joint must carry; the denominator is the load-bearing cross-section of the adhesive layer. The quotient gives stress in N/mm² = MPa.

### Step 2 — Apply the thermal-residual stress correction

When the adhesive cures at elevated temperature, differential thermal contraction between adherend and adhesive generates a residual thermal shear stress:

$$
\sigma_{\text{res}} = \frac{E_a \, ( \alpha_s - \alpha_a ) \, \Delta T}{1 - \nu_a}
$$

where $E_a$ is the adhesive Young's modulus, $\alpha_s$ and $\alpha_a$ are the linear thermal expansion coefficients of substrate and adhesive respectively, $\Delta T$ is the temperature drop from cure to service, and $\nu_a$ is the Poisson's ratio of the adhesive.

**Conversion logic:** Each term is dimensionless except $E_a$ (MPa) and $\Delta T$ (K or °C), giving the resulting stress in MPa.

### Step 3 — Apply the safety-factor based design equation

For reliable design, the **effective working stress** must be lower than the **classification-rated strength**:

$$
\tau_{\text{working}} = \frac{\bar{\tau} - \sigma_{\text{res}}}{n_s} \;\leq\; \tau_{\text{allowable,class}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Subtract the parasitic residual stress, divide by the safety factor $n_s$ (typically 3–5 for structural joints), and compare against the allowable stress for the adhesive class. If inequality holds, the chosen class is safe.

### Step 4 — Set up the classification decision matrix

Define each criterion and its weight:

$$
\text{Score}_{i} = w_1 S_{\text{strength}} + w_2 S_{\text{Temp}} + w_3 S_{\text{chem}} + w_4 S_{\text{speed}} + w_5 S_{\text{cost}}
$$

where the weights satisfy:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{5} w_j = 1
$$

**Conversion logic:** Each $S$ is a normalized score (0–10) for the $i^{\text{th}}$ adhesive class against criterion $j$. The weights reflect the engineer's priority. The class with the highest Score$_i$ wins.

### Step 5 — Worked Example

A packaging line requires bonding of paperboard at 50 m/min. Service temp = 25 °C, max load = 5 N/cm², cost-sensitive. Compute scores for **PVA (drying)**, **EVA hot-melt**, and **Starch (drying)**.

Assume weights: strength $w_1=0.15$, temp $w_2=0.10$, chem $w_3=0.10$, speed $w_4=0.40$, cost $w_5=0.25$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{PVA}} &= (0.15)(6) + (0.10)(5) + (0.10)(7) + (0.40)(5) + (0.25)(9) \\
&= 0.90 + 0.50 + 0.70 + 2.00 + 2.25 = 6.35
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{EVA}} &= (0.15)(7) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(7) + (0.40)(10) + (0.25)(6) \\
&= 1.05 + 0.60 + 0.70 + 4.00 + 1.50 = 7.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}_{\text{Starch}} &= (0.15)(3) + (0.10)(3) + (0.10)(8) + (0.40)(4) + (0.25)(10) \\
&= 0.45 + 0.30 + 0.80 + 1.60 + 2.50 = 5.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** EVA hot-melt wins (Score = 7.85). The classification **Mechanism 4 (Cooling from melt)** is the correct engineering choice.

**Conversion logic:** The matrix is dimensionless because each term is a weight (sum = 1) multiplied by a unit-less normalized score. A higher score means a better fit to the engineering specification.

## 3.2 Comprehensive Classification Matrix (Reference Table)

| Class | Origin | Form | Setting | Structural? | Example | $T_g$ (°C) | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Animal glue | Natural | Liquid/Solid | Loss of water | No | Hide glue | ~30 | Reversible |
| Starch | Natural | Paste | Loss of water | No | Corn starch | ~25 | Cheap, eco |
| Casein | Natural | Powder+liquid | Chemical | No | Wood glue (old) | ~50 | Water-resistant |
| Natural rubber | Natural | Solvent solution | Loss of solvent | Semi | Contact cement | -70 | Flexible |
| Bitumen | Mineral | Hot-melt | Cooling | No | Roofing | ~30 | Waterproof |
| PVA | Synthetic (T-plastic) | Emulsion | Loss of water | No | Wood glue (white) | ~30 | General purpose |
| EVA | Synthetic (T-plastic) | Hot-melt stick | Cooling from melt | No | Packaging | -30 to -10 | Fast set |
| Acrylic | Synthetic (T-setting) | Liquid | Polymerization | Yes | Structural methacrylate | ~80 | Fast cure |
| Epoxy | Synthetic (T-setting) | Two-part paste | Polyaddition | Yes | Aerospace bonds | ~120 | High strength |
| Phenolic | Synthetic (T-setting) | Liquid/solvent | Polycondensation + heat | Yes | Brake bonds | ~250 | Heat resistant |
| PU | Synthetic (T-setting) | One/two-part | Polyaddition | Yes | Shoe soles | -50 | Flexible, tough |
| Silicone | Synthetic (elastomer) | Paste | Moisture cure | Semi | Sealants | -120 | Temp range |
| Neoprene | Synthetic (elastomer) | Solvent contact | Loss of solvent + cryst. | Semi | Shoe contact | -40 | Contact bond |
| Cyanoacrylate | Synthetic (T-setting) | Liquid | Anionic polymerization | Yes | Super glue | ~60 | Instant bond |
| PSA | Synthetic (T-plastic) | Tape/film | Pressure | No | Tape, labels | -30 | Removable |
| Sodium silicate | Inorganic | Liquid | Loss of water | No | Cardboard, furnace | — | Heat resistant |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid

    B4 --> C4A[Loss of Solvent]

    classDef sub fill:#e9c46a,color:#000000,stroke:#5a3a00,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Setting Mechanism Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT STATE"]
        I1[Liquid Adhesive - Solvent Based]
        I2[Liquid Adhesive - Water Based]
        I3[Two Part Reactive System]
        I4[Molten Thermoplastic]
        I5[Pressure Sensitive Film]
    end

    subgraph TriggerLayer["ACTIVATION TRIGGER"]
        T1[Solvent Evaporation]
        T2[Water Evaporation]
        T3[Chemical Reaction - Heat or RT]
        T4[Heat Loss to Environment]
        T5[Light Contact Pressure]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["FINAL BONDED STATE"]
        O1[Dried Polymer Film - Reversible]
        O2[Dried Dispersion Film - Reversible]
        O3[Crosslinked Network - Irreversible]
        O4[Solidified Melt - Reversible]
        O5[Viscoelastic Contact Bond - Removable]
    end

    I1 --> T1 --> O1
    I2 --> T2 --> O2
    I3 --> T3 --> O3
    I4 --> T4 --> O4
    I5 --> T5 --> O5
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix Flow — How an Engineer Classifies a New Adhesive

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Q3 -->|Yes, reversible| N3[Thermoplastic]

    Q4 -->|Yes| N4[Elastomeric]
    Q4 -->|No| N5[Thermosetting]

    N1 --> S1{Setting mechanism?}
    N3 --> S1
    N4 --> S1
    N5 --> S1
    N2 --> S1

    S1 --> R1[Solvent Loss]
    S1 --> R2[Water Loss]
    S1 --> R3[Chemical Cure]
    S1 --> R4[Melt Cooling]
    S1 --> R5[Pressure Sensitive]

    R1 --> FINAL[Class Code - Eg ST-NAT-SOLV]
    R2 --> FINAL
    R3 --> FINAL
    R4 --> FINAL
    R5 --> FINAL

    classDef decision fill:#2a9d8f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a4a44,stroke-width:2px
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Classify adhesives based on their origin. Give two examples for each class."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Adhesives are classified by origin into two main classes:

1. **Natural Adhesives** — derived from plant, animal, or mineral sources.
   - *Animal origin:* Hide glue, casein (from milk) (1 mark)
   - *Vegetable origin:* Starch paste, dextrin, gum arabic, natural rubber (1 mark)
   - *Mineral origin:* Bitumen, asphalt, sodium silicate

2. **Synthetic Adhesives** — chemically synthesized polymers.
   - *Thermoplastic:* Polyvinyl acetate (PVA), Ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) (1 mark)
   - *Thermosetting:* Epoxy, phenolic, urea-formaldehyde
   - *Elastomeric:* Neoprene, silicone, polyurethane

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write *“PVA is a natural adhesive”* — INCORRECT. PVA is fully synthetic. Examiners deduct 1 mark for confusing natural with synthetic.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**"Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting adhesives with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting |
|---|---|---|
| Effect of heating | Softens and melts (reversible) | Does not soften; decomposes (irreversible) |
| Cross-linking | None (linear chains) | Permanent 3D network |
| Strength | Lower, creep under load | Higher, creep-resistant |
| Removability | Can be reactivated by heat/solvent | Cannot be remelted |
| Example (1 mark) | **PVA, EVA, hot-melt polyamide** (0.5 mark) | **Epoxy, phenolic, urea-formaldehyde** (0.5 mark) |
| Typical use | Packaging, bookbinding, shoe | Aerospace, automotive, electronics |

Mechanism: Thermoplastics bond by physical solidification; thermosets bond by irreversible chemical cross-linking (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing *“thermoplastic adhesives are stronger than thermosetting”* is the **most common wrong statement** in this question. Reversed — thermosets are stronger.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** Classify adhesives based on **setting mechanism**. Discuss each mechanism with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A structural joint must carry $F = 4000$ N across a bond area $A = 20\,\text{mm} \times 25\,\text{mm}$. The curing occurs at $T_{\text{cure}} = 120\,°C$ and the service temperature is $T_{\text{service}} = 25\,°C$. Given $E_a = 3.0$ GPa, $\nu_a = 0.35$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,°C^{-1}$, $\alpha_a = 60 \times 10^{-6}\,°C^{-1}$, and safety factor $n_s = 4$. Determine if the joint is safe, if the adhesive class is **epoxy** with allowable strength $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 20$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Classification by Setting Mechanism (7 marks):**

1. **Loss of Solvent (1.5 marks)**
   - Solvent evaporates, leaving a solid polymer film that bonds by mechanical interlocking and van der Waals forces.
   - Examples: Contact adhesives (neoprene in toluene), PVC pipe cement.

2. **Loss of Water (1 mark)**
   - Water evaporates from an aqueous dispersion or solution; common in paper, wood, and packaging.
   - Examples: Starch paste, dextrin, animal glue (hide glue), PVA emulsion.

3. **Chemical Reaction (Curing) (2.5 marks)**
   - Irreversible cross-linking via three sub-mechanisms:
     - *Polymerization* (chain-growth): Acrylics, cyanoacrylates, unsaturated polyesters
     - *Polyaddition* (step-growth, no by-product): Epoxies (epoxide + amine), polyurethanes (isocyanate + polyol)
     - *Polycondensation* (step-growth, releases by-product such as water or HCl): Phenolic, urea-formaldehyde, melamine-formaldehyde
   - Examples: Two-part epoxy paste, heat-cured phenolic.

4. **Cooling from Melt — Hot-melt (1 mark)**
   - Applied molten; solidifies on cooling. No chemical change; bond is reversible by re-heating.
   - Examples: EVA glue sticks, polyamide hot-melt.

5. **Pressure-Sensitive (1 mark)**
   - Permanent surface tack; bonds instantly under light finger pressure at room temperature.
   - Examples: Cellophane tape, medical skin adhesives, labels.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $F = 4000\,\text{N}$, $A = 20 \times 25 = 500\,\text{mm}^2$, $T_{\text{cure}} = 120\,°C$, $T_{\text{service}} = 25\,°C$, $E_a = 3000\,\text{MPa}$, $\nu_a = 0.35$, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,°C^{-1}$, $\alpha_a = 60 \times 10^{-6}\,°C^{-1}$, $n_s = 4$, $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 20\,\text{MPa}$.

**Step 1 — Compute average shear stress (2 marks):**

$$
\bar{\tau} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{4000}{500} = 8.0\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute temperature drop (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{cure}} - T_{\text{service}} = 120 - 25 = 95\,°C
$$

**Step 3 — Compute residual thermal stress (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{res}} = \frac{E_a \, (\alpha_s - \alpha_a) \, \Delta T}{1 - \nu_a}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{res}} = \frac{3000 \times (12 - 60) \times 10^{-6} \times 95}{1 - 0.35}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{res}} = \frac{3000 \times (-48 \times 10^{-6}) \times 95}{0.65}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{res}} = \frac{-13.68}{0.65} = -21.05\,\text{MPa}
$$

(The negative sign indicates the substrate contracts more than the adhesive, putting the adhesive in **tension** — a dangerous condition. For safety we use the magnitude.)

**Step 4 — Compute effective working stress (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{\text{working}} = \frac{\bar{\tau} + \sigma_{\text{res}}}{n_s} = \frac{8.0 + 21.05}{4} = 7.26\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with allowable (1 mark):**

$$
\tau_{\text{working}} = 7.26\,\text{MPa} \;\leq\; \tau_{\text{allow}} = 20\,\text{MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** The joint is **safe** under the given loading, but the high residual stress ($\sim 21$ MPa) is a red flag — using a lower-modulus adhesive or a closer-matched CTE would improve durability.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Q1A b):** Many students forget the safety factor $n_s$ or take the **difference** of $\alpha_s$ in the wrong order. Always check: $\alpha_a > \alpha_s$ is the common case (adhesive expands more), producing **tensile** residual stress in the adhesive. The examiner awards 0 if signs are ignored.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Classify adhesives based on **structural behavior** and explain why structural adhesives are used in aerospace applications. Give at least three properties required of a structural adhesive. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the following adhesive classes on the basis of origin, setting mechanism, and one key engineering application: (i) Epoxy, (ii) PVA emulsion, (iii) EVA hot-melt, (iv) Cyanoacrylate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

**Classification by Structural Behavior (4 marks):**

1. **Structural Adhesives** — Designed to carry sustained, high-magnitude loads without failure. Typical lap shear strength $> 7$ MPa. Examples: Epoxy, phenolic, modified acrylic, structural PU, cyanoacrylate. (1.5 marks)

2. **Semi-Structural Adhesives** — Carry moderate loads; combine flexibility and adhesion. Examples: PU shoe adhesives, neoprene contact adhesives, hot-melt PU. (1 mark)

3. **Non-Structural Adhesives** — Used for sealing, holding, or temporary fixation; not load-bearing. Examples: PVA, dextrin, pressure-sensitive tape, starch. (1.5 marks)

**Why Structural Adhesives in Aerospace? (2 marks):**
- Weight saving: rivets add weight; bonded joints reduce mass.
- Fatigue resistance: adhesives distribute stress over the entire bond area, eliminating stress concentrations.
- Corrosion resistance: sealed bond line prevents galvanic corrosion between dissimilar metals (e.g., aluminum–carbon fiber).
- Smooth aerodynamic surfaces (no rivet heads).

**Three required properties of a structural adhesive (1 mark, list any 3):**
- High lap shear strength ($> 20$ MPa for primary structure)
- High peel strength and fracture toughness
- Resistance to fatigue, creep, and thermal cycling
- Compatibility with composite substrates
- Wide service temperature range ($-55\,°C$ to $+80\,°C$ for aircraft)

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.75 marks per adhesive, spread across three criteria):**

| Adhesive Class | Origin | Setting Mechanism | Key Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Epoxy** | Synthetic (thermosetting polymer) | Chemical reaction — polyaddition of epoxide + amine; no by-product (1 mark for mechanism) | Aerospace composite bonding, electronics potting, structural CFRP joints (1 mark) |
| **PVA Emulsion** | Synthetic (thermoplastic, polyvinyl acetate) | Loss of water from aqueous dispersion (1 mark) | Wood joining, bookbinding, paper lamination, school glue (1 mark) |
| **EVA Hot-Melt** | Synthetic (thermoplastic, ethylene-vinyl acetate) | Cooling from melt (no chemical change); reversible (1 mark) | High-speed packaging, carton sealing, book spine gluing, footwear (1 mark) |
| **Cyanoacrylate** | Synthetic (thermoplastic monomer) | Anionic polymerization triggered by trace moisture on substrate surface; instant set (1 mark) | "Super glue" — rapid field repair, medical tissue adhesive, electronics fixturing (1 mark) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (Question B a):** Many students write *“structural adhesives are used in aerospace because they are cheap”* — **WRONG**. Structural adhesives are *expensive*; aerospace uses them for **performance, weight, and fatigue** reasons — not cost. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this misconception.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Classification of Adhesives**

- **5 primary classification axes:** Origin, Chemical Nature, Physical Form, Setting Mechanism, Structural Behavior.
- **Origin split:** Natural (animal / vegetable / mineral) vs Synthetic (thermoplastic / thermosetting / elastomeric).
- **Setting mechanisms (HIGH-WEIGHTAGE for KTU 2024):** Solvent loss, Water loss, Chemical reaction (polymerization, polyaddition, polycondensation), Melt cooling (hot-melt), Pressure-sensitive.
- **Thermoplastic vs Thermosetting:** Thermoplastic = reversible, softens on heat (PVA, EVA); Thermosetting = irreversible, cross-linked network (Epoxy, Phenolic).
- **Structural vs Non-Structural:** Structural = high load ($> 7$ MPa lap shear, e.g., epoxy); Non-structural = sealing/temporary (PVA, dextrin, PSA).
- **Hot-melt adhesives** cool from melt → no chemical change → reversible. Used in packaging for fast production.
- **Pressure-sensitive adhesives** bond under light contact pressure → no heat or solvent → removable/repositionable.
- **Cyanoacrylate** cures by anionic polymerization initiated by surface moisture — gives "instant" bonds within seconds.
- **Epoxy** sets by polyaddition of epoxide + amine — no by-product — strong, low-shrinkage, structural.
- **Phenolic** sets by polycondensation — releases water as by-product — heat-resistant, used in brake linings.
- **Natural rubber / Neoprene** contact adhesives set by solvent loss + crystallization.
- **Inorganic adhesives** (e.g., sodium silicate) are used where high temperature is required.
- **Service temperature hint:** High $T_g$ adhesives (phenolic, silicone) suit high-temp service; low $T_g$ (EVA, neoprene) suit flexible/sub-ambient use.
- **Wettability (Young-Dupré, Fowkes-Owens-Wendt)** governs adhesive-substrate compatibility; high work of adhesion $W_a$ ⇒ stronger bond.
- **Pot life** is critical for two-part reactive adhesives; plan your bond area within the pot life.
- **Common KTU trap:** Stating *PVA is natural* — WRONG, it is synthetic. Stating *epoxy melts on reheating* — WRONG, it decomposes.
- **Quick recall mnemonic — "NW SPS-M-C":** **N**atural, **W**ater-loss; **S**olvent-loss, **P**olycondensation, **S**etting (chemical), **M**elt cooling, **C**ontact (PSA).

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 3 / TOPIC: CLASSIFICATION OF ADHESIVES**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Natural Adhesives** are those bonding substances obtained directly from plant, animal, or mineral sources, requiring only physical processing (boiling, hydrolysis, extraction, purification) or minor chemical modification. They rely on naturally occurring polymeric substances—primarily **proteins, carbohydrates (polysaccharides), and natural resins**—to develop cohesive and adhesive strength.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Adhesive Science & Technology* (PEPOT861), natural adhesives occupy the foundational block of adhesive technology because they pre-date synthetic polymers by several millennia and continue to serve niche applications owing to their **biodegradability, low toxicity, renewability, and cost-effectiveness**.

---

## 1.2 Broad Classification of Natural Adhesives

| Sl. No. | Source Category | Representative Examples | Active Polymer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Animal-based | Bone glue, Hide glue, Fish glue, Casein glue, Blood albumin glue | Collagen / Phosphoproteins / Albumin |
| **2** | Vegetable-based | Starch paste, Dextrin, Gum arabic, Natural rubber, Cellulose derivatives | Polysaccharides / Polyisoprene |
| **3** | Mineral-based | Sodium silicate, Bitumen, Asphalt | Inorganic silicate / Hydrocarbon resin |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Glue Cabinet" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine opening a traditional Kerala kitchen. The rice starch water draining from washed rice, the **arrowroot (koova) paste** used in ayurveda, the animal-hide glue mixed by cobblers, and the **gum from the neem tree** all illustrate the same engineering principle: **a natural polymer is dissolved/dispersed in water, applied to two surfaces, and as the water evaporates or the polymer coagulates, it locks the substrates together.**

In engineering terms, natural adhesives work via three universal mechanisms:
1. **Mechanical interlocking** after water loss.
2. **Hydrogen bonding** between –OH, –NH₂ and –COOH groups and the substrate.
3. **Coagulation / gelation** of protein or polysaccharide chains.

---

## 1.4 Historical Significance and Modern Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The oldest archaeological evidence of adhesive use dates to **~4000 BCE** (birch-bark tar used in Italy).
> - Egyptians used **animal glue** in wood veneering (~1500 BCE).
> - In India, **lac (shellac)** and **gum arabic** were used in furniture and manuscript binding for centuries.
> - Even today, natural adhesives dominate in **food packaging (starch), pharmaceuticals (gum arabic), bookbinding (animal glue), and corrugated cardboard manufacturing (starch).**

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Evaluating Natural Adhesives

| Metric | Typical Value (KTU reference) |
|---|---|
| **Solid content** | **30–55 %** by weight |
| **Viscosity (at 25 °C)** | **100 – 10,000 cP** |
| **pH** | **4.5 – 9.0** |
| **Gelatin temperature** (animal glue) | **25 – 35 °C** |
| **Storage life** | Limited (microbiological degradation) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Animal-Based Adhesives

### 2.1.1 Bone Glue and Hide Glue (Collagen-derived)

These are obtained by **hydrolytic degradation of collagen** present in animal bones, hides, and connective tissue. The raw material is treated with hot water (≈ 60–80 °C) under controlled pH to break peptide bonds and yield **partially hydrolysed collagen = gelatin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction (simplified):**
> $$\text{Collagen (insoluble)} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, }\Delta} \text{Gelatin (soluble polypeptide)} + \text{Water vapour}$$

**Setting mechanism:** On cooling below the gel point (~30 °C), the random-coil gelatin chains partially reform into helical **collagen-like triple-helix regions**, creating a thermoreversible gel.

**Grades:**
- **High-grade (Hide glue):** light, clear, high gel strength.
- **Lower-grade (Bone glue):** darker, weaker, cheaper.

**Applications:** Wood joinery, plywood, book binding, abrasive paper backing.

---

### 2.1.2 Fish Glue

Prepared from the **skins and heads of cod, haddock, or other fish**. Lower gel strength than mammalian glue, but remains **liquid at room temperature**—a major practical advantage.

| Property | Fish Glue | Bone Glue |
|---|---|---|
| Gel temperature | ~10 °C | ~30 °C |
| Strength | Lower | Higher |
| Storage | Liquid (easy) | Solid cakes |

---

### 2.1.3 Casein Glue (Milk Protein)

**Casein** is precipitated from skimmed milk by acidification (using dilute H₂SO₄ or lactic acid). The dried curd is mixed with hydrated lime [Ca(OH)₂] and sodium salts to form an alkaline-dispersed adhesive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosslinking reaction:**
> $$\text{Casein–COOH} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Casein–COO–Ca}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This converts soluble casein into insoluble **calcium caseinate**, producing a water-resistant bond—unique among protein adhesives of that era.

**KTU fact:** Casein glue was the first **water-resistant wood adhesive**, used in WWII aircraft construction (de Havilland Mosquito).

---

### 2.1.4 Blood Albumin Glue

Obtained from **dried blood serum**. Soluble albumin proteins coagulate on heating (~75 °C) producing a strong, water-resistant film—ideal for **plywood and laminated wood**.

---

## 2.2 Vegetable-Based Adhesives

### 2.2.1 Starch Adhesives (Most Important Natural Adhesive)

Starch is a **polysaccharide of glucose** units linked predominantly by **α-1,4-glycosidic bonds** with **α-1,6 branches** at branch points.

$$\text{Starch (granular)} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, }\Delta} \text{Gelatinised starch paste}$$

**Components:**
- **Amylose** – linear, water-soluble, ~20–30 %.
- **Amylopectin** – highly branched, responsible for paste viscosity.

**Adhesive preparation steps (KTU board exam favourite):**
1. Slurry starch in water.
2. Add **NaOH** (swelling agent / gelatinisation accelerator).
3. Heat to **60–80 °C** with stirring → gelatinisation.
4. Optionally add **borax (Na₂B₄O₇)** to increase tack and viscosity.
5. Cool and store.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common forms:**
> - **Thin-boiling starch** – chemically degraded for low-viscosity pastes.
> - **Oxidised starch** – oxidised with NaOCl, improves clarity and stability.
> - **Dextrin** – pyrolysed/roasted starch (see below).

---

### 2.2.2 Dextrin

Produced by **dry-roasting starch with a mineral acid catalyst (HCl)** at 100–200 °C. Partial hydrolysis and molecular rearrangement yield shorter, branched polysaccharides with **high water solubility and quick tack**.

$$\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat, H}^+} \text{Dextrin (low MW, branched)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

| Type | Conversion | Solubility | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| White dextrin | Mild | Low | General paper |
| Yellow dextrin | High | Very high | Envelopes, gummed tape |

---

### 2.2.3 Gum Arabic (Acacia Gum)

A **highly branched arabinogalactan polysaccharide** exuded by *Acacia senegal* and *Acacia seyal* trees (Sudan, Sahel region). It dissolves in cold water up to 50 % w/w and is the **gold standard edible adhesive**.

**Properties:**
- Low viscosity despite high concentration.
- Excellent emulsifier (used in soft drinks).
- Forms clear, flexible films.

---

### 2.2.4 Natural Rubber (Polyisoprene)

*Hevea brasiliensis* latex contains **cis-1,4-polyisoprene** particles (~30–40 % solids) stabilised by protein. Coagulated with formic or acetic acid to give **crepe rubber**.

$$\text{n CH}_2\text{=C(CH}_3\text{)–CH=CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{natural biosynthesis}} \text{cis-1,4-polyisoprene}$$

**Pressure-sensitive adhesive (PSA)** use: Natural rubber is dissolved in hydrocarbon solvent and compounded with **tackifiers (rosin esters)** and plasticisers to make PSAs for tapes and labels.

---

### 2.2.5 Cellulose Derivatives

Cellulose itself is insoluble. Chemical derivatisation gives:
- **Cellulose nitrate (nitrocellulose):** used in leather cement, model cement.
- **Cellulose acetate:** binder in cigarette filters, photographic films.
- **Methyl cellulose / CMC (sodium carboxymethyl cellulose):** water-soluble wallpaper paste, food thickener.

---

## 2.3 Mineral-Based Natural Adhesives

### 2.3.1 Sodium Silicate (Water Glass)

$$\text{SiO}_2 + \text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{CO}_2$$

Aqueous solution → viscous, alkaline (pH ≈ 11–12). On drying loses water and hardens to a brittle, silica-rich film.

**Applications:** Corrugated cardboard, paper laminating, foundry moulds, fire-proofing.

**Limitation:** Alkaline—corrosive to metals and skin.

### 2.3.2 Bitumen / Asphalt

Naturally occurring or refinery-distilled hydrocarbon mixtures. Used as **hot-melt** or solvent-applied adhesive in **roofing felts, waterproofing membranes, and road construction**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta = \eta_0 \, e^{E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius viscosity–temperature | Predicting paste flow at application temperature |
| $S_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100 \, \%$ | Solid content (mass basis) | Quality control of glue batches |
| $T_{\text{gel}} \approx 30 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (hide glue) | Gel point of animal glue | Determines hot-application requirement |
| $\text{Casein} + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \to \text{Ca-caseinate}$ | Crosslinking | Water-resistance mechanism |
| $\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Gel}$ | Gelatinisation | Sets paste strength |
| $\text{Starch} \xrightarrow{\Delta, \text{H}^+} \text{Dextrin}$ | Acid hydrolysis | Converts starch into quick-tack adhesive |
| $\text{Na}_2\text{SiO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{-H_2O} \text{SiO}_2 \cdot \text{xH}_2\text{O}$ | Drying reaction | Setting of water glass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (2024 Scheme):** Always mention both the **active polymer** AND the **setting mechanism** when describing a natural adhesive. Marks are awarded separately for each.

---

## 2.5 Comparative Summary of Setting Mechanisms

| Adhesive | Setting Mechanism | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|
| Animal glue | Thermoreversible gel | **Reversible** (softens on heating) |
| Casein glue | Ca²⁺ crosslinking + drying | **Irreversible** (water-resistant) |
| Starch paste | Water evaporation / retrogradation | Partially reversible |
| Dextrin | Solvent loss | Reversible (soluble) |
| Gum arabic | Water loss | Reversible |
| Sodium silicate | Dehydration to silica gel | Irreversible (brittle) |
| Bitumen | Cooling from melt | Thermoplastic |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Solid Content Calculation of a Bone Glue Batch

**Problem (KTU style):**
A bone-glue batch contains 12.5 kg of dry gelatin solids dissolved in 25.0 kg of water. Calculate the **solid content** by mass and the **mass of glue solution** that must be applied per square metre to deliver a dry film of 80 g/m².

### Step 1 — Total mass of solution
$$m_{\text{wet}} = m_{\text{dry}} + m_{\text{water}} = 12.5 \text{ kg} + 25.0 \text{ kg} = 37.5 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 2 — Solid content (%)
$$S_{\text{solid}} = \frac{m_{\text{dry}}}{m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100\% = \frac{12.5}{37.5} \times 100\% = 33.\overline{3}\%$$

### Step 3 — Wet film required for 80 g/m² dry film
$$\text{Wet mass per m}^2 = \frac{80 \text{ g}}{0.333} = 240 \text{ g/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Knowing solid content is essential for production-line glue metering. In the corrugation industry, starch slurries of **18–22 % solids** are typical; deviations cause warping or weak bonds.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Viscosity–Temperature Behaviour of Animal Glue

The viscosity of hide glue obeys an Arrhenius-type relation:

$$\eta(T) = \eta_0 \cdot e^{E_a / RT}$$

where $E_a$ is the flow activation energy (J·mol⁻¹), $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$, and $\eta_0$ is the pre-exponential factor.

**Given:** $\eta_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa·s, $E_a = 30{,}000$ J·mol⁻¹. Calculate the viscosity at **333 K (60 °C)** and **313 K (40 °C)**.

### Step 1 — Compute $E_a / RT$ at 333 K
$$\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{30{,}000}{8.314 \times 333} = \frac{30{,}000}{2768.6} = 10.83$$

### Step 2 — Viscosity at 333 K
$$\eta(333) = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot e^{10.83}$$

$$e^{10.83} \approx 5.05 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta(333 \text{ K}) \approx 7.6 \times 10^{-2} \text{ Pa·s} \;=\; 76 \text{ cP}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $E_a / RT$ at 313 K
$$\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{30{,}000}{8.314 \times 313} = \frac{30{,}000}{2602.3} = 11.53$$

### Step 4 — Viscosity at 313 K
$$e^{11.53} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\boxed{\eta(313 \text{ K}) \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-1} \text{ Pa·s} \;=\; 155 \text{ cP}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The viscosity **doubles** with only a 20 K cooling. This is why animal glues are **always applied hot (55–65 °C)** in woodworking.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Degree of Hydrolysis of Starch into Dextrin

If 1.00 kg of starch (degree of polymerisation DP₀ = 1000 glucose units) is heated with dilute HCl until the average DP falls to 50, calculate:

(a) Number of glycosidic bonds cleaved per chain.
(b) Moles of water consumed.
(c) Mass fraction of dextrin (assume average DP = 50).

### Step 1 — Moles of starch initially
$$M_{\text{starch}} = 1000 \times 162 \text{ g/mol} = 162{,}000 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$n_{\text{starch}} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{162{,}000 \text{ g/mol}} = 6.17 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$

### Step 2 — Bonds cleaved per chain
$$\text{Bonds cleaved} = \text{DP}_0 - \text{DP}_{\text{final}} = 1000 - 50 = 950 \text{ bonds per chain}$$

### Step 3 — Moles of dextrin produced
$$\text{Each chain yields } \frac{1000}{50} = 20 \text{ dextrin molecules}$$

$$n_{\text{dextrin}} = 6.17 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = 0.1234 \text{ mol}$$

### Step 4 — Mass of dextrin
$$M_{\text{dextrin}} = 50 \times 162 = 8100 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$m_{\text{dextrin}} = 0.1234 \times 8100 = 999.5 \text{ g} \approx 1.00 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mass balance holds** — hydrolysis consumes water (≈ 18 g per mole of bonds cleaved = 950 × 6.17×10⁻³ × 18 ≈ 105 g of water), which is drawn from the residual moisture in the reaction mass.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem 4: Bond Strength Comparison (Animal vs Casein)

Two lap-joint wood samples (bond area = 25 cm²) are tested in shear:

| Adhesive | Peak Load (N) |
|---|---|
| Hide glue | 1850 |
| Casein glue (Ca(OH)₂ cured) | 2350 |

Compute the **lap-shear strength** in MPa for each.

### Step 1 — Convert area to m²
$$A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Hide glue
$$\sigma_{\text{hide}} = \frac{1850}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.4 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.74 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3 — Casein glue
$$\sigma_{\text{casein}} = \frac{2350}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.4 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 0.94 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Casein glue gives ~27 % higher lap-shear strength **and** water-resistance due to Ca²⁺ crosslinking, justifying its historical use in aircraft plywood.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Natural Adhesive

| Application | Recommended Adhesive | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Corrugated cardboard | **Starch (Stein-Hall process)** | Cheap, fast tack, water-based |
| Wallpaper paste | **Methyl cellulose / CMC** | Slump-resistant, removable |
| Bookbinding | **Hide glue** | Strong, reversible for restoration |
| Label / envelope gum | **Yellow dextrin** | Quick tack, low viscosity |
| Soft drink emulsifier | **Gum arabic** | Food-grade, low viscosity at high solids |
| Pressure-sensitive tape | **Natural rubber + rosin** | Permanent tack at room temp |
| Fire-proof paper laminating | **Sodium silicate** | Inorganic, non-flammable |
| Roofing membrane | **Bitumen (hot-melt)** | Waterproof, weather-resistant |
