# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define natural adhesive. List any three vegetable-based natural adhesives with one application each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A natural adhesive is a bonding material obtained from plant, animal, or mineral sources by simple physical or mild chemical processing, whose adhesive action arises from naturally occurring polymeric substances such as proteins, polysaccharides, or resins.

**Three vegetable-based adhesives:**
1. **Starch paste** — corrugated cardboard manufacturing.
2. **Dextrin** — gummed paper tapes and envelopes.
3. **Gum arabic** — pharmaceutical tablet binder and confectionery glaze.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation tip:** Examiner awards 1 mark for the definition and 2 marks for the three correctly-paired examples. Adding the *active polymer* (e.g., polysaccharide) fetches a bonus mark in stricter papers.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain briefly the role of borax in starch-based adhesives.**

**Model Answer:**

Borax (Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O) acts as a **crosslinking / viscosity modifier** in starch adhesives.

1. **Complexation:** Borate ions form **tetrahedral complexes with the cis-diol groups of starch chains**, creating a lightly crosslinked network.
2. **Effect on viscosity:** Increases paste viscosity rapidly, giving a **short, tacky "peak"** character desirable in paper-bonding.
3. **Effect on tack:** Sharpens and prolongs open time (working time).
4. **Effect on water resistance:** Mild crosslinking improves wet tack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Typical addition level = **0.1 – 0.5 %** of starch weight. Excessive borax makes the paste rubbery and unspreadable.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **manufacture, properties, and applications of animal glue (hide glue)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the chemistry of **casein glue** with the role of lime and sodium salts. Why was casein used in aircraft plywood during WWII?

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

#### (a) Manufacture, Properties and Applications of Hide Glue

**Raw material:** Cattle hides, pig skins, and connective tissue rich in **collagen**.

**Manufacturing steps:**
1. **Curing:** Hides are treated with lime (Ca(OH)₂) for several weeks to loosen hair and open fibrous structure.
2. **Washing and deliming:** Removes excess lime using dilute acid.
3. **Extraction:** Boiling water (55–80 °C) is passed through successive batches of stock; the first extract is highest grade.
4. **Filtration & concentration:** Removes insoluble matter; liquor is concentrated under vacuum.
5. **Drying:** Poured into moulds, cooled into a gel, sliced and air-dried into cakes or pellets.

**Setting chemistry:**
$$\text{Hot gelatin sol} \xrightarrow{\text{Cool below } T_{\text{gel}}} \text{Gel (triple-helix reformation)}$$

**Properties:**
- **Gel temperature:** 28–32 °C (low-grade) up to 35–40 °C (high-grade).
- **Strength:** 0.7–0.8 MPa in lap-shear on wood.
- **Reversibility:** Softens on heating → useful in **restoration work**.
- **pH:** 6.0–7.5; viscosity 100–1000 cP at 60 °C.

**Applications:**
- Wood joinery, furniture, violin-making (highest-grade).
- Bookbinding and gilding.
- Abrasive paper backing.
- Gummed tape manufacture.

#### (b) Casein Glue Chemistry

**Raw material:** Skim milk → protein **casein**.

**Step 1 — Precipitation:**
$$\text{Casein (soluble Ca-caseinate)} + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{Casein (curd)} + \text{Ca}^{2+}$$

**Step 2 — Alkaline dispersion:** Dry curd mixed with water + Ca(OH)₂ + NaOH + Na₂CO₃.

**Step 3 — Crosslinking on setting:**
$$\text{Casein–COO}^- + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{Casein–COO–Ca–OOC–Casein}$$

The lime simultaneously:
- Swells the protein and disperses it.
- Supplies Ca²⁺ for crosslinking.
- Provides alkalinity to keep glue workable.

**Role of sodium salts (NaF, Na₂CO₃):** Act as **retarders** preventing premature gelation, extending pot life.

**WWII significance:** Casein glue was the **first fully water-resistant wood adhesive** (developed in Switzerland in 1920s), and was used to bond the **plywood skin of the de Havilland Mosquito** bomber — a wooden aircraft — providing strong, gap-filling, water-resistant bonds essential for tropical and maritime operations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the Ca²⁺ crosslinking reaction but forget to mention that **lime also disperses the protein**. Both roles carry marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **classification of natural adhesives** with suitable examples. Compare animal and vegetable adhesives in terms of water resistance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat flow diagram, describe the **manufacture of starch paste adhesive** and explain the effect of adding **borax and caustic soda**.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

#### (a) Classification & Comparison

**Classification (with examples):**

| Class | Examples |
|---|---|
| Animal (protein) | Bone glue, Hide glue, Fish glue, Casein, Blood albumin |
| Vegetable (polysaccharide / polyisoprene) | Starch, Dextrin, Gum arabic, Natural rubber, Cellulose derivatives |
| Mineral | Sodium silicate, Bitumen, Asphalt |

**Water-resistance comparison:**

| Adhesive | Water Resistance | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Hide glue | **Poor** (reversible gel) | Physical gel, no crosslink |
| Casein glue | **Excellent** | Ca²⁺ ionic crosslinks |
| Starch paste | **Poor** | Soluble polysaccharide |
| Dextrin | **Poor** | Soluble polysaccharide |
| Sodium silicate | **Good (once set)** | Forms insoluble silica |
| Bitumen | **Excellent** | Hydrophobic |

#### (b) Manufacture of Starch Paste Adhesive

**Flow diagram:**
```
Raw starch (corn / tapioca / potato)
        ↓
Slurry in cold water
        ↓
Add NaOH (0.5–2 % of starch weight) for swelling
        ↓
Heat to 60–80 °C with stirring → Gelatinisation
        ↓
Add borax (0.1–0.5 %) for tack and viscosity
        ↓
Optional: add UF / phenolic resin for water resistance
        ↓
Cool and store → Starch paste adhesive
```

**Role of NaOH (caustic soda):**
- Swells starch granules by breaking H-bonds in crystalline regions.
- Lowers gelatinisation temperature by ~15 °C.
- Improves clarity and film smoothness.

**Role of borax:**
- Crosslinks starch chains via borate–diol complexation.
- Sharpens tack and shortens open time — ideal for **high-speed corrugation lines (≈ 300 m/min)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the order — caustic soda is added **before heating**, borax **after gelatinisation**. Reversing this destroys the gel structure.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Pitfalls)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks lost by KTU students on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing dextrin with dextran** — dextrin is heat/acid-modified starch; dextran is a microbial polysaccharide.
> 2. **Writing "casein is mixed with water only"** — you lose 2 marks for forgetting the lime + sodium salts.
> 3. **Forgetting that hide glue is reversible** — examiners specifically test this property.
> 4. **Stating that gum arabic is from the acacia tree without mentioning the *A. senegal / A. seyal* species** — partial credit only.
> 5. **Missing the role of borax and NaOH in starch adhesives** — almost always a 3–4 mark item.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Natural adhesives = adhesives from **plant, animal, or mineral** sources.
- [x] Active polymers are **proteins, polysaccharides, polyisoprene, or inorganic silicates**.
- [x] **Animal glue** = collagen-derived gelatin; thermoreversible; reversible bond.
- [x] **Casein glue** = Ca²⁺ crosslinked milk protein; **first water-resistant wood adhesive**; used in WWII aircraft.
- [x] **Blood albumin** = coagulates on heating; used in plywood.
- [x] **Starch paste** = gelatinised polysaccharide; cheap, used in corrugation.
- [x] **NaOH** in starch paste = swelling agent; **borax** = crosslinker / tack booster.
- [x] **Dextrin** = acid/heat-degraded starch; yellow dextrin is highly soluble.
- [x] **Gum arabic** = branched arabinogalactan from *Acacia* trees; edible, low viscosity.
- [x] **Natural rubber** = *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene from *Hevea brasiliensis* latex; base for PSA tapes.
- [x] **Cellulose derivatives** (CMC, methyl cellulose) = water-soluble wallpaper pastes.
- [x] **Sodium silicate (water glass)** = inorganic; pH 11–12; sets by dehydration to silica gel.
- [x] **Bitumen / asphalt** = hydrocarbon hot-melt; roofing, waterproofing.
- [x] Solid content formula: $S_{\text{solid}} = (m_{\text{dry}} / m_{\text{wet}}) \times 100\%$.
- [x] Viscosity of animal glue follows $\eta = \eta_0 e^{E_a/RT}$ — drops sharply on heating.
- [x] **Limitations:** biological degradation, thermoplasticity, batch variability, poor water resistance (except casein, silicate, bitumen).
- [x] **Modern relevance:** biodegradability, food/pharma safety, sustainable packaging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rubber-Based Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rubber-Based Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rubber-Based Adhesives** are a versatile class of pressure-sensitive or contact adhesives whose primary film-forming constituent is an elastomeric polymer (natural or synthetic rubber), compounded with tackifiers, plasticizers, fillers, antioxidants, vulcanizing/curing agents, and solvents (or water in latex form) to produce a viscoelastic bond that combines high initial tack with long-term flexibility.

These adhesives function via the unique viscoelastic behavior of the rubber matrix — they remain soft and rubbery over a broad temperature range, are highly conformable to substrate surface irregularities, and form bonds by intimate molecular contact (wetting) followed by physical interlocking or chemical crosslinking at the interface.

### 1.2 Classification of Rubber-Based Adhesives

Rubber-based adhesives can be broadly classified as:

**(a) Natural Rubber (NR) Based Adhesives**
- Derived from latex of *Hevea brasiliensis*
- Poly(cis-1,4-isoprene) backbone
- High initial tack, low cost, easily formulated

**(b) Synthetic Rubber Based Adhesives**

| Subclass | Full Name | Key Monomer | Distinguishing Feature |
|----------|-----------|-------------|------------------------|
| **CR** | Polychloroprene (Neoprene) | Chloroprene | Most widely used; balanced properties |
| **NBR** | Acrylonitrile-Butadiene Rubber | Butadiene + Acrylonitrile | Excellent oil and fuel resistance |
| **SBR** | Styrene-Butadiene Rubber | Styrene + Butadiene | Cheapest; good aging resistance |
| **IIR** | Isobutylene-Isoprene Rubber (Butyl) | Isobutylene + Isoprene | Low gas permeability; weather resistance |
| **EPDM** | Ethylene-Propylene-Diene Monomer | Ethylene + Propylene + Diene | Outstanding ozone and weather resistance |
| **Silicone Rubber** | Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) | Dimethylsiloxane | Extreme temperature service (-60°C to +250°C) |
| **PU** | Polyurethane Rubber | Polyol + Diisocyanate | High strength and abrasion resistance |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Sticky Rubber Band:**
> Imagine a rubber band. By itself, it does not stick to your skin. But if you melt a sticky chewing-gum layer onto its surface, the rubber band gains the *tacky* ability of the gum while retaining its **elastic stretchability**. A rubber-based adhesive works exactly this way: the **rubber component** supplies the **elastic cohesive strength** (so the glue film does not crack), while the **tackifier resin** supplies the **sticky surface energy** (so it grips the substrate). Together they create a film that is simultaneously *soft enough to wet* the surface and *tough enough to resist* peeling — the hallmark of rubber-based bonding.

### 1.4 Salient Engineering Metrics

The performance of rubber-based adhesives is characterized by the following standard metrics:

- **Tack (Loop Tack):** Initial grab, measured in **N/25 mm** (typical range 2–20).
- **Peel Strength:** Resistance to peeling, measured in **N/cm** (typical 5–60).
- **Shear Strength (Holding Power):** Resistance to static creep, measured in **hours** under a defined load.
- **Solid Content:** Percentage non-volatile matter, typically **20–35 %** for solvent-based systems.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Usually between **−70 °C and −40 °C** for the rubber phase, with tackified blends shifted upward.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Adhesion in Rubber-Based Adhesives

The adhesion of rubber-based systems operates through **four overlapping mechanisms**:

1. **Mechanical Interlocking:** The viscous adhesive flows into substrate pores and micro-cavities, anchoring mechanically upon solidification.
2. **Adsorption (Wetting) Theory:** Close molecular contact between adhesive and substrate produces secondary forces — van der Waals, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bonding. The thermodynamic work of adhesion is given by the **Young–Dupré equation**.
3. **Diffusion Theory:** Rubber chain segments interdiffuse across the interface, forming an "interphase" of entangled polymer chains. This is especially important for bonding two rubbery or polymeric substrates.
4. **Chemical Bonding:** Specific functional groups (e.g., chlorine in neoprene, nitrile in NBR) can form **covalent bonds** with reactive sites on the substrate (e.g., -OH on glass, -NH₂ on metals).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **frequently tested** in KTU University Exams. Memorize the symbols and the units carefully.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | $W_a$ = work of adhesion, $\gamma$ = surface energies | **J/m²** | Young–Dupré thermodynamic adhesion |
| 2 | $W_a = 2\,\sqrt{\gamma_s^d \cdot \gamma_l^d}$ | $d$ = dispersive component, $s$ = solid, $l$ = liquid | **J/m²** | Fowkes approximation for non-polar systems |
| 3 | $S = \gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl} - \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | $S$ = spreading coefficient, $\theta$ = contact angle | **J/m²** | Spreading criteria for wetting |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{F}{w}$ | $P$ = peel strength, $F$ = peel force, $w$ = width | **N/cm** or **N/25 mm** | Standard peel test |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $\sigma$ = shear stress, $A$ = overlap area | **MPa** or **N/mm²** | Lap-shear strength |
| 6 | $M_c = \dfrac{2\,\rho\,(1+\tfrac{1}{\chi_1})\,V_1\,v_{2m}^{\tfrac{1}{3}}}{-\ln(1-v_{2m}) + v_{2m} + \chi_1 v_{2m}^2}$ | $M_c$ = mol. wt. between crosslinks, $v_{2m}$ = equilibrium swollen volume fraction | **g/mol** | Flory–Rehner crosslink density |
| 7 | $\nu_e = \dfrac{\rho}{M_c}$ | $\nu_e$ = crosslink density | **mol/cm³** | Number of effective network chains |
| 8 | $\eta_0 = k\,M_w^{3.4}$ | $\eta_0$ = zero-shear viscosity, $M_w$ = weight-average mol. wt. | **Pa·s** | Viscosity–molecular weight (rubbery plateau) |
| 9 | $\tau_c = \tau_0\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $\tau_c$ = creep time, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) | **s** | Creep / holding power |
| 10 | $S_{\%} = \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{m_1} \times 100$ | $m_1$ = initial mass, $m_2$ = final mass | **%** | Solid content of adhesive |

### 2.3 Critical Boundary Conditions for Wetting

For **complete wetting** of the substrate, the spreading coefficient must be **positive**:

$$S \ge 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \gamma_{sv} \ge \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}$$

and the contact angle must satisfy:

$$\theta \to 0^{\circ}$$

A rubber adhesive, by virtue of its low surface energy ($\gamma_{lv} \approx 30\text{–}45$ mN/m), readily spreads on most engineering substrates (metals, wood, plastics, leather, fabric).

### 2.4 Compounding of a Neoprene-Based Contact Adhesive — A Reference Formulation

A **typical KTU-referenced** neoprene contact adhesive formulation (in parts per hundred rubber, **phr**):

| Ingredient | Function | Loading (phr) |
|------------|----------|---------------|
| Polychloroprene (Neoprene AD or W) | Base elastomer | **100** |
| Magnesium Oxide (MgO) | Acid acceptor / scorch retarder | **4 – 8** |
| Zinc Oxide (ZnO) | Activator / acid acceptor | **5 – 10** |
| Phenolic tackifier resin (e.g., tert-butyl phenolic) | Tackifier | **20 – 100** |
| Antioxidant (e.g., butylated hydroxytoluene, BHT) | Aging resistance | **1 – 2** |
| Plasticizer (e.g., dioctyl phthalate) | Flexibility | **5 – 20** |
| Filler (e.g., silica, calcium carbonate) | Rheology / cost | **10 – 50** |
| Solvent (toluene / MEK / hexane blend) | Viscosity control | To make **20 – 30 % solids** |

### 2.5 Vulcanization / Curing Chemistry

Neoprene is cured using a combination of **ZnO + MgO** (metal oxide cure) rather than traditional sulfur systems. The reaction proceeds as:

$$\text{Polymer–S–Cl} + \text{ZnO} \longrightarrow \text{Polymer–S–Zn–S–Polymer} + \text{ZnCl}_2$$

The crosslinked network imparts **cohesive strength** and **heat resistance** to the dried adhesive film, while the surface remains tacky due to the dominance of tackifier resin at the air interface.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rubber-based adhesives are **ubiquitous** in engineering:

- **Footwear industry:** Sole-to-upper bonding (CR contact adhesives).
- **Automotive:** Trunk linings, weather-strip bonding, interior trim (SBR + NBR blends).
- **Aerospace:** High-temperature silicone rubber adhesives for engine seals.
- **Construction:** Butyl rubber tapes for sealing and waterproofing.
- **Electronics:** Silicone adhesives for potting and conformal coating.
- **Packaging:** Reclaimed rubber latex adhesives for labels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peel Strength from Energy Balance

The peel strength of a rubber adhesive tape peeled at an angle $\theta$ from a rigid substrate can be derived from first principles using an **energy balance**.

**Setup:** Consider a flexible rubber tape of width $w$ being peeled from a rigid substrate at a steady angle $\theta$ (most commonly $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ for standard peel tests). The peel front advances by an infinitesimal length $d\ell$ in time $dt$.

**Step 1 — Energy released by peeling:**
The energy released by separation of a length $d\ell$ of interface equals the work of adhesion $W_a$ times the area newly created.

$$\text{Energy released} = W_a \cdot w \cdot d\ell$$

**Step 2 — Elastic energy stored in the peeled tape:**
When peeled, the tape bends through a radius of curvature $R$ and stores elastic strain energy per unit volume $U_{el}$. For a tape of thickness $h$ and elastic modulus $E$, the energy per unit length is:

$$U_{el}^{\prime} = \int_0^h \frac{E}{2} \left(\frac{y}{R}\right)^2 dy \cdot w = \frac{E\,w\,h^3}{24\,R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Work done by external peel force $F$:**
For a quasi-static peel, the work done by the applied force equals the sum of energy released at the interface and the elastic energy stored:

$$F \cdot d\ell = W_a \cdot w \cdot d\ell + U_{el}^{\prime} \cdot d\ell$$

**Step 4 — Substituting and solving for $F$:**

$$\frac{F}{w} = P = W_a + \frac{E\,h^3}{24\,R^2}$$

**Step 5 — Final Peel Strength Equation:**

$$P = W_a + \frac{E\,h^3}{24\,R^2}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The first term $W_a$ is the **intrinsic adhesive contribution** (interface thermodynamics).
- The second term represents **dissipative viscoelastic losses** in the rubber, which dominate practical peel strengths (often 10–100 times larger than $W_a$).
- This is the **Rivlin–Gent explanation** of why rubber peel strengths are vastly greater than the thermodynamic work of adhesion.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solids Content Calculation

A rubber adhesive solution is prepared by dissolving **$m_p$ grams** of rubber + compounding solids in **$m_s$ grams** of solvent. The solids content $S_{\%}$ is required for quality control.

**Step 1 — Total mass of the solution:**

$$m_{total} = m_p + m_s$$

**Step 2 — Mass of solids after complete solvent evaporation:**

$$m_{solid} = m_p \quad \text{(assumes non-volatile additives)}$$

**Step 3 — Solids content in percentage:**

$$S_{\%} = \frac{m_p}{m_p + m_s} \times 100$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Suppose **250 g** of neoprene is dissolved in **750 g** of toluene.

$$S_{\%} = \frac{250}{250 + 750} \times 100 = \frac{250}{1000} \times 100 = 25 \,\%$$

This is within the standard range of 20–30 % solids for contact adhesives.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crosslink Density from Swelling Data

The Flory–Rehner equation relates the equilibrium swelling of a crosslinked rubber to its crosslink density.

**Given:** A neoprene adhesive film of initial mass $m_1 = 0.500$ g is immersed in toluene, and at equilibrium the swollen mass is $m_2 = 2.850$ g. The polymer density $\rho_p = 1.23$ g/cm³, solvent density $\rho_s = 0.867$ g/cm³, and polymer–solvent interaction parameter $\chi_1 = 0.34$.

**Step 1 — Calculate the volume fraction of rubber in the swollen gel:**

$$v_{2m} = \frac{m_1/\rho_p}{(m_1/\rho_p) + ((m_2 - m_1)/\rho_s)}$$

$$v_{2m} = \frac{0.500/1.23}{(0.500/1.23) + ((2.850 - 0.500)/0.867)}$$

$$v_{2m} = \frac{0.4065}{0.4065 + 2.7105} = \frac{0.4065}{3.1170} = 0.1304$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Flory–Rehner equation to find $M_c$:**

$$M_c = \frac{2\,\rho_p\,(1 + \tfrac{1}{\chi_1})\,V_1\,v_{2m}^{\tfrac{1}{3}}}{-\ln(1 - v_{2m}) + v_{2m} + \chi_1 v_{2m}^2}$$

with molar volume of toluene $V_1 = 106.3$ cm³/mol.

**Step 3 — Numerator calculation:**

$$2 \times 1.23 \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{0.34}\right) \times 106.3 \times (0.1304)^{1/3}$$

$$= 2 \times 1.23 \times 3.9412 \times 106.3 \times 0.5064$$

$$\approx 519.97 \quad \text{(intermediate)}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator calculation:**

$$-\ln(1 - 0.1304) + 0.1304 + 0.34 \times (0.1304)^2$$

$$= -\ln(0.8696) + 0.1304 + 0.34 \times 0.01700$$

$$= 0.1397 + 0.1304 + 0.00578 = 0.2759$$

**Step 5 — Compute $M_c$:**

$$M_c = \frac{519.97}{0.2759} \approx 1885 \,\text{g/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Compute crosslink density $\nu_e$:**

$$\nu_e = \frac{\rho_p}{M_c} = \frac{1.23}{1885} = 6.53 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mol/cm}^3$$

This is a **moderate crosslink density**, typical of a well-cured neoprene contact adhesive — high enough to give cohesive strength, low enough to retain flexibility and tack.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Viscosity-Molecular Weight Relationship

The zero-shear viscosity of a polymer solution in its rubbery regime follows the **3.4 power law**:

$$\eta_0 = k\,M_w^{3.4}$$

**For two polymer samples of molecular weight $M_{w,1}$ and $M_{w,2}$:**

$$\frac{\eta_{0,2}}{\eta_{0,1}} = \left(\frac{M_{w,2}}{M_{w,1}}\right)^{3.4}$$

**Worked Example:** A neoprene sample with $M_w = 100{,}000$ gives $\eta_0 = 5$ Pa·s. What is the viscosity if $M_w$ is doubled to 200,000?

$$\frac{\eta_{0,2}}{5} = \left(\frac{200{,}000}{100{,}000}\right)^{3.4} = 2^{3.4}$$

$$2^{3.4} = e^{3.4 \ln 2} = e^{3.4 \times 0.6931} = e^{2.3565} = 10.55$$

$$\eta_{0,2} = 5 \times 10.55 = 52.75 \,\text{Pa·s}$$

This dramatic viscosity rise (10×) explains why **molecular weight control is critical** in adhesive formulation — too high a molecular weight makes the adhesive unspreadable, too low makes the bond weak.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Performance vs. Rubber Type

| Property | NR | CR (Neoprene) | NBR | SBR | Silicone | Butyl |
|----------|----|-----|-----|-----|----------|-------|
| **Tack (Initial)** | **High** | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Peel Strength** | High | **Very High** | Medium | Medium | Low | Low |
| **Oil Resistance** | Poor | Good | **Excellent** | Poor | Excellent | Poor |
| **Heat Resistance** | Poor | Good | Good | Fair | **Excellent** | Fair |
| **Cold Flexibility** | **Excellent** | Good | Fair | Good | **Excellent** | Good |
| **Weather/Ozone** | Poor | Good | Good | Good | **Excellent** | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | **Lowest** | High | Medium |
| **Main Use** | Labels, tapes | Footwear, automotive | Gaskets, fuel seals | General purpose | Aerospace, electronics | Sealants, tapes |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and tests concept recall and brief understanding (Revised Bloom's Level: *Remember / Understand*).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define rubber-based adhesives. List the major types of synthetic rubber used in adhesive formulation with one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1 mark):*
Rubber-based adhesives are elastomer-based bonding agents in which the primary film-forming material is a rubbery polymer (natural or synthetic), combined with tackifiers, plasticizers, fillers, antioxidants, and curing agents, which develop bonds by intimate molecular contact and viscoelastic dissipation.

*Major Synthetic Rubbers and Applications (2 marks — ½ mark each):*

1. **Polychloroprene (Neoprene / CR):** Footwear sole bonding, automotive trim.
2. **Nitrile Rubber (NBR):** Oil-resistant gaskets and fuel hose bonding.
3. **Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR):** General-purpose labels and packaging tapes.
4. **Butyl Rubber (IIR):** Sealing tapes, inner-tube patches.
5. **Silicone Rubber:** High-temperature aerospace seals and electronics potting.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the role of the following compounding ingredients in a neoprene-based contact adhesive:**
**(a) Magnesium oxide (MgO), (b) Phenolic tackifier resin, (c) Toluene (solvent).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

**(a) Magnesium Oxide (MgO):** Acts as an **acid acceptor** that neutralizes HCl released during the curing of polychloroprene, preventing substrate corrosion and scorch. Typical loading: 4–8 phr.

**(b) Phenolic Tackifier Resin:** Provides the **initial tack (stickiness)** of the adhesive film. Phenolic resins are compatible with neoprene and improve surface wetting of the substrate, increasing peel strength dramatically. Typical loading: 20–100 phr.

**(c) Toluene (Solvent):** Serves as the **carrier medium** to dissolve the rubber and reduce viscosity for easy application. It evaporates during the open assembly time, allowing the film to develop green strength. Typical solvent blend: toluene + MEK + hexane in a 3:2:1 ratio.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. You must answer **EITHER (a) OR (b)** of a single question. We provide **Question A and Question B** as independent choice options.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Discuss in detail the mechanism of adhesion in neoprene-based contact adhesives. How does the chlorine atom in polychloroprene influence the bonding characteristics? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The mechanism of adhesion in neoprene-based contact adhesives operates through **four overlapping mechanisms (4 marks):**

1. **Wetting and Adsorption:** The low-viscosity adhesive solution readily spreads on the substrate (contact angle $\theta \to 0$ is desirable), bringing the polar groups of neoprene into intimate contact. The Young–Dupré equation $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ governs this stage.

2. **Mechanical Interlocking:** The fluid adhesive penetrates substrate pores (wood, leather, fabric); upon solvent evaporation, the dried film is mechanically anchored in these micro-cavities.

3. **Diffusion of Chain Segments:** Solvent retention keeps the upper layer of the film mobile, allowing short-range interdiffusion of rubber chain segments into the substrate, forming a strong interphase.

4. **Viscoelastic Energy Dissipation:** Upon peeling, the soft rubbery film dissipates energy through large viscoelastic deformations, raising the practical peel strength 10–100× above the thermodynamic work of adhesion (Rivlin–Gent mechanism).

**Role of Chlorine Atom (3 marks):**

- The **electron-withdrawing chlorine** on the polymer backbone **polarizes** the C–Cl bond, generating a permanent **dipole moment** ($\mu \approx 1.5$ D per C–Cl unit).
- This **polarity** increases the cohesive energy density, raises $T_g$ modestly, and — most importantly — enables **specific dipole-dipole interactions and weak hydrogen bonding** with polar substrates (metals, glass, wood, leather).
- Chlorine also makes the polymer **less flammable** and **oil-resistant** compared to natural rubber.
- The Cl atom participates in the **metal-oxide cure** (ZnO/MgO) to form C–S–Zn–S–C crosslinks, which provide cohesive strength to the dried film.

---

**(b) Explain the role of compounding ingredients in rubber-based adhesive formulation with a neat tabulated reference formulation for neoprene contact adhesive. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Compounding ingredients in a rubber adhesive serve **six critical functions (3 marks):**

1. **Base polymer (elastomer):** Provides the cohesive strength and elasticity. Example: Neoprene W or AD.
2. **Tackifier resins:** Provide initial tack and surface stickiness. Examples: tert-butyl phenolic resin, rosin ester, hydrocarbon resin.
3. **Plasticizers:** Reduce the glass transition temperature and improve flexibility. Example: Dioctyl phthalate (DOP).
4. **Fillers:** Reduce cost, modify rheology, and adjust hardness. Examples: Silica, calcium carbonate, china clay.
5. **Antioxidants/Age resistors:** Prevent oxidative degradation. Example: BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene).
6. **Curing/Vulcanizing agents:** Form crosslinks for cohesive and heat resistance. Examples: ZnO + MgO (for neoprene).

**Reference Neoprene Contact Adhesive Formulation (3 marks):**

| Ingredient | phr | Function |
|------------|-----|----------|
| Polychloroprene (Neoprene) | 100 | Base elastomer |
| MgO | 4–8 | Acid acceptor |
| ZnO | 5–10 | Curing activator |
| Phenolic tackifier resin | 40–80 | Tack provider |
| Antioxidant (BHT) | 1–2 | Aging resistance |
| Plasticizer (DOP) | 5–15 | Flexibility |
| Filler (CaCO₃) | 10–30 | Cost / rheology |
| Solvent (toluene/MEK) | To 25% solids | Viscosity control |

The compounded adhesive is matured for 12–24 hours before packaging to ensure complete dissolution and equilibration.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Describe with a neat flow diagram the contact bonding process used in rubber-based adhesives. What is "open assembly time" and how does it affect bond strength? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Contact Bonding Process (5 marks):**

The contact bonding process is the **signature application method** for rubber-based contact adhesives. The process steps are:

1. **Surface Preparation:** Substrates are cleaned to remove dust, oil, and grease.
2. **Adhesive Application:** Adhesive is applied uniformly to **both** substrates (typically 100–150 g/m² per coat) using a brush, roller, or spray gun.
3. **Drying / Solvent Evaporation:** Substrates are left open in a well-ventilated area for **5–30 minutes** to allow solvent to evaporate. The film transitions from glossy wet to a dry-to-touch state.
4. **Joining Under Pressure:** The two adhesive-coated surfaces are brought into contact and pressed firmly (manual pressure of 0.2–0.5 MPa is sufficient) starting from one edge to avoid air entrapment.
5. **Immediate Green Strength:** The bond develops **instantly** because the rubber-tackifier film is already in a tacky state; no chemical curing is required for green strength.
6. **Final Strength Development:** Full cohesive strength is reached in 24–72 hours as residual solvent diffuses out and any chemical crosslinking completes.

**Open Assembly Time and Its Effect (2 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Open Assembly Time (OAT):** The time between adhesive application and the joining of the two substrates.

- **Too short OAT:** Residual solvent is trapped → bubbles, weak boundary layer, poor cohesion.
- **Too long OAT:** Surface contamination, loss of tack, dry-out → poor adhesion.
- **Optimum OAT:** 5–20 minutes for most neoprene formulations — film should be **tacky to touch** but **not transfer** to a clean finger.

---

**(b) Compare natural rubber (NR) adhesives with neoprene (CR) adhesives in terms of chemical structure, properties, and applications. Why is neoprene preferred over NR for industrial contact adhesives? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Natural Rubber (NR) | Polychloroprene (CR / Neoprene) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Chemical Structure** | *cis*-1,4-polyisoprene | *trans*-1,4-polychloroprene |
| **Repeat Unit** | –(CH₂–C(CH₃)=CH–CH₂)ₙ– | –(CH₂–C(Cl)=CH–CH₂)ₙ– |
| **Tack** | High | Very high |
| **Aging Resistance** | Poor (ozone attacks C=C) | Good (Cl protects double bond) |
| **Oil/Solvent Resistance** | Poor | Good |
| **Heat Resistance** | Up to 80°C | Up to 110°C |
| **Cohesive Strength** | Lower | Higher |
| **Cost** | Lower | Medium |
| **Curing System** | Sulfur cure | ZnO + MgO (metal oxide cure) |
| **Main Applications** | Labels, surgical tapes, PSA tapes | Footwear, automotive, construction, contact bonds |

**Why Neoprene is Preferred for Industrial Contact Adhesives (3 marks):**

1. **Better Aging Resistance:** The Cl atom deactivates the C=C double bond, making neoprene far more **ozone- and UV-resistant** than NR.
2. **Oil and Chemical Resistance:** The polar C–Cl bond resists swelling by hydrocarbons — important for automotive and machinery applications.
3. **Higher Cohesive Strength:** Metal-oxide crosslinking provides a stronger three-dimensional network than simple sulfur-cured NR.
4. **Wider Temperature Service Range:** From −40°C to +110°C, covering most engineering environments.
5. **Faster and More Reliable Green Strength:** The combination of high tack and rapid bond development makes neoprene ideal for high-throughput production lines (e.g., shoe factories).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Rubber-Adhesive Questions — KTU 2024 Scheme:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the solvent role:** Many students describe the four adhesion mechanisms but **omit solvent evaporation and its effect on film formation**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if solvent is not discussed.
> 2. **Confusing "open time" with "closed time":** Open time = before joining; closed time = after joining (under compression). Mixing these terms costs easy marks.
> 3. **Omitting the "Why" behind the Cl atom in neoprene:** Simply saying "chlorine is present" is not enough. You must explicitly link Cl to **polarity, oil resistance, ozone protection, and metal-oxide cure** — this single concept fetches 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Writing the wrong cure system:** Saying "neoprene is sulfur-cured" is **wrong**. The correct statement is: neoprene is cured by **ZnO + MgO**; *sulfur* is used for NR and SBR.
> 5. **Skipping the units in the formula sheet:** Always write units (N/25 mm, g/mol, J/m²). Missing units = 0.5 mark penalty per instance.
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled diagram in Part B:** KTU examiners **reward** neat, labelled flow diagrams. A neat flow chart of the contact bonding process fetches at least 1–2 marks on its own.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Rubber-Based Adhesives**

- [ ] Rubber-based adhesives consist of an **elastomer + tackifier + additives**; they bond via **wetting, mechanical interlocking, diffusion, and viscoelastic dissipation**.
- [ ] Major types: **NR, CR (Neoprene), NBR, SBR, IIR (Butyl), Silicone, PU**.
- [ ] **Neoprene is the most widely used** industrial contact adhesive due to its balanced properties.
- [ ] **Cure systems:** Sulfur for NR/SBR; **ZnO + MgO for neoprene**; peroxide for silicone.
- [ ] **Tackifier resin** is the key to surface stickiness; phenolic resins are preferred for neoprene.
- [ ] **MgO** acts as an **acid acceptor**; **ZnO** activates the cure.
- [ ] **Open Assembly Time (OAT)** = 5–30 min; too short → solvent entrapment; too long → dry-out.
- [ ] **Solid content** of solvent-based rubber adhesives = **20–30 %**.
- [ ] **Peel strength** unit: **N/25 mm**; **Shear strength** unit: **MPa**; **Tack** unit: **N/25 mm**.
- [ ] **Glass transition temperature $T_g$** of the rubber phase is typically **−70 to −40 °C**, raised by tackifier addition.
- [ ] **Crosslink density** is measured by the **Flory–Rehner equation**; high crosslink density → high cohesive strength but low tack.
- [ ] **Viscosity** scales with $M_w^{3.4}$ in the rubbery regime — molecular weight is the dominant formulation lever.
- [ ] **Silicone rubber adhesives** serve the widest temperature range ($-60^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+250^{\circ}\text{C}$) — used in aerospace and electronics.
- [ ] **Nitrile (NBR) adhesives** are the **first choice for oil/fuel contact** — used in gaskets and fuel lines.
- [ ] **Young–Dupré equation** $W_a = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ governs wettability and is the foundation of the adsorption theory.
- [ ] **Viscosity–molecular weight relation:** $\eta_0 = k M_w^{3.4}$ — doubling $M_w$ increases viscosity by **~10.5 times**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TPE-Based Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** TPE-Based Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPEs)** are a unique class of polymeric materials that exhibit the **elastic recovery and mechanical properties of vulcanized rubber** at ambient service temperatures, yet can be **processed and reprocessed as thermoplastics** at elevated temperatures (typically **120 °C – 220 °C**). From an adhesive science perspective, TPE-Based Adhesives are formulated using block-copolymer TPEs (chiefly **SBS – Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene**, **SIS – Styrene-Isoprene-Styrene**, **SEBS – Styrene-Ethylene-Butylene-Styrene**, **TPU – Thermoplastic Polyurethane**, and **POE – Polyolefin Elastomers**) as the primary elastomeric backbone, combined with tackifying resins, plasticizers, stabilizers, fillers, and crosslinkers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TPE-Based Adhesives is classified as a high-weightage topic in Module 3 (Elastomer & Specialty Adhesive Systems). Board questions typically test (i) the **morphology–property relationship** of block copolymers and (ii) the **role of each formulation ingredient** in controlling tack, peel, shear, and creep.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Anchor:** All TPEs used in adhesives are essentially **phase-separated ABA triblock copolymers**, where A = hard styrenic end-blocks (glass transition $T_g \approx 100\,°C$) and B = soft elastomeric mid-block ($T_g \approx -60\,°C$ to $-90\,C$). This dual-$T_g$ architecture is the **single most important concept** in this topic.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Spring-in-a-Cage" Model

Imagine a microscopic **garden hose packed inside a tiny rigid plastic cage**.

| Component | Real Polymer Block | Analogy Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Hard styrene end-blocks (A)** | Rigid glassy domains ($\sim$10–30 nm spheres) | The **plastic cage** – holds the structure together and gives **green strength**. |
| **Soft mid-block (B)** | Rubbery, coiled/elastomeric chain | The **garden hose** – freely stretches and recoils like a spring. |
| **Physical crosslink** | Glassy styrene domains that lock the B-chains at both ends | Tiny **rivets** that hold each spring in place without any chemical bond. |
| **Tackifier resin** | C5/C9 hydrocarbon resin mixed into the soft phase | A **sticky coating** applied to the rubbery spring to make it grab surfaces. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works:** When you heat the system above 100 °C, the **cage (styrene) melts**, allowing the entire network to flow — this is exactly what happens in **hot-melt processing**. Upon cooling, the styrene domains **re-solidify and re-form cages**, instantly restoring elasticity — mimicking **vulcanized rubber behaviour without any sulfur crosslinking**.

---

### 1.3 Why TPEs Revolutionised Adhesive Technology

Before TPEs, the dominant adhesive elastomers were **natural rubber (NR)** and **polychloroprene (CR)** — both requiring **chemical vulcanization (sulfur or metal-oxide cure)**. The advantages of TPEs that displaced these in many applications are summarised below:

- **No chemical crosslinking required** → simpler, faster, solvent-free processing.
- **Reversible thermoplastic flow** → scrap is fully recyclable (closed-loop reprocessing).
- **Tailorable soft/hard block ratios** → one base polymer, many end-properties.
- **Inherent two-phase morphology** → built-in physical crosslinking.
- **Compatible with hot-melt coating** → no solvents, no VOC emissions, complies with **REACH** and **EPA Clean Air Act** limits.
- **Lower energy footprint** than solvent-based rubber adhesive manufacture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metric:** The global TPE market for adhesives and sealants is projected to exceed **USD 5.2 billion by 2027**, with **SIS-based hot-melt pressure-sensitive adhesives (HMPSAs)** commanding the largest share, particularly in **label, tape, hygiene, and packaging** segments.

---

### 1.4 TPE Families Used in Adhesives – At-a-Glance Map

$$\text{TPE Adhesive Family} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Block-Copolymer TPEs} \begin{cases}
\text{SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene)} \\
\text{SIS (Styrene-Isoprene-Styrene)} \\
\text{SEBS (Styrene-Ethylene-Butylene-Styrene)} \\
\text{SEPS (Styrene-Ethylene-Propylene-Styrene)}
\end{cases} \\[8pt]
\text{TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)} \\[4pt]
\text{POE / POP (Polyolefin Elastomers)} \\[4pt]
\text{TPV (Thermoplastic Vulcanizate)} \\[4pt]
\text{MPR / Melt-Processible Rubber}
\end{cases}$$

The remainder of this note focuses primarily on **SBS, SIS, and SEBS** as these are the **core syllabus molecules** at KTU.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Morphology of ABA Triblock Copolymers

The defining structural feature of an SBS / SIS / SEBS molecule is its **block architecture**:

$$\underbrace{A}_{\text{hard}} - \underbrace{B}_{\text{mid}} - \underbrace{A}_{\text{hard}} \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Mol}_\text{wt}\,A \approx 5{,}000\text{–}15{,}000\ \text{g/mol},\ \text{Mol}_\text{wt}\,B \approx 30{,}000\text{–}80{,}000\ \text{g/mol}$$

Upon cooling from the melt, the two chemically incompatible blocks **microphase-separate** due to the positive Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi > 0$. The resulting **equilibrium morphology** depends almost entirely on the **volume fraction of the styrene block ($\phi_S$)**:

$$\phi_S = \frac{V_A}{V_A + V_B}$$

| Volume Fraction $\phi_S$ | Resulting Morphology | Mechanical Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| $\phi_S < 0.10$ | Spheres of A in B-matrix | Weak, plastic, low modulus |
| $0.10 \le \phi_S \le 0.20$ | Cylinders / rods of A in B-matrix | Elastomeric (typical adhesive grade) |
| $0.20 < \phi_S < 0.35$ | Co-continuous gyroid | Stiff elastomer |
| $0.35 \le \phi_S \le 0.65$ | Lamellae | Brittle plastic |
| $\phi_S > 0.65$ | A-matrix with B-spheres | Hard thermoplastic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For adhesive applications the styrene content is deliberately tuned to $\phi_S \approx 0.15$ – $0.30$**. This delivers a **cylindrical or co-continuous morphology** that yields a soft, compliant material with high cohesive strength — the precise balance required for **pressure-sensitive adhesion**.

---

### 2.2 Physical Crosslinking vs. Chemical Crosslinking

| Property | Chemical (Vulcanized) Rubber | TPE (Physical) Crosslink |
|---|---|---|
| Bond type | Covalent (S–S, C–C) | Van der Waals + glassy domain entanglement |
| Reversibility | **Irreversible** | **Reversible** above $T_g$ of A-block |
| Creep resistance | Excellent | Good (but lower than vulcanized) |
| Process window | Single (one-shot cure) | Multiple (melt-reprocessable) |
| Recyclability | **None** | **Full** |

The **styrene-domain dissociation temperature** ($T_\text{dis}$) sets the upper service temperature:

$$T_\text{dis} \approx T_g^A + 60\,°C \approx 160\,°C \text{ (for pure PS domains)}$$

In formulations, plasticizer and tackifier addition lowers $T_\text{dis}$ by **15–30 °C**.

---

### 2.3 Formulation Architecture – The Six Functional Building Blocks

A working TPE-based adhesive formulation always contains six ingredient classes. Each class has a precise mechanistic role:

| # | Ingredient Class | Function | Typical Examples | Typical Loading (phr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base TPE polymer** | Elastomeric backbone, cohesive strength, elastic recovery | SBS, SIS, SEBS | **100 phr** |
| 2 | **Tackifying resin** | Increases surface tack and wet-out on substrates; softens the mid-block | C5 aliphatic, C9 aromatic, hydrogenated C5/C9, terpene, rosin esters | **80–150 phr** |
| 3 | **Plasticizer / Extender oil** | Reduces melt viscosity, lowers $T_g$ of mid-block, softens | Mineral oil (paraffinic/naphthenic), polybutene, DIDP | **10–60 phr** |
| 4 | **Stabilizer package** | Prevents thermal/UV/oxidation degradation during melt processing | Irganox 1010, Irgafos 168, HALS (Tinuvin 770), UV absorbers | **0.5–3 phr** |
| 5 | **Filler / Reinforcement** | Modifies rheology, reduces cost, controls hardness | Calcium carbonate, talc, silica (fumed), TiO$_2$ | **0–50 phr** |
| 6 | **Crosslinker (optional)** | Chemically reinforces the network for high-temperature applications | UV-curable acrylates, e-beam, sulfur, peroxide, phenolic resins | **0–5 phr** |

> [!NOTE]
> **"phr" = parts per hundred parts of resin (TPE).** This is the universal formulation unit in elastomer adhesive technology.

---

### 2.4 Tackifier Selection – Solubility-Parameter Matching

Tackifier selection is **NOT arbitrary**; it is governed by **Hildebrand/Hansen solubility-parameter matching** with the soft mid-block.

$$\Delta \delta_T = \left[ (\delta_{d,1} - \delta_{d,2})^2 + (\delta_{p,1} - \delta_{p,2})^2 + (\delta_{h,1} - \delta_{h,2})^2 \right]^{1/2}$$

| Mid-Block | Recommended Tackifier | Hansen $\delta_T$ (MPa$^{1/2}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Polyisoprene (in SIS)** | C5 aliphatic, polyterpene | $\delta_T \approx 16.5$ – $17.5$ |
| **Polybutadiene (in SBS)** | C5/C9 mixed, C9 aromatic | $\delta_T \approx 17.0$ – $18.5$ |
| **Hydrogenated mid-block (in SEBS)** | Hydrogenated C5, hydrogenated C9, fully hydrogenated DCPD | $\delta_T \approx 16.0$ – $17.0$ |

**Rule of thumb:** $\Delta \delta_T \le 1.0\, \text{MPa}^{1/2}$ for optimum compatibility and tack.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below is the **only set of equations** you must memorise for any KTU problem on TPE-Based Adhesives. Every derivation in Section 3 reduces to one of these.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_S = V_A/(V_A+V_B)$ | Volume fraction of styrene hard block | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_g \text{ (Fox)} = \sum_i w_i / T_{g,i}$ | Fox equation for $T_g$ of copolymer blend | K |
| 3 | $\eta_0 = K \cdot e^{E_\eta/RT}$ | Arrhenius melt-viscosity law (zero-shear) | Pa·s |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma} = (v_\text{coater}) / h_\text{coat}$ | Shear rate in hot-melt coating | s$^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $E_\text{adhesion} = W_A = \gamma_{sv} + \gamma_{lv} - \gamma_{sl}$ | Young–Dupré work of adhesion | mJ/m² |
| 6 | $\Delta \delta_T$ (Hansen 3-D) | Solubility-parameter distance | MPa$^{1/2}$ |
| 7 | $G^* = G' + i G''$ | Viscoelastic complex modulus | Pa |
| 8 | $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ | Loss factor (damping) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Peel Strength}\ \propto \sqrt{(G' \cdot G'')}$ | Chang's Viscoelastic Peel Criterion | N/25 mm |
| 10 | $\text{Creep Compliance}\ J(t) = \epsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ | Time-dependent strain under constant stress | Pa$^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $T_\text{dis} \approx T_g^A + 60\,°C$ | Styrene-domain dissociation temperature | °C |
| 12 | $\text{Lap-Shear}\ \tau = F/(w \cdot L)$ | Static shear strength of bonded joint | MPa |

---

### 2.6 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering

- **Packaging Industry:** Hot-melt SIS adhesives are the **backbone of the EUR-pallet labelling and case-sealing market** because they cure instantly on cooling — eliminating the dwell time of solvent adhesives.
- **Hygiene Non-wovens:** TPE-based construction adhesives enable **stretchable, breathable diaper side-panels**.
- **Automotive Interior Trims:** Sprayable HMPSA based on SEBS bonds **foam, fabric and EPDM rubber** without solvents — a direct response to **EU Directive 2004/42/EC** on VOC.
- **Medical:** Low-cytotoxicity SEBS grades are approved for **transdermal drug-delivery patches** because they do not contain residual vulcanizing agents.
- **Electronics:** UV-curable TPE PSAs provide **reworkable bonding** of display modules — a critical enabler of the right-to-repair movement.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fox Equation Applied to TPE Mid-Block + Tackifier Blend

The Fox equation predicts the glass-transition temperature of a miscible polymer blend from the weight fractions and individual $T_g$ values.

**Starting form (Fox, 1956):**

$$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{blend}}} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the two components.** For a SIS mid-block (polyisoprene) blended with a C5 tackifier:

- Component 1: Polyisoprene (PI) — $T_{g,1} = 203\,\text{K}\ (-70\,°C)$
- Component 2: C5 tackifier — $T_{g,2} = 328\,\text{K}\ (+55\,°C)$

**Step 2 — Define the weight fractions.** Let the tackifier loading be **100 phr**, i.e. **$w_2 = 100/(100+100) = 0.500$**. Then $w_1 = 0.500$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Fox equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{blend}}} = \frac{0.500}{203} + \frac{0.500}{328}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term numerically:**

$$\frac{0.500}{203} = 2.463 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}, \qquad \frac{0.500}{328} = 1.524 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Sum and invert:**

$$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{blend}}} = (2.463 + 1.524) \times 10^{-3} = 3.987 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g^{\text{blend}} = \frac{1}{3.987 \times 10^{-3}} = 250.8\,\text{K} = -22.4\,°C$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation:**

$$T_g^{\text{blend}} \approx -22\,°C \quad \text{vs.}\quad T_g^{\text{PI, neat}} = -70\,°C$$

The tackifier **raises the effective $T_g$ of the soft phase by ~48 °C**. This is exactly the desired effect: the adhesive becomes softer and more compliant at room temperature, which dramatically increases **surface tack and wet-out** without sacrificing cohesion. This is the single most important quantitative derivation you must memorise for the board exam.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Melt-Viscosity for Hot-Melt Coating

A typical TPE hot-melt adhesive is coated at **160 °C**. The melt viscosity must lie between **5,000 cP and 50,000 cP** for clean slot-die coating. The Arrhenius equation governs the temperature dependence:

$$\eta_0(T) = \eta_0(T_\text{ref}) \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{E_\eta}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_\text{ref}}\right)\right]$$

**Given (typical SIS hot-melt formulation):**
- Reference viscosity $\eta_0(180\,°C) = 15{,}000\,\text{cP}$
- Activation energy $E_\eta = 65\,\text{kJ/mol}$ (typical for SIS)
- $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $T_\text{ref} = 453.15\,\text{K}$ (180 °C)
- Target coating temperature $T = 433.15\,\text{K}$ (160 °C)

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_\text{ref}} = \frac{1}{433.15} - \frac{1}{453.15}$$

$$= (2.308 \times 10^{-3}) - (2.207 \times 10^{-3}) = 1.012 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponential argument:**

$$\frac{E_\eta}{R} = \frac{65{,}000}{8.314} = 7817\,\text{K}$$

$$\frac{E_\eta}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_\text{ref}}\right) = 7817 \times 1.012 \times 10^{-4} = 0.791$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the exponential:**

$$e^{0.791} = 2.206$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the new viscosity:**

$$\eta_0(160\,°C) = 15{,}000 \times 2.206 = 33{,}090\,\text{cP}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

$$\eta_0(160\,°C) \approx 33{,}000\,\text{cP}$$

This value lies **within the ideal coating window (5,000 – 50,000 cP)**. Lowering the coating temperature by 20 °C nearly **doubles** the melt viscosity — a critical sensitivity that operators must control to ±2 °C on production lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** This problem has appeared in KTU Module 3 past papers (Dec 2023, May 2024). You are expected to compute in Kelvin, NOT in Celsius, throughout the entire calculation. Failure to convert is the single most common mistake.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chang's Viscoelastic Peel-Strength Criterion

Chang (1991) showed that the **peel strength of a PSA** is maximised when the adhesive's viscoelastic response occurs at the **correct frequency window** corresponding to the peeling rate:

$$P_{\text{peel}} = K \cdot (G' \cdot G'')^{1/2} = K \cdot G^* \cdot \sqrt{\tan\delta}$$

**Given a PSA at 23 °C, peel rate = 300 mm/min (= 0.005 m/s):**
- Storage modulus $G' = 5.0 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}$
- Loss modulus $G'' = 6.5 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}$
- Geometric constant $K = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{3/2}\text{/N}^{1/2}$ (calibration for the specific test geometry)

**Step 1 — Calculate $\tan\delta$:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{6.5 \times 10^4}{5.0 \times 10^4} = 1.30$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric mean modulus:**

$$\sqrt{G' \cdot G''} = \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^4 \times 6.5 \times 10^4} = \sqrt{3.25 \times 10^9} = 5.70 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chang's equation:**

$$P_{\text{peel}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.70 \times 10^4 = 1.14\,\text{N/25 mm width}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

A peel strength of **~1.14 N/25 mm** is in the **typical removable-label PSA range (0.5–3 N/25 mm)**. If $\tan\delta < 1$, the adhesive is too elastic (cohesive failure); if $\tan\delta > 2$, it is too soft (adhesive transfer to substrate). The **sweet spot for removable HMPSA is $\tan\delta \approx 1.2$ – $1.6$** at the application frequency.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – How Each Formulation Lever Affects the Three PSA Performance Axes

| Lever ↑ (Increase) | Tack (Quick-Stick) | Peel Strength | Shear Strength (Holding Power) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tackifier loading** | ↑ then ↓ (peaks at 100–120 phr) | ↑ then ↓ | ↓ (overload softens matrix) |
| **Plasticizer oil** | ↑ | ↑ initially, ↓ long-term | ↓↓ (severe drop) |
| **Styrene content $\phi_S$** | ↓ | ↑ (stiffer response) | ↑↑ |
| **Mid-block molecular weight** | ↓ | ↓ (slower flow) | ↑ |
| **Crosslink density** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑↑↑ |
| **Filler (CaCO$_3$)** | ↓ | slight ↑ | ↑ |
| **Antioxidant** | neutral | neutral | ↑ (long-term ageing) |

> [!TIP]
> **The PSA Performance Triangle** — Tack, Peel, and Shear are the three legs. Improving any one typically degrades the others. This is the **fundamental formulation trade-off** you must discuss in any 14-mark board question.

---

### 3.5 Worked Analytical Problem — Designing an SIS Hot-Melt PSA for a Label Application

**Target specification (given):**
- Loop tack ≥ **800 g/25 mm**
- 180° peel on stainless steel ≥ **1.5 N/25 mm**
- Shear holding power (1 kg, 25 × 25 mm) ≥ **5 hours**
- Softening point (Ring & Ball) ≥ **85 °C**

**Step 1 — Base polymer selection.** Choose an **SIS grade with $\phi_S = 0.22$** (e.g., Kraton D-1161 or equivalent) to balance tack and shear.

**Step 2 — Tackifier.** Pick a **C5 aliphatic resin** at **110 phr** (compatible with polyisoprene mid-block, raises mid-block $T_g$ to ≈ $-20\,°C$).

**Step 3 — Plasticizer.** Add **30 phr paraffinic process oil** to lower melt viscosity to ~25,000 cP at 160 °C.

**Step 4 — Stabilizer.** Add **1 phr Irganox 1010 + 1 phr Irgafos 168** for thermal-oxidative stability during 160 °C processing.

**Step 5 — Predicted performance (using Fox + Chang + Arrhenius):**

$$T_g^{\text{mid+resin}} \approx -22\,°C \quad (\text{excellent room-temperature tack})$$
$$\eta_0(160\,°C) \approx 27{,}500\,\text{cP} \quad (\text{ideal coating})$$
$$G' \approx 4.5 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa},\quad G'' \approx 5.8 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa} \quad (\tan\delta \approx 1.29)$$
$$P_{\text{peel}} \approx 1.6\,\text{N/25 mm} \quad \text{(meets specification)}$$

**Conclusion:** The designed formulation **meets all four specifications** with comfortable safety margin on shear.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Morphology of a Styrenic TPE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw1["Styrene Monomer"] --> synth["Anionic Block Polymerization"]
    raw2["Isoprene Monomer"] --> synth
    synth --> chain["Linear ABA Triblock Chain"]
    chain --> cool["Cool Below Tg Hard Block"]
    cool --> microA["Microphase Separation"]
    microA --> domainA["Hard Styrene Domains"]
    microA --> domainB["Soft Elastomeric Matrix"]
    domainA --- anchor["Physical Crosslinks"]
    domainB --- anchor
    domainA --> final["TPE Morphology"]
    domainB --> final
    final --> out1["Hot Melt Adhesive"]
    final --> out2["Pressure Sensitive Adhesive"]
    final --> out3["Melt Processible Sealant"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This diagram traces the journey from monomer feedstocks through anionic polymerisation, microphase separation on cooling, and the final morphology that delivers adhesive performance. The key message: **morphology is set by thermodynamics, not by the formulator** — but the formulator *exploits* it via additives.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Formulation Architecture of a TPE Hot-Melt PSA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["TPE Polymer 100 phr SBS or SIS or SEBS"] --> F["Hot Melt Adhesive Compound"]
    B["Tackifier Resin 80 to 150 phr C5 C9 Hydrogenated"] --> F
    C["Plasticizer Oil 10 to 60 phr Paraffinic Naphthenic"] --> F
    D["Stabilizers 0.5 to 3 phr Antioxidant UV Absorber"] --> F
    E["Fillers 0 to 50 phr Calcium Carbonate Silica"] --> F
    F --> proc["Hot Melt Coating 150 to 180 C"]
    proc --> end1["Labels"]
    proc --> end2["Packaging Tapes"]
    proc --> end3["Hygiene Nonwovens"]
    proc --> end4["Automotive Interior Trim"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every arrow is a *formulation input*; the central box `F` is the **melt-compounded masterbatch** that emerges from a single- or twin-screw extruder. The four downstream branches represent the **four largest end-use markets** for TPE HMPSA globally.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — The PSA Performance Triangle and Formulation Levers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Increase Tackifier"] --> t1["Tack UP Peel UP Shear DOWN"]
    L2["Increase Plasticizer"] --> t2["Tack UP Shear DOWN Peel DOWN"]

```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hot-Melt Coating Process Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    melter["Melt Tank 160 to 180 C"] --> pump["Gear or Piston Pump"]
    pump --> filter["In-line Strainer"]
    filter --> slot["Slot Die or Roller Die"]
    slot --> substrate["Substrate Web"]
    substrate --> chill["Chill Roller 15 to 25 C"]
    chill --> wind["Winder"]
    wind --> conv["Slitter Rewinder"]
    conv --> ship["Finished Tape or Label"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical process parameters:** (i) Melt-tank nitrogen blanket to prevent oxidative scorching, (ii) slot-die gap typically **0.10 – 0.50 mm**, (iii) coat-weight range **5 – 50 g/m²**, (iv) line speed **100 – 800 m/min**.
