## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define a thermoplastic elastomer (TPE). With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the two-phase morphology of an SBS block copolymer and highlight how it gives rise to elastomeric behaviour without chemical crosslinking.

**Model Answer (concise, ~150 words):**

A thermoplastic elastomer (TPE) is a polymer that processes as a thermoplastic but performs as an elastomer at room temperature. **SBS (Styrene-Butadiene-Styrene)** is an ABA triblock copolymer in which the **end A-blocks are hard polystyrene ($T_g \approx 100\,°C$)** and the **mid B-block is soft polybutadiene ($T_g \approx -90\,°C$)**.

On cooling from the melt, the two blocks microphase-separate: the **polystyrene end-blocks cluster into discrete glassy domains** (~10–30 nm in size) dispersed in a continuous **polybutadiene rubbery matrix**. Each rubbery mid-block has **both of its ends anchored** in a different glassy styrene domain — these domains act as **physical crosslinks** that prevent chain slippage and impart elastic recovery, mimicking the role of sulfur crosslinks in conventional rubber. Upon heating above 100 °C, the styrene domains soften, the physical network breaks down, and the polymer flows as a thermoplastic. On cooling the network **re-forms spontaneously**. Thus **elastomeric behaviour is achieved entirely through physical, reversible crosslinks**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
List the **six functional ingredients** in a TPE-based hot-melt pressure-sensitive adhesive (HMPSA) formulation and state the specific role of the **tackifying resin**. Why is C5 aliphatic resin preferred for an SIS mid-block while a hydrogenated resin is mandatory for SEBS?

**Model Answer (concise, ~140 words):**

The six ingredients are: (1) **Base TPE polymer** (100 phr, cohesive strength), (2) **Tackifying resin** (80–150 phr, surface tack), (3) **Plasticizer oil** (10–60 phr, melt-viscosity reduction), (4) **Stabilizers** (0.5–3 phr, anti-oxidative/UV protection), (5) **Fillers** (0–50 phr, rheology/cost), (6) **Crosslinker** (0–5 phr, optional heat-resistance boost).

The tackifier is **dissolved in the soft mid-block**, raising its effective $T_g$ from ≈ $-70\,°C$ to ≈ $-20\,°C$. This makes the adhesive **soft and compliant at room temperature**, giving high surface tack and wet-out, **without sacrificing cohesion** because the hard styrene domains remain intact.

C5 aliphatic resin is chosen for **SIS** because its **Hansen solubility parameter ($\delta_T \approx 16.5$ – $17.5\,\text{MPa}^{1/2}$) closely matches that of polyisoprene**, ensuring miscibility. For **SEBS**, the mid-block is hydrogenated (saturated) and **non-polar**, so an **aromatic C9 resin would phase-separate**. Only a **fully hydrogenated (C5 or DCPD) resin** is miscible, giving the same tackifying effect with **UV/heat stability**.

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *TPE Morphology & Formulation Design*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** With suitable equations, derive the **Fox-equation estimate of the effective glass-transition temperature** of the soft phase of an SIS-based HMPSA containing **100 phr of C5 tackifier**. Assume $T_g$ of polyisoprene = 203 K and $T_g$ of C5 resin = 328 K. Discuss how this $T_g$ shift influences tack, peel, and shear.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A formulator must select the **base TPE polymer** for a hot-melt packaging adhesive that must bond **polyolefin films at 60 – 80 °C service** and survive **24-month warehouse ageing**. Compare **SBS, SIS, and SEBS** and recommend one grade, justifying your answer with reference to (i) mid-block polarity, (ii) UV stability, (iii) compatibility with C5/C9/hydrogenated tackifiers, and (iv) recyclability.

**Model Solution (Q-A):**

**(a) [7 marks — Step-by-step]**

Step 1 — Convert tackifier loading to weight fraction. The base polymer is **100 parts**, tackifier is **100 parts** (i.e. 100 phr), therefore:

$$w_{\text{PI}} = \frac{100}{100+100} = 0.500, \qquad w_{\text{C5}} = 0.500$$

Step 2 — State the Fox equation:

$$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{blend}}} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$$

Step 3 — Substitute:

$$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{blend}}} = \frac{0.500}{203} + \frac{0.500}{328}$$

Step 4 — Numerator per term:

$$\frac{0.500}{203} = 2.4630 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}, \qquad \frac{0.500}{328} = 1.5244 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

Step 5 — Sum and invert:

$$\frac{1}{T_g^{\text{blend}}} = 3.9874 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1} \implies T_g^{\text{blend}} = 250.8\,\text{K} = -22.4\,°C$$

**Discussion (must appear for full marks):**
- **Tack ↑↑** — Soft phase is now compliant at room temperature ($T_g = -22.4\,°C$ is well below 25 °C). The adhesive flows readily on contact, giving high surface wet-out.
- **Peel ↑** — High tack + retained cohesion (hard styrene domains unaffected) gives strong peel.
- **Shear ↓** — The matrix is now softer; under sustained load it creeps faster, so holding power is reduced.
- **Conclusion:** Adding 100 phr of C5 resin optimises tack and peel but **sacrifices shear**. To recover shear, a small amount (1–3 phr) of **UV crosslinker** is added.

**(b) [7 marks — Comparative table + recommendation]**

| Property | SBS | SIS | SEBS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-block chemistry | Polybutadiene (unsaturated, C=C) | Polyisoprene (unsaturated, C=C) | Hydrogenated poly(ethylene-co-butylene), saturated |
| Mid-block $T_g$ | ≈ −90 °C | ≈ −70 °C | ≈ −55 °C |
| UV / ageing resistance | **Poor** (C=C oxidises) | **Poor** (C=C oxidises) | **Excellent** (saturated) |
| Tackifier compatibility | C5, C9, mixed | C5, polyterpene | Hydrogenated only |
| Recyclability | Good | Good | Good |
| Cost (USD/kg, indicative) | 2 – 3 | 3 – 5 | 5 – 8 |
| Service temperature limit | 70 °C | 70 °C | **120 °C** (after hydrogenation) |
| Bond to polyolefins | Good | Moderate | **Excellent** (low polarity → matches PE/PP) |

**Recommendation (justified):** **SEBS** is the correct choice because:
1. The application requires **24-month ageing** under warehouse conditions — SBS and SIS will yellow and lose tack due to oxidation of the mid-block double bonds; SEBS is **fully saturated** and therefore UV/oxidation stable.
2. Service temperature of **60 – 80 °C** exceeds the $T_\text{dis}$ of SBS/SIS (~70 °C); SEBS remains dimensionally stable up to ~120 °C.
3. Polyolefin substrates (PE, PP) are non-polar and have surface energies in the **29 – 31 mJ/m²** range; the non-polar SEBS mid-block wet-outs these surfaces far better than SBS or SIS.
4. Hydrogenated tackifier resins (e.g., Escorez 5400, Regalite R1100) are **fully miscible** with SEBS, providing the same tack boost as C5 provides to SIS.

**Finalised formulation recommendation:**
SEBS (e.g., Kraton G-1652, 100 phr) + hydrogenated C5 resin (110 phr) + paraffinic oil (30 phr) + Irganox 1076 (1 phr) + Irgafos 168 (1 phr). Coating at **170 – 180 °C**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Processing, Performance & Application*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Describe the **hot-melt coating process** for an SEBS-based HMPSA used to manufacture a self-adhesive label stock. Include the temperature profile, typical coating speeds, coat-weight range, and explain **why nitrogen blanketing of the melt tank is essential**.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Apply **Chang's viscoelastic peel criterion** to evaluate whether an SEBS HMPSA with $G' = 6.0 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}$ and $G'' = 4.5 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}$ at 23 °C and 300 mm/min peel rate will meet a **minimum peel specification of 1.2 N/25 mm** (assume $K = 2.0 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^{3/2}\text{/N}^{1/2}$). Discuss, with reference to the **PSA Performance Triangle**, what formulation change you would recommend if the peel passes but the **shear holding power fails at 3 hours** (target = 5 hours).

**Model Solution (Q-B):**

**(a) [7 marks — Process description]**

The hot-melt coating process for an SEBS HMPSA label stock consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Melt Compounding (off-line).** TPE polymer, tackifier, oil and stabilizers are melt-mixed in a **twin-screw extruder** at **160 – 180 °C** under **nitrogen** to prevent oxidative degradation. The melt is discharged as a strand, cooled on a chilled belt and diced into pellets.

2. **Melt Tank (on-line).** Pellets are fed into a **grid melter or tank melter** at **160 – 180 °C**. A **continuous nitrogen blanket** is maintained above the melt surface — this is essential because:
   - At 160 – 180 °C, SEBS + tackifier are exposed to atmospheric O₂.
   - Oxygen initiates **free-radical chain scission** of the mid-block, lowering molecular weight.
   - Degradation products (ketones, aldehydes, coloured chromophores) cause **discolouration, odour, viscosity drift, and loss of tack**.
   - Nitrogen blanket reduces dissolved O₂ concentration by >95 %, extending pot-life from < 4 hours to > 24 hours.

3. **Pumping.** A **positive-displacement gear pump** delivers the melt at **5 – 100 bar** through a **strainer** (40 – 100 µm filter).

4. **Coating.** The melt is coated onto **release-liner siliconised paper** via a **slot die** or **curtain coater** at **30 – 100 g/m²** coat-weight.

5. **Lamination.** The coated release liner is nipped against the **face-stock** (paper, PE, PP, PET film) at the **chill roll** (15 – 25 °C).

6. **Winding & Slitting.** The finished laminate is wound and slit into **rolls of labels**.

**Typical process parameters:**

- Melt temperature: **160 – 180 °C**
- Line speed: **100 – 400 m/min** (state-of-the-art lines reach 800 m/min)
- Coat weight: **15 – 35 g/m²** (labelstock), up to **50 g/m²** (heavy-duty tape)
- Slot-die gap: **0.15 – 0.40 mm**
- Adhesive viscosity at coating: **10,000 – 40,000 cP**
- Lamination nip pressure: **0.5 – 2.0 bar**

**(b) [7 marks — Chang calculation + recommendation]**

**Step 1 — Compute $\tan\delta$:**

$$\tan\delta = \frac{G''}{G'} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^4}{6.0 \times 10^4} = 0.750$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric-mean modulus:**

$$\sqrt{G' \cdot G''} = \sqrt{6.0 \times 10^4 \times 4.5 \times 10^4} = \sqrt{2.7 \times 10^9} = 5.196 \times 10^4\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chang's criterion:**

$$P_{\text{peel}} = K \cdot \sqrt{G' \cdot G''} = 2.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 5.196 \times 10^4 = 1.04\,\text{N/25 mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to specification:**

$$P_{\text{peel}} = 1.04\,\text{N/25 mm} \quad \text{vs.}\quad P_{\text{spec}} = 1.20\,\text{N/25 mm}$$

**Result:** Peel **fails** the specification by ~13 %. The adhesive is currently **too elastic** ($\tan\delta = 0.75 < 1$, meaning $G' > G''$, the adhesive responds predominantly solid-like at the peel frequency and resists deformation).

**Step 5 — PSA Performance Triangle analysis.** Because peel **failed** AND shear also failed (3 h vs. 5 h target), both **tackifier loading** and **crosslink density** must be revisited.

**Step 6 — Recommended formulation change (justified):**

1. **Increase tackifier loading by 15 phr** (from 110 → 125 phr). This will:
   - Lower the effective $T_g$ of the mid-block (more compliant at RT).
   - Raise $\tan\delta$ to ≈ 1.1, raising peel to ≈ **1.30 N/25 mm** (✓ passes).
2. **Add 1.5 phr of a UV-crosslinkable difunctional acrylate (e.g., SR-238, HDDA) and cure inline with 2 passes of UV at 250 mJ/cm²**. This will:
   - Form **additional covalent bridges** between SEBS mid-blocks.
   - Increase cohesive strength without affecting surface tack.
   - Extend shear from 3 h → **8 – 10 h** (✓ passes).

**Conclusion:** A small, well-targeted adjustment of **tackifier + post-coating UV crosslink** simultaneously solves both performance gaps while maintaining the recyclable thermoplastic character of the bulk polymer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on TPE-Based Adhesives Questions**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error in Fox equation.** Always work in **Kelvin**. Inserting Celsius into the denominator yields garbage and **zero marks** for the calculation step.
> 2. **Confusing SBS and SEBS.** SBS has a **double bond** in every mid-block monomer unit — *saturated*. SEBS is **saturated**. They are *not* interchangeable. Wrong choice for ageing-sensitive applications costs the full 7 marks on part (b).
> 3. **Forgetting the role of nitrogen blanketing.** Examiners consistently test this in the 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question. Stating *"to prevent oxidation"* alone is insufficient — you must specify **free-radical chain scission**, **chromophore formation**, and **viscosity drift**.
> 4. **Treating the PSA Performance Triangle as a free trade-off.** The trade-off is **real but bounded**. Excessive tackifier gives cohesive failure, excessive crosslinker gives adhesive failure. Examiners expect you to quantify, not just describe.
> 5. **Confusing tack with adhesion.** **Tack** is the property of forming a bond with **no external pressure** (instant grab); **adhesion (peel)** requires a finite contact time and pressure. Mixing up the two is a classic 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Omitting units in final answers.** Every numerical answer must carry **units** (N/25 mm, °C, cP, phr). Unit omission is a ½-mark deduction per question on the official scheme.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **TPE = ABA triblock copolymer** with hard styrene end-blocks + soft elastomeric mid-block. **Microphase separation** is the fundamental phenomenon.
- ✅ **Three families dominate adhesives:** **SBS** (low cost, low ageing resistance), **SIS** (highest tack, low ageing), **SEBS** (best ageing & heat resistance, premium price).
- ✅ **Styrene content $\phi_S \approx 0.15$ – $0.30$** is the adhesive-grade window; outside this, you move into plastic or thermoplastic-elastomer-block territory.
- ✅ **Six ingredients always present:** base TPE, tackifier, plasticizer, stabilizer, filler, optional crosslinker.
- ✅ **Tackifier selection is governed by Hansen solubility-parameter matching**; the **Fox equation** quantifies the resulting $T_g$ shift.
- ✅ **Hot-melt processing** occurs at **160 – 180 °C** with viscosity **10,000 – 50,000 cP**; **nitrogen blanketing is non-negotiable**.
- ✅ **Chang's peel criterion:** $P = K \cdot \sqrt{G' \cdot G''}$; $\tan\delta = G''/G'$ — keep it in the **1.0 – 1.6** window for optimal removable PSA performance.
- ✅ **PSA Performance Triangle** is the guiding principle: **Tack ↑ → Shear ↓ → Peel ↑**; **Crosslink density ↑ → Shear ↑ → Tack ↓ → Peel ↓**; **Styrene ↑ → Modulus ↑ → Shear ↑ → Tack ↓**.
- ✅ **Always use Kelvin** in the Fox and Arrhenius equations.
- ✅ **C5 aliphatic resin** for SIS; **hydrogenated C5 / DCPD** for SEBS; **C9 or mixed C5/C9** for SBS.
- ✅ **Recyclability is the unique selling proposition** of TPE adhesives over vulcanized rubber — leverage this in any "advantages" question.
- ✅ **Four pillar end-markets:** labels, packaging tapes, hygiene nonwovens, automotive interior trim.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** When asked to **design** an adhesive, always answer in the fixed order: **(i) base polymer + grade + phr, (ii) tackifier + grade + phr, (iii) plasticizer + grade + phr, (iv) stabilizer package, (v) crosslinker (if needed), (vi) predicted properties, (vii) coating conditions.** Examiners reward structured, complete answers over rambling prose.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyvinyl Acetates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polyvinyl Acetates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polyvinyl Acetate (PVAc / PVA)** is a synthetic, linear, thermoplastic, addition-type homopolymer synthesized by the free-radical chain-growth polymerization of the **vinyl acetate (VAc / CH₃COOCH=CH₂)** monomer. The IUPAC systematic name is **poly(1-acetoxyethylene)**, and it is the world's most widely produced polymer emulsion (by volume), forming the chemical backbone of the familiar "white glue," "school glue," "Elmer's glue," and carpenter's woodworking adhesives.

The repeat unit of PVAc is:

$$\left[ -CH_2 - CH(OCOCH_3) - \right]_n$$

The pendant acetoxy group (–OCOCH₃) is **bulky, polar, and non-crystallizable**, which is why PVAc is essentially **amorphous** with a glass transition temperature (**T_g ≈ 28–32 °C**). This is a critical adhesive parameter — it is the temperature below which the polymer enters a rigid, glassy state and loses tack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "PVA" in the adhesive industry almost universally refers to **poly(VINYL ACETATE)**, *not* poly(vinyl alcohol). This is the single most common source of confusion in examinations. PVA (adhesive) = PVAc; PVOH (alcohol) is a different polymer made by *saponifying* PVAc.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a long freight train (the polymer chain) where every single freight car is **identical** — a flatcar carrying a giant acetate (vinegar-smelling) cargo. Because every car is bulky and the cargo is awkward to pack, the train cars **cannot stack neatly into a tight, crystalline pile**. Instead, they tangle like cooked spaghetti.

This "tangled spaghetti" is **PVAc in its adhesive state**. When you spread it on paper or wood:
- The "spaghetti strands" physically **interdiffuse** into the pores of the substrate.
- The acetate groups form **weak, reversible hydrogen bonds** with –OH groups on cellulose and wood.
- When the water evaporates, the strands become entangled and lock the two surfaces together — purely **mechanical interlocking + secondary bonding**, no chemical reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is white glue white and not transparent?** Because the polymer particles in the water emulsion are ~0.1–1 μm spheres that **scatter light** (Mie scattering). Once the water dries, the spheres fuse (coalesce) into a continuous transparent film — exactly like a milk puddle drying on the floor.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Engineering Metrics (Bold Highlights)

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Monomer Molecular Weight** | **86.09 g/mol** | Vinyl acetate CH₂=CHOCOCH₃ |
| **Repeat Unit MW** | **86.09 g/mol** | One VAc unit per backbone carbon |
| **Density (bulk polymer)** | **~1.19 g/cm³** | Heavier than water |
| **Glass Transition T_g** | **~28–32 °C** | Defines the service temperature limit |
| **Refractive Index n_D** | **~1.47** | Close to glass, hence transparent film |
| **Solubility Parameter δ** | **~19.2 (J/cm³)^½** | Indicates good adhesion to polar substrates |

## 1.4 Vinyl Acetate Monomer — The Building Block

Vinyl acetate is the **vinyl ester of acetic acid**, with a double bond between C1 and C2, and an acetoxy group attached to C2.

$$\text{CH}_2 = \text{CH} - \text{O} - \text{CO} - \text{CH}_3$$

Key monomer properties:
- **Boiling point:** 72.7 °C (volatile, low BP — a major health & safety concern)
- **Flash point:** –8 °C (highly flammable)
- **Reactivity ratio in copolymerization** r₁(VAc) ≈ 0.9–1.0 with most comonomers (good copolymerizability)
- **Electron-donating OCOCH₃ group** activates the double bond toward free-radical attack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEP-2020 / KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO2: "Identify the structure–property relationships of major adhesive polymer families."** PVAc is the flagship example because its *non-crystallizable* pendant group (the very thing that prevents crystallization) is the *direct cause* of its amorphous, sticky, adhesive character.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Routes for PVAc

Vinyl acetate can be polymerized by four mechanisms. The **emulsion polymerization route dominates** commercially because PVAc degrades at temperatures close to its ceiling temperature, and water is an excellent heat sink.

### 2.1.1 Bulk Polymerization
- Monomer + initiator only (no solvent).
- Highly exothermic; difficult heat removal → runaway risk.
- Product: pure polymer; molecular weight **very high (10⁶+)**.
- Used mainly for cast sheets and specialty grades.

### 2.1.2 Solution Polymerization
- Monomer dissolved in a solvent (e.g., ethyl acetate, acetone, methanol).
- Solvent acts as a chain-transfer agent → **lowers molecular weight (10⁴–10⁵)**.
- Direct production of adhesive solution (solvent-borne wood glue, contact adhesive).

### 2.1.3 Suspension Polymerization ("Pearl Polymerization")
- Monomer droplets dispersed in water with a protective colloid (PVA/polyvinyl alcohol actually used here as stabilizer — historical irony!).
- Bead product: ~0.1–2 mm spheres.
- Used for chewing-gum base, hot-melt feedstocks.

### 2.1.4 Emulsion Polymerization (The Workhorse)
- **Water + monomer + surfactant + water-soluble initiator (e.g., K₂S₂O₈).**
- Smith-Ewart mechanism: micelles → particle nucleation → particle growth.
- Final product: milky latex, ~50% solids, particle size 50–500 nm.
- The **only** route used for the iconic white PVA wood glue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why surfactants?** Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) or nonylphenol ethoxylates form micelles. The hydrophobic vinyl acetate monomer diffuses *into* the micelles. The water-soluble persulfate radical enters the micelle and initiates polymerization. The micelle becomes a polymer particle. This compartmentalization dramatically **suppresses termination**, giving very high MW at high rates — a kinetic miracle.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Radical Polymerization of VAc

The classical three-stage free-radical chain mechanism applies:

### Stage 1: Initiation
$$\text{I}_2 \xrightarrow{k_d} 2 \text{I}^\bullet$$
$$\text{I}^\bullet + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)} \xrightarrow{k_i} \text{I–CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{OCOCH}_3)$$

### Stage 2: Propagation
$$\text{I–CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{OCOCH}_3) + \text{CH}_2=\text{CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)} \xrightarrow{k_p} \text{I–CH}_2\text{–CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)–CH}_2-\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}(\text{OCOCH}_3)$$

### Stage 3: Termination (combination or disproportionation)

Combination:
$$2 \text{ PVAc}^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_{tc}} \text{PVAc–PVAc}$$

Disproportionation:
$$2 \text{ PVAc}^\bullet \xrightarrow{k_{td}} \text{PVAc–H} + \text{PVAc}_{(=\text{CH}_2)}$$

## 2.3 Chain Transfer — The Molecular Weight Controller

Vinyl acetate is famous for **chain transfer to monomer** because the VAc methyl-group hydrogen is highly labile. The transfer constant **C_M ≈ 2 × 10⁻⁴ at 60 °C** is unusually high, which is *why* PVAc emulsion polymers have relatively lower MW (~10⁵) than, say, polystyrene.

$$C_M = \frac{k_{tr,M}}{k_p}$$

The Mayo equation (instantaneous DP):
$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{(\bar{X}_n)_0} + C_M \frac{[M]}{[I]}$$

## 2.4 Copolymers of Vinyl Acetate — The "Engineered" PVA Adhesives

Pure PVAc has a T_g of 30 °C — too soft for many applications. By copolymerizing with harder or softer monomers, the T_g and flexibility are tuned. The single most important copolymer is **EVA (Ethylene–Vinyl Acetate)**.

| Comonomer | T_g of homopolymer (°C) | Copolymer T_g effect | Major use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ethylene (E)** | –120 | Lowers T_g, adds flexibility | Hot-melt adhesives, EVA foam |
| **VeoVa 10 / 9/10** (vinyl esters of versatic acid) | ~ –20 to +20 | Hydrophobicity, alkali resistance | Exterior-grade wood glue, latex paint binder |
| **Butyl acrylate** | –54 | Internal plasticization | Pressure-sensitive adhesives |
| **Vinyl chloride (VC)** | +80 | Raises T_g, hardness, flame retardance | Flooring adhesives |
| **Maleic esters / crotonic acid** | — | Internal crosslinking sites | Crosslinkable emulsions |
| **N-methylol acrylamide (NMA)** | — | Self-crosslinking on heating | Durable textile, wood adhesives |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** These six equations appear in over 80% of KTU ESE questions on PVAc. Memorize the symbol meanings *and* units — examiners routinely deduct for unit errors.

| Formula Name | Equation | Variable Meaning | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Carothers Eq. (DP)** | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | p = extent of reaction; $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average DP | dimensionless | Step-growth DP calc |
| **Mayo Eq. (Chain Transfer)** | $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{(\bar{X}_n)_0} + C_M \dfrac{[M]}{[I]}$ | $C_M$ = transfer const., [M]/[I] = mon./init. | dimensionless | MW control |
| **Fox Eq. (Copolymer T_g)** | $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction of comonomer i | K (note: **Kelvin**, not °C) | **Most-examined formula** |
| **Gordon–Taylor Eq.** | $T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g1} + k\, w_2 T_{g2}}{w_1 + k\, w_2}$ | k = Gordon–Taylor const. (≈ 0.5 for VAc–VC) | K | More accurate copolymer T_g |
| **Smith–Ewart Rate (Case 2)** | $R_p = \dfrac{\bar{n}\, k_p [M]_p N}{2}$ | $\bar{n}$ = avg radicals/particle, N = part. conc. | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Emulsion kinetics |
| **Einstein–Batchelor (Viscosity of Latex)** | $\eta_r = 1 + 2.5\phi + 6.2\phi^2$ | $\phi$ = volume fraction of dispersed phase | dimensionless | Latex rheology |
| **Mark–Houwink (Intrinsic Viscosity)** | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | K, a = polymer–solvent constants | dL/g | MW determination |
| **Cox–Merz Rule** | $\eta(\dot\gamma) = \vert G^*(\omega) \vert$ | Links steady & dynamic shear | Pa·s | Latex processability |

> **Critical LaTeX note for KTU answer sheets:** In the Fox equation, $T_g$ and $T_{g,i}$ are absolute temperatures in **Kelvin (K)**. Many students lose 1 mark by plugging in Celsius. Always convert: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

PVAc emulsion adhesives are the **dominant class by volume** of all synthetic adhesives globally, used for:
- **Woodworking joints** (D2/D3/D4 grade — EN 204/205 classification).
- **Paper lamination** (bookbinding, envelopes, corrugated boxes).
- **Wallpaper paste** and **wall-tile mastics**.
- **Concrete admixtures** (PVAc redispersible powder improves flexural strength and adhesion of cementitious renders).
- **Heat-seal coatings** on food packaging (cheese, snack wrappers).
- **Binder in non-woven textiles** and **latex paint** (the largest single use — 60%+ of PVAc production goes to architectural coatings).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The same chemistry that makes PVAc a great adhesive also makes it the standard **binder for interior house paint**. The film formation mechanism is identical — latex particles coalesce above the **Minimum Film Formation Temperature (MFFT ≈ T_g + 5 °C)** as water evaporates and capillary forces drive polymer interdiffusion across particle boundaries.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fox Equation for Copolymer T_g

The **Fox equation** is the most-tested formula in any KTU polymer-adhesive paper. We derive it from the principle of **additivity of free volumes** at the glass transition.

### Step 1: Define the free-volume assumption
At T_g, the free volume fraction f_g of the copolymer equals the weighted sum of the free volume fractions of the pure homopolymers at their respective T_g values.

$$f_g(\text{copolymer}) = w_1 f_{g,1} + w_2 f_{g,2}$$

### Step 2: Introduce the thermal expansion of free volume
Above T_g, free volume expands linearly with the thermal expansion coefficient of the free volume, $\alpha_f$:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

### Step 3: Apply to each component
For component 1 at temperature T:

$$f_1(T) = f_{g,1} + \alpha_{f,1}(T - T_{g,1})$$

For component 2 at temperature T:

$$f_2(T) = f_{g,2} + \alpha_{f,2}(T - T_{g,2})$$

### Step 4: Equate the two expressions at T_g of the copolymer
At the copolymer's glass transition T_g, the free volume of each homopolymer (now forced to coexist in the blend) must be the same:

$$f_{g,1} + \alpha_{f,1}(T_g - T_{g,1}) = f_{g,2} + \alpha_{f,2}(T_g - T_{g,2})$$

### Step 5: Assume the small-Δ approximation
Because $f_g \gg \alpha_f \cdot T$ in magnitude, retain only first-order terms and use the weighted average:

$$\alpha_{f,1}(T_g - T_{g,1}) \approx w_1 \cdot 0, \quad \alpha_{f,2}(T_g - T_{g,2}) \approx w_2 \cdot 0$$

The rigorous result, after equating weighted free volumes and canceling the small $f_g$ terms, reduces to:

$$\frac{w_1 \, \alpha_{f,1}}{T - T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2 \, \alpha_{f,2}}{T - T_{g,2}} = 0$$

### Step 6: Take the limit T → T_g and use the assumption $\alpha_{f,1} \approx \alpha_{f,2}$
This produces the classical Fox equation:

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g,1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g,2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation appears in full in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 outcomes. A 7-mark question can ask for a complete derivation; a 3-mark question can ask for the final equation plus an explanation of why **w_i must be weight fraction, not mole fraction**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Carothers Equation for Number-Average Degree of Polymerization

### Step 1: Define the extent of reaction p
For a step-growth system (adapted for VAc chain-growth with very long chains), let:
- $N_0$ = initial number of monomer molecules.
- $N$ = number of molecules remaining after reaction extent p.
- Each monomer has 2 functional groups, so initial functional groups $N_{f,0} = 2 N_0$.
- Reacted groups $= 2 N_0 \, p$; remaining groups $= 2 N_0 (1-p)$.

### Step 2: Number of bonds formed = number of molecules consumed
Number of new bonds = $N_0 - N$ (each bond consumes one molecule into a chain).
Equivalently, each bond consumes 2 functional groups:

$$2(N_0 - N) = 2 N_0 \, p \implies N = N_0(1-p)$$

### Step 3: Compute the number-average DP
The number-average degree of polymerization is the total number of structural units divided by the total number of molecules (chains + unreacted monomers):

$$\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0(1-p)} = \frac{1}{1-p}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p}}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the polydispersity index at high conversion
At high conversion, $\bar{X}_w = \dfrac{1+p}{1-p}$, hence:

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\bar{X}_w}{\bar{X}_n} = 1 + p$$

For p = 0.99, PDI = 1.99 — close to the theoretical limit of 2.0 for step-growth.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: VAc–Ethylene Copolymer T_g

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU ESE question):**
A copolymer of vinyl acetate and ethylene contains 25 wt% vinyl acetate. Given $T_{g,\text{PVAc}} = 305$ K and $T_{g,\text{PE}} = 150$ K, compute the T_g of the copolymer using the Fox equation. State whether the resulting adhesive is suitable for use as a hot-melt adhesive applied at 160 °C.

### Solution:

Step 1: Identify weight fractions.
- $w_{\text{VAc}} = 0.25$
- $w_E = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$

Step 2: Convert T_g values to Kelvin (already given).

Step 3: Apply the Fox equation.

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_{\text{VAc}}}{T_{g,\text{PVAc}}} + \frac{w_E}{T_{g,\text{PE}}}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.25}{305} + \frac{0.75}{150}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 8.197 \times 10^{-4} + 5.000 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 5.820 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g = \frac{1}{5.820 \times 10^{-3}} = 171.8 \; \text{K} = -101.4 \; °\text{C}$$

Step 4: Engineering interpretation.
- The T_g is far below room temperature; the copolymer is a **flexible, rubbery solid** at ambient.
- Since 160 °C ≫ T_g, the polymer is well into the **molten viscous regime** at application temperature.
- This makes the EVA copolymer an **excellent hot-melt adhesive** — it can be melt-applied, wets porous substrates, and solidifies rapidly on cooling.
- It would, however, be unsuitable for a high-modulus structural adhesive (e.g., load-bearing wood joint).

## 3.4 Worked Example: Chain-Transfer Control of PVAc MW

**Problem:** PVAc is polymerized in ethyl acetate at 60 °C. Initial [M]₀ = 8.0 mol/L, initiator [I] = 0.005 mol/L, transfer constant C_M = 2.0 × 10⁻⁴. The unperturbed DP₀ from kinetics alone is 4000. Find the actual $\bar{X}_n$ at 30% conversion.

### Solution:

Step 1: At 30% conversion, residual monomer:
$$[M] = [M]_0 (1 - 0.30) = 8.0 \times 0.70 = 5.6 \; \text{mol/L}$$

Step 2: Plug into the Mayo equation:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \frac{1}{4000} + (2.0 \times 10^{-4}) \frac{5.6}{0.005}$$

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} + (2.0 \times 10^{-4})(1120)$$

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} + 0.2240$$

$$\frac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = 0.22425$$

$$\bar{X}_n = 4.46$$

Step 3: Interpretation.
The actual DP is **only ~4–5** because VAc is a notorious chain-transfer monomer. This explains why PVAc emulsion polymers always have **moderate MW (10⁴–10⁵)** and why PVAc is rarely used as a tough engineering plastic — it cannot achieve the high MW needed for high tensile strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** In the Mayo equation, the ratio is [M]/[I] at the *current* instant, not the *initial* ratio. Use the **residual** [M] at the conversion of interest, otherwise the MW comes out too high.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: PVAc vs. Other Major Adhesive Polymers

| Property | PVAc | EVA (28% VAc) | Polyurethane | Natural Rubber | Epoxy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **T_g (°C)** | +30 | –25 | –40 to +80 | –72 | +60 to +120 |
| **Cure type** | Physical (drying) | Physical | Moisture / heat | Physical + vulcanization | Chemical (2-pack) |
| **Tack** | High | Medium | Medium | High (PSA) | None |
| **Water resistance** | Poor–Fair | Good | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Heat resistance** | Poor (< 60 °C) | Fair (80 °C) | Good | Fair | Excellent (>150 °C) |
| **Main use** | Wood, paper | Hot-melt, packaging | Footwear, laminates | PSA tapes, labels | Structural, aerospace |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1.0× | 1.2× | 3–4× | 2× | 5–8× |
