# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – April 2024, Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

### Q1(a): Distinguish clearly between "PVA," "PVAc," and "PVOH" as used in the adhesive industry. Why is the industry usage potentially confusing for students?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In the **adhesive industry**, the abbreviation "PVA" almost always refers to **poly(vinyl acetate)**, i.e., PVAc, the homopolymer of vinyl acetate monomer. Chemically, however, the strict abbreviation PVA stands for **poly(vinyl alcohol)** (PVOH), a *different* polymer obtained by the *saponification* (alcoholysis) of PVAc with methanol or sodium hydroxide. The two are chemically distinct: PVAc contains pendant –OCOCH₃ groups, while PVOH contains pendant –OH groups. PVOH is water-soluble and is used as a protective colloid in PVAc emulsions, not as the adhesive itself. The confusion is compounded because students reading European or older textbooks may see "PVA" written for the *acetate* form. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Q1(b)]**

### Q1(b): Why is polyvinyl acetate (PVAc) amorphous, and how does this affect its adhesive behavior?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

PVAc is amorphous because its pendant **acetoxy group (–OCOCH₃) is bulky, irregular, and non-symmetrical**, preventing the polymer chains from packing into an ordered crystalline lattice. The resulting **free volume is high**, depressing the melting point and yielding a low **glass transition temperature (T_g ≈ 28–32 °C)**, just above room temperature. This is ideal for adhesion: at ambient temperature the polymer is in a soft, rubbery, "tacky" state where chain segments have mobility to interdiffuse and wet substrate surfaces, then it cools to a glassy, load-bearing solid. The very irregularity of the side group that prevents crystallization is the *cause* of stickiness. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme, Q11, Module 3]**

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full Module Choice Pattern

**(a)** With the help of a clearly labeled mechanism, describe the free-radical chain-growth polymerization of vinyl acetate by the **emulsion polymerization** route. Identify the roles of surfactant, water-soluble initiator, and the micellar nucleation step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Fox equation**, a 70:30 by weight EVA copolymer is being designed as a hot-melt adhesive. The T_g of pure PVAc is 305 K and that of pure polyethylene is 150 K. **(i)** Calculate the copolymer T_g in °C. **(ii)** Comment on the suitability of this copolymer for a packaging hot-melt applied at 180 °C. **(iii)** State two methods by which the Fox equation can fail for real PVAc copolymers. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a): Emulsion Polymerization Mechanism (7 marks)

The **Smith–Ewart** mechanism of emulsion polymerization of vinyl acetate proceeds in three intervals:

**Interval I – Particle Nucleation (0–10% conversion):**
- Vinyl acetate monomer is dispersed in water (continuous phase) as droplets (~1 μm) stabilized by anionic surfactant (e.g., sodium dodecyl sulfate) above its CMC.
- Surfactant molecules self-assemble into **micelles** (5–10 nm), with hydrophobic tails inward and hydrophilic heads outward. A small fraction of the VAc monomer diffuses into the micelle cores, swelling them to ~5 nm particles.
- A water-soluble thermal initiator (e.g., potassium persulfate, K₂S₂O₈) decomposes into sulfate radicals.
- A radical enters a monomer-swollen micelle and initiates polymerization; the micelle becomes an **active polymer particle**.

**Interval II – Particle Growth (10–60% conversion):**
- The monomer reservoir is the large droplets; monomer diffuses through the aqueous phase into the growing polymer particles, where it polymerizes.
- The Smith–Ewart rate law (Case 2, average $\bar{n}$ = 0.5):
$$R_p = \frac{N \cdot k_p \cdot [M]_p}{2}$$
- where $N$ = number of particles per litre, $k_p$ = propagation rate constant, $[M]_p$ = monomer concentration in the particle.
- Compartmentalization: each radical is isolated in its own particle; termination by bimolecular combination is suppressed → **high MW at high rate** simultaneously. **(3 marks for mechanism)**

**Interval III – Completion (60–100% conversion):**
- Monomer droplets disappear; particles swell with the remaining free monomer.
- Polymerization slows as [M]_p drops; final latex contains 40–60% solids, with PVAc particles 50–500 nm in diameter stabilized by surfactant. **(1 mark)**

**Roles of ingredients:** (3 marks)
- **Surfactant (SDS, NP-40):** Forms micelles, the locus of nucleation; stabilizes the resulting latex particles against coagulation.
- **Water-soluble initiator (K₂S₂O₈):** Generates radicals in the aqueous phase that diffuse into micelles to start chains; persulfate also imparts negative charge on particle surfaces, contributing to colloidal stability.
- **Protective colloid (PVOH, HEC):** Provides steric stabilization in addition to electrostatic stabilization by surfactant.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b): Fox Equation Application (7 marks)

**(i) Calculation:**
- $w_{\text{VAc}} = 0.70$, $w_{\text{PE}} = 0.30$
- $T_{g,\text{PVAc}} = 305$ K, $T_{g,\text{PE}} = 150$ K

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.70}{305} + \frac{0.30}{150}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_g} = 2.295 \times 10^{-3} + 2.000 \times 10^{-3} = 4.295 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$T_g = 232.8 \; \text{K} = -40.4 \; °\text{C}$$

**(ii) Suitability for 180 °C application:**
- The T_g of –40.4 °C is far below 180 °C, so the polymer is in the **molten, low-viscosity state** at the application temperature.
- For a hot-melt adhesive, we require $T_g$ well below application T (to ensure flow and wetting) but $T_m$ of crystalline regions (if any) above service T. EVA with 30% PE is partially crystalline (PE-rich domains) and has a melt point around 80–95 °C.
- **Verdict:** The copolymer is **highly suitable as a packaging hot-melt** — it will melt cleanly at ~90 °C, flow at 180 °C with low viscosity, wet paper/paperboard substrates, and solidify rapidly on cooling.

**(iii) Two limitations of the Fox equation:**
1. **Assumes a homogeneous, single-phase amorphous random copolymer.** If phase separation occurs (e.g., blocky VAc–E sequences) the Fox equation underestimates the real T_g.
2. **Ignores specific interactions** (hydrogen bonding, dipole–dipole) between unlike comonomer units. In strongly interacting systems, Fox predictions can deviate by 10–20 K.
3. (Optional) Requires input $T_{g,i}$ of pure homopolymers that themselves may be ambiguous if the homopolymer is semicrystalline (PE T_g is poorly defined). **(7 marks total)**

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Explain the **carboxylic-acid–PVAc interaction and the role of the protective colloid (PVOH)** in stabilizing a PVAc wood-glue emulsion. Why is PVOH commonly chosen over synthetic surfactants? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PVAc emulsion is to be formulated for **D3-grade water-resistant wood joining (per EN 204)**. Propose a formulation strategy involving (i) the choice of comonomer, (ii) the crosslinker system, and (iii) the plasticizer level. Justify each choice with reference to the underlying chemistry. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a): Role of Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVOH) Protective Colloid (7 marks)

**Mechanism of steric stabilization (3 marks):**
- PVAc latex particles are 50–500 nm spheres of hydrophobic polymer. Without stabilization, they would coagulate via van der Waals attraction.
- PVOH is a **water-soluble, surface-active polymer**. During emulsion polymerization, growing PVAc radicals graft onto PVOH chains, anchoring the PVOH onto the particle surface with the –OH groups facing the water.
- The dangling PVOH chains form a **hydrated steric brush** ~10–20 nm thick, which prevents particle–particle contact by osmotic and entropic repulsion. This is the **steric stabilization** mechanism.
- The steric layer is robust to high shear, high salt, and freeze–thaw cycles — superior to charge-based (electrostatic) stabilization provided by surfactants alone.

**Why PVOH is preferred over synthetic surfactants (4 marks):**
1. **Multifunctionality:** PVOH also serves as a **cohesive strength promoter** in the dried film by hydrogen-bonding with cellulose substrates — the dried film is essentially a PVAc–PVOH interpenetrating network.
2. **Low foam:** Synthetic surfactants such as NP-40 generate foam during high-speed stirring; PVOH is low-foaming, important for production throughput.
3. **Improved film formation:** PVOH reduces the MFFT and aids particle coalescence; pure-surfactant-stabilized latices often crack on drying.
4. **Reactive site:** The –OH groups of PVOH can be post-functionalized (e.g., acetylated, crosslinked with glyoxal) to tune water resistance — impossible with simple alkyl surfactants.
5. **Cost and regulatory status:** PVOH is non-toxic, food-contact approved, and inexpensive — critical for paper/wood adhesives.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b): Formulation Strategy for D3-Grade PVAc Adhesive (7 marks)

**Target:** D3 grade (EN 204) requires the joint to withstand **4 hours in water at 23 °C** without delamination, plus 4 h dry recovery, and a brief hot-water (70 °C) exposure.

**(i) Comonomer choice (2 marks):**
- Use a **VAc–VeoVa 9/10 (or 10) copolymer** (vinyl ester of versatic acid, a branched C₉/C₁₀ carboxylic acid).
- **Justification:** The bulky, branched, hydrophobic C₉–C₁₀ side chain imparts **intrinsic water resistance** to the copolymer — water molecules cannot easily hydrogen-bond with the polymer film. The T_g of VeoVa homopolymer is ~ –20 °C, so adding ~15–20% VeoVa lowers the copolymer T_g to ~15 °C, still above room temperature, and the film remains hard and rigid at service.

**(ii) Crosslinker system (3 marks):**
- Incorporate **2–5% N-methylol acrylamide (NMA)** as a reactive comonomer, OR add an external crosslinker such as **aluminum chloride or zirconium carbonate** (1–2%) that coordinates with the –OH groups of any residual PVOH protective colloid.
- **Justification:** NMA introduces pendant –CH₂–NH–CH₂–OH groups that, on heating (120–150 °C for 5 min, or even ambient cure with acid catalyst), undergo **condensation crosslinking** to form methylene-ether bridges. The crosslinked network is no longer thermoplastic and therefore no longer water-sensitive (no chain disentanglement on water contact). Acid-hardening metal ions crosslink the PVOH through coordinate bonds with multiple –OH groups, also producing a water-resistant network.
- For ambient-cure D3 systems, **isocyanate-functional crosslinkers** (e.g., water-dispersible HDI trimer) at 1–2% give superior performance.

**(iii) Plasticizer level (2 marks):**
- **Limit external plasticizer (e.g., DBP, triethylene glycol) to ≤ 3 phr** (parts per hundred resin).
- **Justification:** Plasticizers are typically small polar molecules that migrate into the polymer film, lowering T_g and improving film formation. However, they also **increase water sensitivity** (plasticizer can be leached out by water, leaving porous film) and **reduce cohesive strength**. For D3, low plasticizer or internal plasticization (via the VeoVa comonomer) is preferred. High plasticizer levels push the formulation back to D1/D2 grade.

**Summary of strategy:** VAc–VeoVa base + NMA + Al/Zr crosslinker + minimal plasticizer = robust D3 formulation. **(7 marks total)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on PVAc questions (KTU 2024 Scheme pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing PVA, PVAc, and PVOH** — the single biggest error. Always write the full name on first use; never use "PVA" alone.
> 2. **Using Celsius in the Fox equation** — T_g must be in **Kelvin**. Examiners check this first.
> 3. **Forgetting chain transfer to monomer in MW calculations** — VAc is a *classic* chain-transfer monomer; the actual MW of PVAc is much lower than predicted by kinetics alone.
> 4. **Stating "PVAc is crystalline"** — wrong, it is amorphous because of the bulky irregular acetoxy group.
> 5. **Confusing EVA with PE** — EVA is a *copolymer*; PE is the *homopolymer of ethylene*.
> 6. **Confusing T_g with MFFT** — T_g is a thermodynamic transition, MFFT is the practical film-formation temperature; MFFT ≈ T_g + 5 °C for PVAc systems.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention "steric stabilization"** when asked about PVOH's role — credit goes to the *mechanism*, not just the ingredient.
> 8. **In mechanism questions, omitting the three classical stages** (initiation, propagation, termination) — examiners award 1 mark per correctly drawn stage.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PVAc (Module 3, PEPOT861)**

### Must-Memorize Facts
- **Monomer:** Vinyl acetate, CH₂=CH–OCOCH₃, MW = **86.09 g/mol**, BP = 72.7 °C.
- **Repeat unit:** [–CH₂–CH(OCOCH₃)–]_n, MW = 86.09 g/mol.
- **Polymer T_g ≈ 28–32 °C** (the defining adhesive parameter).
- **Polymer is amorphous** due to bulky, irregular pendant acetoxy group.
- **Commercial product form:** aqueous emulsion (latex), 40–60% solids, particle size 50–500 nm.
- **Dominant polymerization route:** **emulsion polymerization** (Smith–Ewart mechanism).
- **Initiator:** water-soluble (K₂S₂O₈); **Surfactant:** SDS or nonylphenol ethoxylate; **Protective colloid:** PVOH.
- **PVAc is water-insoluble**; PVOH is water-soluble (saponification product of PVAc).

### Must-Memorize Equations
- **Fox equation:** $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{T_{g,i}}$ (T_g in Kelvin).
- **Carothers:** $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$.
- **Mayo chain transfer:** $\dfrac{1}{\bar{X}_n} = \dfrac{1}{(\bar{X}_n)_0} + C_M \dfrac{[M]}{[I]}$.
- **Smith–Ewart rate:** $R_p = \dfrac{N k_p [M]_p}{2}$ (Case 2).

### Must-Know Concepts
- **D2 / D3 / D4 grade classification** of PVAc wood adhesives (EN 204/205).
- **Crosslinkers for water resistance:** NMA, glyoxal, isocyanate, Al/Zr salts.
- **Copolymers:** EVA (hot-melt), VAc–VeoVa (exterior wood), VAc–VC (flooring), VAc–BA (PSA).
- **Redispersible PVAc powder (RDP):** spray-dried PVAc emulsion with PVOH/anticaking shell — used as cement modifier.
- **MFFT** (Minimum Film Formation Temperature) ≈ T_g + 5 °C; coalescent solvents lower MFFT.
- **MFFT is the engineering version of T_g** for latex film formation.
- **Bond formation mechanism:** wetting → water loss → particle deformation → autohesion → secondary H-bonding → mechanical interlocking.

### Common Application Domains
- **D2:** Interior furniture, paper, envelopes, wallpaper paste.
- **D3:** Kitchen/bathroom wood (post-formable laminates).
- **D4:** Exterior structural wood, marine plywood (crosslinked + isocyanate-hardener).
- **Hot-melt:** Packaging, bookbinding, assembly-line product fastening.
- **Cement modifier:** Tile adhesives, ETICS renders, self-leveling compounds.
- **Binder for paints, non-wovens, and textile finishing.**

> **Last-line exam mantra:** *"Vinyl acetate + water + radical → emulsion → film → adhesion."* If you remember the chain **VAc → emulsion polymerization → latex particle → film formation → H-bond + mechanical interlock → bond**, you have the entire topic in one sentence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polyvinyl Alcohols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polyvinyl Alcohols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVOH / PVA)** is a synthetic, linear, **water-soluble** thermoplastic polymer with the repeating structural unit **—[CH₂—CH(OH)]ₙ—**, produced industrially by the **saponification (alcoholysis/hydrolysis) of polyvinyl acetate (PVAc)**, since the parent monomer vinyl alcohol is thermodynamically unstable and tautomerizes spontaneously to acetaldehyde.

**IUPAC Name:** Poly(1-hydroxyethylene) / Polyethenol
**CAS Number:** 9002-89-5
**Repeat Unit:** —CH₂—CH(OH)—
**Molar Mass of Repeat Unit:** 44.05 g/mol

It is unique among vinyl polymers because the hydroxyl group is directly attached to the polymer backbone, providing:
- High **polarity**
- Strong **hydrogen-bonding capacity**
- Excellent **film-forming** and **adhesive** characteristics
- **Tunable water solubility** (controlled by degree of hydrolysis)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Magnetic Glue that You Can Tune"**

Imagine you are building a wall using tiny LEGO blocks, where every block has a small **magnet (–OH group)** glued to it.

- If you build the wall with **too many magnets (Fully Hydrolyzed PVA, ~99% OH)**, the blocks stick to each other so tightly that water alone cannot pull them apart. The polymer resists cold water but dissolves in hot water. This is **like a strong, water-resistant paper lamination adhesive**.
- If you build the wall with **fewer magnets (Partially Hydrolyzed PVA, 87–89% OH)**, the remaining non-magnetic groups (acetate) act like **lubricating spacers**. Water molecules can easily slip in and pull the structure apart. This is **like a remoistenable envelope gum** — it dissolves in cold water.
- If you sprinkle in **borax (a "super-glue" crosslinker)**, the borate ions act like **bridging clamps** that grab two adjacent magnet groups, instantly forming a thick, tacky slime — this is the same chemistry that powers **Borax-based slime toys** and **industrial tackifier gels**.

**The Bottom Line:** PVA is essentially a "**programmable hydrogen-bonding polymer**" — by adjusting the **degree of hydrolysis** and **molecular weight**, an adhesive formulator can dial in any property from cold-water solubility to strong water-resistant bonding.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Thermodynamic Constants (Highlighted)

| Property | Value / Range | Remarks |
|----------|--------------|---------|
| Density (amorphous) | **1.25 – 1.32 g/cm³** | Increases with degree of crystallinity |
| Glass Transition T (Tg) | **75 – 85 °C** (fully hydrolysed) | **58 – 65 °C** (partially hydrolysed) |
| Melting Point (Tm) | **220 – 240 °C** (decomposes ~200 °C) | Decomposes before true melt |
| Refractive Index | **1.50 – 1.53** | High optical clarity in films |
| Dielectric Constant | **3.5 – 8.0** (varies with moisture) | Strongly hygroscopic |
| Tensile Strength (film) | **40 – 100 MPa** | Depends on MW & DH |
| Elongation at Break | **0 – 600 %** | Plasticised films much higher |
| Hansen Solubility Parameters | δD=15.0, δP=14.5, δH=23.5 (MPa½) | Highly polar, H-bonding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why PVA is Special Among Adhesives:**
> 1. **Only commercially significant vinyl polymer that is water-soluble yet fully synthetic.**
> 2. **Biodegradable** under aerobic and anaerobic conditions.
> 3. **Non-toxic, FDA-approved** for food-contact adhesives.
> 4. **Forms oxygen and aroma barrier films** — critical for packaging laminations.
> 5. **Adheres tenaciously to cellulosic substrates** through H-bonding.

---

## 1.4 Historical & Industrial Context

- **First synthesised:** 1924 by Herrmann and Haehnel (saponification of PVAc).
- **First industrial-scale production:** Started by **Kuraray (Japan)** in 1950; **DuPont** and **Wacker** followed.
- **Current global market:** > **1,200 kilo-tons/year** (2023 estimate).
- **Major producers:** Kuraray (Japan), Sekisui (Japan), Anhui Wanwei (China), Chang Chun (Taiwan), Nippon Gohsei (Japan).
- **KTU Connection:** Listed in PEPOT861 Module 3 alongside EVOH, PVAc, and acrylics as a **water-based synthetic adhesive polymer**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Polyvinyl Alcohol Cannot Be Synthesised by Direct Polymerisation

> [!WARNING]
> A common exam trap: "Why is PVA not prepared by polymerising vinyl alcohol monomer?" The answer lies in **tautomeric instability**.

**Vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH–OH)** exists in equilibrium with its tautomer **acetaldehyde (CH₃–CHO)**:

$$K_{eq} \;=\; \frac{[\text{Acetaldehyde}]}{[\text{Vinyl Alcohol}]} \;\approx\; 10^{\,12} \;\text{ at 25 °C}$$

The keto form is stabilised by ~**42.7 kJ/mol**; therefore any attempt to isolate or polymerise vinyl alcohol gives **polyacetaldehyde (metaldehyde/paraldehyde)** instead.

**Industrial Workaround:** Polymerise **vinyl acetate (VAc)** first → **Polyvinyl acetate (PVAc)** → then **saponify** the acetate groups using methanol + NaOH (or KOH) to obtain PVA.

---

## 2.2 Two-Step Industrial Synthesis — Detailed Mechanism

### Step 1: Polymerisation of Vinyl Acetate to Polyvinyl Acetate (PVAc)

Vinyl acetate is polymerised via **free-radical polymerisation** (solution, suspension, or emulsion). Initiators: AIBN, BPO, or persulphates.

$$\text{n CH}_2\text{=CH–OCOCH}_3 \;\xrightarrow{\text{R}^{\bullet},\,\Delta} \;\; \text{—[CH}_2\text{–CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)]}_n\text{—}$$

The polymerisation is **highly atactic** (random stereochemistry) because the methyl of the acetate is not bulky enough to enforce stereoregularity.

### Step 2: Alcoholysis (Saponification) of PVAc to PVA

PVAc is reacted with **methanol containing a catalytic amount of NaOH (1–5 %)**:

$$\text{—[CH}_2\text{–CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)]}_n\text{—} \;+\; n\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}}\; \text{—[CH}_2\text{–CH(OH)]}_n\text{—} \;+\; n\,\text{CH}_3\text{–COOCH}_3$$

The by-product **methyl acetate** is recovered by distillation and hydrolysed back to methanol + acetic acid, which is recycled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The reaction is NOT a hydrolysis with water** in the commercial sense — it is a **trans-esterification with methanol**. This is crucial because:
> 1. Methanol swells PVAc better than water → uniform reaction.
> 2. The by-product (methyl acetate) is more easily separated than acetic acid.
> 3. It avoids chain degradation that water/H⁺ would cause.

---

## 2.3 Degree of Hydrolysis (DH) — The Master Variable

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Degree of Hydrolysis (also called **Degree of Saponification**) = mole fraction of acetate groups converted to hydroxyl groups, expressed in %.

$$\boxed{\;DH(\%) \;=\; \frac{\text{moles of vinyl alcohol units}}{\text{total moles of VAc + VA units}} \times 100\;}$$

| Grade | DH (%) | Solubility in Cold Water | Typical Adhesive Use |
|-------|--------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **Fully Hydrolyzed (FH)** | 98 – 99 | Insoluble at 25 °C, soluble > 80 °C | High-strength paper laminate, textile sizing, packaging |
| **Intermediate Hydrolyzed (IH)** | 95 – 97 | Soluble at 50 – 70 °C | General-purpose film former |
| **Partially Hydrolyzed (PH)** | 87 – 89 | Soluble at 20 – 30 °C | Remoistenable adhesives, envelope gums |
| **Sub-partially Hydrolyzed (SPH)** | 78 – 85 | Readily soluble at 10 °C | Cold-water laundry bags, water-soluble films |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of numerical and short-answer marks in Module 3.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $DH(\%) = \frac{n_{VA}}{n_{VA}+n_{VAc}} \times 100$ | Degree of hydrolysis | Dimensionless (%) |
| 2 | $\overline{M}_n = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 3 | $\overline{M}_w = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Weight-average molecular weight | g/mol |
| 4 | $PDI = \frac{\overline{M}_w}{\overline{M}_n}$ | Polydispersity Index (for PVA, typically **1.5 – 2.5**) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $[\eta] = K \cdot M^{a}$ (Mark–Houwink) | Intrinsic viscosity → MW | dL/g, g/mol |
| 6 | $\eta_{sp}/c = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 c$ | Huggins equation (viscosity extrapolation) | dL/g |
| 7 | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{M_n}$ | Fox–Flory equation (Tg ∝ 1/Mn) | K |
| 8 | $\ln \eta_{rel} / c = [\eta] + k_{Kraemer}[\eta]^2 c$ | Kraemer equation (alternative extrapolation) | dL/g |
| 9 | $\%\,Crystallinity = \frac{\rho_c(\rho_s - \rho_a)}{\rho_s(\rho_c - \rho_a)} \times 100$ | From density measurement | % |
| 10 | $W_{OH} = 44.05 \times DH$ per repeat | Mass of –OH per repeat unit basis | g |

### Mark-Houwink Parameters for PVA in Water at 25 °C

| Solvent | Temp (°C) | K (×10⁴ dL/g) | a |
|---------|-----------|---------------|------|
| Water | 25 | 4.53 | 0.64 |
| Water | 30 | 3.59 | 0.66 |
| DMSO | 30 | 0.30 | 0.78 |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Adhesive Field

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a chemical / polymer engineer must master PVA:**

1. **Packaging Industry:** PVA is co-extruded as a **sacrificial interlayer** in PET bottles; when washed, the PVA dissolves, allowing easy recycling of PET. Adhesive-grade PVA is used as a **tie-layer** in multilayer films.
2. **Paper & Corrugated Board:** PVA surface sizing increases **ring-crush strength** by 20–30 % — a direct contribution of H-bonding to cellulose fibrils.
3. **Wood Adhesives:** PVA emulsions (PVAc homopolymer with PVA protective colloid) dominate **D3 / D4 wood-bonding classes (EN 204)**.
4. **Textile Warp Sizing:** PVA films provide **abrasion resistance** to cotton/polyester yarns.
5. **Construction:** PVA-modified cement mortars gain **flexural strength** and reduce water permeability.
6. **3D Printing:** Newer PVA grades are used as **water-soluble support material** in FDM (fused-deposition modelling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Hydrolysis from Saponification Stoichiometry

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A sample of partially hydrolysed PVA contains **8.80 g of vinyl alcohol (VA) units** and **4.40 g of vinyl acetate (VAc) units** (per mole of polymer). Calculate:
(a) the **degree of hydrolysis (DH) in %**,
(b) the **average molecular weight per repeat unit**.

### Given Data
- Molar mass of VA unit (—CH₂—CH(OH)—): $M_{VA} = 44.05$ g/mol
- Molar mass of VAc unit (—CH₂—CH(OCOCH₃)—): $M_{VAc} = 86.09$ g/mol
- Mass of VA units = 8.80 g
- Mass of VAc units = 4.40 g

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert mass to moles (mole balance).**

Moles of VA units:
$$n_{VA} = \frac{m_{VA}}{M_{VA}} = \frac{8.80 \;\text{g}}{44.05 \;\text{g/mol}} = 0.1998 \;\text{mol}$$

Moles of VAc units:
$$n_{VAc} = \frac{m_{VAc}}{M_{VAc}} = \frac{4.40 \;\text{g}}{86.09 \;\text{g/mol}} = 0.0511 \;\text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the degree-of-hydrolysis definition.**

$$DH(\%) = \frac{n_{VA}}{n_{VA} + n_{VAc}} \times 100$$

$$DH(\%) = \frac{0.1998}{0.1998 + 0.0511} \times 100 = \frac{0.1998}{0.2509} \times 100$$

$$DH(\%) = 79.63 \;\text{\%} \;\;\text{(Sub-partially Hydrolysed Grade)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average molar mass of the repeat unit.**

$$\overline{M}_{\text{repeat}} = \frac{m_{VA} + m_{VAc}}{n_{VA} + n_{VAc}} = \frac{8.80 + 4.40}{0.1998 + 0.0511}$$

$$\overline{M}_{\text{repeat}} = \frac{13.20 \;\text{g}}{0.2509 \;\text{mol}} = 52.61 \;\text{g/mol}$$

**Verification by algebraic shortcut:**
$$\overline{M}_{\text{repeat}} = M_{VA}\cdot x_{VA} + M_{VAc}\cdot (1-x_{VA})$$
where $x_{VA} = 0.7963$.
$$\overline{M}_{\text{repeat}} = 44.05(0.7963) + 86.09(0.2037) = 35.07 + 17.54 = 52.61 \;\text{g/mol} \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Intrinsic Viscosity and Molecular Weight (Mark–Houwink)

**Problem Statement:**
The intrinsic viscosity of a PVA sample in **water at 25 °C** is found to be **[\eta] = 0.78 dL/g**. Using K = 4.53 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g and a = 0.64, calculate the **viscosity-average molecular weight Mv**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Mark–Houwink equation.**

$$[\eta] = K \cdot M_v^{\,a}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and rearrange for Mv.**

$$M_v^{\,a} = \frac{[\eta]}{K}$$

$$M_v^{\,a} = \frac{0.78}{4.53 \times 10^{-4}} = 1721.85$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Mv.**

$$M_v = (1721.85)^{1/0.64} = (1721.85)^{1.5625}$$

**Step 4 — Take logarithms (log₁₀).**

$$\log M_v = 1.5625 \times \log(1721.85) = 1.5625 \times 3.2360 = 5.0563$$

**Step 5 — Anti-log.**

$$M_v = 10^{5.0563} = 1.137 \times 10^{5} \;\text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_v \approx 1.14 \times 10^{5} \;\text{Da} \;\;\text{(Medium-low MW PVA grade)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** An Mv ≈ 1.1 × 10⁵ Da corresponds to a **medium-viscosity** PVA grade — ideal for **paper-coating and remoistenable adhesive** applications where solution viscosity must stay below ~5,000 cP at 10 % solids.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Glass-Transition Temperature by Fox-Flory Equation

**Problem Statement:**
Estimate the **Tg of a PVA sample with $\overline{M}_n = 25{,}000$ g/mol**, given the Fox–Flory constants for PVA: $T_{g,\infty} = 85\;°C$ and K = 1.5 × 10⁵ K·g/mol.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the Fox–Flory equation.**

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \frac{K}{\overline{M}_n}$$

**Step 2 — Convert K to °C·g/mol (subtract 273.15 from K if required).**

Here K is given in K·g/mol, so we convert $T_{g,\infty}$ to Kelvin first:
$$T_{g,\infty}(K) = 85 + 273.15 = 358.15 \;\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$T_g = 358.15 - \frac{1.5 \times 10^{5}}{25{,}000}$$

$$T_g = 358.15 - 6.0 = 352.15 \;\text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to °C.**

$$\boxed{T_g = 352.15 - 273.15 = 79.0 \;°C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Lower MW PVA has lower Tg → softer films → better tack but reduced cohesive strength. **For pressure-sensitive adhesive (PSA) formulations, a lower Tg is preferred**; for **structural adhesives, higher Tg is needed**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Property vs Grade (Comparative Table)

| Property | FH (98–99 %) | PH (87–89 %) | SPH (78–85 %) |
|----------|--------------|--------------|---------------|
| Cold-water solubility | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Film flexibility | Low (brittle) | High (flexible) | Highest |
| Tensile strength | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| Oxygen barrier | Excellent | Poor | Poor |
| Tack (stickiness) | Low | High | Highest |
| Viscosity at 4 % solids | Low | Medium | High |
| Recommended adhesive use | Lamination, textile warp sizing | Remoistenable, labels | Water-soluble films |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram 1 — Two-Stage Industrial Synthesis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["VAM\nVinyl Acetate\nMonomer"] -->|Free Radical\nPolymerisation\nAIBN or BPO| B["PVAC\nPolyvinyl Acetate\nAtactic"]
    B -->|Methanolysis\nNaOH Catalyst| C["PVA\nPolyvinyl Alcohol\nHydrolysed Polymer"]
    C --> D["Fully\nHydrolysed\n98 to 99 percent"]
    C --> E["Partially\nHydrolysed\n87 to 89 percent"]
    C --> F["Sub Partially\nHydrolysed\n78 to 85 percent"]
    D --> G["Lamination\nAdhesives"]
    D --> H["Textile\nWarp Sizing"]
    E --> I["Remoistenable\nGums"]
    E --> J["Paper Surface\nSizing"]
    F --> K["Water Soluble\nFilms"]
    B -.->|Methyl\nAcetate| L["Recycle\nLoop"]
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram 2 — Mechanism of Adhesion on Cellulosic Substrate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SUB["Cellulosic Substrate (Paper / Wood)"]
        C1["Cellulose Chain 1\nwith OH Groups"]
        C2["Cellulose Chain 2\nwith OH Groups"]
    end
    subgraph ADH["PVA Adhesive Layer"]
        P1["PVA Segment A\nwith OH Groups"]
        P2["PVA Segment B\nwith OH Groups"]
        P3["PVA Segment C\nwith Acetate Groups"]
    end
    C1 -->|Hydrogen\nBond| P1
    P1 -->|Hydrogen\nBond| P2
    P2 -->|Hydrogen\nBond| P3
    P3 -->|Hydrogen\nBond| C2
    C1 -->|Covalent\nCrosslink via\nBorax Bridge| P2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram 3 — PVA Application Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PVA["PVA Polymer\nMaster Grade"]
    PVA --> P1["Film Former\nPackaging"]
    PVA --> P2["Adhesive Base\nPaper Wood"]
    PVA --> P3["Sizing Agent\nTextile Paper"]
    PVA --> P4["Emulsion Stabiliser\nPVAc Latex"]
    PVA --> P5["3D Print Support\nFDM Sacrificial"]
    PVA --> P6["Cement Modifier\nConstruction"]
    P2 --> S1["D3 D4 Wood Glue"]
    P2 --> S2["Envelope Gum"]
    P2 --> S3["Wallpaper Paste"]
    P2 --> S4["Bookbinding"]
    P3 --> T1["Warp Size"]
    P3 --> T2["Surface Size"]
    P1 --> F1["Oxygen Barrier"]
    P1 --> F2["Water Soluble Film"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide for Diagrams:**
> - **Diagram 4.1** is the *process flow* — note the recycle loop of methyl acetate to vinyl acetate monomer (green-chemistry route).
> - **Diagram 4.2** illustrates the *adhesion mechanism* — note the **dual H-bonding** between cellulose–PVA and **PVA–PVA**, plus the **borax bridge** for crosslink reinforcement.
> - **Diagram 4.3** maps the *application landscape* — useful for viva questions on "where is PVA used as an adhesive?"

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing hydrolysis with methanolysis** — many students write "PVA is made by hydrolysing PVAc with water". The industrial process uses **methanol + NaOH**, not water. Marks are deducted for this.
> 2. **Forgetting that vinyl alcohol monomer is unstable** — if asked "Why not polymerise vinyl alcohol directly?", students must mention the **keto-enol tautomerism** (acetaldehyde is more stable by ~42.7 kJ/mol).
> 3. **Confusing PVA with PVAc** — PVAc is the *acetate* polymer (Elmer's glue); PVA is the *alcohol* polymer (water-soluble, used in paper adhesives). They are different polymers.
> 4. **Using wrong Mark-Houwink constants** — K and a values differ by solvent and temperature. Always quote the solvent and temperature in your answer.
> 5. **Mark losses in mechanism questions** — students draw structures without showing **lone pairs** on oxygen or **directional H-bonds** (use dashed arrows).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Polyvinyl Alcohol. Explain why it cannot be prepared by direct polymerisation of vinyl alcohol monomer. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) is a synthetic, water-soluble, linear polymer of the general structure **—[CH₂—CH(OH)]ₙ—**, prepared by the **saponification of polyvinyl acetate**.

**Reason for non-direct synthesis (2 marks):** Vinyl alcohol (CH₂=CH–OH) is thermodynamically unstable. It undergoes **keto-enol tautomerism** to form the more stable **acetaldehyde (CH₃–CHO)**. The energy difference is ~**42.7 kJ/mol** in favour of the keto (aldehyde) tautomer. Therefore, the monomer exists almost exclusively as acetaldehyde, and any attempt to polymerise it gives **polyacetaldehyde** rather than polyvinyl alcohol. Hence, PVA is industrially produced by the **two-step route: polymerise vinyl acetate → saponify PVAc with methanol/NaOH → PVA**.

---

### Question 2: "The degree of hydrolysis controls the water solubility of PVA." Justify with suitable examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Degree of Hydrolysis (DH)** is the mole percentage of vinyl acetate units converted to vinyl alcohol (–OH) units. Since the –OH group forms strong **hydrogen bonds with water**, higher DH increases the polymer's affinity for water — but paradoxically, very high DH leads to *intra/inter-chain* H-bonding that **resists cold-water dissolution** (only soluble above 80 °C).

**Examples (2 marks):**
- **Fully Hydrolysed PVA (98–99 %)** — insoluble in cold water; used for high-strength paper lamination, textile warp sizing, oxygen-barrier films.
- **Partially Hydrolysed PVA (87–89 %)** — readily soluble in cold water (20–30 °C); used as remoistenable adhesive for envelopes, stamps, labels.
- **Sub-partially Hydrolysed PVA (~80 %)** — dissolves even at 10 °C; used for water-soluble laundry bags, hospital disposables.

Thus DH is the **master tuning parameter** for solubility and adhesive application.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern:** Attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full. Each question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** With neat reaction schemes, describe the **industrial synthesis of polyvinyl alcohol** from vinyl acetate monomer. Highlight the role of the catalyst and the by-product recovery loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how **molecular weight and degree of hydrolysis** influence the **adhesive performance** of PVA on cellulosic substrates. Use a property-trend table in your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Polymerisation of Vinyl Acetate (3 marks):**
Vinyl acetate monomer is polymerised in methanol solvent using **AIBN (azobis-isobutyronitrile)** as a free-radical initiator at 60–70 °C. The product is **polyvinyl acetate (PVAc)** — atactic, with Mn ranging from 10,000 to 1,000,000 Da depending on initiator concentration and temperature.

$$\text{n CH}_2\text{=CH–OCOCH}_3 \;\xrightarrow{\text{AIBN, 60-70°C}}\; \text{—[CH}_2\text{–CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)]}_n\text{—}$$

**Step 2 — Saponification with Methanol (3 marks):**
PVAc is reacted with **methanol containing 1–5 % NaOH** at 30–40 °C in a kneader reactor. The methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻) attacks the carbonyl carbon of the acetate, displacing methyl acetate:

$$\text{—[CH}_2\text{–CH(OCOCH}_3\text{)]}_n\text{—} + n\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \;\xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}}\; \text{—[CH}_2\text{–CH(OH)]}_n\text{—} + n\,\text{CH}_3\text{OCOCH}_3$$

The catalyst **NaOH** is essential; without it, the reaction is impractically slow. The degree of hydrolysis is controlled by the **methanol-to-PVAc ratio**, reaction time, and NaOH concentration.

**Step 3 — By-product Recovery (1 mark):**
Methyl acetate (by-product) is distilled off, then hydrolysed to **methanol + acetic acid**. Methanol is recycled to the saponification step; acetic acid is sold or recycled to the monomer plant. This closed-loop makes the process **economically and environmentally attractive**.

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]

**Effect of Molecular Weight (3 marks):**
- **Low MW PVA (20,000 – 50,000 Da):** Low solution viscosity, high solids content possible, good wetting, but **low cohesive strength** and **low peel strength**. Used in remoistenable gums.
- **Medium MW (50,000 – 1,00,000 Da):** Balanced viscosity and bond strength. Standard paper-surface sizing.
- **High MW (1,00,000 – 2,00,000 Da):** High cohesive strength, tough films, but **high solution viscosity** limits solids. Used in textile warp sizing and structural adhesives (with crosslinkers).

**Effect of Degree of Hydrolysis (3 marks):**
- **Low DH (78–87 %):** More acetate groups → less H-bonding → softer, more flexible films, higher tack, **better adhesion to non-polar surfaces** (plastics).
- **High DH (98–99 %):** More –OH groups → maximum H-bonding to **cellulose, glass, metals** → stronger, stiffer, more water-resistant films.

**Property Trend Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Low MW, Low DH | High MW, High DH |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|
| Tack (initial grab) | Very high | Low |
| Cohesive strength | Low | Very high |
| Water resistance | Low | High |
| Solids achievable | High | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Classify the **commercial grades of PVA** based on degree of hydrolysis. Discuss the specific end-use applications of each grade in the adhesive industry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **mechanism of adhesion of PVA on cellulosic substrates**. Explain how **borax (sodium tetraborate)** acts as a **crosslinker** to enhance the cohesive strength of PVA adhesives. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]

**Classification (3 marks):**
Commercial PVA is classified into **four primary grades** based on **degree of hydrolysis (DH)** and **viscosity grade (low/medium/high MW)**:

1. **Fully Hydrolysed (FH) — DH 98–99 %**
2. **Intermediate Hydrolysed (IH) — DH 95–97 %**
3. **Partially Hydrolysed (PH) — DH 87–89 %**
4. **Sub-partially Hydrolysed (SPH) — DH 78–85 %**

**Adhesive Applications by Grade (4 marks):**

| Grade | Key Property | Adhesive Application |
|-------|--------------|---------------------|
| **FH (98–99 %)** | Highest tensile, water-resistant films, best H-bonding to cellulose | Paper lamination, corrugated board, vinyl/wood laminates, food-contact adhesives (FDA-approved) |
| **IH (95–97 %)** | Balanced solubility & film strength | Textile finishing, release coatings, bag adhesives |
| **PH (87–89 %)** | Cold-water soluble, flexible, high tack | **Remoistenable** envelope/seal/label/stamp/bookbinding gums, wallpaper paste |
| **SPH (78–85 %)** | Very low temperature solubility, high flexibility | Water-soluble laundry bags, hospital disposable bags, embroidery backing films |

**Key Engineering Trade-off:** Higher DH gives stronger, more water-resistant films but requires **hot-water dissolution** (above 80 °C). Lower DH gives cold-water solubility but lower cohesive strength. Formulators often **blend grades** to optimise tack + strength.

---

#### ⭐ Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]

**Mechanism of Adhesion on Cellulose (4 marks):**
Cellulose is a linear polymer of D-glucose units with **three free –OH groups per repeat unit** (at C-2, C-3, C-6). PVA also has a **free –OH group on every alternate carbon** of its backbone. Adhesion is primarily due to:

1. **Hydrogen Bonding (primary mechanism):**
   The –OH groups of PVA form **intermolecular hydrogen bonds** with the –OH groups of cellulose. One –OH can act as a H-bond donor (O–H) and the other as an acceptor (lone pair on O). This creates a **dense network of physical crosslinks** at the adhesive–substrate interface.

2. **Mechanical Interlocking:**
   PVA solution penetrates the porous structure of paper/wood, and upon drying, the polymer chains **anchor mechanically** into the substrate pores (micro-mechanical interlock).

3. **Diffusion Interphase (limited):**
   Some mutual diffusion of low-MW PVA chains into swollen cellulose surface.

**Borax as Crosslinker (3 marks):**
Borax (Na₂B₄O₇·10H₂O) in water dissociates to the **borate ion B(OH)₄⁻**, which has four hydroxyl groups and can form **reversible covalent complexes** with two adjacent PVA hydroxyl groups:

$$\text{2 PVA–OH} + \text{B(OH)}_4^{-} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{PVA–O–B(OH)}_2\text{–O–PVA} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Effects:**
- **Instant complexation** → rapid viscosity build-up (used in "slime" toys).
- **Forms reversible crosslinks** → thixotropic behaviour (gel at rest, flows on shear).
- **Improves wet-tack and green strength** of the adhesive.
- **Excess borax** over-crosslinks → brittle, non-tacky film; so a **critical concentration of 0.5–3 %** is maintained.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Examiner Tip:** When asked about crosslinking, mention that the **borate–PVA complex is reversible on heating or pH change** (acidic conditions break the complex). For *permanent* crosslinks, formaldehyde, glyoxal, or diisocyanates are used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Polyvinyl Alcohols" — Module 3**

- **PVA = poly(1-hydroxyethylene) = —[CH₂—CH(OH)]ₙ—**, repeat-unit mass 44.05 g/mol.
- **Cannot be made from vinyl alcohol** (tautomerises to acetaldehyde); must be made by **saponification of PVAc with methanol + NaOH**.
- **Two-step synthesis:** Vinyl acetate → PVAc (radical polymerisation) → PVA (methanolysis).
- **Master variable = Degree of Hydrolysis (DH)**, controls solubility, flexibility, and adhesion.
- **Four commercial grades:** FH (98–99 %), IH (95–97 %), PH (87–89 %), SPH (78–85 %).
- **Cold-water solubility** decreases as DH increases; **PH grade is the cold-water-soluble adhesive workhorse**.
- **Tg = 75–85 °C** (fully hydrolysed) — lowers with decreasing DH and with plasticisation by water/glycerol.
- **Adhesion to cellulose = H-bonding** between –OH of PVA and –OH of cellulose; reinforced by **mechanical interlocking** in porous substrates.
- **Borax forms reversible crosslinks** with PVA via borate–diol complexation; gives thixotropic, high-tack gels.
- **Permanent crosslinkers** (formaldehyde, glyoxal, isocyanates, Zr/Ti chelates) used for water-resistant structural bonds.
- **Applications in adhesives:** remoistenable gums (PH), paper lamination (FH), wood glue (with PVAc emulsion), textile warp sizing, water-soluble films (SPH), 3D-print support.
- **Properties: non-toxic, biodegradable, FDA-approved** for food-contact adhesives.
- **Key formulae to memorise:** $DH(\%)$, Mark–Houwink, Fox–Flory Tg, Huggins equation.
- **Comparison pitfall to avoid:** PVA ≠ PVAc. PVA is the *alcohol* (water-soluble, used in paper); PVAc is the *acetate* (the white glue).
- **Recycling loop:** Methyl acetate by-product → methanol + acetic acid → recycled to monomer plant.
- **Hansen Solubility Parameters** of PVA: $\delta_D = 15.0$, $\delta_P = 14.5$, $\delta_H = 23.5$ MPa$^{1/2}$ — highly polar, H-bonding.
- **Typical Mark–Houwink K = 4.53 × 10⁻⁴ dL/g, a = 0.64** in water at 25 °C — used to compute Mv from intrinsic viscosity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diisocyanates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Diisocyanates # Module 3 — Diisocyanates: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **diisocyanate** is an organic compound containing **two isocyanate functional groups (–N=C=O)** attached to an aromatic or aliphatic backbone. The isocyanate group is highly reactive due to the cumulative electronegativity of the nitrogen, carbon, and oxygen atoms, which creates a strongly electrophilic carbon center. Diisocyanates act as the **hard-segment building blocks** of polyurethane (PU) and polyurea adhesives, reacting with polyols, polyamines, or moisture to form the polymer network. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PEPOT861, diisocyanates are classified under *Module 3 — Raw Materials for Reactive Adhesives*. A student must know structure, nomenclature, %NCO content, reactivity differences (aromatic vs aliphatic), and the role of diisocyanates in 1-K and 2-K polyurethane adhesive systems. The general molecular structure is: $$\text{O=C=N—R—N=C=O}$$ where **R** is an aromatic ring (e.g., toluene, diphenylmethane) or an aliphatic chain (e.g., hexamethylene, isophorone). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Handed Welder Imagine a skilled welder standing in the middle of a workshop holding **two welding torches**, one in each hand. Each torch can join a separate metal plate by spraying molten metal into a joint. The welder's body is **inert** — it does not melt; only the flames do the joining. The diisocyanate molecule behaves exactly like this two-handed welder: - The **central R-backbone** = the welder's sturdy body (does not participate in reaction, only provides structure). - The **two –N=C=O ends** = the two welding torches (highly reactive tips that bond to other molecules). When this welder meets a long-chain polyol (a flexible polymer "plate"), the torches bond to the hydroxyl groups, fusing everything into a single polyurethane network. Add another welder, and you get a cross-linked, structural-grade adhesive. ## 1.3 Why Diisocyanates Are Pivotal in Adhesive Technology Diisocyanates are the **cross-linkers of choice** for structural adhesives because they offer: | Property | Engineering Benefit | |----------|--------------------| | **Two reactive –NCO ends per molecule** | Enables linear chain extension OR cross-linking | | **High electrophilicity of NCO carbon** | Reacts readily with –OH, –NH₂, –SH, –COOH, H₂O | | **Tunable backbone (aromatic/aliphatic)** | Controls flexibility, UV stability, thermal resistance | | **Quantitative %NCO value** | Allows precise stoichiometric formulation | | **Forms hard urethane/urea segments** | Provides mechanical strength, cohesion, heat resistance | > [!NOTE] > **Physical constant to memorize:** The molar mass of the **–NCO group** is **42.02 g/mol**. This is the foundation of every %NCO calculation in KTU problems. ## 1.4 Classification of Diisocyanates (KTU Board Favourite) Diisocyanates are classified along **three orthogonal axes**, each asked frequently in KTU exams: ### (a) By Backbone Chemistry - **Aromatic Diisocyanates** — e.g., **TDI** (Toluene Diisocyanate), **MDI** (Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate), **NDI** (Naphthalene Diisocyanate). - **Aliphatic Diisocyanates** — e.g., **HDI** (Hexamethylene Diisocyanate), **IPDI** (Isophorone Diisocyanate), **HMDI** (Hydrogenated MDI). - **Cycloaliphatic Diisocyanates** — overlap category; e.g., **IPDI**. ### (b) By Functionality - **Functionality = 2** — pure diisocyanates (linear polymer). - **Functionality > 2** — polymeric MDI (crude MDI) gives branched/cross-linked networks. ### (c) By Volatility / Handling - **Monomeric (low molecular weight, volatile)** — TDI, HDI. - **Polymeric / Modified (high molecular weight, low vapor pressure)** — polymeric MDI, MDI prepolymers, HDI biuret, HDI isocyanurate, IPDI trimer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's memory hook:** "**A**romatic = **A**ggressive (fast, strong, yellowing); **A**liphatic = **A**dornable (slow, flexible, UV-stable, non-yellowing)." ## 1.5 The Five Most Important Diisocyanates in Adhesive Practice > [!NOTE] > Memorize the **full name, abbreviation, molecular formula, and molar mass** for each of the following. KTU board questions commonly ask for structures or comparative tables. ### (i) TDI — Toluene 2,4-Diisocyanate Molecular formula: **C₉H₆N₂O₂** Molar mass: **174.16 g/mol** Structure: 2,4-substituted toluene with two –NCO groups. %NCO (theoretical) = **(2 × 42.02) / 174.16 × 100 = 48.25 %** ### (ii) MDI — 4,4′-Methylene Diphenyl Diisocyanate Molecular formula: **C₁₅H₁₀N₂O₂** Molar mass: **250.25 g/mol** Structure: two para-substituted phenyl rings joined by a –CH₂– bridge, each bearing an –NCO group. %NCO (theoretical) = **(2 × 42.02) / 250.25 × 100 = 33.58 %** ### (iii) HDI — Hexamethylene Diisocyanate Molecular formula: **C₈H₁₂N₂O₂** Molar mass: **168.20 g/mol** Structure: OCN–(CH₂)₆–NCO (linear aliphatic chain). %NCO (theoretical) = **(2 × 42.02) / 168.20 × 100 = 49.95 %** ### (iv) IPDI — Isophorone Diisocyanate Molecular formula: **C₁₂H₁₈N₂O₂** Molar mass: **222.29 g/mol** Structure: cyclohexane ring with two –NCO groups (one primary, one secondary NCO). %NCO (theoretical) = **(2 × 42.02) / 222.29 × 100 = 37.80 %** ### (v) HMDI — Hydrogenated MDI (H₁₂MDI) Molecular formula: **C₁₅H₂₂N₂O₂** Molar mass: **262.35 g/mol** Structure: fully hydrogenated MDI with cyclohexyl rings. %NCO (theoretical) = **(2 × 42.02) / 262.35 × 100 = 32.03 %** # Module 3 — Diisocyanates: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Electronic Structure of the –N=C=O Group The isocyanate group is best described by two resonance contributors that together explain its reactivity: $$\text{R—N=C=O} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{R—N⁺}\!\!=\!\!\text{C—O⁻}$$ **Implications for the KTU student:** 1. The **central carbon** carries a strong partial positive charge (δ⁺) because nitrogen is more electronegative than carbon, and oxygen is even more electronegative than nitrogen. This makes the carbon a powerful electrophile, **attacked by nucleophiles** such as –OH, –NH₂, –SH, –COOH, and H₂O. 2. The **oxygen end** is electron-rich and can act as a hydrogen-bond acceptor, which is why urethane linkages form strong intermolecular H-bonds, contributing to cohesive strength in adhesives. ## 2.2 The Five Primary Reactions of –NCO (Master These) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 board expectation:** A 14-mark question on diisocyanates almost always asks you to "explain the reactions of isocyanate groups with various active-hydrogen compounds." Memorize the products of these five reactions. ### Reaction 1 — With Alcohols (forms urethane / carbamate) $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{H—O—R′} \longrightarrow \text{R—NH—CO—O—R′}$$ This is the **fundamental urethane formation reaction** and the basis of 2-K PU adhesives. ### Reaction 2 — With Amines (forms urea) $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{H—NH—R′} \longrightarrow \text{R—NH—CO—NH—R′}$$ Amines react ~**100 to 1000× faster** than alcohols because the nitrogen nucleophile is more reactive than oxygen. ### Reaction 3 — With Water (forms carbamic acid → amine + CO₂) $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow [\text{R—NH—COOH}] \longrightarrow \text{R—NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$ This is the **curing mechanism of 1-K moisture-cure PU adhesives**. The CO₂ evolution must be managed (foaming risk on thick joints). ### Reaction 4 — With Carboxylic Acids (forms mixed anhydride → amide + CO₂) $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{HOOC—R′} \longrightarrow [\text{R—NH—CO—O—CO—R′}] \longrightarrow \text{R—NH—CO—R′} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$ ### Reaction 5 — With Urethane (forms allophanate, branching reaction) $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{R′—NH—CO—O—R″} \longrightarrow \text{R′—N(CO—R)—CO—O—R″}$$ This occurs at elevated temperatures (>120 °C) and creates **cross-links**, increasing rigidity. ## 2.3 Aromatic vs Aliphatic Diisocyanates — The Master Comparison | Property | **Aromatic** (TDI, MDI, NDI) | **Aliphatic** (HDI, IPDI, HMDI) | |----------|------------------------------|---------------------------------| | **–NCO reactivity** | High (fast cure) | Moderate (slower, more controllable) | | **UV stability of product** | Poor — yellows on sunlight exposure | Excellent — non-yellowing | | **Mechanical strength** | Very high (rigid hard segments) | Moderate (more flexible) | | **Thermal resistance** | High | Lower (except IPDI trimers) | | **Cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Vapor pressure (monomer)** | Higher (toxicity concern) | HDI still volatile; IPDI/HMDI safer | | **Typical adhesive use** | Wood, packaging, shoe, structural | Automotive clear-coats, optical laminates, outdoor | | **Steric hindrance** | Planar, accessible NCO | IPDI has hindered secondary NCO (chemoselectivity) | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Every quantity below is examinable. Notice the use of `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Units | Notes | |----------|---------|-------------------|-------| | **% NCO content** | $\%\text{NCO} = \dfrac{42.02 \times f}{M} \times 100$ | $f$ = number of NCO groups per molecule (typically 2); $M$ = molar mass of diisocyanate (g/mol); **42.02** = molar mass of one –NCO group | Theoretical upper bound; commercial samples lower due to hydrolysis/polymerization | | **Equivalent weight of NCO** | $E_{\text{NCO}} = \dfrac{M}{f}$ | g/eq | Mass of diisocyanate containing one mole of NCO | | **Isocyanate Index (R)** | $R = \dfrac{n_{\text{NCO}}}{n_{\text{OH}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | $R = 1.0$ = stoichiometric; $R > 1$ = excess NCO (rigid, brittle PU); $R < 1$ = excess OH (soft, elastic PU) | | **Equivalent weight of polyol** | $E_{\text{OH}} = \dfrac{1000 \times f_{\text{OH}}}{\text{OHV}}$ | g/eq | OHV = hydroxyl value (mg KOH/g) | | **Mixing ratio (parts by mass)** | $\dfrac{m_{\text{iso}}}{m_{\text{polyol}}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{NCO}}}{E_{\text{OH}}} \times R$ | g/g | Used to weigh out 2-K adhesives | | **CO₂ evolved from H₂O–NCO** | $n_{\text{CO}_2} = n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = n_{\text{NCO,consumed}}$ | mol | Stoichiometric 1:1 | | **Moles of NCO per gram** | $\dfrac{f}{M}$ | mol/g | Inversion of $E_{\text{NCO}}$ | ## 2.5 Steric and Electronic Effects Governing Reactivity The relative rates of –NCO with different nucleophiles, ranked from fastest to slowest (in PU adhesive formulation), are: $$\text{aliphatic –NH}_2 \;>\; \text{aromatic –NH}_2 \;>\; \text{primary –OH} \;>\; \text{water} \;>\; \text{secondary –OH} \;>\; \text{tertiary –OH} \;>\; \text{–COOH} \;>\; \text{–SH} \;>\; \text{urea –NH} \;>\; \text{urethane –NH}$$ In **IPDI**, the two NCO groups differ: the **primary NCO** (on the –CH₂– side) reacts 5–10× faster than the **secondary NCO** (on the ring carbon), giving formulators **chemoselectivity** to build block-copolymer adhesives. ## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Diisocyanate Chemistry - **Wood adhesives:** Polymeric MDI replaces formaldehyde-based resins in particleboard/MDF (Module-2 cross-reference). - **Automotive windshield bonding:** Single-component moisture-cure PU using IPDI prepolymers; provides 1-hour safe-drive-away time. - **Flexible packaging laminates:** Two-component solvent-free PU with HDI isocyanurate + polyester polyol for food-contact films. - **Footwear:** Solvent-borne PU adhesives based on MDI prepolymers for shoe sole–upper bonding. - **Electronics / potting:** Aliphatic HDI/IPDI systems for clear, non-yellowing encapsulants. > [!TIP] > When a question says "design an adhesive for **outdoor service with color stability**," your answer must specify an **aliphatic diisocyanate (HDI or IPDI)**. When it says "**maximum strength and heat resistance**," specify **aromatic MDI**. # Module 3 — Diisocyanates: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Why the Theoretical %NCO Formula Has the Form 42.02 × f × 100 / M We start from the **definition of %NCO by mass**: $$\%\text{NCO} = \frac{\text{mass contributed by NCO groups}}{\text{total molecular mass}} \times 100$$ For one isocyanate group (–N=C=O), the atomic composition is **N (14.01) + C (12.01) + O (16.00) = 42.02 g/mol**. For a molecule containing $f$ such groups, the total NCO mass is $f \times 42.02$ g. Substituting and simplifying: $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{NCO}_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{f \times 42.02\;\text{g/mol}}{M\;\text{g/mol}} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{42.02 \times f \times 100}{M} \\[6pt] &= \frac{4202 \times f}{M} \end{aligned}$$ This is the equation used in **every KTU numerical** involving %NCO. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical — %NCO of Pure MDI **Problem:** Calculate the theoretical %NCO of 4,4′-MDI (C₁₅H₁₀N₂O₂, M = 250.25 g/mol, f = 2). **Step 1.** Identify the molar mass of one –NCO group. $$M_{\text{NCO}} = 14.01 + 12.01 + 16.00 = 42.02\;\text{g/mol}$$ **Step 2.** Count the number of NCO groups per molecule. $$f = 2$$ **Step 3.** Insert into the master formula. $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{NCO} &= \frac{42.02 \times 2 \times 100}{250.25} \\[6pt] &= \frac{8404}{250.25} \\[6pt] &= 33.58\;\% \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4.** Verify sanity. Two –NCO groups contribute 84.04 g out of 250.25 g total; ratio ≈ 0.336 ≈ 33.6 %. ✔ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Equivalent Weight of HDI and Mix Ratio with Polyol **Given:** - Diisocyanate: HDI, M = 168.20 g/mol, f = 2. - Polyol: polyester with OHV = 56 mg KOH/g (i.e., $f_{\text{OH}}$ measured). - Required Isocyanate Index R = 1.05. **Step 1.** Compute the NCO equivalent weight. $$E_{\text{NCO}} = \frac{M}{f} = \frac{168.20}{2} = 84.10\;\text{g/eq}$$ **Step 2.** Compute the polyol equivalent weight from the hydroxyl value. $$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{OH}} &= \frac{1000 \times f_{\text{OH, per molecule}}}{\text{OHV}} \quad \text{or simply} \\[4pt] E_{\text{OH}} &= \frac{56{,}100}{\text{OHV}} = \frac{56{,}100}{56} = 1001.79\;\text{g/eq} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3.** Apply the mass mixing ratio. $$\begin{aligned} \frac{m_{\text{iso}}}{m_{\text{polyol}}} &= \frac{E_{\text{NCO}}}{E_{\text{OH}}} \times R \\[6pt] &= \frac{84.10}{1001.79} \times 1.05 \\[6pt] &= 0.0839 \times 1.05 \\[6pt] &= 0.0881 \end{aligned}$$ So for every **100 g of polyol**, use **~8.81 g of HDI** to achieve R = 1.05. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — CO₂ Released in 1-K Moisture-Cure **Problem:** A 1-K PU sealant contains 12.0 g of an MDI prepolymer with %NCO = 4.5 %. The joint absorbs 0.18 g of water before full cure. Determine (a) whether water or NCO is the limiting reagent and (b) the volume of CO₂ released at STP (22.4 L/mol). **Step 1.** Mass of NCO groups present. $$m_{\text{NCO}} = 12.0 \times \frac{4.5}{100} = 0.540\;\text{g}$$ **Step 2.** Moles of NCO. $$n_{\text{NCO}} = \frac{0.540}{42.02} = 0.01285\;\text{mol}$$ **Step 3.** Moles of H₂O. $$n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{0.18}{18.02} = 0.00999\;\text{mol}$$ **Step 4.** Compare. Reaction stoichiometry is 1:1 (R—NCO + H₂O → R—NH₂ + CO₂). H₂O (0.00999) < NCO (0.01285), so **water is limiting**; 0.00999 mol CO₂ is generated. **Step 5.** Volume of CO₂ at STP. $$V_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.00999 \times 22.4 = 0.224\;\text{L} \approx 224\;\text{mL}$$ **Engineering conclusion:** Even 0.18 g of water in a bonded joint produces a quarter-litre of CO₂ — enough to foam thick layers. This is why **thin bondlines (<0.5 mm)** and **drying of substrates** are critical in 1-K PU adhesive practice. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selecting a Diisocyanate for a Given Adhesive Application > [!IMPORTANT] > Use this decision matrix in design-oriented 14-mark KTU questions. | Application | Required Property | Recommended Diisocyanate | Reasoning | |-------------|-------------------|--------------------------|-----------| | Wood particleboard binder | High reactivity, low cost | Polymeric MDI | Cheap, fast, gives strong thermoset network | | Bookbinding / packaging | Moderate strength, flexible | TDI prepolymer | Lower viscosity, good wetting of paper | | Outdoor façade sealant | UV stability, color retention | HDI isocyanurate | Aliphatic, non-yellowing, weatherable | | Automotive windshield | Crash-resistant, fast green strength | IPDI / HMDI prepolymer | Tunable NCO chemoselectivity | | Optical laminates | Crystal clarity, no yellowing | HDI biuret | Aliphatic, low color | | Heat-resistant conveyor belt | Thermal stability | MDI (aromatic) | Rigid aromatic hard segments | | Food-contact film laminate | Solvent-free, low migration | HDI / IPDI (low monomer grades) | Aliphatic, low odor, low toxicity | | Moisture-cure shoe adhesive | Fast set with humidity | MDI prepolymer in MEK | High NCO availability | ## 3.6 Derivation — Why %NCO Decreases on Storage (Pot-life Concept) When a diisocyanate or its prepolymer reacts with atmospheric moisture, each mole of –NCO consumed represents a 42.02 g drop in NCO per mole of reacted groups. If a sample of mass $m_0$ initially shows $\%\text{NCO}_0$ and after time $t$ shows $\%\text{NCO}_t$: $$\Delta n_{\text{NCO consumed}} = \frac{m_0}{M_{\text{NCO}}} \left(\frac{\%\text{NCO}_0 - \%\text{NCO}_t}{100}\right)$$ The **pot life** is defined as the time taken for %NCO to fall by **50 %** of its initial value (or to reach the gel point). This is why **dry nitrogen blanketing** and **molecular-sieve-dried solvents** are mandatory in PU adhesive manufacture. # Module 3 — Diisocyanates: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reaction Flow — The Five Primary Reactions of –NCO ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["R-N=C=O
Isocyanate group"] --> nodeReact1 node1 --> nodeReact2 node1 --> nodeReact3 node1 --> nodeReact4 node1 --> nodeReact5 nodeReact1["With R-OH
Alcohol"] --> nodeProd1["R-NH-CO-O-R
Urethane linkage"] nodeReact2["With R-NH2
Primary amine"] --> nodeProd2["R-NH-CO-NH-R
Urea linkage"] nodeReact3["With H2O
Moisture"] --> nodeProd3["R-NH2 + CO2 gas
Amine + Carbon dioxide"] nodeReact4["With R-COOH
Carboxylic acid"] --> nodeProd4["R-NH-CO-R + CO2
Amide + Carbon dioxide"] nodeReact5["With urethane N-H
At elevated T"] --> nodeProd5["Allophanate
Branching crosslink"] nodeProd1 --> nodeOut["Hard-segment urethane
matrix of PU adhesive"] nodeProd2 --> nodeOut nodeProd3 --> nodeOut nodeProd4 --> nodeOut nodeProd5 --> nodeOut ``` ## 4.2 Classification Architecture — Diisocyanate Family Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD root["Diisocyanates
Two -NCO groups per molecule"] --> branch1 root --> branch2 root --> branch3 branch1["By Backbone"] --> arom["Aromatic"] branch1 --> ali["Aliphatic"] branch1 --> cyclo["Cycloaliphatic"] arom --> TDI["TDI
Toluene diisocyanate"] arom --> MDI["MDI
Methylene diphenyl diisocyanate"] arom --> NDI["NDI
Naphthalene diisocyanate"] ali --> HDI["HDI
Hexamethylene diisocyanate"] cyclo --> IPDI["IPDI

Bonded joint] ``` ## 4.4 Hard–Soft Segment Block Structure in PU Adhesive ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Hard["HARD SEGMENT Rigid"] h1[Diisocyanate -NCO] --> h2[Urethane or urea linkage] h2 --> h3[H-bonding between segments] end subgraph Soft["SOFT SEGMENT Flexible"] s1[Polyol long chain polyester or polyether] --> s2[Free rotation and low Tg] end h3 --> morph["Microphase separated morphology
Hard domains act as physical crosslinks"] s2 --> morph morph --> props["Adhesive properties
Cohesive strength plus peel plus impact"] ``` ## 4.5 Comparative Reactivity Schematic — Aromatic vs Aliphatic vs Cycloaliphatic ```mermaid flowchart TD Q["Pick a diisocyanate for the adhesive"] --> Q1{"Service environment?"} Q1 -- Outdoor / UV-exposed --> AL["Use ALIPHATIC
HDI / IPDI / HMDI
Non-yellowing"] Q1 -- Indoor / structural --> AR["Use AROMATIC
MDI / TDI
Max strength"] Q1 -- High temperature --> AR2["Use AROMATIC MDI
or MDI prepolymer"] Q --> Q2{"Pot life requirement?"} Q2 -- Long pot life --> SL["Use SLOWER NCO
IPDI secondary NCO
