MDI or TDI"] Q --> Q3{"Substrate moisture risk?"} Q3 -- Yes, 1-K moisture cure --> MC["Use MDI prepolymer
Manage CO2 release
Thin bondlines"] ``` # Module 3 — Diisocyanates: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define a diisocyanate. With a neat sketch, show the structure of 4,4′-MDI and indicate its %NCO content.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **diisocyanate** is an organic compound containing two isocyanate (–N=C=O) functional groups, used as the hard-segment precursor in polyurethane adhesives. **Structure of 4,4′-MDI** (a benzene ring on each side of a methylene bridge, each ring para-substituted with –NCO): $$\text{OCN—C}_6\text{H}_4\text{—CH}_2\text{—C}_6\text{H}_4\text{—NCO}$$ **%NCO calculation** (1 mark): $$\%\text{NCO} = \frac{42.02 \times 2 \times 100}{250.25} = 33.58\;\%$$ *(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark structure, 1 mark %NCO.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Compare aromatic and aliphatic diisocyanates with two suitable examples each. Why are aliphatic ones preferred for outdoor applications?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | **Aromatic** (TDI, MDI) | **Aliphatic** (HDI, IPDI) | |--------|-------------------------|---------------------------| | UV stability | Yellow on exposure | Non-yellowing ✔ | | Reactivity | High (fast cure) | Moderate | | Cost | Lower | Higher | **Reason for outdoor preference (1 mark):** The aromatic rings undergo **photo-oxidation** producing quinone-imide chromophores that absorb visible light, causing yellowing and embrittlement. Aliphatic backbones lack these chromophores, so they retain color and mechanical properties under sunlight — hence preferred for façade, automotive, and outdoor sealants. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students **lose 2 marks** by forgetting the **molar mass of –NCO = 42.02** in calculations. > - Another **2 marks** vanish when you **omit the units** (g/mol, %, g/eq). > - In 14-mark answers, **always draw the reaction equations** — describing words alone loses the diagram marks. > - Avoid the trap of writing "isocyanates are toxic" without mentioning the **vapor-pressure-based classification** that distinguishes monomeric vs modified diisocyanates. ### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain in detail any **three reactions of isocyanate groups** with active-hydrogen compounds, showing the product formed in each case. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **classification of diisocyanates** with suitable examples. Compare aromatic and aliphatic diisocyanates as adhesive raw materials. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Three reactions (7 marks; ≈ 2.3 marks each):** **Reaction 1 — With alcohol (urethane formation):** $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{HO—R′} \longrightarrow \text{R—NH—CO—O—R′}$$ This is the **principal curing step** in 2-K PU adhesives where an OH-terminated polyester or polyether reacts with an NCO-terminated prepolymer. The urethane linkage is a hydrogen-bond acceptor and donor, providing cohesive strength. **Reaction 2 — With primary amine (urea formation):** $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{H}_2\text{N—R′} \longrightarrow \text{R—NH—CO—NH—R′}$$ The reaction is **much faster** than with alcohols (≈ 100–1000× faster) due to the higher nucleophilicity of nitrogen. Diamines such as ethylenediamine or MOCA are used in polyurea adhesives and elastomers. **Reaction 3 — With water (moisture cure, evolution of CO₂):** $$\text{R—N=C=O} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow [\text{R—NHCOOH}] \longrightarrow \text{R—NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$$ The carbamic acid intermediate decomposes to a primary amine, which immediately reacts with another –NCO to form a urea. This is the **curing mechanism of 1-K moisture-cure PU sealants and adhesives**. **Part (b) — Classification + comparison (7 marks):** Diisocyanates are classified by **backbone chemistry**, **functionality**, and **commercial form**: - **Aromatic** (TDI, MDI, NDI): rigid π-electron rings → strong hard segments, fast cure, UV-sensitive. - **Aliphatic** (HDI, IPDI, HMDI): saturated chains → flexible, UV-stable, slower cure. - **Cycloaliphatic** (IPDI, HMDI): ring structures combining partial rigidity with UV stability. | Property | **Aromatic** | **Aliphatic** | |----------|--------------|----------------| | UV stability | Yellows | Non-yellowing | | Reactivity | High | Moderate | | Hard-segment rigidity | High | Lower | | Cost | Lower | Higher | | Typical use | Wood, shoe, structural | Outdoor, automotive, optical | **Conclusion:** Aromatic diisocyanates are preferred for **structural, high-strength, indoor** adhesive applications, while aliphatic diisocyanates dominate **outdoor, transparent, weather-resistant** formulations. --- ### Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** What is **%NCO content**? Derive the formula for %NCO of a diisocyanate. Calculate the theoretical %NCO of (i) TDI (M = 174.16) and (ii) HDI (M = 168.20). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **role of diisocyanates in 1-K and 2-K polyurethane adhesive systems**, with reaction schemes. Explain why **mixing ratio** (the isocyanate index R) matters in 2-K systems. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — %NCO derivation + calculations (7 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** %NCO is the mass of isocyanate groups per 100 g of sample, expressed as a percentage. It quantifies the reactive functionality of the diisocyanate or prepolymer. **Derivation (3 marks):** Each –NCO group has atomic composition N (14.01) + C (12.01) + O (16.00) = **42.02 g/mol**. For a molecule of molar mass $M$ containing $f$ –NCO groups: $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{NCO}_{\text{theoretical}} &= \frac{\text{mass of all NCO groups}}{\text{total mass}} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{f \times 42.02}{M} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{4202 \times f}{M} \end{aligned}$$ **Calculations (3 marks; 1.5 each):** For **TDI** ($M = 174.16$, $f = 2$): $$\%\text{NCO}_{\text{TDI}} = \frac{42.02 \times 2 \times 100}{174.16} = \frac{8404}{174.16} = 48.25\;\%$$ For **HDI** ($M = 168.20$, $f = 2$): $$\%\text{NCO}_{\text{HDI}} = \frac{42.02 \times 2 \times 100}{168.20} = \frac{8404}{168.20} = 49.96\;\%$$ Both values are close to 50 %, as expected for low-molecular-weight diisocyanates with two NCO ends. **Part (b) — 1-K and 2-K PU adhesives (7 marks):** **1-K (one-component) moisture-cure PU adhesive (3 marks):** The diisocyanate is supplied as an **NCO-terminated prepolymer** (e.g., MDI prepolymer with %NCO ≈ 4–10 %). After application, atmospheric or substrate-borne moisture diffuses in and reacts: $$\text{Prepolymer—NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Prepolymer—NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2$$ $$\text{Prepolymer—NH}_2 + \text{Prepolymer—NCO} \longrightarrow \text{Urea crosslink}$$ CO₂ evolution limits bondline thickness; primers may be needed on wet substrates. **2-K (two-component) PU adhesive (2 marks):** - **Component A:** polyol (OH-terminated polyester or polyether) + fillers + catalysts. - **Component B:** diisocyanate or NCO-prepolymer. On mixing, –NCO reacts with –OH to form urethane linkages: $$\text{—N=C=O} + \text{HO—} \longrightarrow \text{—NH—CO—O—}$$ **Isocyanate Index R (2 marks):** $$R = \frac{n_{\text{NCO}}}{n_{\text{OH}}}$$ - $R = 1.0$ → stoichiometric (standard balance of strength and flexibility). - $R > 1.0$ → excess NCO → rigid, hard, higher heat resistance but brittle (used in rigid foams, structural joints). - $R < 1.0$ → excess OH → soft, flexible, elastomeric (used in sealants, flexible laminates). **Formulators** choose R based on desired end-use properties; typical structural adhesives use $R = 1.0$–$1.1$, elastomeric sealants use $R = 0.8$–$0.95$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Checklist — Diisocyanates** - **Molar mass of –NCO group = 42.02 g/mol** — non-negotiable number for every calculation. - **Master formula:** $\%\text{NCO} = \dfrac{4202 \times f}{M}$. - **Equivalent weight of NCO:** $E_{\text{NCO}} = M/f$. - **Isocyanate Index R = $n_{\text{NCO}}/n_{\text{OH}}$** — controls rigidity vs flexibility. - **Five reactions to memorize:** with –OH (urethane), –NH₂ (urea), H₂O (CO₂), –COOH (amide + CO₂), urethane (allophanate). - **TDI = 48.25 % NCO, M = 174.16; MDI = 33.58 % NCO, M = 250.25; HDI = 49.96 % NCO, M = 168.20** — these three values cover ~80 % of KTU numerical questions. - **Aromatic diisocyanates** → strong, fast, yellow on UV. - **Aliphatic diisocyanates** → UV-stable, non-yellowing, slower cure, costlier. - **IPDI** has two chemically distinct NCO groups — **primary (fast) and secondary (slow)** — enabling chemoselective formulations. - **1-K moisture cure** releases **CO₂** → use thin bondlines and dry substrates. - **2-K PU adhesives** require stoichiometric mixing by mass: $m_{\text{iso}}/m_{\text{polyol}} = (E_{\text{NCO}}/E_{\text{OH}}) \times R$. - **Modified diisocyanates** (prepolymers, biurets, isocyanurates, polymeric MDI) reduce vapor pressure and toxicity while tuning reactivity. - **Hard segment** = diisocyanate + chain extender (short diol/diamine) → provides cohesion, thermal resistance, modulus. - **Soft segment** = long-chain polyol → provides flexibility, low-T performance, peel strength. - **Always** state units (g/mol, %, g/eq) in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit omission. - **Health note:** Diisocyanates are **sensitizers** (cause occupational asthma); modern prepolymers and modified grades are engineered to reduce monomeric

topics.title: Cyanoacrylate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cyanoacrylate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyanoacrylates** are a distinct family of **single-component, solvent-free, room-temperature-curing structural adhesives** based on α-cyanoacrylate monomers. The general chemical structure is:

$$CH_2 = C(CN) \cdot COOR$$

where the **R** group represents an alkyl substituent (most commonly **methyl**, **ethyl**, **butyl**, **octyl**, or **2-octyl**). These monomers undergo rapid **anionic (chain-growth) addition polymerization** when exposed to trace amounts of moisture or weakly basic species, converting almost instantaneously from a low-viscosity liquid monomer into a rigid, high-molecular-weight thermoplastic polymer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cyanoacrylates are classified under **reactive acrylic adhesives** (Module 3 – Specialty Adhesives). They represent the most commercially significant class of **instant adhesives (IA)** and are characterized by their **"one-part, no-mix, cure-on-demand"** behavior — a property unmatched by any other generic adhesive family.

## 1.2 Historical Context & Discovery

Cyanoacrylates were first synthesized by **Harry Coover** at Eastman Kodak (USA) in **1942**, but their adhesive potential was accidentally discovered in **1951** when Coover's team was developing acrylic polymers for aircraft canopy coatings. The team observed that the monomer stuck to everything it touched and resisted solvent removal. Commercialized as **"Eastman 910"** (later marketed as **Super Glue**), the technology revolutionized the instant-bonding adhesive industry.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Molecular Mousetrap"**
>
> Imagine a spring-loaded mousetrap in its **open, primed state** — full of stored energy, waiting for the slightest trigger. Cyanoacrylate monomers behave exactly like this:
>
> - The **carbon-carbon double bond (C=C)** is the "loaded spring" storing chemical potential energy.
> - The **electron-withdrawing –CN and –COOR groups** keep the monomer in this reactive, primed state.
> - The **trace water molecule** (or any weak base) acts as the **trigger** — a single touch, and the trap snaps shut.
> - Once initiated, the polymerization propagates like a chain reaction, snapping monomer after monomer into the growing polymer chain within **seconds**.
>
> Just as a mousetrap cannot un-snap itself, once the anionic polymerization is initiated, it proceeds to completion almost irreversibly, forming a rigid poly(α-cyanoacrylate) network.

## 1.4 Classification of Cyanoacrylate Adhesives

| Type | R-Group | Key Property | Typical Application |
|------|---------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Methyl Cyanoacrylate (MCA)** | –CH₃ | Lowest viscosity (~2 cP), highest strength, brittle | Metals, ceramics, glass |
| **Ethyl Cyanoacrylate (ECA)** | –C₂H₅ | Low viscosity (~5 cP), general purpose | Most common; DIY, plastics, rubber |
| **Butyl / Isobutyl Cyanoacrylate** | –C₄H₉ | More flexible, lower strength | Slightly porous substrates |
| **Octyl / 2-Octyl Cyanoacrylate** | –C₈H₁₇ | Most flexible, low irritation, biocompatible | **Medical tissue adhesives (FDA approved)** |
| **Methoxethyl Cyanoacrylate** | –CH₂OCH₃ | Low odor, low blooming | Cosmetic-grade bonding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Trade-off:** As the alkyl chain length (R) increases, **flexibility and biocompatibility improve**, but **bond strength and cure speed decrease**. This inverse relationship is a fundamental design parameter in cyanoacrylate formulation.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Viscosity range:** **1 cP to >100,000 cP** (gel grades) — pure MCA monomer is among the lowest-viscosity reactive liquids known.
- **Cure speed (fixture time):** **1 to 60 seconds** on most substrates at 25 °C and 50% RH.
- **Service temperature range:** **–54 °C to +82 °C** (standard grades); up to **+120 °C** with special formulations.
- **Tensile shear strength:** **14–25 MPa** (on steel-to-steel, after 24 h cure).
- **Glass transition temperature (T_g):** **+120 °C to ++165 °C**, depending on R-group and additives.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemistry of Cure — The Anionic Polymerization Mechanism

The cure mechanism is the **heart** of cyanoacrylate behavior and is the most examinable aspect in KTU papers. The polymerization proceeds via **anionic chain-growth addition** with the following elementary steps:

### Step 1: Initiation by Trace Water (or –OH, –NH₂ groups)

A water molecule — present as a thin adsorbed film on virtually any substrate — deprotonates to generate a **hydroxide ion (OH⁻)**. This strong nucleophile attacks the **electrophilic β-carbon** of the C=C double bond (the CH₂= carbon, which is rendered highly electron-deficient by the –CN and –COOR groups).

$$H_2O + CH_2 = C(CN)(COOR) \longrightarrow HO-CH_2-\overline{C}(CN)(COOR)$$

The result is a **carbanion** (a stabilized enolate-type anion) at the α-carbon.

### Step 2: Propagation — Chain Extension

The newly formed carbanion is a potent nucleophile that attacks the β-carbon of another monomer unit, shifting the carbanion to the next monomer. This **chain transfer** repeats with extraordinary rapidity:

$$HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOR)-]_n^- \xrightarrow{+M} HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOR)-]_{n+1}^-$$

### Step 3: Termination

Termination occurs when the propagating carbanion encounters a **protic species** (e.g., water, alcohol, acid), which donates a proton and quenches the chain:

$$HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOR)-]_n^- + H^+ \longrightarrow HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOR)-]_n-H$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The –CN and –COOR groups are the secret of cyanoacrylate reactivity.** These two strongly electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) jointly:
> (a) make the β-carbon extremely electrophilic (susceptible to nucleophilic attack), and
> (b) stabilize the resulting α-carbanion through resonance delocalization, allowing the chain to propagate for milliseconds before terminating.
> Without these EWGs, the monomer would be far less reactive and would not cure on-demand from atmospheric moisture.

## 2.2 Why Cyanoacrylates Cure So Fast — The "Anionic Living Polymerization" View

The polymerization rate is governed by the **electron density at the β-carbon**, which is dictated by the Hammett substituent constants of the –COOR group. As the alkyl group becomes **bulkier and less electron-donating** (methyl → ethyl → butyl → octyl), the β-carbon becomes *more* electrophilic *in principle*, but the **steric hindrance dominates in practice**, slowing the propagation step. Hence:

$$\text{Cure Speed:} \quad \text{Methyl} > \text{Ethyl} > \text{Butyl} > \text{Octyl}$$

$$\text{Flexibility:} \quad \text{Methyl} < \text{Ethyl} < \text{Butyl} < \text{Octyl}$$

## 2.3 Substrate-Specific Curing Behavior

Cyanoacrylates cure on virtually all substrates, but the **cure speed varies by orders of magnitude** depending on the surface chemistry:

| Substrate Class | Surface pH | Cure Speed | Reason |
|-----------------|------------|------------|--------|
| **Acidic surfaces** (e.g., wood, paper, leather) | pH 3–5 | Slow (30–60 s) | Acidic –OH groups neutralize initiating anions |
| **Neutral surfaces** (e.g., metals, glass) | pH ~7 | Moderate (5–20 s) | Surface moisture + metal hydroxides |
| **Basic surfaces** (e.g., concrete, alkaline plastics) | pH 8–10 | Very fast (1–5 s) | Surface –OH/–NH₂ groups act as initiators |
| **Polyolefins (PE, PP)** | — | Very slow / poor | No polar groups; surface pretreatment required |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is assuming cyanoacrylates bond well to **polyolefins (PE, PP, PTFE)**. They do **not** — these low-surface-energy substrates lack the polar initiators required for anionic polymerization. **Primer 770 or corona treatment is mandatory** for such substrates.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Application | Units |
|---|--------------------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $M_w = M_0 \cdot DP_n$ | Molecular weight relation (M₀ = monomer mass, DPₙ = degree of polymerization) | g/mol |
| 2 | $\sigma_{shear} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{bond}}$ | Lap shear strength calculation | MPa |
| 3 | $R_p = k_p [M][M^-]$ | Rate of anionic propagation (Rₚ = rate, kₚ = rate constant, M = monomer, M⁻ = carbanion conc.) | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $t_{fixture} \propto \dfrac{1}{[H_2O]_{surface}}$ | Fixture time inversely proportional to surface moisture | s |
| 5 | $T_{cure} \downarrow \implies t_{fixture} \uparrow$ | Cure slows at low T (rule of thumb: doubles every 8 °C drop) | qualitative |
| 6 | $\eta_{gel} = \eta_0 \cdot 10^{\alpha \cdot c_{thickener}}$ | Viscosity of thixotropic gel grades | cP |
| 7 | $\sigma_{peel} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w \cdot t_{adhesive}}$ | Peel strength per unit width | N/mm |
| 8 | $X_c = 1 - e^{-k t}$ | Conversion fraction as function of time (first-order) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important boundary condition for KTU problems:** The polymerization is **strongly inhibited by acids**. A drop of acid (e.g., vinegar, citric acid) can effectively *deactivate* the cure — this is why cyanoacrylate containers are made of **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** with the bottle tip treated with acidic stabilizers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical sector:** 2-Octyl and n-butyl cyanoacrylates are FDA-approved as **topical tissue adhesives** (e.g., Dermabond®), replacing sutures for minor lacerations.
- **Electronics:** Used for **wire tacking, component staking, and conformal coating repair** on PCBs (low outgassing, fast cure).
- **Automotive:** Rapid assembly of rubber gaskets, weather-stripping, and trim.
- **Forensics:** Cyanoacrylate **fuming chambers** are used to develop latent fingerprints on non-porous surfaces — the monomer polymerizes preferentially in the ridge deposits of the print.
- **Cosmetics:** Lash extension adhesives (ethyl cyanoacrylate-based, low odor).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Polymerization Kinetics for Cyanoacrylate Cure

The cure of cyanoacrylate can be modeled as a **pseudo first-order anionic chain polymerization** with respect to monomer concentration, assuming the carbanion concentration reaches a quasi-steady state almost instantly after initiation.

### Step 1 — Defining the Rate Equation

The rate of disappearance of monomer is governed by the propagation step. The rate of monomer consumption is:

$$R_p = -\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M][M^-]$$

where $k_p$ is the propagation rate constant, $[M]$ is the molar concentration of unreacted monomer, and $[M^-]$ is the molar concentration of active propagating carbanion centers.

### Step 2 — Applying the Steady-State Approximation

Because initiation by trace water is much slower than propagation, and termination by trace acids is similarly slow, the **carbanion concentration is approximately constant** during the bulk of the cure window:

$$[M^-] \approx [M^-]_0 = \text{constant}$$

Substituting this into the rate equation:

$$-\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p [M^-]_0 [M]$$

### Step 3 — Separation of Variables

Rearranging the differential equation to separate $[M]$ and $t$:

$$-\dfrac{d[M]}{[M]} = k_p [M^-]_0 \, dt$$

### Step 4 — Integration

Integrating both sides from initial monomer concentration $[M]_0$ (at $t = 0$) to $[M]$ (at time $t$):

$$\int_{[M]_0}^{[M]} -\dfrac{d[M]}{[M]} = k_p [M^-]_0 \int_0^t dt$$

This yields:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{[M]_0}{[M]}\right) = k_p [M^-]_0 \, t$$

### Step 5 — Conversion to Monomer Conversion Fraction

Let $X$ be the fraction of monomer that has polymerized, defined as:

$$X = \dfrac{[M]_0 - [M]}{[M]_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad [M] = [M]_0 (1 - X)$$

Substituting into the integrated rate law:

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{[M]_0}{[M]_0 (1-X)}\right) = k_p [M^-]_0 \, t$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right) = k_p [M^-]_0 \, t$$

### Step 6 — Final Working Equation

Solving explicitly for the conversion fraction:

$$X = 1 - e^{-k_p [M^-]_0 \, t}$$

Or, equivalently, in a form that allows the experimental determination of the lumped rate constant $k_{eff} = k_p [M^-]_0$:

$$\boxed{X = 1 - e^{-k_{eff} \, t}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** This equation has the same form as a first-order kinetic decay. The **effective rate constant $k_{eff}$** lumps together the propagation constant and the active carbanion concentration. In practice, $k_{eff}$ is **strongly dependent** on temperature, surface pH, and moisture content. This explains why KTU numerical problems often give you $k_{eff}$ and ask you to compute $X$ at a given $t$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A thin film of ethyl cyanoacrylate (initial monomer concentration $[M]_0 = 9.0$ mol/L) cures on a steel substrate. The effective first-order rate constant is $k_{eff} = 0.45 \text{ s}^{-1}$. Calculate the monomer conversion after **(a)** 2 seconds, **(b)** 5 seconds, and **(c)** the time required to reach 99% conversion.

**Solution:**

**(a) At t = 2 s:**

$$X = 1 - e^{-(0.45)(2)} = 1 - e^{-0.90} = 1 - 0.4066 = 0.5934 \approx 59.3\%$$

**(b) At t = 5 s:**

$$X = 1 - e^{-(0.45)(5)} = 1 - e^{-2.25} = 1 - 0.1054 = 0.8946 \approx 89.5\%$$

**(c) For X = 0.99:**

$$0.99 = 1 - e^{-k_{eff} t}$$

$$e^{-k_{eff} t} = 0.01$$

$$k_{eff} t = \ln(100) = 4.605$$

$$t = \dfrac{4.605}{0.45} \approx 10.23 \text{ seconds}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation with Other Adhesive Families

| Property | Cyanoacrylate | Epoxy | Polyurethane | Hot-Melt (EVA) |
|----------|---------------|-------|--------------|----------------|
| **Cure mechanism** | Anionic (moisture) | Step-growth (amine/anhydride) | Moisture or heat | Solidification on cooling |
| **Number of parts** | **1** | 2 | 1 or 2 | 1 |
| **Fixture time** | **1–60 s** | 5 min – 24 h | 1 min – 1 h | Seconds (on cooling) |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 14–25 | 15–35 | 5–40 | 3–10 |
| **T_g (°C)** | 120–165 | 80–180 | –40 to +80 | 60–100 |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid (brittle) | Rigid (with modifier) | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Gap-filling** | Poor (thin bond line) | Excellent | Good | Good |
| **Temperature resistance** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Low |
| **Key weakness** | Poor on polyolefins; acidic surfaces | Slow cure; mixing | Sensitive to moisture in storage | Thermoplastic — softens on heating |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Monomer Structure & Polymer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    monomer["Monomer: CH2=C-CN-COOR"] -->|"H2O or -OH Initiator"| initiation["Initiation: Carbanion formation at alpha-C"]
    initiation -->|"Monomer addition"| propagation["Propagation: Chain extension"]
    propagation -->|"H+ from water/acid"| termination["Termination: Proton transfer"]
    termination --> polymer["Polymer: -CH2-C-CN-COOR-]n"]
    propagation -.->|"Brought close by substrate moisture"| substrate["Polar substrate surface -OH or -NH2 groups"]
    substrate -->|"Acts as initiator"| initiation
```

## 4.2 Cure Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Storage["Storage State (Bottle)"]
        A1["Pure liquid monomer CH2=C-CN-COOR"] --> A2["Acidic stabilizer SO2 or sulfonic acid"]
        A2 --> A3["Anionic polymerization INHIBITED"]
    end

    subgraph Application["Application State (Bond Line)"]
        B1["Adhesive dispensed onto substrate"] --> B2["Thin film forms, exposed to atmosphere"]
        B2 --> B3["Trace water adsorbed from surface and air"]
    end

    subgraph Cure["Curing State (Bond Line)"]
        C1["H2O + Monomer: forms hydroxyl carbanion"] --> C2["Carbanion attacks next monomer: chain extends"]
        C2 --> C3["Exothermic polymerization 5 to 10 kJ per mole"]
        C3 --> C4["Within 1 to 60 seconds: solid rigid polymer formed"]
    end

    A3 -.->|"Dispense"| B1
    B3 -.->|"Initiates"| C1
    C4 --> D1["Final Bond: Cured poly-cyanoacrylate layer"]
```

## 4.3 Influence of R-Group on Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Methyl -CH3"] -->|"Lowest viscosity, fastest cure, highest strength, brittle"| P1["MCA Applications: Metals, glass, ceramics"]
    R2["Ethyl -C2H5"] -->|"Balanced properties, general purpose"| P2["ECA Applications: DIY, plastics, rubber"]
    R3["Butyl -C4H9"] -->|"Improved flexibility, lower strength"| P3["n-Butyl CA Applications: Slightly flexible bonds, medical close-tissue"]
    R4["Octyl -C8H17"] -->|"Maximum flexibility, biocompatible, slowest cure"| P4["2-Octyl CA Applications: Medical topical tissue adhesive Dermabond"]
```

## 4.4 Substrate Compatibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start["Substrate Type"] --> Polar{"Polar groups present?"}
    Polar -->|"Yes: metals, glass, wood, rubber, most plastics"| Good["EXCELLENT bond: 5 to 20 second cure"]
    Polar -->|"Minimal: PE, PP, PTFE, silicones"| Poor["POOR bond: requires primer or surface treatment"]
    Poor --> Primer["Apply Primer 770: contains amine accelerator"]
    Primer --> Treated["Surface now bonded by cyanoacrylate effectively"]
    Good --> Note["Note: Acidic surfaces wood paper slow cure 30 to 60 seconds"]
    Treated --> Note
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define cyanoacrylate adhesives. Mention any two important properties. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cyanoacrylates are **single-component, solvent-free, room-temperature-curing adhesives** based on **alkyl α-cyanoacrylate monomers** of the general formula $CH_2 = C(CN) \cdot COOR$, where R is an alkyl group (commonly methyl, ethyl, or octyl). They cure via **anionic addition polymerization** initiated by trace moisture or weakly basic species on substrate surfaces.

**Two Important Properties:**
1. **Very fast fixture time** (1–60 seconds) at room temperature without the need for mixing, heat, or catalysts.
2. **High bond strength on a wide range of substrates** including metals, plastics, rubber, ceramics, and biological tissues (lap shear strength 14–25 MPa on steel).

---

### Question 2: Why is the bottle of cyanoacrylate adhesive made of high-density polyethylene? [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The bottle of cyanoacrylate adhesive is made of **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** because of two critical reasons:

1. **HDPE is essentially impermeable to atmospheric moisture** — its dense, semicrystalline structure prevents water vapor ingress that would otherwise initiate premature polymerization inside the container.
2. **The bottle and the dispensing tip are treated with acidic stabilizers** (e.g., SO₂, methanesulfonic acid, or citric acid) on the inner surface. These acidic species act as **anionic polymerization inhibitors**, ensuring the monomer remains liquid during storage. When the adhesive is dispensed in a thin film, the surface-to-volume ratio increases dramatically, the acid dissipates, and atmospheric moisture initiates the cure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer that "HDPE is used because it does not react with cyanoacrylate." This is **partially correct but incomplete**. The key point that earns full marks is the **combination of moisture barrier + acid stabilization**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question is split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

#### (a) Explain the anionic polymerization mechanism of ethyl cyanoacrylate curing on a metal surface. Discuss the role of trace moisture and the electron-withdrawing groups. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The curing of ethyl cyanoacrylate on a metal surface proceeds via **anionic chain-growth polymerization** in three distinct stages:

**Stage 1 — Initiation:**
A thin film of adsorbed water (always present on metallic surfaces) provides the initiator. The water molecule, often in conjunction with surface metal hydroxides (e.g., Fe–OH, Al–OH), generates a **hydroxide ion (OH⁻)**. This nucleophile attacks the **electrophilic β-carbon** of the monomer, breaking the C=C double bond and producing a **stabilized α-carbanion**:

$$H_2O + CH_2 = C(CN)(COOC_2H_5) \rightarrow HO-CH_2-\overline{C}(CN)(COOC_2H_5)$$

**Stage 2 — Propagation:**
The carbanion is a potent nucleophile. It attacks the β-carbon of the next monomer, extending the chain and shifting the carbanion to the new terminal unit. This repeats at an extremely high rate, with a propagation rate constant $k_p$ on the order of $10^3$ to $10^4$ L mol⁻¹ s⁻¹:

$$HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOC_2H_5)-]_n^- + M \rightarrow HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOC_2H_5)-]_{n+1}^-$$

**Stage 3 — Termination:**
The propagating carbanion is quenched by proton transfer from trace water, alcohol, or surface acid groups, yielding the dead polymer:

$$HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOC_2H_5)-]_n^- + H^+ \rightarrow HO-[-CH_2-C(CN)(COOC_2H_5)-]_n-H$$

**Role of Trace Moisture:** Without moisture, no initiating species are generated, and the monomer remains stable indefinitely. The amount of moisture on the substrate **directly controls the cure speed**: dry conditions lead to very slow cure; humid conditions lead to almost instantaneous cure.

**Role of Electron-Withdrawing Groups (–CN and –COOR):** These groups jointly render the β-carbon strongly electrophilic and stabilize the resulting α-carbanion through **resonance delocalization** of the negative charge into the C=O and C≡N π-systems. Without these EWGs, the polymerization would not proceed at the required speed, and the monomer would be far less reactive.

#### (b) Discuss the effect of alkyl chain length of the R-group on the properties of cyanoacrylate adhesives. Compare methyl, ethyl, and octyl cyanoacrylates. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The alkyl group **R** in the cyanoacrylate monomer profoundly influences viscosity, cure speed, flexibility, and biocompatibility. The general trends are summarized below:

| Property | Methyl CA | Ethyl CA | Octyl CA |
|----------|-----------|----------|----------|
| **Viscosity** | Lowest (~2 cP) | Low (~5 cP) | Higher (~50–100 cP) |
| **Cure speed** | Fastest | Fast | Slowest |
| **Bond strength (shear, MPa)** | Highest (20–25) | High (15–20) | Moderate (8–12) |
| **Flexibility / elongation** | Brittle | Slightly flexible | Most flexible |
| **T_g (°C)** | ~165 | ~140 | ~80 |
| **Biocompatibility** | Poor (irritant) | Poor (irritant) | Excellent (FDA approved) |
| **Odor / blooming** | Strong | Strong | Low |
| **Cost** | High | Lowest | Highest |

**Detailed Discussion:**

- **Methyl cyanoacrylate (R = –CH₃):** The smallest alkyl group gives the lowest steric hindrance at the β-carbon, resulting in the fastest propagation rate and highest bond strength. However, the polymer is highly rigid (T_g ~165 °C) and brittle, with poor impact resistance. It is also the most irritating to skin and tissues, limiting its medical use.

- **Ethyl cyanoacrylate (R = –C₂H₅):** The slightly bulkier ethyl group introduces modest steric hindrance, slowing the cure marginally while slightly improving flexibility. It represents the **best balance of properties for general-purpose industrial use** and is the most widely produced variant globally.

- **Octyl / 2-Octyl cyanoacrylate (R = –C₈H₁₇):** The long aliphatic chain dramatically reduces the glass transition temperature to ~80 °C, producing a flexible polymer. The bulky group severely slows propagation, so cure is slow (often requiring 30–60 s or an accelerator). However, the long chain **drastically reduces tissue irritation and hydrolysis byproducts**, making it the only FDA-approved variant for medical tissue bonding (e.g., **Dermabond®**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is to state that "longer R-groups make stronger bonds." This is **FALSE** — bond strength *decreases* with increasing R-chain length. The trade-off is **strength vs. flexibility vs. biocompatibility**. Examiners specifically award marks for correctly identifying this **inverse relationship**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]:**

#### (a) With the help of a kinetic equation, derive the expression for monomer conversion as a function of time during cyanoacrylate cure. Given $k_{eff} = 0.35$ s⁻¹, calculate the time to reach 95% conversion. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The anionic polymerization of cyanoacrylate is modeled as a **pseudo first-order reaction** with respect to monomer, since the active carbanion concentration reaches a quasi-steady state:

$$-\frac{d[M]}{dt} = k_{eff} [M]$$

Separating variables and integrating from $[M]_0$ at $t=0$ to $[M]$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{[M]_0}^{[M]} \frac{d[M]}{[M]} = -k_{eff} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln[M] - \ln[M]_0 = -k_{eff} t$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{[M]_0}{[M]}\right) = k_{eff} t$$

The monomer conversion fraction is defined as $X = 1 - \frac{[M]}{[M]_0}$, which upon substitution gives:

$$X = 1 - e^{-k_{eff} t}$$

**Numerical Calculation:** For $k_{eff} = 0.35$ s⁻¹ and $X = 0.95$:

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-0.35 \, t}$$

$$e^{-0.35 \, t} = 0.05$$

$$-0.35 \, t = \ln(0.05) = -2.996$$

$$t = \frac{2.996}{0.35} \approx 8.56 \text{ seconds}$$

Hence, the time to reach 95% conversion is approximately **8.56 seconds**.

#### (b) Explain why cyanoacrylates do not bond well to polyethylene and polytetrafluoroethylene. What surface treatments can be used to enable bonding? [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Model Answer:**

**Why Poor Bonding Occurs:**

Polyethylene (PE) and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE, Teflon) are classified as **low-surface-energy, non-polar polymers** with surface energies in the range of **18–31 mJ/m²**. The cyanoacrylate cure mechanism requires **polar initiating groups** (–OH, –NH₂, or adsorbed H₂O) on the substrate to generate the initial carbanion. Since PE and PTFE have no such polar groups, the following problems arise:

1. **No initiation sites:** The surface lacks the nucleophilic species required to start the anionic polymerization. Cure is therefore extremely slow (minutes to hours) or fails entirely.
2. **Poor wetting:** The high contact angle of cyanoacrylate monomer droplets on these surfaces (>90°) prevents intimate molecular contact between adhesive and substrate, resulting in mechanical rather than chemical adhesion.
3. **Weak acid-base interaction:** Even if some polymerization occurs, the lack of polar interactions at the interface yields very low adhesive strength (typically < 1 MPa lap shear).

**Surface Treatments to Enable Bonding:**

1. **Corona or plasma treatment:** Exposes the surface to ionized gas, creating polar functional groups (–OH, –C=O, –COOH) that increase surface energy to > 50 mJ/m² and provide anionic initiation sites.
2. **Chemical etching:** For PTFE, treatment with **sodium-naphthalenate complex (Tetra-Etch®)** chemically defluorinates the surface, producing a carbon-rich, wettable layer.
3. **Use of Primer 770 or 793:** Henkel Loctite primers are amine-containing accelerators that are applied to the substrate before the cyanoacrylate. They provide the initiating nucleophilic species externally, dramatically accelerating cure on polyolefins.
4. **Mechanical abrasion:** Light sanding creates micro-roughness and exposes fresh, more reactive material.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often cite only "corona treatment" as the universal answer. The examiner expects a **discussion of the underlying chemistry** (lack of polar initiators) and at least **two distinct treatment methods**. Surface treatment without mechanistic explanation will earn at most 3 of 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **General formula:** $CH_2 = C(CN) \cdot COOR$ — the two **EWGs** (–CN and –COOR) are the *reason* these monomers are reactive.
- **Cure mechanism:** **Anionic chain-growth addition polymerization**, initiated by trace water or basic species on the substrate.
- **Three stages:** **Initiation → Propagation → Termination** (proton transfer).
- **One-part system:** No mixing required; cure is *triggered* by contact with the substrate.
- **Cure speed order:** **Methyl > Ethyl > Butyl > Octyl.** Bond strength order: **Octyl < Butyl < Ethyl < Methyl.**
- **Storage:** HDPE bottles with **acid stabilizers** (SO₂, sulfonic acid) prevent premature cure.
- **Acidic surfaces** (wood, paper) **slow cure**; **basic surfaces** (concrete) **accelerate cure**.
- **Poor on polyolefins** (PE, PP, PTFE) — require corona treatment or **Primer 770**.
- **Key kinetics equation:** $\boxed{X = 1 - e^{-k_{eff} t}}$ — first-order in monomer.
- **Bond strength range:** **14–25 MPa** (lap shear on steel).
- **Service temperature:** **–54 °C to +82 °C** (standard); up to +120 °C for specialty grades.
- **Medical grades:** 2-Octyl and n-butyl cyanoacrylates (e.g., Dermabond®).
- **Forensic use:** **Cyanoacrylate fuming** develops latent fingerprints.
- **Polymer property:** Cured poly(cyanoacrylate) is a **thermoplastic** — it will soften above its T_g (120–165 °C).
- **Common abbreviation:** **"CA"** (cyanoacrylate); **"MCA"** = methyl; **"ECA"** = ethyl; **"OCA"** = octyl.
- **Exothermicity:** Cure releases **5–10 kJ per mole** of monomer converted — a thin bond line is essential to dissipate this heat.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Cyanoacrylate | PEPOT861 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Acrylic Adhesives — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Acrylic adhesives** are a broad, industrially significant class of structural and semi-structural adhesives formulated primarily from **acrylate** and **methacrylate monomers** (esters of acrylic acid, $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOR}$, and methacrylic acid, $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3\text{)–COOR}$), which polymerize via a **free-radical chain-growth mechanism** to form a crosslinked, thermoset polyacrylate network capable of load-bearing structural bonding across a wide range of substrates including metals, plastics, ceramics, and composites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition Box**
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT861, Module 3), acrylic adhesives are formally defined as *"a versatile class of reactive adhesives based on the polymerization of acrylic and methacrylic esters, characterized by tunable cure chemistry, dual-cure capability (anaerobic/UV/heat), and high cohesive strength on untreated metal surfaces."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **gluing two LEGO bricks** together. A normal glue (like PVA) is like wet cement that dries by *evaporation of water* — it shrinks, and the bricks must fit precisely. Now imagine a glue that **chemically "snaps" the two bricks together by spawning millions of tiny molecular grappling hooks** that interlock on both sides. That is exactly what an acrylic adhesive does.

Think of it this way:
- The **monomer** is like a "Velcro precursor" — a sticky liquid that doesn't yet have hooks.
- The **initiator** (e.g., a peroxide or UV photon) is the "spark" that triggers hook-formation.
- Once sparked, the monomers **link hands** rapidly to form a long polymer chain — like a chain of paper clips.
- These chains crosslink, creating a 3D "Velcro jungle" that grips both surfaces.
- Because the hooks are on *both* sides, the bond works on **untreated, oily, or even slightly contaminated metals** — a unique superpower of acrylics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The "two-part metal-bonding" superpower of acrylics is the most commonly asked 3-mark and 14-mark question in Module 3. Always mention **"untreated metal compatibility"** as the distinguishing feature.

## 1.3 Classification of Acrylic Adhesives (KTU Module 3)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups acrylic adhesives into **five major sub-classes** based on cure mechanism and formulation:

| Sub-Class | Cure Trigger | Typical Monomer | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anaerobic Acrylics** | Metal contact + absence of air | PEGDMA, HEMA | Thread-locking, shaft-hub |
| **Cyanoacrylates** | Surface moisture (anionic) | Methyl/Ethyl cyanoacrylate | "Super glue", medical, electronics |
| **Toughened / Two-Part Acrylics (MMA)** | Peroxide + Amine accelerator | Methyl methacrylate | Structural metal bonding, automotive |
| **UV/Visible Light-Curable Acrylics** | Photoinitiator + UV | Acrylate oligomers | Glass, optical, electronics potting |
| **Pressure-Sensitive Acrylics (PSA)** | Solvent/emulsion removal | 2-EHA, butyl acrylate | Tapes, labels, medical dressings |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Cure shrinkage:** **3–5 %** (low, compared to epoxies at ~1–2 %)
- **Service temperature range:** **–55 °C to +150 °C** (specialty grades: up to **+200 °C**)
- **Tensile shear strength (mild steel, MMA grade):** **20–35 MPa**
- **Peel strength (aluminium):** **8–25 N/mm**
- **Mix ratio (two-part):** typically **1:1** or **10:1** by volume
- **Open time:** **30 s – 30 min** depending on grade
- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) of poly(methyl methacrylate):** **+105 °C**
- **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$) of poly(ethyl acrylate):** **–24 °C**

## 1.5 Key Acrylic Monomers — Molecular Vocabulary

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these five — they appear in every KTU 2-mark and 3-mark question paper.

1. **Methyl methacrylate (MMA)** — $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3\text{)–COOCH}_3$ — High $T_g$, hard, structural.
2. **Ethyl acrylate (EA)** — $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOC}_2\text{H}_5$ — Low $T_g$, flexible.
3. **Butyl acrylate (BA)** — $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOC}_4\text{H}_9$ — Very low $T_g$, soft, PSA base.
4. **2-Ethylhexyl acrylate (2-EHA)** — $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOCH}_2\text{CH(C}_2\text{H}_5\text{)C}_4\text{H}_9$ — Lowest $T_g$, primary PSA monomer.
5. **Acrylic acid (AA)** — $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOH}$ — Reactive co-monomer for adhesion promotion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymerization Mechanism — Free-Radical Chain Growth

Acrylic adhesives cure by **free-radical addition polymerization**, which proceeds through three classical stages: **initiation**, **propagation**, and **termination**.

### Step 1: Initiation
A peroxide initiator (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, BPO) decomposes under heat, metal redox, or UV to give primary radicals.

$$\text{RO–OR} \xrightarrow{\Delta \,/\, \text{UV} \,/\, \text{Metal}^{n+}} 2\,\text{RO}^{\bullet}$$

The primary radical adds to the acrylic double bond to create the **active chain radical** ($R_1^{\bullet}$).

$$\text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOR} \rightarrow \text{RO–CH}_2\!-\!\overset{\bullet}{\text{CH}}\text{–COOR}$$

### Step 2: Propagation
The active chain radical attacks the next monomer in a chain-extension sequence, repeating rapidly.

$$R_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} R_{n+1}^{\bullet}$$

### Step 3: Termination
Two growing chain radicals combine (coupling) or disproportionate to terminate.

$$R_m^{\bullet} + R_n^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} P_{m+n} \quad \text{(combination)}$$

$$R_m^{\bullet} + R_n^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} P_m + P_n \quad \text{(disproportionation)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** Always state the rate constants in the order $k_d, k_i, k_p, k_t$ during derivation. Examiners look for the **standard sequence** — a student who mixes up $k_p$ and $k_t$ loses 2 marks on a 14-mark question.

## 2.2 Role of Each Formulation Component

| Component | Function | Example | Typical Loading (phr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monomer(s)** | Reactive base, builds polymer | MMA, BA, EA | 60–90 |
| **Reactive diluent / Toughener** | Reduce viscosity, improve impact | PEGDMA, Epoxy-methacrylate | 5–25 |
| **Initiator** | Generate free radicals | BPO, DCP, AIBN | 0.5–3 |
| **Accelerator / Activator** | Lower decomposition temperature | Tertiary amine, metal soap (cobalt, vanadium) | 0.1–2 |
| **Stabilizer / Inhibitor** | Prevent premature gelation | MEHQ, BHT, hydroquinone | 0.01–0.5 |
| **Adhesion promoter** | Bond to difficult surfaces | Silane (e.g., γ-MPS), phosphoric acid ester | 0.5–3 |
| **Filler** | Reduce shrinkage, modify rheology | Alumina, silica, calcium carbonate | 0–40 |
| **Thickener / Thixotrope** | Anti-sag, gap-fill | Fumed silica | 0–5 |

## 2.3 Cure Mechanism of Each Acrylic Sub-Class

### 2.3.1 Two-Part (Toughened) MMA Acrylics — Redox Cure
- **Part A:** MMA monomer + elastomeric toughener (e.g., carboxyl-terminated butadiene-acrylonitrile, CTBN) + accelerator (amine).
- **Part B:** BPO initiator + monomer.
- On mixing, the **amine reduces BPO** at room temperature, generating radicals that initiate polymerization of MMA around the elastomer phase — yielding a **"sea-island" morphology** of rubber particles in a glassy poly-MMA matrix.

### 2.3.2 Anaerobic Acrylics
- Cure is **inhibited by oxygen** in the air, but proceeds rapidly in the **metal-ion-catalyzed, oxygen-free environment** between close-fitting parts (e.g., threaded fasteners).
- Hydrogen peroxide + saccharin + cumene hydroperoxide + transition metal ions form the redox system.

### 2.3.3 Cyanoacrylates
- Polymerize by **anionic mechanism** triggered by **trace water** (the hydroxyl ion is the initiator) on the substrate surface.

$$n\,\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CN)–COOR} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_2\text{O, base}} \text{Poly(alkyl cyanoacrylate)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Cyanoacrylates are the **fastest-curing commercial adhesives** — fixture in **5–30 seconds**. The two-term "anionic, base-catalyzed" descriptor is mandatory in the KTU answer script.

### 2.3.4 UV-Curable Acrylics
- A **photoinitiator** (e.g., benzophenone, Irgacure 184) absorbs UV photons and cleaves to form radicals.
- Cure depth is governed by the **Beer–Lambert law** — see derivation in §3.4.

### 2.3.5 Pressure-Sensitive Acrylics (PSA)
- Do **not cure** by chemical reaction; they are **permanently tacky** linear or lightly crosslinked polyacrylates (usually BA-co-2EHA-co-AA) coated on a backing and dried.
- Adhesion is governed by **viscoelastic contact** (Dahlquist criterion: $G' < 0.1\,\text{MPa}$ at use temperature).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_p = k_p \left(\dfrac{f\,k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{0.5} [M]$ | Steady-state rate of polymerization | $\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{0.5}}$ | Number-average degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\tau_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_d}$ | Initiator half-life | s |
| 4 | $E_a = 2.303\,R \left[\dfrac{\log(k_2/k_1)}{1/T_1 - 1/T_2}\right]$ | Arrhenius activation energy (two-temperature method) | J/mol |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{\gamma}$ | Viscous shear stress (PSA) | Pa |
| 6 | $G' < 0.1\,\text{MPa}$ | Dahlquist criterion for PSA tack | Pa |
| 7 | $I(x) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\epsilon C x}$ | Beer–Lambert for UV cure depth | W/m² |
| 8 | $T_g = T_{g,1} w_1 + T_{g,2} w_2$ | Fox equation (weight fraction $w$) | K |
| 9 | $T_g(\text{K}) \approx T_g(\text{°C}) + 273.15$ | Absolute conversion | K |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Engineering stress (lap shear) | MPa |
| 11 | $\text{Conversion}\,\% = \dfrac{[\text{M}]_0 - [\text{M}]_t}{[\text{M}]_0} \times 100$ | Monomer-to-polymer conversion | % |
| 12 | $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ | Rate of initiation | $\text{mol L}^{-1}\text{s}^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:** $[M]$ = monomer concentration, $[I]$ = initiator concentration, $f$ = initiator efficiency (typically 0.5–0.8), $k_d, k_i, k_p, k_t$ = rate constants for decomposition, initiation, propagation, and termination, $R_p$ = rate of polymerization, $R_i$ = rate of initiation, $\bar{X}_n$ = number-average degree of polymerization, $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = universal gas constant $= 8.314\,\text{J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Acrylic Adhesives

Acrylic adhesives dominate four major engineering sectors:

1. **Automotive** — hem-flange bonding of car doors, hoods, decklids (replacing spot welds), and structural composite bonding.
2. **Aerospace** — secondary structural bonding of aluminium and composite fairings.
3. **Electronics** — SMD staking, conformal coating, encapsulation, and display lamination.
4. **Medical devices** — disposable device assembly, drug-delivery patches (PSA), and skin adhesives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"oil-tolerant"** or **"untreated-metal-bondable"** nature of acrylics is the single most cited engineering advantage — it eliminates the surface-preparation step required by epoxies, saving **15–30 minutes per part** in production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Steady-State Rate of Polymerization ($R_p$)

The free-radical polymerization rate derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark sub-question** in KTU Module 3. We derive it from first principles.

### Step 1 — Rate of Initiation
A peroxide initiator I homolytically cleaves to give two primary radicals.

$$\text{I} \xrightarrow{k_d} 2\,\text{R}^{\bullet}$$

Because not all radicals successfully initiate a chain, the **effective** rate of initiation is:

$$R_i = 2\,f\,k_d\,[I]$$

> Each molecule of I that decomposes gives 2 radicals; only a fraction $f$ of these go on to actually start a chain.

### Step 2 — Rate of Termination
Two growing chain radicals can terminate by **combination** or **disproportionation**, both with the same second-order rate constant $k_t$:

$$R_t = 2\,k_t\,[R^{\bullet}]^2$$

The factor of 2 follows the convention used in polymer kinetics; some texts drop it as $R_t = k_t [R^{\bullet}]^2$. The form chosen must be consistent throughout — be explicit in the answer script.

### Step 3 — Steady-State Assumption
The rate of change of radical concentration $[R^{\bullet}]$ is set to zero (radicals are produced as fast as they are consumed):

$$\dfrac{d[R^{\bullet}]}{dt} = R_i - R_t = 0$$

Substituting:

$$2\,f\,k_d\,[I] = 2\,k_t\,[R^{\bullet}]^2$$

Solving for $[R^{\bullet}]$:

$$[R^{\bullet}] = \left(\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}\right)^{0.5}$$

### Step 4 — Rate of Propagation
The monomer is consumed primarily by propagation:

$$R_p = -\dfrac{d[M]}{dt} = k_p\,[M]\,[R^{\bullet}]$$

Substitute $[R^{\bullet}]$ from Step 3:

$$R_p = k_p\,[M]\,\left(\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}\right)^{0.5}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{R_p = k_p\,[M]\,\left(\dfrac{f\,k_d\,[I]}{k_t}\right)^{0.5}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important equation** in acrylic adhesive kinetics. Memorize it; it carries **3–5 marks** in any KTU Module 3 14-mark question.

### Step 5 — Number-Average Degree of Polymerization
$\bar{X}_n$ is the rate of chain propagation divided by the rate of chain termination (i.e., how many monomers are added per radical chain on average):

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p\,[M]\,(f k_d [I] / k_t)^{0.5}}{2\,f\,k_d\,[I]}$$

Simplifying:

$$\boxed{\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{k_p\,[M]}{2\,(f\,k_d\,k_t\,[I])^{0.5}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Initiator Half-Life

The first-order decomposition of initiator follows:

$$-[I] = [I]_0\,e^{-k_d t}$$

The half-life is the time at which $[I] = [I]_0/2$:

$$\dfrac{[I]_0}{2} = [I]_0\,e^{-k_d \tau_{1/2}}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(0.5) = -k_d \tau_{1/2}$$

$$\tau_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For BPO, $\tau_{1/2} = 1\,\text{h}$ at **73 °C**. For AIBN, $\tau_{1/2} = 1\,\text{h}$ at **65 °C**. These numbers are commonly quoted in KTU viva voce.

## 3.3 Derivation — Activation Energy by Two-Temperature Method

When cure studies are performed at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ giving rate constants $k_1$ and $k_2$:

$$\ln k_1 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R\,T_1}$$
$$\ln k_2 = \ln A - \dfrac{E_a}{R\,T_2}$$

Subtracting:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

Solving for $E_a$:

$$\boxed{E_a = 2.303\,R\,\dfrac{\log(k_2/k_1)}{\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}}}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Q:** The cure rate constant of an acrylic adhesive is $k_1 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{min}^{-1}$ at $T_1 = 50\,°\text{C}$ and $k_2 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{min}^{-1}$ at $T_2 = 70\,°\text{C}$. Calculate the activation energy.

**Solution:**

Convert to Kelvin: $T_1 = 323.15\,\text{K}$, $T_2 = 343.15\,\text{K}$.

Compute $1/T_1 - 1/T_2$:

$$\dfrac{1}{323.15} - \dfrac{1}{343.15} = 3.094 \times 10^{-3} - 2.914 \times 10^{-3} = 1.80 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

Compute $\log(k_2/k_1)$:

$$\log\!\left(\dfrac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}\right) = \log(5) = 0.6990$$

Substitute:

$$E_a = 2.303 \times 8.314 \times \dfrac{0.6990}{1.80 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$E_a = 19.147 \times 3883.3 \approx 7.44 \times 10^{4}\,\text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 74.4\,\text{kJ/mol}}$$

This is in the expected range for BPO/amine cure systems (**60–100 kJ/mol**). ✓

## 3.4 Derivation — Beer–Lambert Cure Depth (UV Acrylics)

For UV-cured acrylic adhesives, the intensity of UV at depth $x$ inside the adhesive is:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\epsilon C x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity at the surface
- $\epsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹)
- $C$ = photoinitiator concentration
- $x$ = depth

The **practical cure depth** is reached when $I \approx 0.05\,I_0$ (5 % threshold for adequate radical generation):

$$x_{cure} = \dfrac{\log(I_0 / 0.05\,I_0)}{\epsilon\,C} = \dfrac{\log 20}{\epsilon\,C} = \dfrac{1.301}{\epsilon\,C}$$

### Numerical Example

> **Q:** A UV acrylic adhesive contains $C = 0.02\,\text{mol L}^{-1}$ of a photoinitiator with $\epsilon = 350\,\text{L mol}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$. Estimate the practical cure depth.

**Solution:**

$$x_{cure} = \dfrac{1.301}{350 \times 0.02} = \dfrac{1.301}{7.0} = 0.186\,\text{cm}$$

$$\boxed{x_{cure} \approx 1.86\,\text{mm}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sub-Class Comparison

| Property | Anaerobic | Cyanoacrylate | 2-Part MMA | UV Acrylic | PSA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cure time** | 5–30 min | 5–30 s | 5–60 min | 1–30 s | None |
| **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 10–20 | 15–25 | 20–35 | 15–30 | < 1 |
| **Substrates** | Metals | Most | Metals, plastics | Glass, PC | Skin, paper |
| **Gap fill (mm)** | < 0.25 | < 0.20 | up to 5 | < 5 | N/A |
| **Surface prep** | Required | None | Minimal | Required | None |
| **Temperature range (°C)** | –55 to +150 | –55 to +80 | –55 to +150 | –55 to +200 | –40 to +120 |
| **Toxicity** | Low | Low–Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Acrylic Adhesive Classification — Master Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acrylic Adhesives] --> B[Cure by Free-Radical Polymerization]
    B --> C1[Anaerobic Acrylics]
    B --> C2[Cyanoacrylates]
    B --> C3[Two-Part MMA Toughened]
    B --> C4[UV Visible Light Curable]
    B --> C5[Pressure Sensitive Acrylics]
    C1 --> D1[Trigger: Metal ions and O2 exclusion]
    C1 --> E1[Use: Thread-locking, shaft-hub]
    C2 --> D2[Trigger: Surface moisture and anionic]
    C2 --> E2[Use: Super glue, electronics, medical]
    C3 --> D3[Trigger: Redox BPO and amine]
    C3 --> E3[Use: Automotive hem-flange, marine]
    C4 --> D4[Trigger: Photoinitiator and UV photons]
    C4 --> E4[Use: Glass lamination, displays]
    C5 --> D5[Trigger: Viscoelastic contact no cure]
    C5 --> E5[Use: Tapes, labels, medical dressings]
```

## 4.2 Two-Part MMA Acrylic — Sea-Island Morphology Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PartA[PART A RESIN SIDE] --> MMA1[MMA Monomer]
    PartA --> Tough[Elastomer CTBN]
    PartA --> Amine[Amine Accelerator]
    PartB[PART B HARDENER SIDE] --> MMA2[MMA Monomer]
    PartB --> BPO[Benzoyl Peroxide]
    Mix[On Mixing 1 to 1] --> Redox[Redox Generates Radicals]
    Redox --> PolyMMA[Poly MMA Matrix]
    PolyMMA --> Island[Rubber CTBN Domains]
    PolyMMA --> Cross[Crosslinked Network]
    Island --> Cross
```

## 4.3 Cure Cycle Block Architecture — Acrylic Adhesive

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Substrate Untreated Metal or Plastic] --> S1[Apply Primer Optional]
    S1 --> S2[Dispense Adhesive]
    S2 --> S3[Initiator Activated Heat UV Redox]
    S3 --> S4[Induction Period 0.5 to 5 min]
    S4 --> S5[Rapid Polymerization Gel Point]
    S5 --> S6[Vitro Cooling Through Tg]
    S6 --> S7[Full Cure 24 h or UV Dose]
    S7 --> S8[Crosslinked Network Service Ready]
```

## 4.4 Function vs Formulation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Function Needed] --> Q1{Substrate Type}
    Q1 -->|Metal| P1[Use Two-Part MMA or Anaerobic]
    Q1 -->|Plastic| P2[Use Cyanoacrylate or UV Acrylic]
    Q1 -->|Skin| P3[Use Pressure-Sensitive Acrylic]
    Q1 -->|Glass| P4[Use UV Acrylic]
    P1 --> R1[Formulation: MMA plus CTBN plus BPO plus Amine]
    P2 --> R2[Formulation: Ethyl CA plus Stabilizer plus Thickener]
    P3 --> R3[Formulation: 2EHA co BA co AA on Backing]
    P4 --> R4[Formulation: Acrylate Oligomer plus Photoinitiator]
```

## 4.5 Cure Profile — Conversion vs Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Time t=0 Conversion 0 percent] --> A1[Induction Phase 0 to 5 min]
    A1 --> A2[Acceleration Phase 5 to 15 min]
    A2 --> A3[Auto-Acceleration Trommsdorff 15 to 30 min]
    A3 --> A4[Deceleration 30 to 60 min]
    A4 --> A5[Plateau Full Cure Greater than 95 percent]
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **Q1.** Define acrylic adhesives. List any four important acrylic monomers used in adhesive formulation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Acrylic adhesives are a class of reactive adhesives based on the polymerization of acrylic and methacrylic esters (monomers of the type $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{CH–COOR}$ or $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CH}_3\text{)–COOR}$), which cure by **free-radical chain-growth mechanism** to form a crosslinked polyacrylate network. They are widely used in structural bonding of metals, plastics, and composites because they bond to **untreated, oily surfaces** and offer rapid cure at room temperature.
>
> Four important acrylic monomers are:
> 1. **Methyl methacrylate (MMA)** — high $T_g$, hard structural base.
> 2. **Butyl acrylate (BA)** — low $T_g$, flexible PSA base.
> 3. **2-Ethylhexyl acrylate (2-EHA)** — very low $T_g$, primary PSA monomer.
> 4. **Acrylic acid (AA)** — adhesion-promoting co-monomer with reactive –COOH group.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the role of an initiator and an accelerator in a two-part acrylic adhesive system?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> In a two-part (Part A / Part B) acrylic adhesive:
> - The **initiator** (e.g., benzoyl peroxide, BPO) is a thermally labile compound that, when activated, homolytically cleaves to release free radicals that initiate monomer polymerization.
> - The **accelerator** (e.g., a tertiary amine such as N,N-dimethylaniline, or a transition-metal soap such as cobalt naphthenate) **reduces the activation energy** for peroxide decomposition, enabling cure to occur at **room temperature** via a redox mechanism.
> Together, initiator + accelerator form a **redox couple** that generates radicals at ambient conditions, allowing structural bonding without external heat input — the key engineering advantage of two-part acrylics.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
>
> **Q A.** (a) Explain the **free-radical polymerization mechanism** of acrylic adhesives. Derive the steady-state rate of polymerization and the number-average degree of polymerization. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **chemistry, formulation, and engineering applications** of two-part toughened methacrylate (MMA) adhesives, with emphasis on their ability to bond **untreated metal surfaces**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Mechanism (3 marks):** Acrylic adhesives polymerize by free-radical chain-growth, proceeding through three stages:

1. **Initiation:** Peroxide initiator (I) decomposes: $\text{I} \xrightarrow{k_d} 2\,\text{R}^{\bullet}$. Radical adds to monomer: $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{R–M}^{\bullet}$ (rate $k_i$).
2. **Propagation:** $R_n^{\bullet} + M \xrightarrow{k_p} R_{n+1}^{\bullet}$.
3. **Termination:** $R_m^{\bullet} + R_n^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k_t} P_{m+n}$ (combination) or $P_m + P_n$ (disproportionation).

**(ii) Derivation of $R_p$ (3 marks):** See §3.1 above. Apply steady-state assumption: $R_i = R_t$ giving $[R^{\bullet}] = (f k_d [I] / k_t)^{0.5}$. Substitute into $R_p = k_p [M] [R^{\bullet}]$:

$$R_p = k_p [M] \left(\dfrac{f k_d [I]}{k_t}\right)^{0.5}$$

**(iii) Derivation of $\bar{X}_n$ (1 mark):**

$$\bar{X}_n = \dfrac{R_p}{R_i} = \dfrac{k_p [M]}{2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{0.5}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Chemistry (2 marks):** Two-part toughened MMA adhesives use **methyl methacrylate** as the base monomer. Part A contains MMA, a **carboxyl-terminated butadiene-acrylonitrile (CTBN) rubber** toughener, and an **amine accelerator** (e.g., N,N-dimethyl-p-toluidine). Part B contains MMA and **benzoyl peroxide (BPO)**. On mixing 1:1, the amine reduces BPO via a redox reaction at room temperature, generating radicals that polymerize MMA.

**(ii) Sea-island morphology (2 marks):** The CTBN rubber precipitates as **10–500 nm islands** dispersed in the poly-MMA matrix, creating a "sea-island" structure that arrests crack propagation, yielding **fracture toughness $G_{IC} \approx 800–1500\,\text{J/m}^2$** (vs. ~150 J/m² for unmodified poly-MMA).

**(iii) Untreated-metal bonding (2 marks):** The system tolerates **drawing oils, mill oils, and light rust** because (a) the amine accelerator and BPO form a redox couple that does not require clean surfaces, (b) the rubber phase provides compliance to absorb differential thermal expansion, and (c) the methacrylate backbone **chelates with surface metal oxides**. This eliminates the **solvent-wipe + grit-blast + primer** sequence required by epoxies, saving 15–30 min per part in production.

**(iv) Applications (1 mark):** Automotive hem-flange bonding, marine deck-to-hull joints, wind-turbine blade root assembly, and railway carriage skin-to-frame bonding.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
>
> **Q B.** (a) Classify acrylic adhesives based on cure mechanism. Explain the cure mechanism of **cyanoacrylates** and **UV-curable acrylics** with suitable chemical equations. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** What are the **advantages and limitations** of acrylic adhesives compared to epoxy and polyurethane adhesives? Discuss **five major industrial applications** of acrylic adhesives. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**(i) Classification (2 marks):** Acrylic adhesives are classified as: (1) Anaerobic acrylics, (2) Cyanoacrylates, (3) Two-part toughened methacrylates, (4) UV/visible light-curable acrylics, (5) Pressure-sensitive acrylics.

**(ii) Cyanoacrylate cure (3 marks):** Cyanoacrylates (e.g., methyl 2-cyanoacrylate, $\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CN)–COOCH}_3$) polymerize by an **anionic, base-catalyzed mechanism**. The **trace moisture** on substrate surfaces provides hydroxyl ions ($\text{OH}^-$) that initiate polymerization:

$$\text{CH}_2\!=\!\text{C(CN)–COOR} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{^-\text{OOC–CH(CN)–CH}_2\text{–R}}$$

The carbanion then rapidly propagates through the monomer double bond, achieving **fixture strength in 5–30 seconds**. Cure is inhibited by acids (low pH surfaces cure slowly).

**(iii) UV-curable acrylics (2 marks):** UV-curable acrylics contain a **photoinitiator** (e.g., benzophenone or Irgacure 184). Upon absorption of UV photons:

$$\text{PI} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{PI}^{\bullet} \rightarrow R^{\bullet}$$

The generated radical $R^{\bullet}$ initiates polymerization of the acrylate oligomer. Cure depth follows the **Beer–Lambert law**, $I(x) = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\epsilon C x}$. The major advantage is **on-demand, solvent-free, room-temperature cure with spatial control** (only the irradiated region cures).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Advantages vs. epoxy (3 marks):**
- **Room-temperature cure** (epoxies need 60–180 °C for full cure).
- **No surface preparation** required — bonds oily, untreated metals.
- **Higher elongation at break** (~30–100 % vs. 2–6 % for rigid epoxies), giving better impact resistance.
- **Faster fixture time** (5 min vs. 30 min for epoxies).
- **Lower sensitivity to mix ratio** (1:1 ± 20 % is acceptable; epoxies need ± 5 %).

**(ii) Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Flammable monomers** with pungent odour and potential health hazards.
- **Higher cost** per kg than epoxies.
- **Limited high-temperature performance** (≤ 150 °C, vs. 200 °C for high-$T_g$ epoxies).
- **Poor UV stability** in transparent applications (yellowing).
- **Lower solvent and chemical resistance** than crosslinked epoxies.

**(iii) Five major applications (2 marks):**
1. Automotive hem-flange bonding (replacing spot welds).
2. Thread-locking and retaining compounds (anaerobic).
3. "Super glue" consumer instant bonding (cyanoacrylate).
4. UV-cured display and optical-component assembly.
5. Pressure-sensitive tapes and medical skin adhesives.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing up $k_p$ and $k_t$** in the rate equation — examiners deduct **2 marks** flat. Always state which is which.
> 2. **Forgetting the $f$ (efficiency factor)** in $R_i = 2 f k_d [I]$ — losing **1 mark**.
> 3. **Writing "polymerization" without specifying "free-radical"** — a generic statement that scores only 1/3 marks for Part A Q1.
> 4. **Confusing cyanoacrylate "anionic" cure with "free-radical"** cure — these are **two distinct mechanisms** and must not be interchanged.
> 5. **Omitting the "untreated metal" advantage** in any MMA question — this is the **defining engineering feature** and is worth 2 marks in a 14-mark answer.
> 6. **Writing the Beer–Lambert equation as $I = I_0 e^{-\epsilon C x}$** (natural log form) instead of the $10$-base form expected in KTU — both are correct mathematically, but use the **base-10 form** to match the syllabus.
> 7. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in the activation-energy calculation — examiners deduct 1 mark for unit error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Acrylic adhesives = free-radical polymerizing esters of acrylic/methacrylic acid.
- [x] **Five sub-classes:** Anaerobic, Cyanoacrylate, 2-Part MMA, UV, PSA — know **one application** for each.
- [x] **Five monomers to memorize:** MMA, EA, BA, 2-EHA, AA — know structure + $T_g$ trend.
- [x] **Three polymerization stages:** Initiation, Propagation, Termination — name all three rate constants.
- [x] **Master equation:** $R_p = k_p [M] (f k_d [I] / k_t)^{0.5}$ — derive it from scratch in 5 steps.
- [x] **Degree of polymerization:** $\bar{X}_n = R_p / R_i = k_p [M] / [2 (f k_d k_t [I])^{0.5}]$.
- [x] **Half-life:** $\tau_{1/2} = 0.693 / k_d$.
- [x] **Activation energy (two-T method):** $E_a = 2.303 R \log(k_2/k_1) / (1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$.
- [x] **Beer–Lambert cure depth:** $x_{cure} \approx 1.301 / (\epsilon C)$ for UV acrylics.
- [x] **Cyanoacrylate = anionic, base-catalyzed** (NOT free-radical).
- [x] **Anaerobic = inhibited by O₂, accelerated by metal ions.**
- [x] **2-Part MMA = redox BPO + amine, room-temperature cure, sea-island morphology.**
- [x] **PSA = no chemical cure, Dahlquist criterion $G' < 0.1$ MPa.**
- [x] **Distinctive advantage:** bonds untreated, oily metals — no surface prep needed.
- [x] **Service temperature:** –55 to +150 °C; specialty grades up to +200 °C.
- [x] **Tensile shear on steel:** 20–35 MPa for toughened MMA grade.
- [x] **Cure shrinkage:** 3–5 % (slightly higher than epoxies).
- [x] **Formulation components:** monomer (60–90 phr), reactive diluent (5–25), initiator (0.5–3), accelerator (0.1–2), stabilizer (0.01–0.5), adhesion promoter (0.5–3), filler (0–40).
- [x] **Top 5 applications:** automotive hem-flange, thread-locking, super-glue instant bond, UV display assembly, PSA tapes/medical.
- [x] **Limitations:** flammable, pungent, ≤150 °C, UV yellowing, lower chemical resistance than epoxy.
- [x] **Two-part mix ratios:** typically 1:1 or 10:1 by volume.
- [x] **Typical BPO decomposition:** $\tau_{1/2} = 1$ h at 73 °C.
- [x] **Typical AIBN decomposition:** $\tau_{1/2} = 1$ h at 65 °C.
- [x] **Remember to convert all temperatures to Kelvin** in kinetic calculations.
- [x] **Final examiner's mantra:** "Acrylics = oil-tolerant, fast-cure, room-temperature structural bonding via free-radical (or anionic) polymerization."

topics.title: Hot Melt Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hot Melt Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HOT MELT ADHESIVE (HMA) — KTU Board Definition**
> A **Hot Melt Adhesive (HMA)** is defined as a **100% solid, solvent-free, thermoplastic bonding compound** that is applied to a substrate in its **molten (liquid) state** at temperatures typically ranging from **120 °C to 200 °C**, and which develops its ultimate bond strength through **physical solidification (cooling)** rather than through chemical curing, solvent evaporation, or water loss. The bonding mechanism is therefore classified as a **purely physical, reversible thermoplastic fusion process**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, HMAs belong to the family of **"hot-applied, thermally-reversible adhesives"** and are classified under **Category 1 of Non-structural Pressure Sensitive / Physically Setting Adhesives**. Their set-time is purely a function of **heat transfer rate** between the molten adhesive film and the bonded substrates, and their working life is **theoretically infinite** in the solid state (no pot-life limitation as seen in epoxies or polyurethanes).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wax-Seal" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy for B.Tech Students**
> Imagine a **lit candle wax** dripping onto an envelope. While hot, the wax flows and wets the paper. Within seconds of cooling, it hardens into a solid seal that holds the envelope shut. If you later want to remove the seal, you simply **reheat the wax** — it melts again! This is *exactly* how a Hot Melt Adhesive works: it is applied **hot and molten**, it **wets the substrate**, then **solidifies on cooling** to lock the joint. Since the bond is purely physical, **HMAs can be re-melted and re-bonded** (thermoplastic memory) — unlike a thermosetting epoxy which chemically crosslinks and burns instead of melting if reheated.

This **thermoplastic reversibility** is the single most distinguishing feature of an HMA when compared to reactive adhesives (epoxy, PU, phenolic) — and is the reason HMAs dominate **high-speed packaging, bookbinding, and hygiene-product assembly lines** where cycle times of **< 1 second** are mandatory.

## 1.3 Core Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Parameters)

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Board Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Softening Point (Ring & Ball):** **80 °C – 160 °C** (typical HMA working window).
> - **Application Temperature:** **120 °C – 200 °C** (melt viscosity target ~ **1,000 – 20,000 cP**).
> - **Open Time:** **1 – 60 seconds** (window before bond becomes unworkable).
> - **Set Time:** **0.5 – 10 seconds** (time to reach handling strength).
> - **Solid Content:** **100 % w/w** (no VOC, no water).
> - **Heat Resistance:** up to **70 °C – 90 °C** for general-purpose EVA grades.

## 1.4 Schematic Bond-Formation Cycle

The HMA bonding process can be visualised as a **four-stage thermal cycle**:

| Stage | Process | Physical State | Energy Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Melting | Solid → Molten | Heat **IN** |
| **II** | Application & Wetting | Molten (low viscosity) | Heat dissipation begins |
| **III** | Cooling & Solidification | Molten → Solid | Heat **OUT** (to substrate) |
| **IV** | Crystallisation / Vitrification | Solid (crystalline or amorphous glass) | No further change |

---

## 1.5 Distinguishing HMAs from Other Adhesive Classes (KTU Comparative Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-frequency comparison** that KTU examiners routinely use in 3-mark and 14-mark questions:

| Property | HMA | Solvent-Based | Water-Based | Reactive (Epoxy/PU) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Solid content | **100 %** | 20 – 60 % | 40 – 60 % | 100 % |
| Setting mechanism | Cooling | Solvent evap. | Water evap. | Chemical cure |
| Bond reversibility | **Yes (remeltable)** | No | No | No (irreversible) |
| Pot life | Infinite | Limited | Limited | Limited (minutes–hrs) |
| VOC emission | **Zero** | High | Low | Zero |
| Set time | **Seconds** | Minutes–hrs | Minutes–hrs | Minutes–hrs–days |
| Bond strength | Moderate | High | High | **Very High** |

This places HMAs in a unique niche: **fast, clean, reversible, and VOC-free**, but with **lower ultimate strength and heat resistance** than reactive adhesives.
