# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Q: Define the term "Epoxy Equivalent Weight" (EEW) and explain its significance in formulating epoxy adhesives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Epoxy Equivalent Weight (EEW) is defined as the **mass of epoxy resin (in grams) that contains one mole of epoxide (oxirane) groups**. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$ EEW = \frac{M_n}{n_{epoxy}} $$

where $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight and $n_{epoxy}$ is the average number of epoxide groups per molecule.

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. **Stoichiometric calculation:** EEW is essential for determining the correct **resin-to-hardener ratio**. The corresponding hardener equivalent weight (e.g., AHEW for amines) must be used in a 1:1 equivalent ratio to achieve complete network formation.
2. **Quality control:** Variations in EEW between batches indicate changes in molecular weight distribution, affecting viscosity, cure behavior, and final adhesive properties.
3. **Practical usage:** Typical DGEBA-based resins have EEW in the range of **170–2000 g/eq**; lower EEW = higher crosslink density after cure.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**Q: Differentiate between "pot life" and "gel time" of a two-part epoxy adhesive. Why is the distinction important in practical bonding applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Pot Life (1 mark):** Pot life is the **time interval during which a mixed two-part adhesive remains workable** (i.e., low enough viscosity for application, typically <10,000 cP) after mixing resin and hardener. It represents the *practical* working time at room temperature.

**Gel Time (1 mark):** Gel time is the precise instant when the **mixed adhesive transitions from a viscous liquid to an elastic gel** through incipient network formation (crosslink density reaches the percolation threshold). Beyond gel point, flow ceases and the system is no longer workable.

**Distinction & Importance (1 mark):**
- Pot life is **always shorter than gel time** because the system becomes unworkable due to viscosity rise *before* true gelation.
- Importance: In structural bonding (e.g., aircraft wing assembly), the operator must apply the entire adhesive film **well within the pot life** but the bond will continue to develop strength even after gelation. Misjudging this window can produce a defective joint that appears set but has poor crosslink conversion.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**Q (a)** Describe the **synthesis of Diglycidyl Ether of Bisphenol-A (DGEBA)** from Bisphenol-A and epichlorohydrin with a balanced chemical equation. Explain the role of NaOH in the synthesis. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **mechanism of epoxy ring opening by a primary amine** hardener. Explain why tertiary amines cannot act as stoichiometric hardeners but serve as accelerators. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution Q (a): DGEBA Synthesis

**Step 1: Reaction Scheme (3 marks)**

The synthesis proceeds in **two stages**:

**Stage 1 — Etherification (addition of epichlorohydrin to BPA):**

Bisphenol-A reacts with epichlorohydrin in a 1:2 molar ratio under acid or base catalysis at **60–80 °C**, forming a chlorohydrin intermediate:

$$ \text{HO-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-OH} + 2 \text{ClCH}_2\text{-CHO-CH}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{BTMAAC catalyst}} \text{ClCH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{-O-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-C(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{-C}_6\text{H}_4\text{-O-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-CH}_2\text{Cl} $$

**Stage 2 — Dehydrohalogenation (epoxide ring formation with NaOH):**

NaOH abstracts HCl from each chlorohydrin end, closing the epoxide ring:

$$ \text{Chlorohydrin intermediate} + 2 \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{DGEBA} + 2 \text{NaCl} + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Overall balanced equation (2 marks):**

$$ \text{BPA} + 2 \text{ECH} + 2 \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{DGEBA} + 2 \text{NaCl} + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

$$ \text{C}_{15}\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{C}_3\text{H}_5\text{ClO} + 2\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_{21}\text{H}_{24}\text{O}_4 + 2\text{NaCl} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**Step 2: Role of NaOH (2 marks)**

NaOH serves **two simultaneous roles**:
1. **HCl scavenger / deprotonating agent** — drives the equilibrium toward epoxide ring closure
2. **Epoxide ring formation** — the OH⁻ ion performs an intramolecular SN2 attack on the chlorinated carbon, displacing Cl⁻ and closing the three-membered oxirane ring

Without NaOH, the chlorohydrin intermediate is stable and the epoxide ring will not form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial practice:** The synthesis uses an **excess of epichlorohydrin (typically 10:1 molar ratio to BPA)** to drive the reaction to the difunctional product and minimize formation of higher-molecular-weight oligomers. The reaction is typically carried out at 60–80 °C under atmospheric pressure, and the resulting DGEBA is washed thoroughly with water to remove NaCl byproduct.

---

#### Model Solution Q (b): Amine Curing Mechanism

**Step 1: Mechanism of Primary Amine Ring Opening (4 marks)**

The reaction proceeds via **nucleophilic SN2 attack** of the amine nitrogen lone pair on the **less-hindered carbon** of the oxirane ring:

**Initiation — Primary amine opens first epoxide:**

The lone pair on the primary amine nitrogen attacks the terminal CH₂ of the oxirane, breaking the C–O bond and forming a new C–N bond. Simultaneously, the oxygen picks up a proton (from the amine or from another proton donor) to form a hydroxyl group:

$$ \text{R-NH}_2 + \underset{\underset{\text{O}}{\diagup\diagdown}}{\text{CH}_2-\text{CHR}'} \longrightarrow \text{R-NH-CH}_2-\underset{\text{OH}}{\overset{\text{}}{\text{CH}}}-\text{R}' $$

**Propagation — Secondary amine opens second epoxide:**

The newly formed secondary amine can attack another epoxide group on a different DGEBA chain:

$$ \text{R-NH-CH}_2\text{CH(OH)R}' + \underset{\text{O}}{\overset{\diagup\diagdown}{\text{CH}_2-\text{CHR}''}} \longrightarrow \text{R-N(CH}_2\text{CHR}'\text{OH})\text{-CH}_2\text{-CH(OH)-R}'' $$

**Catalytic effect of generated –OH groups:**

The hydroxyl groups generated during the ring opening **catalyze the further reaction** by activating additional epoxide rings through hydrogen bonding. This autocatalytic behavior is responsible for the **Kamal-Sourour cure kinetics** observed in DSC studies.

**Step 2: Why Tertiary Amines Cannot Be Stoichiometric Hardeners (2 marks)**

Tertiary amines (R₃N) have **no N–H bond** and therefore **cannot donate a hydrogen to the epoxide oxygen** during ring opening. The reaction would be:

$$ \text{R}_3\text{N} + \text{epoxide} \longrightarrow \text{R}_3\text{N}^+ \text{-CH}_2\text{-CH(O}^-\text{)-R}' $$

This produces a **zwitterionic alkoxide**, not a stable crosslink. Without proton transfer to form a stable hydroxyl group, no permanent network forms.

**Step 3: Tertiary Amines as Accelerators (1 mark)**

However, tertiary amines (e.g., **2,4,6-tris(dimethylaminomethyl)phenol — DMP-30** or **benzyldimethylamine — BDMA**) are excellent **anionic polymerization accelerators** for epoxy resins. They initiate anionic ring-opening homopolymerization, especially in conjunction with anhydride hardeners. The tertiary amine abstracts a proton from traces of hydroxyl/water and generates an alkoxide that propagates the ring opening.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**

**Q (a)** List and briefly explain **four different types of hardeners** used for curing epoxy adhesives, citing one specific example for each and its typical application area. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** A DGEBA epoxy resin with **EEW = 185 g/eq** is to be cured with **Jeffamine D-230** (a polyetheramine, MW ≈ 230 g/mol, functionality = 2, active H = 4). Calculate (i) the AHEW, (ii) the phr of Jeffamine required, and (iii) the stoichiometric mix ratio by weight for a 500 g batch. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution Q (a): Four Hardener Types

**1. Aliphatic Polyamines (1.5 marks)**
- **Examples:** DETA, TETA, IPDA
- **Mechanism:** Primary/secondary amine N–H opens oxirane ring
- **Cure conditions:** Room temperature to 80 °C
- **Applications:** General-purpose structural adhesives, civil engineering crack repair, marine bonding
- **Properties:** Short pot life, moderate Tg, good chemical resistance

**2. Aromatic Amines (1.5 marks)**
- **Examples:** 4,4'-Methylenedianiline (MDA), 4,4'-Diaminodiphenylsulfone (DDS)
- **Mechanism:** Aromatic N–H opens epoxide at elevated temperature
- **Cure conditions:** 150–200 °C, 2–6 hours
- **Applications:** Aerospace primary structure, carbon-fiber-reinforced composites, high-Tg laminates
- **Properties:** Excellent Tg (160–250 °C), superior chemical resistance, low exotherm

**3. Anhydrides (1.5 marks)**
- **Examples:** Methyltetrahydrophthalic anhydride (MTHPA), Nadic methyl anhydride (NMA)
- **Mechanism:** Esterification + ring opening (requires accelerator)
- **Cure conditions:** 80–200 °C with tertiary amine accelerator (e.g., BDMA at 0.5–2 phr)
- **Applications:** Electrical/electronic encapsulation, filament winding, casting
- **Properties:** Long pot life, low exotherm, excellent electrical insulation, low shrinkage

**4. Latent Curing Agents (1.5 marks)**
- **Examples:** Dicyandiamide (DICY), boron trifluoride-amine complexes (BF₃·MEA)
- **Mechanism:** Dormant at room temperature; activated by heat (>120 °C for DICY; >80 °C for BF₃)
- **Cure conditions:** 150–180 °C for DICY
- **Applications:** One-part pre-preg adhesives, automotive hem-flange bonding, structural film adhesives
- **Properties:** Excellent shelf life (12–24 months), clean processing, fiber-reinforced prepreg

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus fifth category:** Polythiols (mercaptans) — used in rapid-curing flexible adhesives; Polyamides — used in surface-tolerance formulations.

---

#### Model Solution Q (b): Stoichiometric Calculation

**Given:**
- EEW (DGEBA) = 185 g/eq
- MW (Jeffamine D-230) = 230 g/mol
- Functionality (f) = 2 (difunctional primary amine)
- Active hydrogens (n_H) = 2 × 2 = **4** (each NH₂ has 2 active H's)

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Jeffamine D-230:** Although f = 2 amine groups, EACH primary amine contributes **2 active hydrogens**. So $n_H = 2 \times 2 = 4$ total active H's per molecule.

**Step (i): Calculate AHEW (2 marks)**

$$ AHEW = \frac{MW}{n_H} = \frac{230 \text{ g/mol}}{4 \text{ eq/mol}} $$

$$ \boxed{AHEW = 57.5 \text{ g/eq}} $$

**Step (ii): Calculate phr of Jeffamine (2 marks)**

$$ \text{phr} = \frac{AHEW}{EEW} \times 100 = \frac{57.5}{185} \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{phr} = 31.08 \text{ parts Jeffamine per 100 parts DGEBA}} $$

**Step (iii): Calculate mix ratio for 500 g batch (3 marks)**

In a 500 g total batch, the DGEBA is 100 parts out of (100 + 31.08) = 131.08 parts.

$$ \text{Mass of DGEBA} = \frac{100}{131.08} \times 500 \text{ g} = 381.4 \text{ g} $$

$$ \text{Mass of Jeffamine D-230} = \frac{31.08}{131.08} \times 500 \text{ g} = 118.6 \text{ g} $$

**Verification:** 381.4 + 118.6 = 500.0 g ✓

$$ \text{Mix ratio by weight} = \frac{381.4}{118.6} = 3.216 : 1 \text{ (resin : hardener)} $$

$$ \boxed{\text{For 500 g batch: 381.4 g DGEBA + 118.6 g Jeffamine D-230}} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A **very common error** is to assume functionality = active hydrogens. For a difunctional primary amine (e.g., Jeffamine D-230, DETA), the **active H count is 2 × number of primary amine groups = 4**. Mistaking f=2 for n_H=2 would yield an AHEW of 115 g/eq and an incorrect phr of 62.2 — twice the correct value. This results in massive over-catalysis, exothermic runaway, and brittle, under-crosslinked cured adhesive. Examiners award 0 marks if this distinction is missed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Epoxides (Module 3):**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Epoxide = 3-membered cyclic ether (oxirane)** with C–O–C angle of 60° (vs. ideal 109.5°)
- [x] **Ring strain = ~27.5 kcal/mol** is the driving force for curing reactivity
- [x] **DGEBA** = most common epoxy resin; EEW = 170–2000 g/eq depending on n value
- [x] **Curing = ring opening** by nucleophile (amine, thiol, anhydride, hydroxyl)
- [x] **Primary amine** = bifunctional crosslinker (each H opens one epoxide)
- [x] **Tertiary amine** = accelerator only, NOT a hardener (no N–H)
- [x] **Anhydride** = requires tertiary amine accelerator; used in electrical applications
- [x] **DICY** = latent hardener for one-part heat-cured adhesives (shuttle life >12 months)

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- [x] $EEW = M_n / n_{epoxy}$
- [x] $AHEW = M_{amine} / n_H$
- [x] $\text{phr} = (AHEW / EEW) \times 100$
- [x] $E_r = 3 \rho_x RT$ (rubbery modulus above Tg)
- [x] Flory-Rehner equation (for crosslink density from swelling)

### Numerical Benchmarks (For Comparison Answers)
- [x] Lap shear strength (structural epoxy): **15–40 MPa**
- [x] Tg (cured DGEBA + amine): **80–180 °C**
- [x] Cure shrinkage (epoxy): **<2%** (lowest among thermosets)
- [x] Pot life (DGEBA + DETA): **15–30 min at 25 °C**
- [x] Activation energy for cure: **50–80 kJ/mol** typical
- [x] Bondline thickness optimum: **0.1–0.5 mm** for structural bonds

### Common Exam Traps
- [x] Don't confuse **functionality (f)** with **active hydrogens (n_H)**
- [x] Off-stoichiometric mixing → defective network (brittle or flexible but weak)
- [x] Always mention **surface preparation** when asked about adhesive joint strength
- [x] Epoxies are **brittle neat** — toughening is REQUIRED for structural impact applications
- [x] Higher Tg does NOT always mean better adhesive — depends on service temperature

### High-Yield Keywords for Written Answers
- Ring strain, oxirane, DGEBA, EEW, AHEW, phr, stoichiometric ratio, crosslink density, Kamal-Sourour kinetics, latent hardener, toughened epoxy, CTE, Tg, gel point, vitrification, pot life, lap shear, peel strength, cohesive vs. adhesive failure, surface preparation, substrate wetting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Study Tip:** When asked in KTU exams to "discuss epoxy adhesives" or "describe the chemistry of epoxides," always structure your answer as: **(1) Definition + Structure, (2) Synthesis of DGEBA, (3) Curing mechanism, (4) Hardener types, (5) Properties, (6) Applications**. This 6-part framework covers the typical 14-mark question and earns full marks by demonstrating comprehensive understanding of Module 3 outcomes.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — METHODS OF ADHESIVE APPLICATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Adhesive application* is the controlled deposition of a liquid, paste, film, or molten polymeric bonding agent onto a substrate surface using a defined mechanical or pneumatic technique, such that the deposited mass, geometry, and uniformity match the design requirements of the bonded joint. The **method of application** determines wet film thickness (WFT), surface coverage, adhesive penetration into porous substrates, and ultimately the joint strength.

In simple words, "application" is the bridge between *formulating* an adhesive and *using* it. The best adhesive in the world will fail if it is laid down in the wrong thickness, in the wrong pattern, or with air bubbles trapped inside.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Painter's Toolkit**
> Imagine you want to paint a wall. Would you use a toothbrush? A roller? A spray gun? Of course not — you pick the tool that matches the surface area, the viscosity of the paint, and the finish you need. Adhesive application follows exactly the same logic. A thin cyanoacrylate needs a needle-tip dispenser; a thick epoxy paste needs a trowel; a contact adhesive on a large laminate needs a roller; and a hot melt on a packaging line needs a slot-die extruder. The **substrate geometry**, the **adhesive rheology (viscosity)**, and the **production volume** jointly dictate the correct method.

### 1.1 Why Application Method Matters in Joint Design

The application step controls four critical engineering parameters:

1. **Wet Film Thickness (WFT)** — the thickness of the adhesive layer as deposited, *before* any solvent loss or curing.
2. **Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** — the final thickness after solvent evaporation or chemical reaction.
3. **Surface Coverage (g/m² or µm)** — the mass of adhesive deposited per unit area of substrate.
4. **Wetting & Contact Angle (θ)** — the ability of the adhesive to spread and penetrate surface micro-roughness.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why does the application method influence bond strength?"* The textbook answer is: because it controls **WFT → DFT → cohesive/adhesive failure mode**, and **wetting efficiency → contact angle → actual bonded area**.

### 1.2 Master Classification of Adhesive Application Methods

The methods are classified along three axes: **mechanism of deposition**, **adhesive physical state**, and **production scale**.

| Axis | Categories |
|---|---|
| Mechanism | Contact (brush/roller), Projection (spray/extrusion), Pre-formed (film/tape) |
| Physical State | Liquid, Paste, Molten (hot melt), Solid (film) |
| Scale | Manual (lab/prototype), Semi-automatic, Fully-automatic (robotic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3 Focus:**
> The module emphasizes the following canonical methods: **Brush/Roller/Spatula**, **Spray (air-spray, airless, electrostatic)**, **Hot-melt extrusion/slot-die**, **Film/tape lamination**, **Dip-coating**, **Screen printing**, **Needle dispensing**, and **Roll-to-roll coating**. Each is treated below with engineering depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Method 1 — Manual Application: Brush, Roller, Spatula, Trowel

**Operating Principle:**
The adhesive is forced onto the substrate by mechanical contact with a fibrous, porous, or smooth applicator. The applicator shears the adhesive and lays it down as a film whose thickness depends on the adhesive viscosity, the surface tension of the substrate, and the pressure applied by the operator.

**Typical Adhesives Used:**
- Epoxy pastes
- Polyurethane contact adhesives
- Solvent-based rubber cements
- Construction-grade tile adhesives

**Engineering Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Governing Factor |
|---|---|---|
| WFT achievable | **100 µm – 3 mm** | Viscosity of adhesive |
| Coverage uniformity | ±15 % | Operator skill |
| Suitable viscosity | 1,000 – 50,000 mPa·s | Brush bristle density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Manual methods are *low-throughput* and *operator-dependent*. They are recommended for prototype work, repair, field assembly, and small-batch production. They are *not* preferred for high-volume KTU industrial case studies.

## 2.2 Method 2 — Spray Application (Air-Spray, Airless, Electrostatic)

**Operating Principle:**
The liquid adhesive is atomized into fine droplets (typically **5 µm – 200 µm** in diameter) and projected onto the substrate. Atomization is achieved either by a high-velocity air stream (air-spray) or by forcing the fluid through a small orifice under high pressure (airless). In **electrostatic spray**, the droplets are electrically charged so that they are attracted to a grounded substrate, dramatically reducing overspray.

**Spray Categories (KTU Exam Favourite):**

1. **Conventional Air-Spray** — uses compressed air (typically **2 – 4 bar**) to atomize; produces fine finish but ~30–50 % overspray loss.
2. **Airless Spray** — fluid pressure (**80 – 250 bar**) without air; high transfer efficiency (~70 %), suitable for high-viscosity adhesives.
3. **HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure)** — low air pressure, high volume; >65 % transfer efficiency; meets environmental regulations.
4. **Electrostatic Spray** — charged droplets (typically **30 – 100 kV**); >85 % transfer efficiency; ideal for conductive substrates.

**Critical Process Variables:**

- **Atomization pressure (P)** — higher pressure → smaller droplet size → thinner WFT.
- **Fluid viscosity (η)** — typically **50 – 500 mPa·s** for spray; higher requires heated lines.
- **Nozzle orifice diameter (d)** — controls flow rate.
- **Gun-to-substrate distance (D)** — typically **15 – 30 cm**; too close causes runs, too far causes dry-spray.

## 2.3 Method 3 — Hot-Melt Extrusion / Slot-Die Coating

**Operating Principle:**
A thermoplastic adhesive (EVA, polyamide, PUR) is melted in a heated reservoir (**120 – 200 °C**) and pushed by a screw or piston extruder through a shaped die. For wide webs, a **slot-die** is used: the molten adhesive exits as a uniform curtain across the web width, giving exceptionally uniform WFT.

**Engineering Parameters:**

- **Melt viscosity (η_melt):** typically **5,000 – 50,000 mPa·s** at application temperature.
- **Line speed (v):** **1 – 600 m/min** in packaging lines.
- **WFT uniformity:** ±2 % — far superior to spray.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:**
> Hot-melt slot-die coating is the standard for **bookbinding**, **diaper construction**, **labels**, and **automotive interior trim**. The advantage is **zero solvent** (100 % solids adhesive), so there is no drying oven — line speed is limited only by cooling time.

## 2.4 Method 4 — Roller Coating & Roll-to-Roll Lamination

**Operating Principle:**
The adhesive is metered onto a rotating roller, which transfers a controlled film onto the moving substrate. Variants include:

- **Direct (forward) roll coater** — both rolls rotate in the direction of substrate travel.
- **Reverse roll coater** — applicator roll rotates opposite to web; gives finer control at low WFT.
- **Gravure roll coater** — engraved cells meter precise adhesive volume; used for **5 – 50 µm WFT** precision coatings.

**Roll-to-Roll Lamination:** Two webs are nipped together between heated rolls with adhesive in between; common in **flexible packaging**.

## 2.5 Method 5 — Film & Tape Application (Pre-Formed Adhesives)

**Operating Principle:**
The adhesive is supplied as a **B-stage film** (partially cured) or a **pressure-sensitive tape**. The film is placed between the two adherends, then heat and pressure are applied to cause flow and cure.

**Advantages:** zero solvent emission, precise thickness, no pot-life issues, automation-friendly.

## 2.6 Method 6 — Dip Coating & Screen Printing

- **Dip Coating:** The substrate is immersed in the adhesive bath and withdrawn at a controlled speed. WFT is governed by the **Landau–Levich equation** (see formula sheet).
- **Screen Printing:** Adhesive is forced through a mesh onto defined areas. Used in **PCB assembly** and **gasket fabrication**.

## 2.7 Method 7 — Needle / Syringe Dispensing (Micro-Application)

Used for **cyanoacrylates**, UV-curable adhesives, and **micro-electronics**. Volumetric precision **±0.5 %**. Driven by pneumatic, mechanical, or piezoelectric actuators.

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in every Part B question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DFT = WFT \times \dfrac{\rho_{wet}}{\rho_{dry}} \times \dfrac{\% \, solids}{100}$ | WFT → DFT conversion | µm |
| 2 | $Coverage = \dfrac{m}{A}$ — mass of adhesive per unit area | m = mass, A = area | g/m² |
| 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \eta \, L}$ — Poiseuille flow through nozzle | r = orifice radius, ΔP = pressure drop, η = viscosity, L = orifice length | m³/s |
| 4 | $h_{0} = 0.94 \, \dfrac{(\eta \, U)^{2/3}}{\rho \, g}$ — Landau–Levich dip-coating | U = withdrawal speed, ρ = density, g = gravity | m |
| 5 | $W_{spray} = C_{d} \, A_{n} \, \sqrt{2 \, \Delta P / \rho}$ | C_d = discharge coefficient (~0.6), A_n = nozzle area | kg/s |
| 6 | $\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ — Young's equation (wetting) | γ = surface energies, θ = contact angle | J/m² |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{v}{h}$ — shear rate in roll nip | v = web speed, h = nip gap | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $t_{cure} = f(T, \text{activator})$ — time to green strength | T = temperature | s |

**Boundary Conditions & Validity:**

- **Poiseuille's law** assumes **laminar flow**, **Newtonian fluid**, and **steady state**.
- **Landau–Levich** assumes **low capillary number (Ca < 0.1)**.
- **Spray discharge coefficient** is empirical; use **0.6** as default KTU exam value.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Wet-to-Dry Film Thickness Conversion

This is the single most-tested calculation in adhesive application. Students who lose marks here lose them because they confuse *solids by mass* with *solids by volume*.

**Problem (KTU-Style):**
A polyurethane adhesive is spray-applied at a **WFT of 250 µm**. The adhesive contains **45 % solids by mass**, and the densities are $\rho_{wet} = 1.05 \, g/cm^3$ and $\rho_{dry} = 1.20 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate the **DFT** after full solvent evaporation.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

We begin with the mass balance: the mass of *solids* in the wet film must equal the mass of the dry film after the solvent has evaporated.

$$
m_{solids, wet} = m_{dry}
$$

Expanding both sides in terms of volume, area, thickness, and density:

$$
A \cdot WFT \cdot \rho_{wet} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\% \, solids}{100}\right) = A \cdot DFT \cdot \rho_{dry}
$$

The area $A$ cancels from both sides, leaving:

$$
WFT \cdot \rho_{wet} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\% \, solids}{100}\right) = DFT \cdot \rho_{dry}
$$

Solving for $DFT$:

$$
DFT = \dfrac{WFT \cdot \rho_{wet} \cdot (\% \, solids / 100)}{\rho_{dry}}
$$

Now substitute the numerical values $WFT = 250 \, \mu m$, $\rho_{wet} = 1.05 \, g/cm^3$, $\rho_{dry} = 1.20 \, g/cm^3$, and $\% \, solids = 45$.

$$
DFT = \dfrac{250 \, \mu m \times 1.05 \, g/cm^3 \times 0.45}{1.20 \, g/cm^3}
$$

Carrying out the arithmetic in stages:

$$
250 \times 1.05 = 262.5
$$

$$
262.5 \times 0.45 = 118.125
$$

$$
118.125 \div 1.20 = 98.4375
$$

$$
\boxed{DFT \approx 98.4 \, \mu m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** A 250 µm wet film that is 45 % solids and slightly denser when dry should yield a DFT in the range of 80–110 µm. Our answer of 98.4 µm lies squarely in this range — the calculation is dimensionally and physically consistent.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Flow Rate Through a Spray Nozzle (Poiseuille)

**Problem:**
An airless spray nozzle has an orifice of **radius r = 0.25 mm** and **length L = 5 mm**. The pressure differential is **ΔP = 100 bar**, and the adhesive viscosity is **η = 200 mPa·s**. Find the volumetric flow rate.

**Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI base units.**

$$
r = 0.25 \, mm = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \, m
$$

$$
L = 5 \, mm = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, m
$$

$$
\Delta P = 100 \, bar = 100 \times 10^{5} \, Pa = 1.0 \times 10^{7} \, Pa
$$

$$
\eta = 200 \, mPa \cdot s = 0.200 \, Pa \cdot s
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Poiseuille's equation.**

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \eta \, L}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $r^{4}$.**

$$
r^{4} = (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^{4} = 3.90625 \times 10^{-15} \, m^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
\pi \times r^{4} \times \Delta P = \pi \times 3.90625 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
= \pi \times 3.90625 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\approx 1.2272 \times 10^{-7}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
8 \times \eta \times L = 8 \times 0.200 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 8.0 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 6 — Divide.**

$$
Q = \dfrac{1.2272 \times 10^{-7}}{8.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.534 \times 10^{-5} \, m^{3}/s
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to a more practical unit.**

$$
Q \approx 15.3 \, cm^{3}/s \approx 920 \, cm^{3}/min
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A nozzle of this size can deliver roughly **1 litre per minute** at the given pressure. In a real production line, this is a *high-flow* configuration suitable for wide-web spray at line speeds of **30–50 m/min**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix in Part B answers whenever the question says *"Suggest a suitable application method for ___".***

| Substrate Geometry | Adhesive State | Production Volume | Recommended Method | DFT Achievable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flat, large panel | Liquid, low-viscosity | High | **Roller / curtain coating** | 20 – 200 µm |
| Flat, large panel | Hot melt | Very high | **Slot-die extrusion** | 30 – 500 µm |
| Complex 3-D shape | Liquid | Medium | **Airless / HVLP spray** | 25 – 300 µm |
| Small electronic part | Cyanoacrylate / UV | Low | **Needle dispenser** | 10 – 100 µm |
| Continuous web (film) | Pre-formed B-stage | High | **Film / tape lamination** | 25 – 200 µm |
| Defined 2-D pattern | Paste | Medium | **Screen printing** | 50 – 500 µm |
| Pipe / small fitting | Low-viscosity liquid | Low | **Dip coating** | 5 – 50 µm |
| Construction joint | Epoxy paste | Field | **Trowel / spatula** | 1 – 5 mm |

**Selection Logic (the "Why" behind the matrix):**

- **Viscosity governs method choice.** A 5,000 mPa·s paste cannot be sprayed without dilution; it must be rolled, trowelled, or extruded.
- **Production volume governs automation.** Brushes are fine for one-off joints; high-volume lines demand slot-die or gravure coaters.
- **Geometry governs deposition pattern.** A 3-D moulded part can only be coated by spray or dip; a flat panel can use any method.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Adhesive Application Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Adhesive Reservoir] --> B[Pumping Stage]
    B --> C[Pressure Regulation Block]
    C --> D[Heating Block for Hot Melt]
    C --> E[Filtration Stage]
    E --> F[Dispensing Head Selection]
    F --> F1[Brush or Roller Manual]
    F --> F2[Airless Spray Nozzle]
    F --> F3[Slot Die Coater]
    F --> F4[Needle Dispenser]
    F1 --> G[Substrate Preparation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Wet Film Lay Down]
    H --> I[Joining or Curing Station]
    I --> J[Final Bonded Assembly]
    H --> K[Quality Control Sensors]
    K --> L[Feedback to Pressure Regulator]
    L --> C
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Reservoir → Pump → Regulator** chain is common to *every* liquid-adhesive system.
- The **Heating Block** is engaged only for hot-melt systems; it raises the adhesive to **120–200 °C** to drop viscosity from solid-like to flowable.
- The **Filtration Stage** removes particulates that would clog small orifices (especially in needle dispensers).
- The **Dispensing Head** is a *selection node* — the operator chooses one of four branches based on the application method required.
- The **Feedback Loop (K → L → C)** represents modern closed-loop control: a thickness sensor (laser or beta-gauge) at the laydown point corrects the pressure regulator in real time, holding WFT within ±2 %.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Application Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Bonded Joint Design] --> B{Adhesive Viscosity?}
    B -->|Less than 500 mPa.s| C{Precision Required?}
    B -->|500 to 50000 mPa.s| D{Substrate Shape?}
    B -->|Greater than 50000 mPa.s| E{High Volume?}
    C -->|Yes Micro dot| F1[Needle Dispenser]
    C -->|No Wide area| F2[Air Spray or HVLP]
    D -->|Flat panel| F3[Roller or Curtain Coater]
    D -->|3D complex shape| F4[Airless Spray]
    E -->|Yes Production line| F5[Hot Melt Slot Die]
    E -->|No Field repair| F6[Spatula or Trowel]
    F1 --> G[Verify WFT via DFT check]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    G --> H{Joint Strength OK?}
    H -->|Yes| Z[Approved Process]
    H -->|No| Y[Adjust WFT or Method]
    Y --> B
```

**Reading the Decision Tree:**

- The **first branch** is viscosity — the single most decisive physical property.
- **Precision** (micro-dot dispensing) and **wide-area coverage** (spray) are mutually exclusive for low-viscosity adhesives — different head geometries.
- For **complex 3-D shapes**, airless spray is preferred because the high-pressure jet reaches recessed areas that a roller cannot touch.
- The **WFT/DFT verification step** is a regulatory requirement under ISO 17025 and is *mandatory* for aerospace and medical-device bonding.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Cue:** When asked *"Draw a flowchart for method selection,"* reproduce a simplified version of this decision tree. Always show the *feedback loop* (rejection branch returning to the viscosity check) — examiners award marks for showing iterative process control.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between air-spray and airless spray methods of adhesive application. List any two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Air-Spray | Airless Spray |
|---|---|---|
| Atomization mechanism | Compressed air at 2–4 bar | Hydraulic pressure at 80–250 bar |
| Droplet size | 5–50 µm (finer) | 50–200 µm (coarser) |
| Overspray loss | 30–50 % | 10–30 % |
| Viscosity range | 50–200 mPa·s | Up to 500 mPa·s |
| Finish quality | Excellent | Good |

**Air-Spray advantages:** (i) superior atomization for fine finishes, (ii) suitable for low-viscosity solvent-based adhesives.
**Airless Spray advantages:** (i) handles higher-viscosity adhesives without thinning, (ii) higher transfer efficiency reduces VOC emission.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**State the significance of Wet Film Thickness (WFT) in adhesive bonding.**

**Model Answer (3 key points):**

1. **Defines adhesive volume per unit area** — WFT directly determines the mass of adhesive available to fill surface roughness and form the load-bearing bondline.
2. **Controls failure mode** — too low a WFT causes *adhesive failure* (interfacial); too high a WFT causes *cohesive failure within the adhesive layer* and may starve the joint of clamp pressure.
3. **Governs DFT after cure** — through the relation $DFT = WFT \times (\rho_{wet}/\rho_{dry}) \times (\%solids/100)$, which is critical for maintaining design gap tolerances.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

### ▶ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with neat sketches the **roller coating** method of adhesive application. Explain the role of the metering roll and the applicator roll in controlling WFT.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A contact adhesive is roll-coated at a WFT of **180 µm**. The solids content is **30 % by mass** and the densities are $\rho_{wet} = 0.92 \, g/cm^3$, $\rho_{dry} = 1.05 \, g/cm^3$. Calculate (i) the DFT, and (ii) the coverage in **g/m²** if the substrate width is **1.2 m** and the line speed is **25 m/min**.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Roller Coating Description:**

Roller coating uses two or more precision-ground cylindrical rolls rotating in contact with each other and the substrate.

- **Metering Roll:** A smooth, hardened-steel roll that runs *against* the applicator roll at a controlled gap. Its function is to *scrape off* excess adhesive, leaving a precisely metered film on the applicator roll.
- **Applicator Roll:** The rubber-covered roll that *transfers* the metered film onto the moving substrate. Shore-A hardness typically **60–80**.
- **Backup Roll:** In *reverse-roll* coaters, a third roll rotates opposite to the substrate direction; this provides finer control at very low WFTs.

The nip gap between applicator and substrate determines WFT; a typical range is **20–300 µm**. Speed ratios between rolls control the lay-down pattern.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks awarded for:** naming all three rolls, identifying the metering function, and stating at least one numerical range.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Calculation of DFT:**

Using the equation derived in Section 3.1:

$$
DFT = \dfrac{WFT \cdot \rho_{wet} \cdot (\%solids/100)}{\rho_{dry}}
$$

Substituting $WFT = 180 \, \mu m$, $\rho_{wet} = 0.92 \, g/cm^3$, $\rho_{dry} = 1.05 \, g/cm^3$, and $\%solids = 30$:

$$
DFT = \dfrac{180 \times 0.92 \times 0.30}{1.05}
$$

Computing the numerator:

$$
180 \times 0.92 = 165.6
$$

$$
165.6 \times 0.30 = 49.68
$$

Dividing by the denominator:

$$
DFT = \dfrac{49.68}{1.05} \approx 47.3 \, \mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{DFT \approx 47.3 \, \mu m}
$$

**(ii) Calculation of Coverage (g/m²):**

Coverage is mass per unit area of the *wet* film. The wet adhesive density is **0.92 g/cm³ = 920 kg/m³** (or 0.92 g/cm³).

$$
Coverage = WFT \times \rho_{wet}
$$

Converting WFT to metres: $180 \, \mu m = 180 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \, m$.

$$
Coverage = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \, m \times 920 \, kg/m^{3} = 0.1656 \, kg/m^{2}
$$

Converting to g/m²:

$$
\boxed{Coverage \approx 165.6 \, g/m^{2}}
$$

**Bonus verification:** Mass flow rate = Coverage × width × speed.

$$
\dot{m} = 0.1656 \, kg/m^{2} \times 1.2 \, m \times 25 \, m/min
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 4.97 \, kg/min
$$

This is a sensible value for a mid-scale industrial line.

---

### ▶ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **hot-melt slot-die coating** method. Explain why this method is preferred over spray coating for **flexible packaging laminations**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of Poiseuille's equation, calculate the flow rate of an adhesive through an airless spray nozzle. Given: orifice radius $r = 0.4 \, mm$, length $L = 8 \, mm$, $\Delta P = 80 \, bar$, viscosity $\eta = 350 \, mPa \cdot s$. Comment on whether the flow remains laminar.

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Hot-Melt Slot-Die Coating:**

In slot-die coating, molten adhesive is pumped by a precision gear pump through a slotted die, exiting as a uniform curtain across the web width.

**Why preferred over spray for flexible packaging:**

1. **Zero solvent emissions** — the adhesive is **100 % solids** (e.g., EVA, polyamide, PUR); no VOC release, no drying oven, lower carbon footprint.
2. **Superior WFT uniformity** — slot-die achieves ±2 % uniformity, vs ±15 % for spray; critical for barrier laminates in food packaging.
3. **High line speeds** — slot-die lines run at **200–600 m/min**, far above typical spray speeds.
4. **No overspray loss** — spray losses of 20–50 % are eliminated, lowering adhesive cost per square metre.
5. **Tight control of coat weight** — gear-pump rpm × web speed sets the coat weight to within ±0.5 g/m².

**Process steps:** Solid pellets → hopper → heated melt tank (**120–200 °C**) → gear pump → filter → slot die → coating onto moving web → chill roll → nip lamination.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks awarded for:** listing at least three of the five advantages, mentioning the absence of drying oven, and quoting at least one numerical range.

**(b) Poiseuille Calculation:**

Convert to SI units:

$$
r = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \, m, \quad L = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \, m
$$

$$
\Delta P = 80 \times 10^{5} \, Pa = 8.0 \times 10^{6} \, Pa
$$

$$
\eta = 350 \times 10^{-3} \, Pa \cdot s = 0.350 \, Pa \cdot s
$$

Apply Poiseuille's equation:

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \eta \, L}
$$

Compute $r^{4}$:

$$
r^{4} = (4.0 \times 10^{-4})^{4} = 2.56 \times 10^{-14} \, m^{4}
$$

Numerator:

$$
\pi \times r^{4} \times \Delta P = \pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-14} \times 8.0 \times 10^{6} = \pi \times 2.048 \times 10^{-7} \approx 6.434 \times 10^{-7}
$$

Denominator:

$$
8 \times \eta \times L = 8 \times 0.350 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.24 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Divide:

$$
Q = \dfrac{6.434 \times 10^{-7}}{2.24 \times 10^{-2}} = 2.87 \times 10^{-5} \, m^{3}/s \approx 28.7 \, cm^{3}/s
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 1.72 \, L/min}
$$

**Laminarity Check — Reynolds Number:**

$$
Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\eta}
$$

with $\rho \approx 1000 \, kg/m^{3}$ and $D = 2r = 0.8 \, mm = 8 \times 10^{-4} \, m$.

Velocity $v$ from $Q = A \cdot v$, where $A = \pi r^2 = 5.03 \times 10^{-7} \, m^{2}$:

$$
v = \dfrac{2.87 \times 10^{-5}}{5.03 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 57.1 \, m/s
$$

Then:

$$
Re = \dfrac{1000 \times 57.1 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}}{0.350} \approx 130.5
$$

Since $Re \ll 2300$, **flow is firmly laminar**, validating the use of Poiseuille's equation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing %solids by mass with %solids by volume.** The KTU textbook convention is **%solids by mass**. If the question does not specify, default to mass — but state your assumption explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions in Poiseuille problems.** Bar must be converted to Pascal, mm to m, mPa·s to Pa·s. A wrong unit in *one* line cascades into a wrong answer.
> 3. **Missing the Reynolds-number check.** Examiners award 1–2 marks for verifying the laminar assumption. Omitting this is a free-mark loss.
> 4. **Writing "spray" without specifying which type.** Always distinguish **air-spray, airless, HVLP, electrostatic**.
> 5. **Ignoring the feedback loop in process diagrams.** Modern closed-loop control is part of the 2024 syllabus; a flowchart without feedback looks dated.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **WFT → DFT conversion** is governed by solids content and density ratio: $DFT = WFT \cdot (\rho_{wet}/\rho_{dry}) \cdot (\%solids/100)$.
- **Coverage** in g/m² = WFT (in m) × wet density (in kg/m³) × 1000.
- **Method selection** is driven by **viscosity → substrate geometry → production volume**, in that order.
- **Spray methods** — distinguish *air*, *airless*, *HVLP*, and *electrostatic*; each has a characteristic pressure range and overspray percentage.
- **Roller coating** — the metering roll controls WFT by gap-setting; the applicator roll transfers film to substrate.
- **Slot-die hot-melt** is the gold standard for **100 % solids, high-speed, low-emission** coating.
- **Poiseuille's equation** $Q = \pi r^4 \Delta P / (8\eta L)$ applies only to **laminar, Newtonian, steady-state** flow — verify with $Re < 2300$.
- **Young's equation** $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ links application method to wetting efficiency.
- **Closed-loop WFT control** (sensor → feedback → regulator) is the modern industrial norm.
- **Always state the assumption** about %solids basis and density reference temperature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesives in the Electronics Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adhesives in the Electronics Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Electronics-grade adhesives** are highly specialised polymeric bonding systems (predominantly epoxies, silicones, polyurethanes, acrylates, and bismaleimides) formulated with functional fillers to perform a defined electrical, thermal, mechanical, or protective function within microelectronic, optoelectronic, and printed-circuit assemblies. They are engineered to meet stringent purity, cure profile, and reliability requirements dictated by semiconductor packaging standards (e.g., **JEDEC**, **IPC-SM-817**, **MIL-STD-883**).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic is mapped to the understanding of how an adhesive is *not merely a glue* but acts as a **functional material** — it can conduct current, dissipate heat, absorb stress, or block electromagnetic radiation, all while bonding two substrates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to distinguish between **Isotropic Conductive Adhesives (ICA)**, **Anisotropic Conductive Adhesives (ACA / ACF)**, **Non-Conductive Adhesives (NCA)**, and their specific assembly contexts (die-attach, flip-chip, SMT, underfill, encapsulation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Smart Sponge"**
> Imagine a kitchen sponge that you soak with a special fluid.
> • If the fluid contains tiny **copper wires going every direction**, the sponge conducts electricity in *all directions* — this is an **Isotropic Conductive Adhesive (ICA)**, like a sponge full of randomly oriented copper threads.
> • If the fluid contains **wires only stacked vertically**, the sponge conducts electricity *only when squeezed from top to bottom* — this is an **Anisotropic Conductive Adhesive (ACA)**, where conduction happens in one direction (Z-axis) only when pressure is applied during bonding.
> • If the sponge has **no wires at all but is sticky**, it just holds components in place and relies on *physical contact* for conduction — this is a **Non-Conductive Adhesive (NCA)**.
> In the same way, electronics adhesives are polymer "sponges" loaded with metallic, ceramic, or carbon fillers whose **type, shape, loading, and orientation** decide whether the bond carries current, carries heat, or just holds two chips together.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The key metrics that every KTU exam answer on this topic should mention are:

- **Volume Resistivity (ρᵥ)** for conductive grades: target typically **< 10⁻⁴ Ω·cm** for ICA, **< 10⁻⁶ Ω·cm** for sintered nano-silver.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)**: target **1 – 30 W/m·K** depending on filler (Ag, AlN, BN).
- **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g)**: typically **> 125 °C** for lead-free reflow compatibility.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)**: ideally **< 30 ppm/°C** below T_g, matched to silicon (**≈ 2.6 ppm/°C**) or copper (**≈ 17 ppm/°C**).
- **Ionic Purity (Na⁺, Cl⁻)**: **< 10 ppm** to prevent corrosion and electrochemical migration.
- **Cure Temperature**: **80 – 200 °C** (snap-cure epoxies) or **< 150 °C** for temperature-sensitive flex circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values above are standard industry benchmarks. They should be cited verbatim in KTU Part A and Part B answers to demonstrate syllabus-aligned awareness.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electronics Adhesives

The hierarchy is best remembered in **three layers**:

### Layer A — By Function
1. **Structural bonding adhesives** – hold components mechanically.
2. **Conductive adhesives** – provide electrical pathways.
3. **Thermal management adhesives** – dissipate heat from die to heat-sink.
4. **Encapsulants / Potting compounds** – protect against moisture, ions, mechanical shock.
5. **Underfills** – reinforce solder joints in flip-chip / BGA packages.

### Layer B — By Conduction Mechanism
| Type | Full Form | Conduction Direction | Filler Loading | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ICA** | Isotropic Conductive Adhesive | X, Y, Z (all axes) | 25 – 35 vol% Ag flakes | Die-attach, chip bonding |
| **ACA / ACF** | Anisotropic Conductive Adhesive / Film | Z-axis only | 5 – 10 vol% Ni/Au-coated polymer spheres or Ag balls | Flip-chip on flex, COG, FOG (LCD, OLED) |
| **NCA** | Non-Conductive Adhesive | None (rely on contact) | < 2 vol% or none | Flip-chip where bumps touch directly |
| **ECAs** | Electrically Conductive Adhesives (umbrella term) | — | — | Generic term covering ICA & ACA |

### Layer C — By Polymer Matrix
- **Epoxy-based** (most common — DGEBA, novolac, biphenyl) — high T_g, low CTE, excellent chemical resistance.
- **Silicone-based** (PDMS, silicone elastomers) — high flexibility, wide temp range (-55 °C to +200 °C), used for stress-buffer and potting.
- **Acrylate / Cyanate ester** — fast UV/heat cure, used in display lamination.
- **Polyurethane / Bismaleimide (BMI)** — high-temperature structural aerospace electronics.
- **Polyimide / PBO** — ultra-high reliability, used in wafer-level packaging.

## 2.2 Why Each Sub-Class Exists — The Engineering "Why"

- **ICA → Die Attach**: Chips (silicon dice) must be electrically and thermally connected to the lead-frame or substrate. The adhesive is loaded with silver flakes that touch each other to form a 3-D percolation network.
- **ACA → Flip-Chip on Flex / Display**: Connections must be ultra-fine pitch (< 50 µm). Random filler in all directions would cause short circuits, so conductive particles are placed in a *low concentration* such that they only contact in the Z-axis under bonding pressure.
- **NCA → Cost-effective flip-chip**: The polymer captures the bumps and holds them against the pads; conduction happens through direct metal-metal contact.
- **Underfill → Reliability**: Solder joints in flip-chip BGA undergo thermomechanical fatigue due to **CTE mismatch** between silicon (~2.6 ppm/°C) and PCB (~17 ppm/°C). Underfill redistributes the stress, increasing thermal-cycling life by 10×–100×.

## 2.3 Curing Mechanisms in Electronics Adhesives

1. **Thermal cure** (most common): 150 °C / 60 – 90 min, or 175 °C / 30 min. Latent hardeners (dicyandiamide, anhydrides) provide one-component stability.
2. **UV / Visible-light cure** (acrylates, epoxies): For reel-to-reel flex circuits and display lamination. Initiated by photoinitiators (e.g., **Irgacure 651**, **BAPO**).
3. **Dual-cure (UV + heat)**: For shadowed regions where UV cannot penetrate.
4. **Moisture-cure silicones**: For potting — atmospheric humidity triggers crosslinking.
5. **Snap-cure epoxies**: Cure in 30 – 60 s at 200 °C for high-throughput SMT lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The choice of cure dictates *production line speed*. A modern smartphone SMT line places and cures ~50,000 components per hour; therefore **snap-cure** and **UV/heat dual-cure** adhesives dominate high-volume manufacturing.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{R \cdot A}{L}$ | Volume resistivity from measured R, A, L | Ω·cm |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{\rho_v \cdot L}{A}$ | Resistance of conductive bond-line | Ω |
| 3 | $k_{eff} = k_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2\phi \cdot \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)}}{1 - \phi \cdot \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)}}$ | **Lewis-Nielsen model** for effective thermal conductivity of filled adhesive | W/m·K |
| 4 | $\sigma = \sigma_0 (\phi - \phi_c)^t$ | **Percolation equation** — conductivity appears only above critical filler loading φ_c | S/cm |
| 5 | $\sigma_c \approx 0.16$ vol (3-D) | Percolation threshold for randomly packed spheres | dimensionless |
| 6 | $CTE_{eff} = \alpha_m (1-\phi) + \alpha_f \phi$ (rule of mixtures) | Effective CTE of filled adhesive | ppm/°C |
| 7 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L_0 \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion of bond-line | m |
| 8 | $E_{eff} = E_m \cdot \dfrac{(E_f + 2E_m) + 2\phi(E_f - E_m)}{(E_f + 2E_m) - \phi(E_f - E_m)}$ | **Kerner model** for modulus of filled composite | GPa |
| 9 | $N_f = A \cdot (\Delta T)^{-n}$ | **Coffin-Manson** fatigue-life for soldered/underfilled joints | cycles |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Rheological viscosity under shear — controls dispensing, stencil-printing | Pa·s |
| 11 | $T_g$ shift: $\Delta T_g \approx k_f \cdot \phi$ | Filler raises T_g of adhesive | °C |
| 12 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{t}{k \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance of bond-line | K/W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In your answer sheets, **never use the vertical bar `|` inside table cells** for absolute values — KTU digital answer evaluators may misinterpret markdown. Use \vert or \mid in LaTeX form. The formulas above are written as inline LaTeX `$ ... $` to ensure they survive rendering.

## 2.5 Filler Engineering — The Heart of Electronics Adhesives

| Filler | Shape | Function | Typical Loading |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Silver flakes** (1 – 10 µm) | Flake | Electrical conduction (ICA) | 25 – 35 wt% |
| **Silver nano-particles** (10 – 100 nm) | Sphere | Sintering, low-temp cure | 60 – 90 wt% (paste) |
| **Ni / Au-coated polymer spheres** (3 – 5 µm) | Sphere | Anisotropic conduction (ACA) | 5 – 10 vol% |
| **Carbon black / CNT / Graphene** | Fibrous / platelet | EMI shielding, antistatic | 1 – 5 wt% |
| **Boron Nitride (BN), AlN, Al₂O₃** | Platelet / sphere | Thermal conduction, electrical insulation | 30 – 60 vol% |
| **Silica (fused / spherical)** | Sphere | CTE reduction, thixotropy | 30 – 70 wt% |

The **percolation threshold (φ_c)** is the minimum filler fraction at which a continuous conductive pathway forms. Below φ_c, the adhesive is an insulator; above φ_c, conductivity rises sharply by 8–10 decades within a few vol% change. This is the **single most important concept** behind the design of ICAs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Percolation-Based Conductivity of an ICA

The conductivity of a metal-filled polymer composite is governed by percolation theory. The derivation below is exactly what a KTU Part B (14-mark) question will test.

> **Problem Setup:** A silver-flake filled epoxy is used as a die-attach adhesive. The volume fraction of silver is φ, the critical percolation threshold is φ_c, and the conductivity of pure silver is σ_Ag. Derive the conductivity of the composite above φ_c.

### Step 1 — Define the Percolation Scaling Law

According to the **scaling theory of percolation** (Kirkpatrick, 1973), the conductivity of a composite near the percolation threshold follows a power law:

$$
\sigma = \sigma_0 \cdot (\phi - \phi_c)^t
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = conductivity of the filled composite (S/cm)
- $\sigma_0$ = a prefactor (≈ conductivity of the metallic filler)
- $\phi$ = actual filler volume fraction
- $\phi_c$ = critical volume fraction (percolation threshold)
- $t$ = critical exponent (≈ 1.65 in 3-D, ≈ 2.0 in 2-D)

### Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions

At $\phi = 1$ (pure silver), the conductivity must equal that of bulk silver:
$$
\sigma(1) = \sigma_0 (1 - \phi_c)^t = \sigma_{Ag}
$$

Solving for σ₀:
$$
\sigma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_{Ag}}{(1 - \phi_c)^t}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back to Obtain the Working Equation

$$
\sigma = \sigma_{Ag} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\phi - \phi_c}{1 - \phi_c} \right)^t
$$

For practical ICA formulations, $\phi_c \approx 0.16$ (volume fraction) for randomly packed spherical silver particles. The exponent $t$ is taken as **1.65** for a 3-D continuum.

### Step 4 — Numerical Illustration

Take a die-attach ICA with **30 vol% silver flakes** ($\phi = 0.30$), $\phi_c = 0.20$ (typical for flakes, lower than spheres because flakes percolate more easily), and $t = 1.65$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \sigma_{Ag} \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.30 - 0.20}{1.00 - 0.20} \right)^{1.65} \\
&= \sigma_{Ag} \cdot (0.10 / 0.80)^{1.65} \\
&= \sigma_{Ag} \cdot (0.125)^{1.65} \\
&\approx \sigma_{Ag} \cdot 0.036
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma_{Ag} \approx 6.3 \times 10^5 \text{ S/cm}$, the composite conductivity is:
$$
\sigma \approx 0.036 \times 6.3 \times 10^5 \approx 2.27 \times 10^4 \text{ S/cm}
$$

This corresponds to a **volume resistivity of**:
$$
\rho_v = \dfrac{1}{\sigma} = \dfrac{1}{2.27 \times 10^4} \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Ω·cm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** This value (≈ 44 µΩ·cm) is right inside the **< 100 µΩ·cm** target for commercial ICAs, confirming the formulation is acceptable. The derivation shows *why* loading silver flakes above 25 vol% is non-negotiable for die-attach use.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Thermal Conductivity (Lewis-Nielsen Model)

The thermal conductivity of a particle-filled adhesive is critical for **thermal interface materials (TIMs)** and **heat-sink attach**.

### Step 1 — State the Lewis-Nielsen Equation

For spherical filler particles in a continuous polymer matrix:
$$
k_{eff} = k_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2\phi \cdot \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)}}{1 - \phi \cdot \psi \cdot \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)}}
$$

where:
- $k_m$ = thermal conductivity of matrix (epoxy ≈ 0.2 W/m·K)
- $k_f$ = thermal conductivity of filler (AlN ≈ 180 W/m·K, BN ≈ 30 W/m·K, Ag ≈ 429 W/m·K)
- $\phi$ = volume fraction of filler
- $\psi$ = factor accounting for maximum packing (≈ 0.605 for random spheres, 0.907 for fibres)

### Step 2 — Numerical Illustration: AlN-filled Epoxy

Take $k_m = 0.20$ W/m·K, $k_f = 180$ W/m·K (aluminium nitride), $\phi = 0.50$, $\psi = 0.605$.

First, compute the bracket terms:
$$
A = \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)} = \dfrac{180 - 0.2}{180 + 0.4} = \dfrac{179.8}{180.4} \approx 0.9967
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator: } 1 + 2\phi A &= 1 + 2(0.50)(0.9967) = 1 + 0.9967 = 1.9967 \\
\text{Denominator: } 1 - \phi \psi A &= 1 - (0.50)(0.605)(0.9967) = 1 - 0.3015 = 0.6985
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $k_{eff}$

$$
k_{eff} = 0.20 \cdot \dfrac{1.9967}{0.6985} = 0.20 \cdot 2.858 \approx 0.572 \text{ W/m·K}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 50 vol% AlN-filled epoxy (typical TIM formulation) achieves ~0.57 W/m·K — a **3× improvement** over neat epoxy. For higher conductivity (5 – 10 W/m·K), one must use **hybrid fillers** (e.g., Ag-coated BN, or sintered Ag nano-paste reaching > 200 W/m·K).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application → Adhesive Type → Key Property

| Electronic Application | Adhesive Type | Filler / Matrix | Critical Property | Reason for Choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Die-attach (power ICs)** | ICA (Ag-epoxy) | 25 – 35 wt% Ag flakes in epoxy | Low ρᵥ, high k | Electrical + thermal path from die to leadframe |
| **Die-attach (high power, SiC, GaN)** | Sintered nano-Ag paste | 60 – 90 wt% Ag nano-particles | k > 150 W/m·K, T_g > 300 °C | Survives 200 °C junction temps |
| **Flip-chip on flex (ACF)** | Anisotropic Conductive Film | Ni/Au-coated polymer spheres in epoxy | Z-axis contact only | Fine pitch (< 50 µm), no short-circuit risk |
| **Flip-chip (NCA)** | Non-Conductive Paste | Unfilled epoxy | Low α, fast cure | Low cost; conduction via direct bump contact |
| **SMT component bonding** | Snap-cure epoxy / acrylate | Filled epoxy | High green strength | Holds components during reflow |
| **BGA / CGA underfill** | Capillary underfill epoxy | Silica-filled epoxy | CTE 25 – 35 ppm/°C, T_g > 125 °C | Stress redistribution; fatigue life ↑ |
| **Heat-sink attach** | Thermal interface adhesive (TIA) | AlN/BN-filled silicone | High k (1 – 5 W/m·K) | Heat transfer from die to heat-sink |
| **Potting / encapsulation** | Silicone or epoxy | Unfilled or lightly filled | Low modulus, moisture barrier | Mechanical + environmental protection |
| **Display lamination (LCD/OLED)** | UV-curable acrylate / OCA | Acrylic, pressure-sensitive | Optical clarity (T > 90%), low haze | Bonds touch-panel to display |
| **EMI/RFI shielding** | Conductive silicone | Ag/Cu/Ni-filled silicone | ρᵥ < 0.01 Ω·cm, flexible | Gasket + electrical continuity |
| **Wafer bonding (3-D stacking)** | BCB, polyimide | Spin-on dielectric | Planarization, low α | TSV insulation, 3-D IC integration |
| **Photovoltaic module assembly** | EVA encapsulant (ethylene-vinyl acetate) | Crosslinked EVA | T_g ≈ -30 °C, UV stability | Encapsulates Si cells in module |

## 3.4 Derivation: Thermal Resistance of a Bond-Line

For a die-attach layer of thickness $t$, area $A$, and thermal conductivity $k$:

$$
R_{th} = \dfrac{t}{k \cdot A}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **the** equation every power-electronics engineer uses to estimate junction-to-case thermal resistance. Halving the bond-line thickness halves the resistance — but bond-lines are typically **25 – 50 µm** to avoid bond-line read-through (BLRT) defects. Thus, increasing $k$ is the primary engineering lever.

## 3.5 Stress in Bonded Assemblies — CTE Mismatch

The shear stress at the die-attach interface due to CTE mismatch between silicon (α_Si ≈ 2.6 ppm/°C) and substrate (α_sub) during a thermal excursion ΔT is approximated by:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{E_b \cdot (\alpha_{sub} - \alpha_{Si}) \cdot \Delta T \cdot d}{2 t_b (1 - \nu)}
$$

where:
- $E_b$ = Young's modulus of bond-line
- $d$ = diagonal of die
- $t_b$ = bond-line thickness
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic for KTU answer:** This equation shows *why* low-modulus, low-CTE adhesives are preferred for large die-attach (e.g., IGBT modules). CTE-matched substrates (e.g., direct-bond-copper with α ≈ 7 ppm/°C) drastically reduce τ.
