# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper PEPOT861]**
**Q: Differentiate between Isotropic Conductive Adhesives (ICA) and Anisotropic Conductive Adhesives (ACA). Mention one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **ICA (Isotropic)** | **ACA (Anisotropic)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction direction** | All three axes (X, Y, Z) | Only Z-axis (through-thickness) |
| **Filler loading** | 25 – 35 vol% (above percolation threshold) | 5 – 10 vol% (below percolation threshold) |
| **Filler type** | Ag flakes, Ag particles | Ni/Au-coated polymer spheres |
| **Short-circuit risk** | Yes, if bridged | No, because lateral paths are broken |
| **Pitch capability** | Coarse (> 200 µm) | Fine (< 50 µm) |
| **Typical application** | Die-attach, chip bonding | Flip-chip on flex, COG displays |

**Application one-liners:**
- ICA → Silver-filled epoxy used to bond power transistors to leadframes, providing both electrical contact and thermal path.
- ACA → Anisotropic Conductive Film used in LCD panel bonding (chip-on-glass) where ITO electrodes are spaced < 30 µm apart.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Q: What is underfill? Why is it used in flip-chip BGA packages?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Underfill is a silica-filled, low-CTE epoxy resin dispensed by capillary action into the gap between a flip-chip die and its substrate (PCB or interposer), then thermally cured to form a solid polymer reinforcing layer.

**Why used — two reasons (2 marks):**
1. **CTE mismatch compensation:** Solder joints between silicon die (CTE ≈ 2.6 ppm/°C) and PCB (CTE ≈ 17 ppm/°C) experience enormous thermomechanical shear strain during on/off power cycles, leading to solder fatigue and cracking. The underfill redistributes this stress from the brittle solder joints to the much larger epoxy bulk, increasing thermal-cycling life by 10× – 100×.
2. **Mechanical reinforcement:** Underfill protects the joints against drop, vibration, and mechanical shock — crucial for mobile and automotive electronics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For "adhesives in electronics" questions, the **most common mark-loss zones** are:
> 1. Confusing *volume resistivity* with *surface resistivity* — always specify volume.
> 2. Forgetting to mention **filler percolation** in any ICA-related answer (carries 2 – 3 marks).
> 3. Writing "ACF is conductive in all directions" — this is a fatal conceptual error costing 4 – 5 marks.
> 4. Missing the **CTE-mismatch mitigation** argument in underfill / die-attach questions.
> 5. Not giving any *numerical* property values (T_g, CTE, ρᵥ) — examiners explicitly reward quantification with 1 – 2 bonus marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3 Comprehensive]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Classify the adhesives used in the electronics industry based on function and conduction mechanism. With a neat sketch, explain the conduction mechanism in Anisotropic Conductive Adhesives (ACA).

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the percolation-based conductivity equation for a metal-filled conductive adhesive. An isotropic conductive adhesive contains 30 vol% silver flakes. The conductivity of bulk silver is 6.3 × 10⁵ S/cm and the critical percolation threshold is 20 vol% with a critical exponent of 1.65. Calculate the conductivity of the composite and its volume resistivity.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Classification (4 marks):**
1. **By function** — structural bonding, electrical conduction, thermal conduction, encapsulation/potting, underfill reinforcement.
2. **By conduction mechanism**:
   - **ICA (Isotropic Conductive Adhesive):** conducts in all directions; filler loading above percolation threshold (≈ 25 – 35 vol% Ag); used in die-attach and chip-to-substrate bonding.
   - **ACA (Anisotropic Conductive Adhesive):** conducts only in Z-axis; loading below percolation threshold (5 – 10 vol% Ni/Au-coated polymer spheres); used in fine-pitch flip-chip on flex and chip-on-glass displays.
   - **NCA (Non-Conductive Adhesive):** no conductive filler; relies on direct metal-to-metal contact of solder bumps; used in cost-sensitive flip-chip.
   - **Thermal adhesives:** loaded with AlN, BN, Al₂O₃ for heat dissipation; used as thermal interface materials (TIM) and heat-sink attach.

**Mechanism of ACA conduction — sketch explanation (3 marks):**
- The adhesive matrix contains **a low volume fraction of conductive spheres** (Ni/Au-coated polymer particles, 3 – 5 µm diameter).
- In the **un-bonded state**, these particles are *isolated* from each other by the insulating polymer — no conduction path exists in any direction.
- During **thermo-compression bonding** (180 °C, 80 – 120 MPa, 5 – 20 s), the chip bumps press against the substrate pads through the adhesive film.
- The conductive particles get **trapped and deformed between the bump and the pad**, creating a **Z-axis (vertical) electrical path**.
- In the **X and Y directions**, particles remain separated by polymer, so **no short-circuit** occurs even at pitches as fine as 10 – 20 µm.
- After heat curing, the polymer locks the particles in place, providing a permanent mechanical, electrical, and environmental bond.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The conductivity of a metal-filled polymer above the percolation threshold follows a power-law:

**Step 1:** Percolation scaling equation:
$$
\sigma = \sigma_0 \cdot (\phi - \phi_c)^t
$$

**Step 2:** Boundary condition at $\phi = 1$ (pure metal): $\sigma(1) = \sigma_{Ag}$:
$$
\sigma_0 = \dfrac{\sigma_{Ag}}{(1 - \phi_c)^t}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:
$$
\sigma = \sigma_{Ag} \cdot \left( \dfrac{\phi - \phi_c}{1 - \phi_c} \right)^t
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
Given: $\phi = 0.30$, $\phi_c = 0.20$, $t = 1.65$, $\sigma_{Ag} = 6.3 \times 10^5$ S/cm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= 6.3 \times 10^5 \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.30 - 0.20}{1.00 - 0.20} \right)^{1.65} \\
&= 6.3 \times 10^5 \cdot (0.10 / 0.80)^{1.65} \\
&= 6.3 \times 10^5 \cdot (0.125)^{1.65} \\
&\approx 6.3 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.036 \\
&\approx 2.27 \times 10^4 \text{ S/cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Volume resistivity:
$$
\rho_v = \dfrac{1}{\sigma} = \dfrac{1}{2.27 \times 10^4} \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Ω·cm} = 44 \text{ µΩ·cm}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** This 44 µΩ·cm value is well within the **commercial ICA target of < 100 µΩ·cm**, confirming the formulation is suitable for die-attach use.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Comprehensive — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the role of adhesives in the following electronic packaging applications: (i) Die-attach, (ii) Underfill in flip-chip BGA, (iii) Heat-sink attach. Mention the type of adhesive and the key required property for each.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the Lewis-Nielsen model for predicting the effective thermal conductivity of a particle-filled adhesive. An AlN-filled epoxy has 50 vol% AlN in epoxy. Take $k_{AlN} = 180$ W/m·K, $k_{epoxy} = 0.20$ W/m·K, $\psi = 0.605$. Calculate the effective thermal conductivity.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

| Application | Adhesive Type | Key Required Property | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Die-attach** | Isotropic Conductive Adhesive (ICA — Ag-filled epoxy) | Low volume resistivity (< 100 µΩ·cm) + High thermal conductivity (2 – 10 W/m·K) | The bond must carry current from die to leadframe AND conduct heat away from the junction. Ag flakes form a 3-D percolation network. |
| **(ii) Underfill in flip-chip BGA** | Capillary-flow silica-filled epoxy (75 – 90 wt% silica) | Low CTE (25 – 35 ppm/°C) + High T_g (> 125 °C) + Low ionic impurity | The underfill redistributes CTE-mismatch stress from silicon (2.6 ppm/°C) to PCB (17 ppm/°C), extending solder-joint fatigue life 10× – 100×. |
| **(iii) Heat-sink attach** | Thermal Interface Adhesive (TIA) — silicone or epoxy filled with AlN, BN, or Ag | High thermal conductivity (1 – 10 W/m·K) + Low thermal resistance bond-line | Replaces messy thermal grease; mechanical bond + heat path in one step. Critical for high-power LEDs, IGBTs, CPUs. |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** In all three applications, the adhesive is no longer a passive glue — it is a **functional material** that simultaneously fulfils mechanical, thermal, and sometimes electrical roles. The choice of filler (Ag for conduction, AlN/BN for insulation + heat, silica for CTE control) defines the application.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Theoretical Discussion (4 marks):**

The **Lewis-Nielsen model** (1973) predicts the effective thermal conductivity of a particle-filled composite, accounting for:
- The conductivity of matrix ($k_m$) and filler ($k_f$).
- The volume fraction of filler ($\phi$).
- The maximum packing fraction of the filler ($\psi$), which depends on particle shape (0.605 for random spheres, 0.907 for aligned fibres).

The model is derived from a **modified Maxwell effective-medium theory** with a correction factor $\psi$ that accounts for the fact that filler particles cannot exceed the random close-packing limit. It is widely used in the design of thermal interface materials (TIMs) and conductive adhesives.

**Lewis-Nielsen Equation:**
$$
k_{eff} = k_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2\phi \cdot \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)}}{1 - \phi \cdot \psi \cdot \dfrac{(k_f - k_m)}{(k_f + 2k_m)}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $k_m = 0.20$ W/m·K, $k_f = 180$ W/m·K, $\phi = 0.50$, $\psi = 0.605$.

**Step 1:** Compute the bracket term:
$$
A = \dfrac{k_f - k_m}{k_f + 2k_m} = \dfrac{180 - 0.20}{180 + 0.40} = \dfrac{179.8}{180.4} = 0.9967
$$

**Step 2:** Compute numerator and denominator:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 1 + 2 \phi A = 1 + 2(0.50)(0.9967) = 1 + 0.9967 = 1.9967 \\
\text{Den} &= 1 - \phi \psi A = 1 - (0.50)(0.605)(0.9967) = 1 - 0.3015 = 0.6985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $k_{eff}$:
$$
k_{eff} = 0.20 \cdot \dfrac{1.9967}{0.6985} = 0.20 \cdot 2.858 \approx 0.572 \text{ W/m·K}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** A 50 vol% AlN-filled epoxy achieves an effective thermal conductivity of **0.572 W/m·K**, a **~3× improvement** over neat epoxy (0.20 W/m·K). This is suitable for moderate-power LED heat-sink attach; for high-power applications (e.g., EV inverter IGBTs), sintered nano-silver pastes (> 200 W/m·K) are used instead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision list — memorize these for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ☐ **Electronics adhesives are *functional* materials** — they conduct current, conduct heat, block EMI, or redistribute stress, in addition to bonding.
- ☐ **Three conduction classes** — ICA (3-D, Ag flakes, 25 – 35 vol%), ACA (Z-axis only, Ni/Au spheres, 5 – 10 vol%), NCA (no filler, relies on direct bump contact).
- ☐ **Percolation threshold** — the minimum filler loading to form a continuous conductive network. Below it → insulator; above it → conductor. Power-law: $\sigma = \sigma_0 (\phi - \phi_c)^t$, with $t \approx 1.65$ in 3-D.
- ☐ **Die-attach uses ICA** — must provide both electrical and thermal conduction; typical formulation is Ag-flake filled epoxy, $\rho_v < 100$ µΩ·cm, $k \approx 2$ – $10$ W/m·K.
- ☐ **ACA is used for fine pitch (< 50 µm)** — chip-on-glass, flip-chip on flex, LCD/OLED bonding; conduction only under bonding pressure.
- ☐ **Underfill redistributes CTE-mismatch stress** between silicon (2.6 ppm/°C) and PCB (17 ppm/°C); increases thermal-cycling life by **10× – 100×**; typically silica-filled epoxy (75 – 90 wt% silica).
- ☐ **Thermal interface adhesives (TIA)** replace thermal grease + mechanical clip; loaded with AlN, BN, or Ag for $k$ up to ~10 W/m·K.
- ☐ **Sintered nano-silver pastes** dominate high-power die-attach (SiC, GaN, EV inverters) — k > 150 W/m·K, sinter at < 300 °C under pressure, survive 200 °C+ junction temperatures.
- ☐ **Cure mechanisms** — thermal (most common, 150 – 200 °C), UV (acrylates, displays), dual cure (UV + heat), moisture-cure (silicones), snap-cure (high-throughput SMT, 30 s at 200 °C).
- ☐ **Standard benchmarks to remember**: $\rho_v < 10^{-4}$ Ω·cm (ICA), $k > 1$ W/m·K (thermal), $T_g > 125$ °C (lead-free reflow compatible), CTE < 30 ppm/°C (matched to Si/PCB), ionic impurity < 10 ppm (Na⁺, Cl⁻).
- ☐ **Major applications**: die-attach, flip-chip ACF, underfill, heat-sink attach, SMT component bonding, display lamination, EMI gaskets, wafer bonding (BCB/polyimide), photovoltaic encapsulation (EVA).
- ☐ **Lewis-Nielsen model** is the standard equation for predicting $k_{eff}$ of filled composites — must memorise formula and the $\psi$ values (0.605 random spheres, 0.907 aligned fibres).
- ☐ **Coffin-Manson equation** $N_f = A (\Delta T)^{-n}$ governs solder-joint fatigue life; underfill increases $A$ dramatically.
- ☐ **Industry standards to cite in answers**: JEDEC (semiconductor reliability), IPC-SM-817 (adhesive standards), MIL-STD-883 (military electronics), RoHS (lead-free compliance).
- ☐ **Common exam mistake to avoid**: do NOT confuse "ACF conducts in all directions" — this is the most penalised conceptual error. ACF is *anisotropic* — Z-axis only.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adhesives in the Automotive and Aerospace Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Adhesives in the Automotive and Aerospace Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Industrial Adhesive Applications

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT861 – Adhesive Science & Technology)**, **Industrial Adhesives** are defined as specialized bonding agents formulated to meet the rigorous mechanical, thermal, chemical, and environmental performance criteria demanded by large-scale manufacturing sectors such as the **Automotive** and **Aerospace** industries. Unlike general-purpose household adhesives, industrial adhesives are engineered to replace or supplement traditional joining methods such as **welding, riveting, and mechanical fastening**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This module shifts focus from fundamental adhesion theories (covered in Modules 1 & 2) toward *application-specific engineering*. The automotive and aerospace sectors represent the two highest-value application domains for advanced structural adhesives, with combined global market valuation exceeding **USD 15 Billion** as of 2024.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

### 1.2.1 Automotive Structural Adhesives
**Automotive structural adhesives** are high-strength polymeric bonding agents, typically based on **epoxy, polyurethane (PU), or modified acrylic chemistries**, that are capable of bearing significant static and dynamic (fatigue) loads in vehicle body structures. They are primarily used in the **Body-in-White (BIW)** assembly stage to bond dissimilar materials, reduce vehicle weight, and improve crashworthiness.

### 1.2.2 Aerospace Structural Adhesives
**Aerospace structural adhesives** are ultra-high-performance bonding systems, predominantly based on **epoxy, phenolic, bismaleimide (BMI), or cyanate ester resins**, designed to maintain structural integrity under extreme service conditions including cryogenic temperatures ($-55^\circ C$), elevated temperatures ($>180^\circ C$), high humidity, and exposure to aviation fuels and hydraulic fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Performance Benchmark:** Aerospace adhesives must comply with strict qualification standards such as **MIL-STD-401** (Sandwich Construction) and **ASTM D1780** (Standard Practices for Adhesive Bonding of Aluminum), while automotive adhesives typically conform to **ISO 17025** and OEM-specific standards (e.g., GMW, TL, VW specifications).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Analogy: The "Invisible Weld"**

Imagine two steel plates that need to be joined. A traditional welder uses a torch to melt and fuse the metal — this is strong but introduces heat distortion, adds weight (from weld beads and rivets), and creates stress concentration points. Now, imagine instead that you paint a thin liquid layer between the two plates, clamp them, and place them in a low-temperature oven. After an hour, the plates are joined with a bond that is often **stronger than the metal itself**, the joint is **lighter**, it **doesn't corrode at the seam**, and it **absorbs crash energy** like a cushion.

> That invisible painted layer is the **industrial structural adhesive**.

- In the **automotive industry**, this "invisible weld" allows car manufacturers to build lighter, safer, and more fuel-efficient vehicles.
- In the **aerospace industry**, it allows engineers to bond advanced composites (carbon fiber reinforced polymers) to metals without drilling thousands of holes that would weaken the structure.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Whenever you board a modern aircraft or drive a modern car, you are placing your life in the hands of *adhesive joints*. The Boeing 787 Dreamliner uses adhesive bonding in **over 50% of its primary airframe structure**, and a typical electric vehicle (EV) contains **15–20 kg of structural adhesives**.

---

## 1.4 Why Adhesives? — The Driving Forces

The shift from mechanical fastening to adhesive bonding in these industries is driven by four interlinked engineering motivations:

| Driving Force | Automotive Impact | Aerospace Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Lightweighting** | Every 10% weight reduction → ~6–8% fuel economy gain | Every 1 kg saved → ~$3,000 fuel savings over aircraft lifetime |
| **Stress Distribution** | Uniform load transfer across entire joint area | Eliminates point-stress of rivets, improving fatigue life by 10× |
| **Dissimilar Material Joining** | Bonds aluminum to steel to composites to plastics | Critical for CFRP-aluminum hybrid structures |
| **Sealing & NVH Control** | Reduces cabin noise; seals against water/dust | Seals fuel tanks, pressure cabins, eliminates galvanic corrosion |

---

## 1.5 Standard Industrial Metrics in Bold

- **Tensile Shear Strength:** $\mathbf{20 \text{ to } 50 \text{ MPa}}$ (automotive); $\mathbf{30 \text{ to } 70 \text{ MPa}}$ (aerospace).
- **Service Temperature Range:** $\mathbf{-40^\circ C \text{ to } +180^\circ C}$ (automotive); $\mathbf{-55^\circ C \text{ to } +230^\circ C}$ (aerospace BMI/cyanate ester).
- **Cure Time:** $\mathbf{30 \text{ seconds to } 60 \text{ minutes}}$ depending on chemistry and heat activation.
- **Elastic Modulus (E-modulus):** $\mathbf{1 \text{ GPa (toughened PU) to } 5 \text{ GPa (rigid epoxy)}}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Automotive Industry — Adhesive Chemistry & Applications

### 2.1.1 Classification of Automotive Adhesives

Automotive adhesives are categorized by function and chemistry. The following structured breakdown aligns with KTU Module 3 learning outcomes:

**A. By Load-Bearing Function:**

1. **Structural Adhesives** — Bear static and dynamic loads (e.g., body panels, chassis).
2. **Semi-Structural Adhesives** — Bear moderate loads with some elastic behavior (e.g., trim attachment).
3. **Non-Structural Adhesives** — Sealing and holding functions only (e.g., gaskets, weatherstripping).

**B. By Chemical Base:**

- **Epoxy Adhesives:** Two-part (resin + hardener); offer high strength, chemical resistance, and low shrinkage. Used in CFRP/aluminum joining.
- **Polyurethane (PU) Adhesives:** Flexible, crash-energy-absorbing, excellent for plastics and composites. Cure via moisture reaction.
- **Modified Acrylic Adhesives:** Fast-curing (5–60 min at room temperature), tolerant of oily surfaces, used in hem-flange bonding.
- **Rubber-Toughened Epoxies:** Combine rigidity with peel strength; ideal for crash-resistant structures.
- **Hot-Melt Adhesives (EVA, PA):** Thermoplastic, fast set, used for interior trim and headliners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Epoxy Dominates Aerospace while PU/Acrylic Dominate Automotive:**
> Aerospace demands long-term thermal endurance → favors **crosslinked thermosets (epoxy, BMI)**.
> Automotive demands short cycle times (60–90 sec in paint ovens at 180°C) → favors **fast-cure PU/acrylic with induction or resistance heating**.

---

### 2.1.2 Key Automotive Applications

| Application | Adhesive Type | Function | Substrate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hem Flange Bonding** (door, hood, trunk) | Toughened Epoxy / PU | Replaces spot welds; prevents panel flutter | Steel, Aluminum |
| **Windshield Bonding** | One-component PU (moisture cure) | Structural bond + sealing | Glass to steel/aluminum |
| **Carbon Fiber Composite Bonding** | Epoxy film adhesive | Joins CFRP substructures | CFRP to CFRP/CFRP to metal |
| **Battery Pack Assembly (EVs)** | Thermally conductive epoxy/PU | Bonds cells to cooling plates, provides thermal management | Aluminum, copper, Li-ion cells |
| **Direct Glazing** | Silane-modified PU (SMP) | High-elasticity, no primer required | Glass to composites |
| **NVH Damping** | Viscoelastic epoxy / butyl rubber patches | Converts vibration to heat | Body panels |

### 2.1.3 The Hem Flange Process (Detailed)

The **hem flange** is the folded edge of a sheet metal panel (e.g., car door outer panel folded over the inner panel). The adhesive is applied as a continuous bead before folding and then cured in the e-coat oven ($\approx 180^\circ C$ for 20–30 min).

**Why adhesive here?**
- Increases joint stiffness by up to **30×** versus a mechanical clinch alone.
- Distributes crash load across entire hem, preventing catastrophic unzipping.
- Eliminates "oil-canning" (panel vibration) at high speeds.

---

## 2.2 Aerospace Industry — Adhesive Chemistry & Applications

### 2.2.1 Classification of Aerospace Adhesives

Aerospace adhesive selection is governed by **FAR Part 25** (Airworthiness Standards – Transport Category Airplanes) and OEM-specific specifications (Boeing BMS, Airbus AIMS, etc.).

**A. Film Adhesives:**
Supplied as solid B-staged films (semi-cured). They offer:
- Uniform bondline thickness (controlled by carrier scrim).
- Excellent for large-area composite layups.
- Example: **AF-163** (3M) — modified epoxy film, $177^\circ C$ cure.

**B. Paste Adhesives:**
Two-part systems used for potting, fillet bonding, and repair. Example: **EA-9394** (Henkel/Loctite) — high-temperature epoxy paste.

**C. Foaming Adhesives:**
Expand during cure to fill gaps in honeycomb sandwich panels. Example: **FM-300** (Cytec/Solvay) — epoxy film with expanding core.

**D. High-Temperature Resins:**
- **BMI (Bismaleimide):** Service temp up to $230^\circ C$. Used in supersonic and military aircraft.
- **Cyanate Esters:** Service temp up to $250^\circ C$, low moisture absorption. Used in space applications.
- **Polyimides:** Service temp up to $300^\circ C+$. Used in engine nacelles.

### 2.2.2 Key Aerospace Applications

| Application | Adhesive Type | Function | Performance Demand |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Honeycomb Sandwich Panels** | Epoxy film (FM-300, FM-410) | Bonds face sheets to honeycomb core | Peel strength, fatigue resistance |
| **CFRP Primary Structure** | Modified epoxy film (AF-163) | Bonds stringers, ribs, skins | $-55^\circ C$ to $+80^\circ C$ service |
| **Metal-to-Composite Bonding** | Epoxy paste with primer | Wing leading edge, control surfaces | Galvanic isolation |
| **Fuel Tank Sealing** | Polysulfide / fluorosilicone | Seals integral fuel tanks | Fuel resistance, flexibility |
| **Radome Bonding** | Low-dielectric epoxy | Bonds fiberglass fairings | RF transparency |
| **Engine Nacelle** | Silicone / high-temp epoxy | Acoustic & thermal bonding | $200^\circ C$ continuous service |

### 2.2.3 The Honeycomb Sandwich Structure (Detailed)

A **honeycomb sandwich** consists of:
- Two thin, stiff **face sheets** (e.g., 0.5 mm aluminum or CFRP).
- A lightweight **honeycomb core** (aluminum, Nomex aramid, or paper).
- **Adhesive bondlines** that transfer shear loads between core and faces.

**Why adhesives are irreplaceable here:**
- Mechanical fasteners would crush the thin core and create leak paths.
- The adhesive must cure at low pressure to avoid core crushing.
- The bondline must absorb thermal expansion mismatch between face and core.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the most critical for solving numerical problems in KTU examinations on this topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Sheet — Exam Quick Reference**

| Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Shear stress (τ), Force (F), Bonded Area (A) | $MPa$ | Lap-shear strength calculation |
| $P_L = \tau \cdot w \cdot L$ | Load capacity of single lap joint | $N$ | Single-lap joint design |
| $\sigma_{peel} = \dfrac{M \cdot E}{I}$ | Peel stress in flexible adherend | $MPa$ | Peel strength analysis |
| $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion mismatch; $\alpha$ = CTE | $mm$ | Differential expansion stress |
| $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear modulus from Young's modulus & Poisson's ratio | $GPa$ | Adhesive layer shear deformation |
| $E_{eff} = \dfrac{E_1 t_1 + E_2 t_2}{t_1 + t_2}$ | Effective modulus of bimetal strip | $GPa$ | Dissimilar material joint stiffness |
| $W_{cr} = \sigma_{th} \cdot t$ | Critical buckling load per unit width | $N/mm$ | Sandwich panel face wrinkling |
| $F_{crash} = \int_0^{\delta} \tau(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon$ | Energy absorption per unit volume | $J/m^3$ | Crash-energy-absorbing adhesive |

**Key Material Constants to Memorize:**
- CTE of aluminum: $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6} /K$
- CTE of carbon steel: $\alpha_{Fe} = 12 \times 10^{-6} /K$
- CTE of CFRP (longitudinal): $\alpha_{CFRP,L} \approx 0$ to $1 \times 10^{-6} /K$
- Poisson's ratio of epoxy: $\nu_{epoxy} \approx 0.35$
- Density reduction with adhesive bonding (vs. riveting): **15–25%**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why These Equations Matter in Industry:**
> 1. The **lap-shear equation** determines how many rivets (or how much adhesive area) a wing-to-fuselage joint needs to survive 100,000 flight cycles (≈ 30 years of service).
> 2. The **CTE mismatch equation** explains why aluminum-to-CFRP joints in aircraft require careful adhesive selection — without compensation, a 100°C climb on a runway could induce **50 MPa of thermal stress**, potentially delaminating the joint.
> 3. The **crash-energy integral** is why modern car doors use **toughened PU adhesives** — the adhesive's stress-strain curve is engineered to "plateau," absorbing energy like a crumple zone.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Lap-Shear Strength of a Single-Lap Adhesive Joint

**Problem Statement:**
A single-lap joint in an automotive door hem flange is constructed from two steel sheets, each of width $w = 25 \text{ mm}$ and overlap length $L = 10 \text{ mm}$. The joint is loaded in tension with a force $F = 8000 \text{ N}$. The adhesive used is a toughened epoxy with a shear modulus $G = 1.2 \text{ GPa}$ and bondline thickness $t = 0.2 \text{ mm}$. Determine:
(a) The average shear stress in the adhesive.
(b) The average shear strain in the adhesive.
(c) Whether the joint can survive a 1.5× safety factor, given the adhesive's ultimate shear strength $\tau_u = 35 \text{ MPa}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Define the Bonded Shear Area**

The bonded shear area is the rectangular region where the two adherends overlap:

$$
A = w \cdot L
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
A = 25 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 250 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Average Shear Stress**

By definition, the average shear stress is the applied force divided by the bonded area:

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{F}{A}
$$

$$
\tau_{avg} = \frac{8000 \text{ N}}{250 \text{ mm}^2} = 32 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 32 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Average Shear Strain**

Using Hooke's law for shear deformation, the shear strain is related to shear stress by the shear modulus:

$$
\gamma = \frac{\tau_{avg}}{G}
$$

Substituting (note: $G = 1.2 \text{ GPa} = 1200 \text{ MPa}$):

$$
\gamma = \frac{32 \text{ MPa}}{1200 \text{ MPa}} = 0.0267
$$

In percent:

$$
\gamma = 2.67 \%
$$

**Step 4: Safety Factor Check**

The applied stress must be compared to the ultimate strength divided by the safety factor:

$$
\tau_{allowable} = \frac{\tau_u}{n} = \frac{35 \text{ MPa}}{1.5} = 23.33 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $\tau_{avg} = 32 \text{ MPa} > \tau_{allowable} = 23.33 \text{ MPa}$, the joint **fails** the safety check.

**Step 5: Required Overlap Length for Safe Design**

Rearranging the shear stress equation to solve for required overlap:

$$
L_{req} = \frac{F \cdot n}{w \cdot \tau_u}
$$

$$
L_{req} = \frac{8000 \times 1.5}{25 \times 35} = \frac{12000}{875} = 13.71 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** The overlap must be increased from 10 mm to at least 13.71 mm (a 37% increase) to satisfy the 1.5× safety factor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Stress in a Metal-to-Composite Aerospace Joint

**Problem Statement:**
A wing skin joint bonds a **CFRP panel** (CTE $\alpha_1 = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} /K$, modulus $E_1 = 140 \text{ GPa}$) to an **aluminum rib** (CTE $\alpha_2 = 23 \times 10^{-6} /K$, modulus $E_2 = 70 \text{ GPa}$). The joint length is $L = 500 \text{ mm}$. The aircraft climbs from ground temperature $T_1 = 25^\circ C$ to cruise temperature $T_2 = -55^\circ C$. The bondline adhesive has shear modulus $G = 0.8 \text{ GPa}$ and thickness $t = 0.15 \text{ mm}$. Determine the thermal shear stress developed in the adhesive.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Compute the Temperature Change**

$$
\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = -55 - 25 = -80^\circ C
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Differential Free Thermal Strain**

If the two adherends were not bonded, they would freely expand/contract by different amounts. The differential free strain is:

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{thermal} = (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \cdot \Delta T
$$

Substituting:

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{thermal} = (23 - 0.5) \times 10^{-6} \times (-80)
$$

$$
\Delta \varepsilon_{thermal} = 22.5 \times 10^{-6} \times (-80) = -1.8 \times 10^{-3}
$$

The negative sign indicates net **contraction** (aluminum contracts much more than CFRP).

**Step 3: Compute the Differential Free Thermal Displacement**

The actual differential displacement that the adhesive must accommodate is:

$$
\Delta L = L \cdot \Delta \varepsilon_{thermal}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 500 \text{ mm} \times (-1.8 \times 10^{-3}) = -0.9 \text{ mm}
$$

So the aluminum wants to contract by 0.9 mm more than the CFRP — this 0.9 mm displacement must be transferred through the adhesive shear layer.

**Step 4: Model the Adhesive Shear Deformation**

For a thin adhesive layer of thickness $t$, the shear strain is approximately the relative displacement divided by the thickness:

$$
\gamma_{adhesive} = \frac{\Delta L}{t}
$$

$$
\gamma_{adhesive} = \frac{0.9 \text{ mm}}{0.15 \text{ mm}} = 6.0
$$

> [!WARNING]
> This strain is **600%** — far beyond the elastic limit of any practical adhesive. This indicates that the joint would fail *in shear* if the temperature drop occurs as a single rigid bonded block.

**Step 5: Practical Engineering Interpretation**

In a real aerospace joint, this differential expansion is mitigated by:

1. Using a **flexible adhesive** (low $G$, high elongation at break).
2. Making the joint **slender** (long, thin) so strain distributes along the length.
3. **Compliance** through intermediate elastomeric layers.
4. **Continuous scarf** or **stepped-lap** joints rather than simple overlap.

The actual peak shear stress in a long, slender bonded joint under thermal load is given by the **Volkersen analysis**:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{G \cdot t \cdot (\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \cdot \Delta T \cdot \omega}{2 \cdot \sinh(\omega L/2)}
$$

where:

$$
\omega = \sqrt{\frac{G}{t} \left( \frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)}
$$

For long joints, $\sinh(\omega L/2) \to \infty$, and the stress remains bounded — this is the fundamental reason why aircraft lap joints are designed to be long and thin.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Automotive vs. Aerospace Adhesives

| Parameter | Automotive Industry | Aerospace Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Adhesive Chemistries** | PU, toughened epoxy, modified acrylic, hot-melt | Epoxy film, BMI, cyanate ester, polyimide |
| **Typical Cure Temperature** | 160–200°C (e-coat oven compatible) | 120–180°C (autoclave or oven) |
| **Service Temperature Range** | $-40$ to $+180^\circ C$ | $-55$ to $+230^\circ C$ |
| **Tensile Shear Strength** | 20–35 MPa | 30–50 MPa (epoxy film) |
| **Peel Strength Requirement** | High (crash resistance) | Moderate (mostly shear-loaded) |
| **Cycle Time / Throughput** | 60–90 seconds (mass production) | Hours to days (low-volume, high-quality) |
| **Primary Substrates** | Steel, aluminum, plastics, glass | CFRP, aluminum, titanium, honeycomb |
| **Cost Sensitivity** | Very high (cents per joint) | Low ($100s per kg of adhesive acceptable) |
| **Key Performance Driver** | Crash energy absorption | Fatigue life under cyclic loading |
| **Regulatory Framework** | OEM specs, ISO 17025 | MIL-STD-401, FAR Part 25, ASTM D1780 |
| **Surface Prep Method** | Degrease + phosphate / e-coat | Chromic acid anodize + sol-gel primer |
| **Failure Mode Tolerance** | Progressive, energy-absorbing | Predictable, must not propagate |
| **Repairability** | Often replace entire component | Bonded repair patches standard practice |
| **Volume of Use** | 15–25 kg per vehicle | 200–400 kg per aircraft (B787: ~800 kg) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Volume of Adhesive in a Hem Flange

**Problem Statement:**
A car manufacturer produces 500,000 vehicles per year. Each door has a hem flange perimeter of 1.2 m. The adhesive bead cross-section is a triangle with base 4 mm and height 3 mm. The adhesive density is $1.4 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate the annual adhesive consumption for door hem flanges (4 doors per vehicle).

### Solution:

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of triangular bead:**

$$
A_{bead} = \frac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}
$$

$$
A_{bead} = \frac{1}{2} \times 4 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm} = 6 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Volume of adhesive per door:**

$$
V_{door} = A_{bead} \times L_{hem}
$$

$$
V_{door} = 6 \text{ mm}^2 \times 1200 \text{ mm} = 7200 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3: Convert to cm³:**

$$
V_{door} = 7200 \text{ mm}^3 = 7.2 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 4: Volume per vehicle (4 doors):**

$$
V_{vehicle} = 4 \times 7.2 = 28.8 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 5: Mass per vehicle:**

$$
m_{vehicle} = V_{vehicle} \times \rho
$$

$$
m_{vehicle} = 28.8 \text{ cm}^3 \times 1.4 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 40.32 \text{ g} \approx 40 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 6: Annual consumption:**

$$
m_{annual} = 500{,}000 \times 40.32 \text{ g} = 20{,}160{,}000 \text{ g} = 20{,}160 \text{ kg}
$$

**Conclusion:** Approximately **20 metric tons** of hem flange adhesive are consumed annually for just one model line — illustrating why adhesive cost (€/kg) is a critical procurement parameter in the automotive industry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Automotive Body-in-White (BIW) Adhesive Bonding Workflow

This block diagram illustrates the end-to-end process flow for adhesive application in a typical automotive Body-in-White assembly line, from surface preparation through final cure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input: Steel or Aluminum Sheet Panels] --> nodeB[Step 1: Surface Degreasing and Pre-treatment]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Step 2: Substrate Decision}
    nodeC -->|Steel Panels| nodeD[Step 3a: Phosphate Conversion Coating]
    nodeC -->|Aluminum Panels| nodeE[Step 3b: Zirconium Oxide Conversion Coating]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Step 4: Adhesive Application Station]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 5: Robot-Controlled Bead Dispensing]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 6: Panel Joining and Hem Flange Folding]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 7: Resistance Spot Welding for Initial Fix]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 8: Cathodic E-coat Dipping]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 9: E-coat Oven Cure at 180 C for 30 min]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Step 10: Adhesive Curing Concurrent with Paint Bake]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Output: Bonded BIW Assembly]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Quality Control: Ultrasonic and Destructive Testing]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Aerospace Composite Bonding Workflow

This diagram maps the controlled process flow for primary structural adhesive bonding in aerospace composite assembly, emphasizing the autoclave-based cure cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[Input: CFRP Prepreg Layup and Metal Substrate] --> nodeQ[Step 1: Surface Preparation of Metal]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Step 1a: Chromic Acid Anodizing for Aluminum]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Step 1b: Application of Sol-Gel Corrosion Inhibiting Primer]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Step 2: Adhesive Film Placement]
    nodeT --> nodeU[Step 2a: Cutting Epoxy Film to Geometry]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Step 2b: Stacking Film with Carrier Scrim]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Step 3: Honeycomb Core Insertion if Applicable]
    nodeW --> nodeX[Step 4: Vacuum Bagging of Assembly]
    nodeX --> nodeY[Step 5: Autoclave Cure Cycle]
    nodeY --> nodeZ[Step 5a: Ramp to 80 C and Hold 60 min]
    nodeZ --> nodeAA[Step 5b: Ramp to 175 C at 0.5 C per min]
    nodeAA --> nodeAB[Step 5c: Hold at 175 C for 120 min under 7 bar pressure]
    nodeAB --> nodeAC[Step 6: NDI Inspection]
    nodeAC --> nodeAD{NDI Result}
    nodeAD -->|Pass| nodeAE[Output: Qualified Bonded Airframe Component]
    nodeAD -->|Fail| nodeAF[Repair or Reject]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Decision Logic for Adhesive Selection

This decision-flow diagram shows how an engineer selects the appropriate adhesive chemistry based on application constraints in either automotive or aerospace contexts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeBA[Start: Adhesive Selection Request] --> nodeBB{Is the part primary load-bearing?}
    nodeBB -->|Yes| nodeBC{Service temperature above 150 C?}
    nodeBB -->|No, secondary| nodeBN{Is substrate plastic or composite?}
    nodeBC -->|Yes| nodeBD[Select BMI or Cyanate Ester]
    nodeBC -->|No| nodeBE[Select Toughened Epoxy Film]
    nodeBN -->|Yes| nodeBO[Select Polyurethane or Modified Acrylic]
    nodeBN -->|No, metal| nodeBP[Select Epoxy Paste or Rubber-toughened Epoxy]
    nodeBD --> nodeBQ{Is bonding area large or honeycomb?}
    nodeBE --> nodeBQ
    nodeBO --> nodeBR{Is fast cycle time critical?}
    nodeBP --> nodeBR
    nodeBQ -->|Yes| nodeBS[Use Epoxy Film with Expanding Core]
    nodeBQ -->|No| nodeBT[Use Standard Epoxy Film or Paste]
    nodeBR -->|Yes, less than 60 sec| nodeBU[Use Modified Acrylic with Induction Cure]
    nodeBR -->|No, minutes acceptable| nodeBV[Use Two-part Epoxy or PU]
    nodeBS --> nodeBW[Output: Recommended Adhesive System]
    nodeBT --> nodeBW
    nodeBU --> nodeBW
    nodeBV --> nodeBW
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture: Hem Flange Joint Anatomy

This block diagram describes the layered cross-sectional structure of an automotive hem flange joint, identifying the bondline and material stack-up.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeCA[Outer Panel: 0.7 mm Steel] --> nodeCB[Hem Fold Region: 180 degree bend]
    nodeCB --> nodeCC[Adhesive Bondline: 0.2 mm Toughened Epoxy]
    nodeCC --> nodeCD[Inner Panel: 0.8 mm Steel]
    nodeCD --> nodeCE[Cavity Filler: Structural Foam Optional]
    nodeCE --> nodeCF[Outer Skin Contact Zone]
    nodeCF --> nodeCG[Final Assembly: Clinch and Weld Spots for Initial Fixity]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block Architecture: Honeycomb Sandwich Panel

This block diagram represents the layered architecture of a typical aerospace honeycomb sandwich panel, with adhesive bondlines as critical load-transfer interfaces.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeDA[Upper Face Sheet: 0.5 mm Aluminum or CFRP] --> nodeDB[Upper Bondline: 0.1 mm Epoxy Film Adhesive FM300]
    nodeDB --> nodeDC[Honeycomb Core: 10 mm Nomex or Aluminum Hexagonal Cells]
    nodeDC --> nodeDD[Lower Bondline: 0.1 mm Epoxy Film Adhesive FM300]
    nodeDD --> nodeDE[Lower Face Sheet: 0.5 mm Aluminum or CFRP]
    nodeDE --> nodeDF[Edge Closeout: Potting Compound for Sealing]
    nodeDF --> nodeDG[Mechanical Fastener: Hi-Lite for Local Reinforcement]
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define structural adhesive and list two properties that distinguish it from a general-purpose adhesive. [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

A **structural adhesive** is an adhesive capable of forming a permanent bond capable of bearing significant static and dynamic loads in a load-bearing assembly, with a typical lap-shear strength exceeding **7 MPa** (the structural threshold defined by ASTM D5573).

**Two distinguishing properties:**

1. **High load-bearing capacity:** Structural adhesives exhibit lap-shear strengths of 20–50 MPa, comparable to or exceeding the strength of the adherends themselves. General-purpose adhesives (e.g., white glue) typically fail below 5 MPa.

2. **Environmental durability:** Structural adhesives maintain their mechanical properties under thermal cycling, moisture, chemical exposure, and fatigue loading for the design life of the structure (15 years for automotive, 30 years for aerospace). General-purpose adhesives degrade rapidly under such conditions.

3. *(Any one more valid point such as creep resistance, substrate versatility, or ability to bond dissimilar materials may earn the third mark.)*

---

### Question 2: Why is surface preparation considered the most critical step in aerospace adhesive bonding? [3 Marks]

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

Surface preparation is the single most critical step in aerospace adhesive bonding because **80% of adhesive joint failures are attributable to inadequate surface preparation.**

**Three key reasons:**

1. **Removes weak boundary layers:** Contaminants (oils, oxides, dust) form a weak layer between adhesive and adherend. Chromic acid anodizing (CAA) on aluminum creates a fresh, porous oxide layer that mechanically interlocks with the adhesive primer (sol-gel), providing a stable, high-energy surface.

2. **Creates chemical compatibility:** Aerospace surface treatments (e.g., **BR-127** or **AC-130** primers) introduce chemical groups that form covalent bonds with the epoxy adhesive, creating a "primary bond" interface rather than mere adhesion.

3. **Prevents environmental degradation:** Proper anodizing and priming prevent **galvanic corrosion** at aluminum-CFRP interfaces and inhibit hydration of the oxide layer, which would otherwise cause bondline degradation in humid service conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 2024 Scheme, ESE Pattern]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive — Automotive Applications

**a)** Explain in detail the role of **polyurethane (PU) adhesives in modern automotive windshield bonding**. Discuss the chemistry, application process, mechanical performance, and the safety implications if the bond fails. *(7 Marks)*

**b)** With the aid of a labeled sketch, describe the **hem flange bonding process** used in automotive body panels. Explain why adhesives are used in addition to spot welding, and discuss how the adhesive contributes to **crash energy management**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a): PU Adhesives in Windshield Bonding

**Step 1 — Chemistry (2 marks):**
Automotive windshield adhesives are **one-component, moisture-curing polyureanes** based on **isocyanate-terminated prepolymers**. The reaction proceeds as:

$$
R-NCO + H_2O \rightarrow R-NH_2 + CO_2
$$

$$
R-NH_2 + R-NCO \rightarrow R-NH-CO-NH-R \text{ (urea linkage)}
$$

The reaction proceeds from the outside inwards, with skin formation in 30–60 minutes and full cure in 24 hours depending on humidity. **Silane-modified PUs (SMPs)** are increasingly used as they offer primerless adhesion to glass and painted metals.

**Step 2 — Application Process (2 marks):**
1. Glass and body aperture are cleaned with isopropanol or primer.
2. Adhesive is applied via a heated ($\approx 40$–$60^\circ C$) robotically-controlled bead to the glass or body pinch weld.
3. Glass is positioned using a "glass setting robot" with optical alignment.
4. The vehicle is parked for 30+ minutes to allow the adhesive to develop green strength before driving.

**Step 3 — Mechanical Performance (2 marks):**
- Lap-shear strength on glass-to-steel: **3–6 MPa** (after full cure).
- Elongation at break: **300–500%** (elastic, crash-absorbing).
- Modulus: **3–8 MPa** at 10% strain.
- Shore A hardness: **50–70**.

**Step 4 — Safety Implications (1 mark):**
If the windshield bond fails, the **airbag deployment system may not function correctly** — many modern airbags use the windshield as a reaction surface to cushion the occupant. Additionally, roof crush resistance is reduced by up to **30%**, and the windshield contributes to NVH sealing; failure causes cabin noise and water ingress.

---

#### Model Solution (b): Hem Flange Bonding & Crash Energy Management

**Step 1 — Sketch and Process Description (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.4 for the hem flange block diagram.)*

The hem flange process involves:
1. Outer panel (e.g., 0.7 mm steel) is stamped with a flanged edge.
2. Inner panel (e.g., 0.8 mm steel) is placed against the outer.
3. A continuous bead of toughened epoxy or PU adhesive is applied along the flanged edge.
4. The flange is folded 180° around the inner panel edge using a hemming die.
5. The assembly is held by resistance spot welds and passes through the e-coat oven at **180°C for 20–30 min**, simultaneously curing the adhesive and the paint.

**Step 2 — Why Adhesives in Addition to Spot Welds? (2 marks):**
- Spot welds provide **point fixity**; they cannot seal the gap between the panels.
- The adhesive **fills the gap** completely, eliminating water traps that cause corrosion.
- The continuous adhesive bondline **distributes loads** uniformly between spot welds, preventing the panels from vibrating independently (oil-canning) at high speeds.
- The adhesive **reinforces the hem fold**, which is the weakest geometric region due to bending.

**Step 3 — Crash Energy Management (2 marks):**
During a side or frontal crash, the deformed door panel must transfer load to the inner structure without separating catastrophically. The toughened adhesive exhibits a **plateau-shaped stress-strain curve**:

$$
\sigma(\varepsilon) = \sigma_0 + H \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where the low hardening modulus $H$ allows the adhesive to absorb large amounts of energy (large area under the curve) before fracture. This is quantified by the **toughness integral**:

$$
G_c = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma(\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon
$$

A typical crash-optimized PU adhesive has $G_c \approx 30 \text{ to } 50 \text{ kJ/m}^2$ — comparable to a lightweight crumple zone. Tests have shown that adhesively-bonded door assemblies maintain passenger survival space in 64 km/h side-impact crashes, whereas spot-welded-only doors fail at ~50 km/h.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Comprehensive — Aerospace Applications

**a)** Describe the **honeycomb sandwich panel** structure used in aircraft. With neat sketches, explain how the adhesive bondline transfers loads and identify the failure modes that the adhesive must be designed to resist. *(7 Marks)*

**b)** Compare **bismaleimide (BMI) and cyanate ester** adhesives for high-temperature aerospace applications. Discuss the chemistry, maximum service temperature, moisture absorption characteristics, and one specific application for each. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a): Honeycomb Sandwich Panel

**Step 1 — Structure Description (2 marks):**
A honeycomb sandwich panel consists of:
- **Two face sheets** (aluminum, CFRP, or fiberglass; 0.3–1.0 mm thick).
- **A honeycomb core** (aluminum foil, Nomex aramid paper, or fiberglass; 3–25 mm cell size, 10–50 mm thick).
- **Two adhesive bondlines** (epoxy film adhesive, 0.1–0.3 mm thick) bonding faces to core.

The faces carry bending loads; the core carries shear loads and stabilizes the faces against buckling.

**Step 2 — Load Transfer Mechanism (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.5 for the sandwich architecture block diagram.)*

When the panel is loaded in bending:
- The **top face** experiences **compression**; the **bottom face** experiences **tension**.
- The **core** carries the **transverse shear**.
- The **adhesive bondline** must transfer:
  1. **In-plane shear** (face to core sliding tendency),
  2. **Tension/compression normal to the panel** (peel stress from face buckling),
  3. **Cleavage** at panel edges where the core terminates.

The bondline is therefore designed for both high lap-shear strength (in-plane) and high peel strength (through-thickness). A standard aerospace film adhesive such as **FM-300** offers lap-shear of 35 MPa and climbing-drum peel strength of 80 N·mm/mm.

**Step 3 — Failure Modes (2 marks):**
The adhesive must resist:
1. **Honeycomb core shear failure** (cell walls buckle before adhesive yields).
2. **Face sheet-core debonding** (adhesive peel failure at low through-thickness stress).
3. **Face wrinkling** (buckling of face into core, demanding high adhesive peel strength).
4. **Edge delamination** (stress concentration at panel cut-outs and fastener holes).
5. **Environmental degradation** (humidity, jet fuel, hydraulic fluid ingress along the core).

---

#### Model Solution (b): BMI vs. Cyanate Ester Adhesives

| Parameter | Bismaleimide (BMI) | Cyanate Ester |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemistry** | Bismaleimide monomers crosslink via Michael addition with allyl or amine co-monomers | Cyanate ester (–O–C≡N) groups trimerize to form triazine rings |
| **Cure Temperature** | 200–250°C (post-cure required) | 220–250°C (with catalyst) |
| **Maximum Service Temp** | 230°C continuous; 250°C peak | 250°C continuous; 300°C peak |
| **Glass Transition (Tg)** | 240–290°C | 250–290°C |
| **Moisture Absorption** | 1.5–3.0% (moderate) | 0.5–1.5% (low) |
| **Toughness** | Brittle; often toughened with rubber or thermoplastics | Inherently tougher than BMI; lower crosslink density |
| **Cost** | High | Very high (~$500/kg) |
| **Specific Application** | Supersonic aircraft skins (e.g., Concorde successor, Rafale), engine nacelles | Spacecraft structures, satellite antennae, high-speed missile fins |

**Chemistry Detail (for full 7 marks, both must be covered):**

**BMI Cure Reaction:**

$$
\text{BMI} + \text{allylphenol} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{ene reaction} \rightarrow \text{network}
$$

The reaction proceeds through an "ene" reaction followed by Diels-Alder and Michael addition steps, producing a highly crosslinked thermoset.

**Cyanate Ester Cure Reaction:**

$$
3 \, R-O-C \equiv N \xrightarrow{\text{heat + catalyst}} \text{Triazine ring} + \text{Polycyanurate network}
$$

The triazine ring network is highly symmetric, contributing to low dielectric loss and excellent moisture resistance.

**Specific Application Choice:**

- **BMI:** Used in **fighter aircraft engine bay structures** (e.g., Eurofighter Typhoon) where sustained temperatures exceed 200°C.
- **Cyanate Ester:** Used in **spacecraft bus structures and antenna substrates** where low outgassing and dimensional stability under thermal cycling are critical.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic (Based on KTU 2024 Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "structural" with "strong" (–1 to –2 marks):** A structural adhesive is defined not just by *strength* but by *durability under design loads and environment for the design life*. A 50 MPa adhesive that degrades in 6 months is NOT structural.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention surface preparation (–2 marks):** In any aerospace bonding question worth 7+ marks, omitting surface preparation (anodizing, primer) will cost a minimum of 2 marks. This is the most-tested sub-topic.
>
> 3. **Writing "epoxy" without specifying cure temperature (–1 mark):** Always specify whether the epoxy is room-temperature cure, $120^\circ C$ cure, or $175^\circ C$ cure — these are very different formulations.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the "Why" — the engineering rationale (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme values *application understanding*. Stating "epoxy is used in aerospace" without explaining *why it dominates* (high Tg, low creep, chemical resistance) loses marks.
>
> 5. **Numerical errors in unit conversion (–1 mark):** Students commonly confuse $MPa = N/mm^2$ and $GPa = 10^3 MPa$. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.
>
> 6. **In Part B questions, failing to provide diagrams:** Even simple block diagrams earn 1–2 marks. Always include at least a block-level sketch for "explain" or "describe" questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEPOT861 Module 3**

### Automotive Industry Key Points:
- [ ] **Body-in-White (BIW)** is the main adhesive-intensive assembly stage.
- [ ] **Hem flange** bonding uses **toughened epoxy or PU**; cured in e-coat oven at **180°C / 20–30 min**.
- [ ] **Windshield** uses **one-component moisture-cure PU** with elongation 300–500%.
- [ ] **EV battery packs** use **thermally conductive epoxy/PU** for cell-to-cooling-plate bonding.
- [ ] Adhesives reduce vehicle weight by **15–25%** vs. all-welded/riveted construction.
- [ ] Crash energy management uses **toughness integral $G_c$** of adhesive.
- [ ] Automotive cycle time constraint: **60–90 seconds** in paint oven.

### Aerospace Industry Key Points:
- [ ] **Boeing 787 uses adhesive bonding in >50%** of primary structure.
- [ ] **Honeycomb sandwich panels** are the dominant adhesive application; use **epoxy film adhesives (FM-300, AF-163)**.
- [ ] **Surface preparation is critical** — chromic acid anodizing (CAA) + sol-gel primer (e.g., BR-127) is the aerospace standard.
- [ ] **BMI adhesives**: service up to **230°C**, used in military/fighter aircraft.
- [ ] **Cyanate esters**: service up to **250°C+**, used in spacecraft, low moisture absorption.
- [ ] **Epoxy film adhesives** dominate at **30–50 MPa lap-shear** strength.
- [ ] **Fuel tank sealing** uses **polysulfide or fluorosilicone** sealants.
- [ ] Failure analysis emphasizes **environmental degradation** (humidity, jet fuel, thermal cycling).

### Cross-Cutting Concepts to Memorize:
- [ ] **Lap-shear equation:** $\tau = F/A$ — fundamental to ALL joint calculations.
- [ ] **CTE mismatch equation:** $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ — explains thermal stress in metal-composite joints.
- [ ] **Volkersen analysis** is the analytical basis for long lap joints under thermal load.
- [ ] **Galvanic corrosion** must be prevented at aluminum-CFRP interfaces via proper primer selection.
- [ ] **Peel strength** is often the limiting factor in flexible adherends; design to minimize peel via scarf or stepped-lap geometry.
- [ ] The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **sustainability**: bio-based epoxies and recyclable thermoplastic adhesives are emerging trends worth mentioning in 14-mark questions for extra credit.

### Critical Numerical Values to Recall:
- Tensile shear strength range: **20–50 MPa** (industry standard)
- Service temp ranges: **$-40$ to $+180^\circ C$** (auto), **$-55$ to $+230^\circ C$** (aero)
- Adhesive volume in B787: **~800 kg per aircraft**
- Adhesive weight per car: **15–20 kg**
- Safety factor for adhesive joints: **1.5 to 3.0** (depending on criticality)

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" or "contrast" automotive and aerospace adhesives in a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as a **table first, then explanatory paragraphs**. Tables are visually scannable for examiners and demonstrate systematic thinking — they typically earn an extra 1–2 marks over pure prose answers.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Adhesives in Building Construction** are specially formulated polymeric bonding agents designed to develop load-bearing or sealing joints between dissimilar or similar civil engineering substrates such as concrete, masonry, steel, wood, glass, ceramics, and insulation panels, while withstanding structural, thermal, chemical, and environmental stresses during the service life of the building.

In the context of KTU 2024 syllabus, construction adhesives are classified as **structural adhesives** (load-bearing, e.g., epoxy, structural polyurethane) and **non-structural adhesives** (sealing, cosmetic, or panel-locking, e.g., silicone sealants, mastics, tile adhesives).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a building like a giant **Lego model** held together not just by friction (like the studs on Lego bricks) but by a chemical "magic glue" that welds the bricks into a single monolithic block. Traditional construction relied on **mechanical fastening** (nails, bolts, rivets, welding) — these create stress concentrations at fastener points. Modern construction adhesives instead distribute the load **uniformly across the entire bonded area**, much like how double-sided tape on a bathroom mirror holds it more uniformly and with less stress than screws.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEPOT861 Module 3):**
> Students must be able to *classify, select, and justify* adhesives for distinct construction scenarios: structural joints, tile fixing, flooring, façade panels, roofing, and concrete repair.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Board-Exam Essential)

- **Tensile Bond Strength**: typically **> 1.5 MPa** for non-structural and **> 10 MPa** for structural grades
- **Open Time**: working time before assembly (5–60 min)
- **Service Temperature Range**: **−40 °C to +80 °C** for general, **up to 200 °C** for high-temp silicones
- **Elongation at Break**: 50–600 % (varies by chemistry)
- **Cure Time**: 24 h handling, 7 d full strength (typical epoxy)

## 1.4 Classification at a Glance

| Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| **Chemistry** | Epoxy, Polyurethane (PU), Acrylic, Silicone, Phenolic, Polyvinyl Acetate (PVA), Cement-Polymer |
| **Load Capacity** | Structural vs Non-Structural |
| **Cure Mechanism** | Thermosetting, Thermoplastic, Pressure-Sensitive |
| **Substrate Pair** | Concrete-Concrete, Wood-Wood, Metal-Metal, Mixed |
| **Form** | Paste, Liquid, Tape, Film, Mastic |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Mechanism of Construction Adhesive Bonding

The bonded joint in a construction assembly develops strength through a **synergistic combination** of the following mechanisms operating in parallel:

1. **Mechanical Interlocking** — Adhesive flows into surface pores and micro-roughness of the substrate; upon curing, it forms a 3D "anchor." For concrete, surface roughness profile **Ra** typically must be **> 75 µm** for mechanical keying.
2. **Adsorption (Wetting) Theory** — The liquid adhesive must **wet the substrate** to develop van der Waals forces. This is governed by the **Young-Dupré equation** (see formula sheet).
3. **Chemical Bonding** — Reactive groups (–OH, –NCO, –COOH) covalently bond to substrate functional groups (e.g., –OH in concrete and wood).
4. **Diffusion Theory** — For polymer-polymer joints, inter-diffusion of chain segments across the interface creates an "interphase."
5. **Electrostatic Interaction** — Charge transfer at metal-adhesive interfaces contributes minor strength.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Adhesive Selection in Construction

Construction environments expose joints to a uniquely punishing combination of:
- **Cyclic thermal stress** (diurnal and seasonal)
- **Moisture ingress** (rain, groundwater, humidity)
- **Alkaline substrate attack** (concrete pH = 12.5–13.5)
- **UV exposure** (facades, roofing)
- **Dynamic mechanical loading** (wind, seismic, footfall)
- **Differential thermal expansion** between bonded materials (e.g., concrete α = 10×10⁻⁶ /K vs steel α = 12×10⁻⁶ /K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An adhesive must be selected such that its **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** lies *below* the lowest expected service temperature, otherwise the joint becomes brittle and cracks under sub-Tg thermal cycling.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are board-exam relevant. Memorize the symbols, units, and the physical meaning of each.

| Formula | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lap Shear Stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{b \cdot L}$ | $\tau$ = shear stress [MPa], $P$ = applied load [N], $b$ = bond width [mm], $L$ = overlap length [mm] | Single-lap joint strength |
| **Peel Force (T-peel)** | $F = \dfrac{w \cdot G_c}{1 - \cos\theta}$ | $F$ = peel force per width [N/mm], $w$ = width, $G_c$ = critical strain energy release rate [N/mm], $\theta$ = peel angle | Wallpaper, panel, membrane peel strength |
| **Young-Dupré (Wetting)** | $W_a = \gamma_L \left(1 + \cos\theta\right)$ | $W_a$ = work of adhesion [mJ/m²], $\gamma_L$ = surface energy of liquid [mJ/m²], $\theta$ = contact angle [°] | Predicts whether adhesive will wet substrate |
| **Thermal Stress in Joint** | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $\sigma_T$ = thermal stress [MPa], $E$ = Young's modulus [MPa], $\Delta\alpha$ = differential expansion coeff [1/K], $\Delta T$ = temperature change [K] | Joint design under thermal cycling |
| **Lap Shear (corrected)** | $P_{\max} = \tau \cdot b \cdot L \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = joint efficiency factor (0.3–0.7) | Accounts for stress concentration at lap ends |
| **Cure Shrinkage Stress** | $\sigma_s = E \cdot \varepsilon_{sh}$ | $\varepsilon_{sh}$ = volumetric shrinkage strain [-] | Predicts internal stress during cure |
| **Modulus Ratio Limit** | $\dfrac{E_a}{E_s} \leq 1.0$ | $E_a$ = adhesive modulus, $E_s$ = substrate modulus | Rule of thumb to minimize peel stress |

## 2.4 Why This System Matters in Civil Engineering

- **Reduces dead load** by eliminating the need for thick fasteners and connector plates.
- **Improves aesthetics** by hiding fixings (curtain wall, glass facades).
- **Provides sealing + bonding** in one operation (PU sealants, silicone).
- **Enables dissimilar material joining** (e.g., FRP-strengthening strips to concrete in retrofitting).
- **Corrosion-free**: unlike steel bolts, adhesive joints do not corrode — critical in coastal/marine construction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Lap Length for a Structural Single-Lap Joint

The classical **Volkersen analysis** for a single-lap adhesive joint gives the shear stress distribution along the overlap. We will derive the **minimum overlap length** beyond which the joint cannot be made stronger by simply adding more bonded area.

### Step 1 — Define the Shear-Lag Equation

The differential shear strain across the adhesive layer is governed by the relative displacement of the two adherends.

$$
\tau(x) = \dfrac{G_a}{t_a} \left[ u_1(x) - u_2(x) \right]
$$

where:
- $\tau(x)$ = shear stress in adhesive at position $x$ along overlap
- $G_a$ = shear modulus of adhesive [MPa]
- $t_a$ = adhesive layer thickness [mm]
- $u_1(x)$, $u_2(x)$ = displacements of upper and lower adherends at $x$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium of the Adherend

For the upper adherend under axial load $P(x)$:

$$
\dfrac{dP_1(x)}{dx} = b \cdot \tau(x)
$$

where $b$ = width of joint.

### Step 3 — Constitutive Equation for Adherend

$$
\dfrac{du_1}{dx} = \dfrac{P_1(x)}{E_1 A_1}
$$

where $E_1$ and $A_1$ are the Young's modulus and cross-sectional area of adherend 1.

### Step 4 — Combine and Differentiate

Differentiating the constitutive equation and combining with the equilibrium and shear-lag equations:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 \tau(x)}{dx^2} - \lambda^2 \, \tau(x) = 0
$$

where the **shear-lag parameter** is:

$$
\lambda^2 = \dfrac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \dfrac{1}{E_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{E_2 A_2} \right)
$$

### Step 5 — General Solution

$$
\tau(x) = C_1 \cosh(\lambda x) + C_2 \sinh(\lambda x)
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions

At the ends of the overlap $x = 0$ and $x = L$:

- $P_1(0) = P_0$, $P_2(L) = P_0$
- $P_1(L) = 0$, $P_2(0) = 0$

Solving yields the **peak shear stress at the lap end**:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda P_0}{2 b} \coth\left( \dfrac{\lambda L}{2} \right)
$$

### Step 7 — Critical Lap Length

The **critical overlap length $L_c$** is defined as the value beyond which the increase in average strength is negligible. Setting $\tau_{\max} = \tau_y$ (yield strength of adhesive):

$$
L_c = \dfrac{2}{\lambda} \cosh^{-1}\left( \dfrac{2 b \, \tau_y}{\lambda P_0} \right)
$$

In practice, the engineering rule is:

$$
L_c \approx \dfrac{2}{\lambda} \quad \text{(for} \; \lambda L \to \infty \text{)}
$$

This shows that making the lap longer than $L_c$ wastes material; designers typically use $L \approx L_c$ for cost-effective joints.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A steel-steel single-lap joint uses an epoxy adhesive. Given:
- $E_1 = E_2 = 200 \, 000$ MPa, $t = 5$ mm (adherend thickness)
- $G_a = 800$ MPa, $t_a = 0.2$ mm (adhesive)
- $b = 25$ mm, $P_0 = 10\,000$ N
- Find the shear-lag parameter $\lambda$ and critical lap length $L_c$.

### Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area

$$
A_1 = A_2 = b \cdot t = 25 \times 5 = 125 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\lambda^2$

$$
\lambda^2 = \dfrac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \dfrac{2}{E \cdot A} \right)
$$

$$
\lambda^2 = \dfrac{800}{0.2} \left( \dfrac{2}{200\,000 \times 125} \right)
$$

$$
\lambda^2 = 4000 \times \dfrac{2}{25\,000\,000}
$$

$$
\lambda^2 = 4000 \times 8 \times 10^{-8} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mm}^{-2}
$$

$$
\lambda = 1.789 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{mm}^{-1}
$$

### Step 3 — Critical Lap Length

$$
L_c = \dfrac{2}{\lambda} = \dfrac{2}{1.789 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 111.8 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

A lap length greater than **~112 mm** offers diminishing returns. A designer would choose **$L \approx 50$–60 mm** with end tapers (spew fillet) to reduce the peak $\tau$ at the lap ends, rather than extending the overlap indefinitely.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory aid for boards:** the **Volkersen analysis** is a guaranteed 7-mark question in ESE Module 3. Memorize (a) the governing ODE, (b) the form of the solution, and (c) the physical meaning of $\lambda$.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Adhesive Selection for Construction Substrates

| Substrate Pair | Recommended Adhesive | Why? | Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Concrete–Concrete** | Epoxy or Polymer-modified cement | High cohesive strength, alkaline resistance | PVA (degrades in alkaline) |
| **Steel–Concrete** | Epoxy paste | Wets metal, bonds to mineral | Silicone (no load capacity) |
| **Wood–Wood (load)** | Phenol-Resorcinol / PU | Waterproof, creep-resistant | PVA (moisture-sensitive) |
| **Glass–Metal (curtain wall)** | Structural Silicone (SSG) | UV stable, flexible, transparent | Epoxy (yellows, brittle) |
| **Ceramic Tile–Cement board** | Cementitious (C2TE) per EN 12004 | Thermal compatibility, no slip | Solvent-based rubber (creep) |
| **PVC Pipe–PVC Pipe** | Solvent cement (PVC-S) | Chemical fusion of surface | Epoxy (poor on plastic) |
| **EPDM Membrane–Concrete** | PU or neoprene contact adhesive | Flexible, weather-resistant | Epoxy (rigid) |
| **FRP Strip–Concrete (retrofit)** | Epoxy impregnation resin | Saturates fiber, bonds to concrete | PU (poor wet-out) |
| **Insulation Board–Wall** | PU foam adhesive | Cures with slight expansion, fills gaps | Structural epoxy (over-engineered) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Construction Adhesive System

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how a construction adhesive system is selected, applied, and validated on site. This block-level representation is more useful than a physical sketch because it captures the decision logic KTU examiners test in theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Requirement] --> B[Substrate Identification]
    B --> C[Surface Preparation]
    C --> D[Adhesive Chemistry Selection]
    D --> E[Application Method]
    E --> F[Curing Stage]
    F --> G[Quality Control Test]
    G --> H{Test Result}
    H -->|Pass| I[In-Service Loading]
    H -->|Fail| J[Re-work Process]
    J --> C
    I --> K[Long-term Monitoring]
    K --> L[End of Service Life]
    L --> M[De-bonding and Recycle]

    A1[Load Type: Static / Dynamic / Cyclic] --> A
    A2[Environment: Temp, Moisture, UV, Chemicals] --> A

    B1[Concrete / Masonry / Steel / Wood / Glass / Plastic] --> B

    C1[Cleaning: Solvent wipe, Shot blast] --> C
    C2[Priming: Silane, Epoxy primer] --> C
    C3[Profiling: Grinding, Acid etch] --> C

    D1[Structural Epoxy for high load] --> D
    D2[PU for flexible joints] --> D
    D3[Silicone for sealing] --> D
    D4[Cement-polymer for tiles] --> D
    D5[Phenolic for wood] --> D

    E1[Hand cartridge] --> E
    E2[Bead extrusion] --> E
    E3[Notched trowel] --> E
    E4[Spray] --> E

    F1[Room temp cure 24 h] --> F
    F2[Heat accelerated] --> F
    F3[Moisture cure] --> F

    G1[Pull-off test] --> G
    G2[Lap shear test] --> G
    G3[Peel test] --> G
    G4[Visual inspection] --> G
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Single-Lap Adhesive Joint with Stress Distribution

The following diagram illustrates the **non-uniform shear stress distribution** predicted by the Volkersen analysis. Note the stress peaks at the two ends of the overlap and the low-stress region in the middle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph J[Single-Lap Adhesive Joint]
        S1[Adherend 1: Steel] -->|Bonded area| S2[Adherend 2: Steel]
        S1 -.->|Peak shear τ_max| S2
        S1 -.->|Low shear center| S2
        S1 -.->|Peak shear τ_max| S2
    end

    Load[Applied Tensile Load P] --> S1
    S2 --> Reaction[Reaction at Fixture]

    A1[Concentrated stress at lap end] --> Stress
    A2[Uniform only at infinite lap] --> Stress
    Stress[Stress concentration drives failure] --> Failure
    Failure[Joint failure initiates at lap end] --> Cone[Spew fillet: Tapered end geometry reduces peak τ]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Multi-Stage Cure Profile of a Two-Part Epoxy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Mix two-part epoxy: Resin + Hardener] --> N[Liquid phase: 0 to 30 min]
    N --> O[Working time / open time: 30 to 90 min]
    O --> P[Green strength: 4 to 8 h]
    P --> Q[Handling strength: 24 h]
    Q --> R[Full mechanical strength: 7 days]
    R --> S[Post-cure optional: 80 °C for 2 h]
    S --> T[Service: stable until Tg is reached]

    T1[Exothermic peak temperature occurs at 60 to 90 min] --> N
    T2[Shrinkage stress peaks at gelation] --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a process state. The arrows indicate the temporal sequence. Side-inputs (e.g., T1, T2) are critical **KTU 2024 exam-relevant annotations** that highlight non-obvious physical phenomena the examiner expects you to mention in a 14-mark question.
