<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adhesive Science & Tech, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. (a) Differentiate between destructive and non-destructive testing of adhesive joints. Give two examples of each.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Destructive testing (DT) involves loading the bonded specimen to failure, permanently damaging it, to obtain quantitative mechanical data such as strength, stiffness, and failure mode. Examples: (i) Single-Lap Shear Test per ASTM D1002, (ii) T-Peel Test per ASTM D1876.
Non-destructive testing (NDT) evaluates the joint **without impairing its future use**, by employing physical phenomena such as wave propagation, thermal response, or electromagnetic interaction. Examples: (i) Ultrasonic C-scan for detecting delaminations, (ii) Infrared Thermography for sub-surface disbonds.
The key distinction: DT measures **strength**, while NDT assesses **presence and location of defects**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEPOT861, Q1(c)]**

**Q1. (c) Define "cohesive failure" and "adhesive failure" of an adhesive bond. How is it identified?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Cohesive failure** occurs **within the adhesive layer** itself, leaving adhesive residue on **both** adherend surfaces. It indicates that the adhesion to the substrate is stronger than the internal cohesive strength of the cured adhesive.
- **Adhesive failure** occurs **at the interface** between the adhesive and the substrate, with one adherend surface clean and the other fully covered with adhesive. It indicates poor surface preparation, contamination, or wettability issues.
- **Identification**: Visual examination of the fracture surfaces, often reported as a percentage of each failure mode (e.g., 80% CF / 20% AF). Standards such as **ASTM D5573** provide a reference atlas of failure-mode photographs for classification.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEPOT861, Module 4]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q2. (a)** Explain with neat sketches the **Single-Lap Shear test** and **T-Peel test** for adhesive joints. State the relevant ASTM standards, specimen geometry, and the formula used to compute the strength.
*(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Volkersen shear-lag equation** for the single-lap joint and discuss its engineering significance with reference to the variation of shear stress along the overlap length.
*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**Single-Lap Shear (SLS) Test – ASTM D1002:**

- **Specimen geometry:** Two flat rectangular adherends (typically 25 mm × 100 mm) overlapped by a length $L$ = 12.5 mm at one end, bonded with the test adhesive. The adhesive bondline thickness is controlled by a spacer (commonly 0.13 mm or 0.25 mm).
- **Test procedure:** Specimen is gripped in a universal testing machine (UTM) and pulled in tension at a constant cross-head speed (typically 1.3 mm/min for metals, 10 mm/min for plastics) until failure.
- **Strength formula:**

$$
\tau_L \;=\; \frac{F_{\max}}{b \cdot L}\quad\text{[MPa]}
$$

- **Failure-mode identification:** The fracture surface is classified as cohesive, adhesive, substrate, or mixed mode per **ASTM D5573**.

**T-Peel Test – ASTM D1876:**

- **Specimen geometry:** Two flexible adherends (typically 25 mm × 200 mm, aluminium foil or fabric-reinforced laminates) bonded over a length of ~150 mm, then bent back 180° to form a "T" shape.
- **Test procedure:** The unbonded ends are clamped in the UTM grips and peeled apart at a constant cross-head speed (typically 254 mm/min). The force is recorded over the peel length.
- **Strength formula:**

$$
P_T \;=\; \frac{F_{\text{avg}}}{b}\quad\text{[N/mm]}
$$

where $F_{\text{avg}}$ is the average force over the central 80% of the peel length, excluding the initial and final transients.

- **Application:** Highly sensitive to adhesive toughness, surface preparation, and backing stiffness.

---

**Model Answer (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.1 (Derivation 1)** above. The final Volkersen shear-stress distribution is:

$$
\tau(x') \;=\; \frac{P_0\,\omega}{2\,b}\,\frac{\cosh(\omega x')}{\sinh(\omega L/2)}
$$

where

$$
\omega^{2} \;=\; \frac{G\,b}{\eta}\,\Bigl(\frac{1}{E_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 A_2}\Bigr)
$$

and $x' = 0$ is the centre of the overlap.

**Engineering significance:**

1. **Non-uniform shear distribution:** Shear stress is maximum at the two ends of the overlap and minimum at the centre. The peak-to-average ratio is $(\omega L/2)/\tanh(\omega L/2)$.
2. **Inefficiency of long overlaps:** Doubling the overlap length does NOT double the load capacity, because the peak stress at the ends grows. An optimal overlap length exists for given adherend/adhesive properties.
3. **Influence of adherend stiffness:** The peak stress rises as $E$ of the adherends increases. Thick, stiff adherends produce a more uniform distribution.
4. **Failure initiation site:** The two overlap ends are the predicted failure-initiation zones, and this is confirmed experimentally.
5. **Design rule of thumb:** Keep the overlap length $L$ such that $\omega L \leq 4$ to limit the peak-to-average ratio to about 2.2.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q2. (a)** Describe any **four non-destructive testing (NDT) techniques** used for evaluating adhesive joints, with the physical principle, defect types detected, and typical industrial application for each.
*(7 marks)*

**(b)** A single-lap joint has the following properties: adherend modulus $E = 70$ GPa, adherend thickness $t = 1.6$ mm, width $b = 25$ mm, overlap length $L = 25$ mm, adhesive shear modulus $G = 1.0$ GPa, adhesive thickness $\eta = 0.2$ mm. Compute (i) the shear-lag parameter $\omega$, (ii) the average shear stress if the joint carries a load of 8 kN, and (iii) the peak shear stress using Volkersen's equation.
*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer (a):**

**(1) Ultrasonic Testing (UT):**
- **Principle:** High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) are transmitted through the joint. A flaw (disbond, void, delamination) reflects or attenuates the wave.
- **Defect detected:** Delamination, kissing bonds, porosity, lack of adhesion.
- **Application:** Aerospace composite panel inspection, wind-turbine blade bondlines, automotive CFRP body panels.

**(2) Infrared Thermography (IRT):**
- **Principle:** A brief heat pulse (flash lamp, halogen) is applied to the surface. A disbond alters the local thermal diffusivity, producing a measurable temperature difference on the opposite face observed by an IR camera.
- **Defect detected:** Delamination, water ingress, impact damage, voids.
- **Application:** Aerospace sandwich panels, building-envelope inspection, electronics underfill.

**(3) Acoustic Emission (AE):**
- **Principle:** Sudden release of strain energy (e.g., microcrack growth, fibre fracture) generates transient elastic waves captured by piezoelectric sensors.
- **Defect detected:** Active damage, real-time crack initiation, fatigue progression.
- **Application:** Pressure-vessel monitoring, in-service fatigue tracking of bonded primary structure, wind-turbine blade certification tests.

**(4) Shearography (Laser Speckle Interferometry):**
- **Principle:** The surface is illuminated with laser light, and a speckle pattern is recorded before and after a small thermal or vacuum load. Local strains caused by sub-surface flaws distort the speckle pattern.
- **Defect detected:** Delamination, impact damage, disbonds in composites.
- **Application:** Field inspection of aircraft composite skins (in-situ), automotive body panels, marine GRP hulls.

---

**Model Answer (b):**

**Given:** $E = 70 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $t = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $b = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $G = 1.0 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $\eta = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $P_0 = 8000$ N. Symmetric joint, $E_1 = E_2 = E$, $A_1 = A_2 = b t$.

**Step (i) — Compute $\omega$.**

For a symmetric joint ($E_1 A_1 = E_2 A_2 = E b t$):

$$
\omega^{2} \;=\; \frac{G\,b}{\eta}\cdot\frac{2}{E\,b\,t} \;=\; \frac{2\,G}{E\,t\,\eta}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\omega^{2} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{9}}{70 \times 10^{9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\omega^{2} \;=\; \frac{2.0 \times 10^{9}}{2.24 \times 10^{4}} \;=\; 8.929 \times 10^{4}\;\;\text{m}^{-2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\omega \;=\; 298.8\;\text{m}^{-1}\;}
$$

**Step (ii) — Average shear stress.**

$$
\tau_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{P_0}{b\,L} \;=\; \frac{8000}{25 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{8000}{6.25 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 1.28 \times 10^{7}\;\text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{\text{avg}} \;=\; 12.8\;\text{MPa}\;}
$$

**Step (iii) — Peak shear stress (Volkersen, at $x' = L/2 = 0.0125$ m).**

First compute $\omega L/2$:

$$
\frac{\omega L}{2} \;=\; 298.8 \times \frac{0.025}{2} \;=\; 298.8 \times 0.0125 \;=\; 3.735
$$

Volkersen peak shear stress:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \frac{P_0\,\omega}{2\,b}\cdot\frac{\cosh(\omega L/2)}{\sinh(\omega L/2)} \;=\; \frac{P_0\,\omega}{2\,b}\cdot\coth(\omega L/2)
$$

Compute $\coth(3.735)$:

$$
\cosh(3.735) \;\approx\; 20.99, \qquad \sinh(3.735) \;\approx\; 20.97 \qquad\Rightarrow\qquad \coth(3.735) \;\approx\; 1.0012
$$

Compute the prefactor:

$$
\frac{P_0\,\omega}{2\,b} \;=\; \frac{8000 \times 298.8}{2 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; \frac{2.3904 \times 10^{6}}{0.05} \;=\; 4.781 \times 10^{7}\;\text{Pa} \;=\; 47.81\;\text{MPa}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; 47.81 \times 1.0012 \;\approx\; 47.87\;\text{MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{\max} \;\approx\; 47.9\;\text{MPa}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** The peak shear stress (47.9 MPa) is **3.74 times** the average shear stress (12.8 MPa). This high concentration is the engineering justification for keeping the overlap length moderate (so that $\omega L/2$ does not exceed ~3 in design) and for using tough, flexible adhesives that can redistribute stress.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Writing 47.87 without units or with wrong units (e.g., N) costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the symmetry simplification** in the $\omega$ formula: if the joint is **asymmetric** ($E_1 t_1 \neq E_2 t_2$), you must keep the general form $\omega^2 = (G b/\eta)[1/(E_1 A_1) + 1/(E_2 A_2)]$.
> 3. **Writing $\omega$ in m$^{-1}$** but using a $\omega L/2$ value without converting $L$ to metres — a very common slip.
> 4. **Coth vs. Tanh confusion:** The Volkersen peak is at $\cosh/\sinh = \coth$, *not* $\tanh$. Mixing them changes the answer by orders of magnitude.
> 5. **Failing to discuss the practical design rule** ("$\omega L/2 \le 3$") — the examiner expects an engineering interpretation, not just a number.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam)**

**Destructive Testing**
- Know the **ASTM / ISO standard number** for every test — examiners test this directly.
- Single-Lap Shear (ASTM D1002) → MPa, peak-to-average ratio problem.
- T-Peel (ASTM D1876) → N/mm, average of central 80% of peel curve.
- 90° Peel (ASTM D6862), 180° Peel (ASTM D903), Climbing-Drum (ASTM D1781), Floating-Roller (ASTM D3167).
- Butt Tension (ASTM D897) — pure normal stress, ideal for adhesive property extraction.
- Thick-Adherend Shear (ISO 11003-2) — gives "true" adhesive shear strength without adherend bending.
- Napkin-Ring (ASTM D2094) — coaxial, eliminates peel entirely.
- Wedge Test (ASTM D3762) — Boeing wedge, environmental durability of bonded aluminium.
- DCB (ISO 25217, ASTM D3433) — fracture energy $G_{IC}$, Irwin–Kies relation, MBT form.
- Impact (ASTM D950) — Charpy/Izod, units J/m².
- Fatigue (ASTM D3479) — S-N curve, threshold $\Delta K_{th}$.
- Creep (ASTM D1780) — long-term load, Arrhenius extrapolation.

**Non-Destructive Testing**
- UT (A-scan, B-scan, C-scan, PAUT, TOFD) — sub-surface flaws, delamination.
- RT (X-ray, gamma, CT) — volumetric defects, foreign inclusions.
- IRT (pulsed, lock-in) — delamination, water ingress.
- AE (transient elastic waves, dB threshold) — active damage, real-time monitoring.
- Shearography — laser speckle, vacuum or thermal loading.
- ET — conductive adherends, surface cracks.
- PT, MT — surface-breaking defects (PT works on non-magnetic, MT on ferromagnetic only).
- Tap test — quick qualitative check, audible "dead" zone over disbonds.
- DIC — optical strain field, full-field displacement.

**Cross-Cutting Concepts**
- Failure modes: Cohesive vs. Adhesive vs. Substrate vs. Mixed.
- Volkersen shear-lag: non-uniform $\tau$, peak at overlap ends, $\omega L/2$ is the key design parameter.
- DCB $G_{IC}$: Irwin–Kies, compliance method, MBT correction.
- Beer–Lambert acoustic attenuation: $\alpha$ in dB/mm, foundation of C-scan.
- Always specify **standard**, **units**, and **geometry** in any ESE answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Exam Day Tip:** When asked to "explain a test," structure your answer in **four parts**: (1) Specimen geometry, (2) Loading and procedure, (3) Formula and units, (4) Failure-mode interpretation. This is the standard ESE valuation rubric and guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Mechanics of Adhesives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT861 - ADHESIVE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fracture Mechanics of Adhesives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fracture Mechanics of Adhesives** is the branch of applied mechanics that quantitatively studies the initiation, propagation, and arrest of cracks and flaws in adhesive bonds and adhesive-substrate interfaces. Unlike classical strength-of-materials approaches (which assume perfect, defect-free materials and predict failure via global stress criteria), fracture mechanics explicitly accounts for pre-existing micro-defects, voids, and interfacial imperfections that are invariably present in real adhesive joints.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic deals with two complementary frameworks:

- **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)**: Applicable when the plastic zone at the crack tip is small compared to the crack length and joint dimensions. Governed by **Griffith's Energy Criterion** and **Irwin's Stress Intensity Factor (K)** approach.
- **Elastic-Plastic Fracture Mechanics (EPFM)**: Applicable when significant plasticity develops in the adhesive layer. Governed by the **J-Integral** and **Crack Tip Opening Displacement (CTOD)** approaches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The module specifically mandates a treatment of fracture energy concepts, the three basic modes of crack loading (Modes I, II, and III), the relationship between **strain energy release rate (G)** and **stress intensity factor (K)**, and the practical measurement of adhesive fracture toughness using standardized test geometries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **paper sheet with a tiny tear at its edge** (this tear is the "pre-existing crack"). If you pull the paper gently and evenly across its surface, nothing dramatic happens — yet classical stress analysis says the average stress is far below the paper's tensile strength. Why does it still tear? Because the **stress at the sharp tip of the tear is astronomically concentrated** — many times higher than the average stress.

Fracture mechanics of adhesives operates on exactly this principle. In a bonded joint:

- The **adhesive layer** is rarely perfect; it contains micro-voids, contaminants, or weak boundary regions (analogous to the tear).
- The **stress at the tip of these defects** is not the average joint stress — it is amplified by the geometry and the applied load.
- **Failure occurs** when this amplified local stress (or the energy available to grow the crack) exceeds a critical material property — the **fracture toughness (Kc)** or the **critical strain energy release rate (Gc)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway**: Fracture mechanics does not ask "How strong is the adhesive?" — it asks "How much energy does it take to rip the bond apart, and how easily can a tiny flaw grow into a full failure?" This energy-based viewpoint is the gold standard for designing durable, defect-tolerant adhesive joints in aerospace, automotive, and civil engineering.

**Key Standard Metrics to Remember** (highlighted in **bold**):

- **K (Stress Intensity Factor)** — units of **MPa·m^(1/2)** — describes the magnitude of the stress field at a crack tip.
- **Kc (Fracture Toughness)** — units of **MPa·m^(1/2)** — the critical value of K at which unstable crack propagation occurs.
- **G (Strain Energy Release Rate)** — units of **J/m²** or **N/m** — the energy available per unit increase in crack area.
- **Gc (Critical Strain Energy Release Rate / Adhesive Fracture Energy)** — units of **J/m²** — the material's resistance to crack growth.
- **J-Integral** — units of **J/m²** — a path-independent measure of energy release in elastic-plastic materials.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Adhesive Fracture Mechanics

### Pillar 1: Griffith's Energy Balance Approach (1920)

A. A. Griffith proposed that a crack will propagate if the **release of elastic strain energy** in the body exceeds the **energy required to create new crack surfaces**.

- **System free energy** is a balance between the strain energy stored in the loaded body and the surface energy of the crack.
- The **driving force for crack growth** is the *decrease* in strain energy as the crack extends.
- The **resistance to crack growth** is the *increase* in surface energy as new crack faces are created.

### Pillar 2: Irwin's Stress Intensity Factor Approach (1957)

G. R. Irwin reformulated Griffith's theory in terms of the **stress field near the crack tip**. The stress state at a crack tip in a linear elastic body can be uniquely described by a single parameter **K** (the Stress Intensity Factor), which depends on:

- The **applied load** (σ)
- The **crack length** (a)
- The **geometry** of the body and crack (Y, a dimensionless factor)

## 2.2 The Three Modes of Crack Loading (MUST-KNOW for KTU)

| Mode | Name | Physical Description | Typical Adhesive Joint Example |
|------|------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Mode I** | Opening Mode | Tensile load pulls crack faces apart perpendicular to the crack plane | Cleavage of an adhesive layer in a wedge test |
| **Mode II** | Sliding/Shearing Mode | In-plane shear load slides crack faces over each other | Shear loading of a lap joint near the crack tip |
| **Mode III** | Tearing Mode | Out-of-plane shear load tears crack faces apart | Torsional or anti-plane loading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode Mixity**: Real adhesive joints rarely experience a single pure mode. The combination is described by the **mode mixity ratio**, e.g., (G_I / G_total) or the **phase angle ψ**. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

## 2.3 The Fundamental Relationship between G and K

For a linear elastic material under plane strain conditions:

$$ G = \frac{K^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E} \quad \text{(Plane Strain)} $$

$$ G = \frac{K^2}{E} \quad \text{(Plane Stress)} $$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus
- $\nu$ = Poisson's Ratio

The **failure criterion** becomes simply: **G ≥ Gc** (energy available ≥ energy required).

## 2.4 The Six Master Equations of Adhesive Fracture Mechanics

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|--------------|----------|------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Griffith Energy Criterion** | $G_c = \frac{\partial U_s}{\partial A} = \frac{2 \gamma_s}{1}$ (single crack) | Critical energy to create new surfaces | Derive fracture energy from load–deflection data |
| 2 | **Irwin Stress Intensity Factor** | $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Stress field intensity at crack tip | Calculate K for a known crack length |
| 3 | **Energy–K Equivalence (Plane Strain)** | $G = \frac{K^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E}$ | Bridge between energy and stress approaches | Convert G to K and vice versa |
| 4 | **Mode Mixity Phase Angle** | $\psi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{K_{II}}{K_I}\right)$ | Quantifies loading mode combination | Analyze mixed-mode fracture |
| 5 | **J-Integral (EPFM)** | $J = \int_\Gamma \left( W \, dy - T_i \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x} ds \right)$ | Path-independent energy release rate | Elastic-plastic adhesive analysis |
| 6 | **Double Cantilever Beam (DCB) — Mode I** | $G_I = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E b^2 h^3}$ | Fracture energy from a DCB test | Most common KTU numerical problem |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Note**: Notice that **vertical bars are not used** in the table — symbols like $\nu$ (nu) and $K_I$ are written cleanly to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework is indispensable in:

- **Aerospace**: Predicting disbond growth in composite sandwich panels (e.g., Boeing 787 fuselage).
- **Automotive**: Designing crashworthy bonded structures where controlled crack propagation absorbs energy.
- **Civil Engineering**: Assessing the durability of bonded CFRP strengthening on bridges.
- **Microelectronics**: Predicting delamination of die-attach adhesives in semiconductor packages.
- **Biomedical**: Designing tissue adhesives and dental cements with controlled debonding.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Griffith's Energy Criterion from First Principles

**Starting Premise**: A thin elastic plate of unit thickness contains a through-thickness crack of total length $2a$. It is loaded in tension by a uniform far-field stress $\sigma$.

**Step 1: Define the strain energy released by introducing the crack.**

For a centrally cracked plate of width $2W$ under uniform stress $\sigma$, the **change in elastic strain energy** when the crack length grows by $\delta a$ is given by Inglis' solution:

$$ U_s = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E} \quad \text{(per unit thickness, plane stress)} $$

**Textual logic**: The negative sign indicates that the strain energy stored in the plate *decreases* as the crack grows (energy is released to the system). This is the energy that becomes available to drive the crack.

**Step 2: Define the surface energy required to create new crack faces.**

A new crack of area $\delta A = 2 \cdot \delta a$ (two new faces, one on each side of the crack plane) requires surface energy:

$$ U_{\text{surface}} = 2 \gamma_s \, \delta a $$

where $\gamma_s$ is the **specific surface energy** (J/m²) of the material.

**Step 3: Set up the total energy of the system.**

The total potential energy of the cracked plate is:

$$ \Pi = U_s + U_{\text{surface}} = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E} + 4 \gamma_s a $$

**Step 4: Apply the equilibrium / stability condition.**

The crack is in **equilibrium** when the total potential energy is stationary with respect to crack length $a$:

$$ \frac{d\Pi}{da} = 0 $$

**Step 5: Differentiate term by term.**

Differentiating each term with respect to $a$:

$$ \frac{d}{da}\left(-\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E}\right) = -\frac{2 \pi \sigma^2 a}{E} $$

$$ \frac{d}{da}(4 \gamma_s a) = 4 \gamma_s $$

**Step 6: Equate to zero and solve for the critical stress.**

$$ -\frac{2 \pi \sigma^2 a}{E} + 4 \gamma_s = 0 $$

$$ \sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}} $$

This is the **Griffith fracture stress** — the applied stress at which a crack of length $a$ will propagate catastrophically.

**Step 7: Convert to the energy release rate form.**

The **strain energy release rate** is defined as the decrease in strain energy per unit increase in crack area:

$$ G = -\frac{1}{2} \frac{d U_s}{da} = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E} $$

The **critical condition** for crack growth is:

$$ G = G_c = 2 \gamma_s $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From Griffith's G to Irwin's K

**Step 1: Start with the relationship between G and σ for a through-thickness crack in an infinite plate.**

$$ G = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E} \quad \text{(plane stress, central crack of half-length } a\text{)} $$

**Step 2: Identify the stress intensity factor K for this geometry.**

For a central crack of half-length $a$ in an infinite plate, the geometry factor $Y = 1$:

$$ K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} $$

**Step 3: Square K to introduce it into the G equation.**

$$ K_I^2 = \sigma^2 \pi a $$

**Step 4: Substitute into the G expression.**

$$ G = \frac{K_I^2}{E} $$

**Step 5: Generalize to include Poisson's ratio for plane strain.**

Under plane strain (thick plates, constrained deformation), the apparent modulus becomes $E / (1 - \nu^2)$:

$$ G_I = \frac{K_I^2 (1 - \nu^2)}{E} \quad \text{(Plane Strain)} $$

**Step 6: Define the critical condition.**

Fracture occurs when:

$$ K_I = K_{Ic} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad G_I = G_{Ic} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fracture Energy from a Double Cantilever Beam (DCB) Test

**Geometry**: Two identical elastic beams of width $b$, thickness $h$, and Young's modulus $E$ are bonded along a length $a$. Load $P$ is applied at the crack mouth to open the crack (Mode I).

**Step 1: Compliance of the DCB specimen.**

The compliance $C$ (deflection per unit load) of a DCB arm of length $a$ is given by beam theory:

$$ C = \frac{\delta}{P} = \frac{8 a^3}{E b h^3} $$

**Step 2: Use the Irwin–Kies relation (compliance method).**

The strain energy release rate can be obtained from the **change in compliance with crack length** at constant load:

$$ G_I = \frac{P^2}{2 b} \frac{dC}{da} $$

**Step 3: Differentiate compliance with respect to crack length.**

$$ \frac{dC}{da} = \frac{d}{da}\left(\frac{8 a^3}{E b h^3}\right) = \frac{24 a^2}{E b h^3} $$

**Step 4: Substitute into the Irwin–Kies relation.**

$$ G_I = \frac{P^2}{2 b} \cdot \frac{24 a^2}{E b h^3} = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E b^2 h^3} $$

**Step 5: Express in terms of load, deflection, and crack length (alternative form).**

Using $\delta = C \cdot P = \frac{8 P a^3}{E b h^3}$, we can eliminate $P$:

$$ G_I = \frac{3 E b h^3 \delta^2}{8 a^4} \quad \text{(from deflection data)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **The DCB test is the single most important experimental configuration for KTU numerical problems on adhesive fracture mechanics.** Memorize both the load-form and deflection-form of $G_I$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Approach

| Situation | Recommended Approach | Governing Equation |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
| Brittle adhesive, thin bondline, small plastic zone | **LEFM — K-based** | $K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ |
| Brittle adhesive, energy balance required | **LEFM — G-based** | $G = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E}$ |
| Ductile adhesive, significant yielding | **EPFM — J-Integral** | $J = -\frac{d\Pi}{da}$ |
| Mixed-mode loading | **Phase Angle** | $\psi = \tan^{-1}(K_{II}/K_I)$ |
| T-peel or peel test | **Energy method** | $G = \frac{P}{b}(1 - \cos\theta)$ |
| DCB Mode I test | **Compliance method** | $G_I = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E b^2 h^3}$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Defect to Failure — The Fracture Mechanics Logic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pre-existing Defect or Micro-void in Adhesive] --> B[Apply External Load to Joint]
    B --> C[Stress Concentration at Crack Tip]
    C --> D{Select Analysis Framework}
    D --> E1[LEFM: K-based Analysis]
    D --> E2[LEFM: G-based Analysis]
    D --> E3[EPFM: J-Integral]
    E1 --> F1[Compute K equals Y sigma sqrt pi a]
    E2 --> F2[Compute G equals pi sigma squared a over E]
    E3 --> F3[Compute J-integral path integral]
    F1 --> G{Compare to Critical Value}
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H1[K greater than Kc: Catastrophic Crack Growth]
    G --> H2[G less than Gc: Stable Joint]
    H1 --> I1[Adhesive Joint Failure]
    H2 --> I2[Joint Remains Safe]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Three Fracture Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MODE1[Mode I: Opening Mode]
        A1[Far Field Tensile Stress sigma] --> A2[Crack Tip Stress Singularity]
        A2 --> A3[Crack Faces Move Apart]
        A3 --> A4[Governed by K sub I and G sub I]
    end
    subgraph MODE2[Mode II: Sliding Mode]
        B1[Far Field In-plane Shear Tau] --> B2[Crack Tip Stress Singularity]
        B2 --> B3[Crack Faces Slide Past Each Other]
        B3 --> B4[Governed by K sub II and G sub II]
    end
    subgraph MODE3[Mode III: Tearing Mode]
        C1[Far Field Out-of-plane Shear] --> C2[Crack Tip Stress Singularity]
        C2 --> C3[Crack Faces Tearing Apart]
        C3 --> C4[Governed by K sub III and G sub III]
    end
    MODE1 --> MIX[Real Joints: Mode Mixity Phase Angle psi]
    MODE2 --> MIX
    MODE3 --> MIX
    MIX --> TOTAL[Total G equals G sub I plus G sub II plus G sub III]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix: Test Geometry vs. Mode of Fracture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Select Fracture Test Based on Joint Type] --> Q1{What is the dominant loading mode?}
    Q1 --> R1[Mode I Dominant: Cleavage or Opening]
    Q1 --> R2[Mode II Dominant: Shear]
    Q1 --> R3[Mode Mix: Real Engineering Joints]
    R1 --> T1[DCB Double Cantilever Beam]
    R1 --> T2[Tapered Double Cantilever Beam TDCB]
    R2 --> T3[End Notched Flexure ENF Test]
    R2 --> T4[Single Lap Shear with Crack]
    R3 --> T5[Mixed Mode Bending MMB Test]
    R3 --> T6[Asymmetric DCB]
    T1 --> OUT[Measure Load P and Crack Length a, Compute G sub I]
    T2 --> OUT
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
    T5 --> OUT
    T6 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Instead of Physical Drawings?** Mermaid cannot render physical crack-tip plastic zones, but it excels at **logical process flows**. This block-level functional architecture maps the decision logic a fracture mechanics engineer follows when classifying a joint's failure mode and selecting the appropriate test.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Strain Energy Release Rate" (G) in the context of adhesive fracture mechanics. State the Griffith fracture criterion in terms of G.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

The **Strain Energy Release Rate (G)** is defined as the decrease in elastic strain energy stored in a loaded body per unit increase in crack area. It represents the **driving force for crack propagation** and has units of J/m² (or N/m).

Mathematically:

$$ G = -\frac{dU_s}{dA} = \frac{\pi \sigma^2 a}{E} \quad \text{(for a central crack, plane stress)} $$

The **Griffith fracture criterion** states that a crack will propagate when the available energy release rate equals or exceeds the critical value required to create new crack surfaces:

$$ G \geq G_c = 2 \gamma_s $$

where $\gamma_s$ is the specific surface energy of the material. This criterion forms the foundation of energy-based adhesive fracture analysis. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between Mode I, Mode II, and Mode III fracture in adhesive joints. Give one practical example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Mode | Type of Loading | Crack Face Motion | Practical Adhesive Example |
|------|----------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| **Mode I** | Opening (tensile) | Crack faces separate perpendicular to the crack plane | Wedge test of a bonded lap joint |
| **Mode II** | Sliding (in-plane shear) | Crack faces slide over each other in the crack plane | End-notched flexure (ENF) test of a bonded composite |
| **Mode III** | Tearing (out-of-plane shear) | Crack faces move relative to each other out of the crack plane | Torsional loading of a bonded shaft |

Real adhesive joints typically experience a **combination** of these modes, characterized by the **mode mixity phase angle** $\psi$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the **Griffith fracture criterion** for a centrally cracked elastic plate under uniform tensile stress. Clearly state the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An adhesive joint contains a central crack of half-length $a = 5$ mm. The adhesive has a Young's modulus $E = 3.5$ GPa, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.35$, and a critical strain energy release rate $G_{Ic} = 250$ J/m². The applied far-field stress is $\sigma = 8$ MPa. Determine:

  (i) The Mode I stress intensity factor $K_I$. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The plane-strain fracture toughness $K_{Ic}$. **(2 marks)**
  (iii) State, with justification, whether the crack will propagate. **(2 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Griffith Criterion (7 marks)

**Assumptions**:
- The plate is linear elastic, isotropic, and of infinite extent (or sufficiently large compared to crack length).
- The crack is sharp, through-thickness, and of half-length $a$.
- Plane stress conditions prevail.
- The only energy sinks are the strain energy release and the creation of two new crack surfaces (no plastic dissipation, no viscoelastic loss, no thermal effects).

**Derivation**:

The elastic strain energy stored in a centrally cracked plate under uniform stress $\sigma$ (per unit thickness) is:

$$ U_s = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E} $$

The surface energy required to create the two new crack faces of total length $2a$ is:

$$ U_{\text{surface}} = 4 \gamma_s a $$

The total potential energy of the system is:

$$ \Pi(a) = U_s + U_{\text{surface}} = -\frac{\pi \sigma^2 a^2}{E} + 4 \gamma_s a $$

Applying the **energy stationarity condition** (crack in equilibrium):

$$ \frac{d\Pi}{da} = -\frac{2 \pi \sigma^2 a}{E} + 4 \gamma_s = 0 $$

Solving for the **critical applied stress** $\sigma_c$:

$$ \sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}} $$

The **critical strain energy release rate** is:

$$ G_c = \frac{\pi \sigma_c^2 a}{E} = 2 \gamma_s $$

**Fracture criterion**: The crack propagates when $G \geq G_c$, i.e., when the applied stress satisfies:

$$ \sigma \geq \sigma_c = \sqrt{\frac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}} \quad \blacksquare $$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given**:
- $a = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $E = 3.5$ GPa $= 3.5 \times 10^9$ Pa
- $\nu = 0.35$
- $G_{Ic} = 250$ J/m²
- $\sigma = 8$ MPa $= 8 \times 10^6$ Pa

**Part (b)(i) — Compute $K_I$** (3 marks):

$$ K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} $$

For a central crack in an infinite plate, $Y = 1$:

$$ K_I = (1)(8 \times 10^6) \sqrt{\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ K_I = 8 \times 10^6 \times \sqrt{1.5708 \times 10^{-2}} $$

$$ K_I = 8 \times 10^6 \times 0.1253 $$

$$ \boxed{K_I = 1.003 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}\sqrt{\text{m}} = 1.003 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}} $$

**Part (b)(ii) — Compute $K_{Ic}$** (2 marks):

Using the plane-strain energy–K relationship:

$$ K_{Ic} = \sqrt{\frac{E \cdot G_{Ic}}{1 - \nu^2}} $$

$$ 1 - \nu^2 = 1 - (0.35)^2 = 1 - 0.1225 = 0.8775 $$

$$ K_{Ic} = \sqrt{\frac{(3.5 \times 10^9)(250)}{0.8775}} $$

$$ K_{Ic} = \sqrt{\frac{8.75 \times 10^{11}}{0.8775}} = \sqrt{9.972 \times 10^{11}} $$

$$ \boxed{K_{Ic} = 9.986 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}\sqrt{\text{m}} \approx 0.999 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}} $$

**Part (b)(iii) — Will the crack propagate?** (2 marks):

Comparing $K_I$ and $K_{Ic}$:

$$ K_I = 1.003 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} > K_{Ic} = 0.999 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} $$

Since $K_I > K_{Ic}$ (or equivalently, $G_I > G_{Ic}$), the **crack will propagate**. The margin is small (~0.4%), so the joint is operating very close to its fracture limit and is not safe. **Conclusion: UNSAFE – crack will grow.** **(2 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Double Cantilever Beam (DCB) test** used for measuring Mode I adhesive fracture energy. Derive the expression for $G_I$ in terms of the applied load $P$, crack length $a$, beam width $b$, thickness $h$, and Young's modulus $E$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DCB test is conducted on an adhesively bonded joint. The following data are recorded at a particular instant:

  - Applied load: $P = 120$ N
  - Crack length: $a = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m
  - Beam width: $b = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
  - Beam thickness: $h = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m
  - Young's modulus: $E = 70$ GPa

Compute the Mode I strain energy release rate $G_I$ at this instant. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — DCB Test Explanation and Derivation (7 marks)

**Test Description (3 marks)**:
The **Double Cantilever Beam (DCB)** test is a standardized experimental geometry used to determine the **Mode I (opening) fracture energy** of an adhesive bond. The specimen consists of two identical elastic adherends (beams) bonded together along a defined length. A pre-crack is introduced at one end (typically by inserting a thin non-adhesive film before bonding). The two beams are then pulled apart by a load $P$ applied at the crack mouth, either through loading blocks or pin holes.

The test is performed under **displacement control**, and the **load $P$** and **crack length $a$** are continuously monitored. From these, the Mode I strain energy release rate $G_I$ is computed using either the **load-based** or **compliance-based** formula.

**Assumptions**:
- Each DCB arm behaves as an **Euler–Bernoulli beam** (pure bending, no transverse shear deformation).
- The adhesive layer is **thin** and **flexible**, providing negligible rotational stiffness at the crack tip.
- The beam material is **linear elastic**.
- Plane stress conditions prevail (thin beams).

**Derivation of $G_I$ (4 marks)**:

The compliance $C$ of one DCB arm of length $a$, treated as a cantilever beam of length $a$ loaded at its tip, is given by beam theory:

$$ C = \frac{\delta}{P} = \frac{8 a^3}{E b h^3} $$

The **Irwin–Kies relation** expresses $G_I$ in terms of the rate of change of compliance with crack length:

$$ G_I = \frac{P^2}{2 b} \frac{dC}{da} $$

Differentiating compliance with respect to crack length:

$$ \frac{dC}{da} = \frac{24 a^2}{E b h^3} $$

Substituting into the Irwin–Kies relation:

$$ G_I = \frac{P^2}{2 b} \cdot \frac{24 a^2}{E b h^3} = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E b^2 h^3} $$

This is the **standard DCB formula** for Mode I adhesive fracture energy. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given**:
- $P = 120$ N
- $a = 0.04$ m
- $b = 0.025$ m
- $h = 0.005$ m
- $E = 70 \times 10^9$ Pa

**Step 1: Write down the DCB formula.**

$$ G_I = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E b^2 h^3} $$

**Step 2: Compute numerator.**

$$ 12 P^2 a^2 = 12 \times (120)^2 \times (0.04)^2 = 12 \times 14400 \times 0.0016 = 276.48 \text{ N}^2 \cdot \text{m}^2 $$

**Step 3: Compute denominator.**

$$ E b^2 h^3 = (70 \times 10^9) \times (0.025)^2 \times (0.005)^3 $$

$$ = (70 \times 10^9) \times (6.25 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.25 \times 10^{-7}) $$

$$ = (70 \times 10^9) \times (7.8125 \times 10^{-11}) $$

$$ = 5.46875 \text{ N} \cdot \text{m}^2 $$

**Step 4: Divide to obtain $G_I$.**

$$ G_I = \frac{276.48}{5.46875} = 50.56 \text{ J/m}^2 $$

$$ \boxed{G_I \approx 50.6 \text{ J/m}^2} $$

**Interpretation**: This value represents the energy required to propagate the crack by one square meter of new fracture surface. If the adhesive's $G_{Ic}$ is greater than 50.6 J/m², the crack is stable at this load level. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** in numerical problems — this single mistake cascades into a wildly incorrect answer and costs 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Using the plane-stress formula** $G = K^2 / E$ when the problem specifies a **thick** or **constrained** joint — the **plane-strain** form $G = K^2 (1 - \nu^2)/E$ is required.
> 3. **Confusing the geometry factor $Y$** — for a **central crack in an infinite plate**, $Y = 1$, but for an **edge crack**, $Y \approx 1.12$, and for a **finite-width plate**, $Y$ depends on $a/W$.
> 4. **Mixing up $G$ and $G_c$** — $G$ is the **driving force** (energy available), $G_c$ is the **resistance** (material property). Always state: "Crack grows when $G \geq G_c$."
> 5. **Skipping units** in derivations — KTU examiners explicitly deduct marks for inconsistent or missing units in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this the night before the exam.**

- **Fracture mechanics** studies crack initiation and growth in materials with pre-existing flaws; it is the correct framework for adhesive joints (which always contain micro-defects).
- The **strain energy release rate** $G$ is the energy available to drive the crack; the **fracture toughness** $G_c$ (or $K_{Ic}$) is the material's resistance.
- The **Griffith criterion** states: crack propagates when $G \geq G_c = 2\gamma_s$.
- The **Irwin stress intensity factor** is $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$, and the **critical condition** is $K \geq K_c$.
- The **energy–K equivalence** is $G = K^2 / E$ (plane stress) or $G = K^2 (1 - \nu^2) / E$ (plane strain).
- **Mode I** = opening (tensile), **Mode II** = in-plane shear, **Mode III** = out-of-plane shear (tearing).
- The **mode mixity phase angle** is $\psi = \tan^{-1}(K_{II}/K_I)$.
- The **DCB formula** is $G_I = \frac{12 P^2 a^2}{E b^2 h^3}$ — the single most important numerical equation in this module.
- The **J-integral** is the elastic-plastic generalization of $G$, used when significant plasticity develops.
- The **Irwin–Kies relation** $G = \frac{P^2}{2b} \frac{dC}{da}$ links load, compliance, and crack length for any specimen geometry.
- Real adhesive joints always exhibit **mixed-mode** fracture; pure modes are laboratory idealizations.
- The **units** to memorize: $G$ and $G_c$ in J/m² (or N/m); $K$ and $K_c$ in MPa·m^(1/2).
- **For LEFM to be valid**, the plastic zone at the crack tip must be small compared to the crack length — a condition satisfied by most rigid structural adhesives.
- **For EPFM (J-integral, CTOD)**, use it when the adhesive yields significantly — typical for toughened epoxies and rubber-modified systems.
- **Peel test fracture energy** is $G = \frac{P}{b}(1 - \cos\theta)$, where $\theta$ is the peel angle — important for pressure-sensitive adhesives and flexible laminates.
- Always **draw a free-body diagram** and **label the crack length** clearly when solving numerical problems; KTU examiners reward clear, structured solutions.
